Arvind, Sr. Standing Counsel for Department.\n\n                Dates of hearing      : 03.04.2018\n                                        04.04.2018\n                                        05.04.2018\n                                        19.04.2018\n                Date of Pronouncement : 11.05.2018\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n                                       Page 3 of 331\n\n\n                                     ORDER\n</pre><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_1\">PerBench\n\n\n      These appealsare preferred by the assessees as well as the Revenue against the\n\nrespective orders of the CIT(A) pertaining to assessment years 2007-2008 to 2015-2016.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_2\">Since the issues involved in these appeals are interconnected and interrelated, these were\n\nheard together and are being adjudicated through this single consolidated order for the\n\nsake of convenience. We, however, prefer to adjudicate them one after the other as under:\n</p>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1\">2.    IT(IT)A Nos. 1190/Bang/2014, 949&amp;950/Bang/2017\n\n\nThough grounds raised in these appeals are almost similar, but for the sake of reference\n\nwe extract the grounds raised in ITA No.1190/Bang/2014 as under:\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_2\">Based on the facts and in the circumstances of the case, the Ld CIT(A) and the Ld\nAO have:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_3\">1    Erred in holding the payment made by the Appellant to Google Ireland\n     Limited in relation to purchase of advertisement space for resale to the\n     advertisers in India under the Google AdWords Reseller Agreement (&#x27;the\n     Agreement&#x27;) to be in the nature of &#x27;Royalty&#x27; under the Act and the India-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_3\">     Ireland Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement (`lndia-lreland Treaty&#x27;)\n     stating that the issue is covered under the appeals for earlier years.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_4\">2.    Erred in holding that the amount payable towards purchase of\n      advertisement space to be in the nature of &#x27;Royalty&#x27; under the Act, even\n      after acknowledging that the Appellant is distributing/reselling the\n      advertisement space to the advertisers in India.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_5\">                                          IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                           IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                         IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_1\">                                      Page 4 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_6\">3.   Erred in holding the amount payable by the Appellant to Google Ireland\n     Limited as Royalty by attributing the same towards right to use of\n     Trademark, even after concluding that the assesse company was permitted\n     to use the trademarks of Google for the purpose of marketing and\n     distribution of AdWords program.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_7\">4    Erred in holding that the AdWords program is complex computer software,\n     the right to use has been granted to the Appellant without appreciating the\n     fact that AdWords program is a standard advertisement service through\n     which the advertiser is able to advertise its products or services on the\n     Google website.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_8\">5.   Erred in holding that Google Ireland Limited has granted the Appellant the\n     right to use of the AdWords program, which is a complex computer program\n     without parting with the copyright, thus granting licence to use the software\n     without appreciating the fact that the Appellant is only involved in marketing and\n     distribution/resale of the advertisement space to the advertisers in India.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_9\">6.   Erred in holding that the training provided in relation to the advertisement\n     program, its functionality, tools available etc. to the distribution team of the\n     appellant who markets and distributes the same to advertisers in India\n     tantamount to rendering of services to the Appellant even after concluding\n     that such training is restricted to use of the AdWords program and not how\n     to develop the AdWords program.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_10\">7.   Erred in confirming that the distribution rights granted under the\n     distribution/ reseller agreement are itself -IP rights&quot; covered by &quot;similar\n     property&quot; used in Sec 9(1)(vi) of the Act, after holding that as per the\n     distribution/reseller agreement Google Ireland Limited has agreed to\n     provide advertisement space to the Appellant through AdWords program\n     for distribution to the Indian Advertisers.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_11\">8.   Erred in confirming that Distribution Agreement/Reseller Agreement\n     cannot be read without the service agreement (ITES agreement) between the\n     Appellant and Google Ireland Limited and the Appellant has been granted\n     right to use intellectual property owned by Google Ireland Limited without\n                                                IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                      IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                               IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_2\">                                            Page 5 of 331</span>\n\n           appreciating the fact that ITES service agreement is a separate agreement\n           under which the Appellant performs an independent global outsourcing\n           function for Google Ireland Limited for which it receives arm&#x27;s length\n           consideration and is not linked in any manner to the function of sale of\n           advertisement space to the Indian advertisers being performed by the\n           Appellant.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_12\">9.         Without appreciating the facts of the case, erred in holding that the amount\n           payable by the Appellant to Google Ireland Limited towards purchase of\n           advertisement space to be in the nature of &#x27;Royalty&#x27; under Section 9(1)(vi) of the\n           Act.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_13\">     10.   Erred in upholding the order of the Ld JDIT that the amount payable by the\n           Appellant to Google Ireland Limited is towards right to use of trademark\n           and copyrighted computer program and process. hence is in the nature of\n           &#x27;Royalty&#x27; as per the <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_1\">Article 12</a> of the India Ireland Double Taxation\n           Avoidance Agreement.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_14\">     11.   Erred in not following the principle laid down by Hon&#x27;ble Mumbai Tribunal\n           in the case of Yahoo India Pvt Ltd (140 TTJ (Mumbai) 195) and Pinstorm\n           Technologies Pvt Ltd (2012) 54 SOT 78 (Mumbai) on similar facts by stating\n           that the facts and issues are completely different and at no stage the Mumbai\n           Tribunal consider what exactly is the AdWords Program, nor did it have\n           occasion to examine the right to use trademark or other IP rights.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_15\">     12.   Erred in not following the decision of the Calcutta Tribunal in the case of Income\n           Tax Officer vs Right Florists Pvt Ltd (ITA No.1336/Ko1/2011) on similar facts.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_16\">The Appellant craves, to consider each of the above grounds of appeal without prejudice\nto one another and craves leave to add, alter, delete or modify all or any of the above\ngrounds of appeal.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_17\">3.         In IT(IT)A Nos.949 &amp;950/Bang/2017,the appellant has also assailed the order of\n\nthe CIT(A) denying the benefit of beneficial ownership by raising following\n\ncommongrounds:-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_4\">                                                    IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                          IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                   IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_3\">                                                Page 6 of 331</span>\n\nBeneficial ownership of payments under the DTAA\n\nNot adhering to the rule of consistency by not following the Ld CIT(A)&#x27;s decision for AY 203-14 in\nthe Appellant&#x27;s very own case and taking contradictory views without considering:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_5\">     \uf0b7   Certificates issued by the Irish Revenue Authorities stating that GIL is a tax resident of\n         Ireland and its world-wide income is taxed in Ireland\n\n     \uf0b7   Fact that there was neither any change in facts of the Appellant vis-\u00e0-vis the previous year\n         nor was any new material fact brought on record by the Ld AO\n\n     \uf0b7   Not providing an opportunity of being heard to the Appellant and thereby violating\n         principles of natural justice\n\n     \uf0b7   Rule of consistency requires that when contradictory views are taken vis-\u00e0-vis the previous\n         years, reasons for such contradictory views would need to be specified\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_18\">4.       During the course of pendency of the appeal, assessee has also moved an application\n\nfor the admission of the following additional grounds:\n</p>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_6\">&quot;Withholding liability on Royalty to arise only on payment basis under the India-Ireland Double\nTaxation Avoidance Agreement (&#x27;DTAA&#x27;)\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_7\"> 13. Without prejudice to the argument that the payments made by the Appellant to Google\n         Ireland are not in the nature of Royalty as per <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_2\">Article 12</a> of India-Ireland DTAA, the\n         Learned Assessing Officer / Learned Commissioner of Income-Tax (Appeals) erred in\n         holding that the Appellant is liable to withhold tax on amounts payable to Google Ireland\n         disregarding that &#x27;Royalty&#x27; income for a non-resident is taxable only on receipt basis\n         under the India-Ireland DTAA.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_8\">The Appellant craves, to consider the above ground of appeal without prejudice to other grounds of\nappeal and craves leave to add, alter, delete or modify the above ground of appeal.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_19\">5.       Though various grounds are raised but they all relate to the characterization of the\n\npayment made by the appellant to M/s. Google Ireland Ltd., with regard to purchase of\n                                                IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                      IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                               IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_4\">                                            Page 7 of 331</span>\n\nadvertisement space for resale to the advertisers in India under the Google AdWords\n\nprogram distribution agreement. The facts in all these years are almost similar subject to\n\nvariation in quantum. Therefore, we record the facts for the assessment year 2013-14 in\n\nIT(IT)A No. 1190/Bang/2014. The facts in brief borne out from the record in this regard\n\nare that the assessee company i.e., Google India Pvt. Ltd., (hereinafter called as &quot;GIPL&quot;),\n\nhaving its registered office at Bangalore was incorporated on 16.12.2003, as a wholly\n\nowned subsidiary of Google International LLC, US. The GIPL is engaged in the business\n\nof providing Information Technology (IT) and Information Technology enabled Services\n\n(ITES) to its group companies. It also acts as a distributor for AdWord programs in India.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_20\">The assessee company&#x27;s activities was classified by the CIT(A) in its order in 3 segments\n\nwhich are as under:\n</p>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_9\">   \uf0d8 IT services :- GIPL has entered into a service agreement with Google Inc to render software\n       development services.Google India&#x27;s research and development units at Bangalore, Hyderabad\n       and Gurgaon provide IT services including application development, maintenance and testing\n       services. For these services, GIPL, is remunerated at cost plus 17.5%\n\n   \uf0d8 IT enabled services :- GIPL has entered into a service agreement with Google Ireland Limited\n       (Google Ireland) to render IT enabled services. Its service centre at Hyderabad and Gurgaon\n       provide IT enabled services relating to the administration of advertisements in accordance with\n       the guidelines provided by Google Ireland and provide customer support services. For these\n       services, GIPL is remunerated at cost plus 15.5%.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_10\">   \uf0d8 Marketing and distribution services for the AdWords program :- Under the Google\n       AdWords Program Distribution Agreement dated 12.12.2005 (Agreement) entered into\n       between GIPL and Google Ireland, Google India is appointed as a non-exclusive authorised\n       distributor of AdWords program to the advertisers in India.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_11\">                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_5\">                                       Page 8 of 331</span>\n\n\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_21\">6.    The main issue raised in this appeal is with regard to the non-deduction of TDS on\n\npayments made by the appellant to M/s. Google Ireland Ltd., as the AO characterized the\n\npayment made by the appellant to M/s. Google Ireland Ltd., as royalty. Under the Adword\n\nProgram distribution agreement, the agreement was entered into between GIPL and\n\nGoogle Ireland Ltd., (hereinafter called as &quot;GIL). The GIPL was granted the marketing\n\nand distribution rights of Adwords Program to advertisers in India and the GIPL is\n\nremunerated on cost plus market basis for the distribution services under Adword\n\nPrograms.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_22\">7.    On verification of the financial statements for the assessment year\n\n2012-13, the AO noticed that GIPL had credited a sum of Rs.111,491,99,289/- to the\n\naccount of M/s. Google Ireland Ltd., (GIL) without deduction of tax at source. The GIL\n\nhad also not obtained the Nil deduction certificate on the sums payable to it from the\n\ndepartment. Reliance upon the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/1329583/\" id=\"a_3\">section 195</a> of the IT Act (hereinafter called\n\nas an &quot;Act&quot;) was placed according to which any person responsible for paying to non-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_23\">resident, not being a company, or to a foreign company, any interest or any other sum\n\nchargeable under provisions of the Act (not being income chargeable under the head\n\nsalaries) shall at the time of credit of such income to the account of the payee or at the\n\ntime of payment there-of in cash or by the issue of cheque or draft by any other mode\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_6\">                                       Page 9 of 331</span>\n\nwhichever is earlier, deduct income tax thereon at the rates in force, and the AO issued a\n\nshow cause notice to the appellant (GIPL) as to why he should not be treated as assessee\n\nin default in respect of tax not deducted at source on sums payable to GIL under <a href=\"/doc/1049794/\" id=\"a_4\">section\n\n201(1)</a> of the Act. The GIPL was asked to furnish details of sums paid or payable for the\n\nfinancial year 2012-13, relevant to the impugned assessment year and the deduction of tax\n\nat source thereon. In response thereto, the assessee has filed the submissions where from\n\nit was noticed by the AO that during the impugned assessment year, GIPL had credited a\n\nsum of Rs.111,491,99,289/- to the account of GIL towards marketing and distribution\n\nrights of Adword Program without deduction of tax at source.                           A sum of\n\nRs.795,28,87,731/-      had     been     remitted       during        the       financial       year\n\n2012-13 and the balance of Rs.319,63,11,558/- was outstanding.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_24\">8.    The AO has further observed that assessee company is an Indian company and the\n\npayee is foreign company based in Ireland, with their residential status being that of non-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_25\">resident and as per section 5(2) of the Act, the total income of any previous year of any\n\nperson who is a non-resident includes all incomes from whatever source derived which is\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_26\">(a) is received or is deemed to have been received in India in such year or by or on behalf\n\nof such person or (b) accrued or arised or is deemed to have been accrued or arised to him\n\nin India during such year.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_27\">                                              IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                               IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                             IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_7\">                                         Page 10 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_28\">9.    The AO further observed that <a href=\"/doc/1369261/\" id=\"a_5\">section 9</a> of the Act deals with the income deemed to\n\naccrue or arise in India and as per <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_6\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the Act, inter alia, any income by\n\nway of royalty payable by a person who is a resident, shall be deemed to accrue or arise\n\nin India to the recipient except where the royalty is payable in respect of any right, property\n\nor information used or services utilised for the purpose of business or profession, carried\n\non by such person outside India or for the purpose of making or earning any income from\n\nany source outside India.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_29\">10.   The AO accordingly issued a show cause to the assessee to explain as to why the\n\nsum payable to GIL towards marketing and distribution rights of Adword Programme\n\nshould not be treated as royalty as per provisions of <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_7\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the Act. Assessee\n\nwas also show caused to explain as to why provision of <a href=\"/doc/1049794/\" id=\"a_8\">section 201(1)</a> should not be\n\ninvoked in this case and it be treated as assessee in default in respect of the tax not\n\ndeducted at source. In response to show cause notice, the appellant filed the submissions\n\non various dates where from it was observed by the AO that under the distribution\n\nagreement, the GIPL has acquired marketing and distribution rights over Adword\n\nProgrammes for the territory of India from GIL. The distribution of Adword Programme\n\ninvolves 3 parties i.e., Licensor, the reseller or distributor and the advertiser. In this case,\n\nthe licensor is GIL, the distributor or reseller is GIPL and the end users are the advertisers.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_30\">The assessee has taken a stand that the amount payable to the GIL is not in the nature of\n                                                     IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                      IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                           IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                    IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_8\">                                                Page 11 of 331</span>\n\nroyalty either under the Act or the India-Ireland Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement\n\n(hereinafter called as &quot;DTAA&quot;). The gist of submissions made by the GIPL are extracted\n\nby the AO in his order and for the sake of reference, we extract the same as under:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_12\">      \uf0b7   As per Agreement between the assessee company and Google Ireland, GIPL is appointed as\n          a mere non-exclusive Distributor/Reseller of AdWords program to the advertisers in India;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_13\">      \uf0b7   Distribution fee is payable to Google Ireland on distribution of AdWords inIndia and is not\n          in relation to any &#x27;transfer of any right&#x27; or any &#x27;right to use&#x27; any patent/ invention etc.;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_14\">      \uf0b7   The Agreement does not involve any use of patents, invention, model, design, secret formula\n          or process or trade mark or similar property. Further, all the rights, title and interest in and\n          to all information &amp; data including the user data (i.e. data provided by users) are owned by\n          Google Ireland;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_15\">      \uf0b7   Further, the distribution fee payable under the Agreement is not in relation to any knowledge\n          concerning a patent or invention and is neither concerned with use or right to use of any\n          scientific equipment.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_16\">      <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"89798900\" id=\"span_9\">\uf0b7   Reliance was placed on decisions of the Hon&#x27;ble Mumbai ITAT in the case of Pin storm\n          Technologies Private Limited v. ITO(ITA No 4332/Mum/2009) and <a href=\"/doc/89798900/\" id=\"a_9\">Yahoo India Pvt Ltd v DCIT</a> [(2011) 59\n          DIR 1].</span>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_17\">      \uf0b7   Therefore the sums paid/payable are not taxable under the <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_10\">Income Tax Act,1961</a> (&quot;Act&quot;) and\n          India-Ireland DTAA.\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_31\">11.       The AO has examined the explanations and the submissions explaining the\n\nfunctioning of the appellant but was not convinced with the contentions of the assessees\n\nand he concluded that the payments made by the assessee to GIL is the royalty payment\n\nfor which assessee is under obligation to deduct tax at source as stipulated under <a href=\"/doc/1329583/\" id=\"a_11\">section\n\n195</a> of the IT Act. Since the assessee failed to discharge its obligations, the asesssee\n\ncompany/GIPL was held to be assessee in default in respect of tax not deducted at source\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_10\">                                       Page 12 of 331</span>\n\non amounts credited to GIL. The AO accordingly worked out the tax liability of the\n\nappellant under <a href=\"/doc/1049794/\" id=\"a_12\">section 201(1)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/613881/\" id=\"a_13\">201(1A)</a> for the impugned assessment year and issued\n\na demand notice of Rs.258,84,40,845/-.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_32\">12.   Besides, it was also held by the AO that GIL is not the beneficial owner of the\n\namounts received/receivable from the appellant in relation to sale of advertisement space\n\nunder the agreement on the ground that Google Inc., owns the search engine and Adword\n\nProgramm and other hodings are also involved in Adword program and are entitled to\n\nshare the revenue generated under the Adword agreement\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_33\">13.   Aggrieved, assessee preferred an appeal before the CIT(A) and the CIT(A), on the\n\npoint of characterization of the payment made by GIPL to GIL has held that it is a royalty\n\npayment, following its earlier order for the assessment year 2006-07 to 2012-13. Since\n\nthere is no discussion in the order of the CIT(A) while holding the nature of payment by\n\nGIPL to GIL as royalty, except the reference of its earlier order for assessment year 2006-\n\n07 to 2012-13,we are required to examine the findings given by the CIT(A) in that order.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_34\">The order of the CIT(A) for 2006-07 to 2012-13 has already been confirmed by the\n\nTribunal against which an appeal is pending before the Hon&#x27;ble High Court of Karnataka.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_35\">The Hon&#x27;ble High Court of Karnataka has passed orders in ITA Nos.879/2017 and\n\n898/2017 dated 15..2017 and 20.11.2017 respectively that the present appeals are to be\n\ndisposed off independently without being influenced with the order of the Tribunal dated\n                                              IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                               IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                             IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_11\">                                         Page 13 of 331</span>\n\n23.10.2017 passed for the assessment years 2006-07 to 2012-13. Therefore, we cannot\n\nlook to the order of the Tribunal for assessment years 2006-07 to 2012-13, confirming the\n\norder of the CIT(A) in this regard but we are supposed to adjudicate the issues of nature\n\nof payment in the light of the finding of CIT(A) for assessment year 2006-07 to 2012-13\n\nindependently as it was relied on by the CIT(A) while adjudicating the impugned issue\n\nwithout being influenced by the order of the Tribunal. In appeal filed before the CIT(A)\n\nagainst the assessment orders for assessment years 2006-07 to 2012-13, the assessee has\n\ndisputed the findings of the AO with regard to nature of payment made by GIPL to GIL.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_36\">14.    The CIT(A) while adjudicating the characterization of nature of payment made by\n\nGIPL to GIL, has examined and analysed the agreement entered into by the\n\nassessee/appellant with GIL and Google AdWord Programs how it operates in the light\n\nof information available on internet and also in the light of various judicial\n\npronouncements rendered by the Tribunal and held that nature of payment made by GIPL\n\nto GIL is royalty. The relevant observation of the CIT(A) are extracted hereunder for\n\nthe sake of reference:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_18\">&quot;Before I proceed to examine the issues raised before me, it is pertinent to give a proper\nfinding with respect to the Adwords program. From the discussion reproduced in the body\nof this order, it is very clear that the Adword program is actually computer software by\nwhich the advertiser is able to publish an advertisement on the &quot;Google Website&quot;. And it is\nafter utilizing a complex computer program, the clients gain access to the advertiser&#x27;s webpage.\nThe contention of the assessee that AdWords is an automated, self-serve system through which\n                                                  IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                        IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                 IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_12\">                                             Page 14 of 331</span>\n\nadvertisers independently develop a set of keywords that relate to their business and manage\n                                              their own account online from the Goodie website\n                                              and therefore, it has simply rented out space on the\n                                              &quot;Goodie Website&quot; and charged the advertiser as\n                                              newspapers are akin to doing, is a fallacy. It is\n                                              evident that the Adwords program is a complex\n                                              computer program developed and owned by Google\n                                              Ireland. A computer programme is a process when it\n                                              executes instructions lying in it in passive state. It is\n                \u2022\n                                              built    upon    and    uses    sophisticated     computer\ntechnology as under:-\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_19\">&quot;The AdWords system was initially implemented on top of the MySQL database engine. After\nthe system had been launched, management decided to use Oracle                     instead. The system\nbecame much slower, so eventually it was returned to MySQL. Eventually, Google developed\na custom distributed Relational database management .system (RDBMS) known as Google\nFl specifically for the needs of the Ad business, which requires strong consistency, high\nscalability across data centers and powerful SQL queries. The interface has also been\nrevamped to offer better work flow with additional new features, such as Spreadsheet Editing,\nSearch Query Reports, and better conversion metrics.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_37\">10.2. Google Fl is a Relational Database Management System and it is undoubtedly a computer\nprogram within the meaning of the Act as is evident from the following illustration:-\n</p>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_20\">10.3. As per Wikipedia, a computer program, or just a program, is a sequence of instructions,\nwritten to perform a specified task with a computer. A computer requires programs to function,\ntypically executing the program&#x27;s instructions in a central processor. The program has an\nexecutable form that the computer can use directly to execute the instructions. The same\nprogram in its human-readable source code form, from which executable programs are derived\n(e.g., compiled), enables a programmer to study and develop its algorithms. A collection of\ncomputer programs and related data is referred to as the software.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_38\">                                                      IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                       IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                            IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                     IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_13\">                                              Page 15 of 331</span>\n\n10.4. The definition of computer program in the copyright Act of 1957, is as under:\n</p>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_21\">&quot;<a href=\"/doc/545792/\" id=\"a_14\">Section 2(ffc)</a> &quot;computer programme&quot; means a set of instructions expressed in words, codes, schemes\nor in any other form, Including a machine readable medium, capable of causing a computer to perform\na particular task or achieve a particular result.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_39\">10.5. In a recent decision, the Delhi Bench of the Hon&#x27;ble Tribunal in Gracemac Corporation\nv. Assistant Director of Income-tax 008 ITR (Trib) 522 had an occasion to examine the term\n&quot;Computer Program&quot; vis-a-vis the term &quot;Process&quot; used in the Act as well as the Indo-US Tax\nTreaty and observed &quot;It is a golden rule of interpretation of statutes that the language of the\nstatute should be read as it is. The intention of the Legislature is primarily to be gathered from\nthe language used, which means that attention should be paid to what has been said as also to what\nhas not been said. As a consequence a construction which requires for its support addition or\nsubstitution of words or which results in rejection of words as meaningless has to be avoided.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_40\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"752722\" id=\"span_14\"><a href=\"/doc/752722/\" id=\"a_15\">Director General, Telecommunication v. T. N. Peethambaram</a> [1986] 4 SCC 348 relied on.\nThe use of punctuation marks cannot be said redundant.</span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_41\">The need for interpretation arises when words used in the statute are, on their own terms,\nambivalent and do not manifest the intention of the Legislature. Where the word is plain and\nunambiguous, it is not for judges to invent fancied ambiguities as an excuse for failing to give\neffect to its plain meaning because they themselves consider that the consequences of doing so\nwould be inexpedient, or even unjust or immoral.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_42\">The words of a statute are first understood in their natural, ordinary or popular sense and phrases\nand sentences are construed according to their grammatical meaning unless that leads to some\nabsurdity or unless there is something in the context, or in the object of the statute to suggest the\ncontrary. If the language of the statute is not clear and there is need to resort to aids of\nconstruction, such aids can be either internal or external.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_43\">                                                   IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                         IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                  IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_15\">                                              Page 16 of 331</span>\n\n\nIn the definition of &quot;royalty&quot; in clause (v) of Explanation 2 to <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_16\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the Income-tax\nAct, 1961, the Legislature has used a comma after the word &quot;copyright&quot; indicating the legislative\nintent to treat &quot;copyright&quot; independently of the words &quot;literary, artistic or scientific work&quot;.\nTherefore, &quot;copyright&quot; cannot be read in conjunction with the words literary, artistic or\nscientific work by substituting the comma with the word &quot;of&#x27;. The definition of &quot;royalty&quot; is\ninclusive. Deletion of the comma and addition of the word &quot;of&#x27; between words &quot;copyright&quot; and\n&quot;literary&quot; would limit the scope royalty only to copyright work relating to literary, artistic or\nscientific work including films or video tapes for use in connection with television or tapes for\nuse in connection with radio broadcasting. The term &quot;copyright&quot; is wide enough to include other\nworks such as dramatic or musical work, computer programme, cinematograph film and a sound\nrecording as included in clauses (a), (b), (d) and (e) of <a href=\"/doc/1129646/\" id=\"a_17\">section 14</a> of the Copyright Act, 1957.\nThe language employed in clause (v) of Explanation 2 to <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_18\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the 1961 Act is plain,\nclear and unambiguous and is not capable of two meanings. Therefore, there is no need\nsubstitution of the comma by the word &quot;of&#x27;.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_44\">The expression &quot;copyrighted article&quot; is not defined either in the 1961 Act or in th, 7-. Double\nTaxation Avoidance Agreement between India and the U.S.A. (DTAA). Nor there a definition in\nthe <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_19\">Copyright Act, 1957</a> of &quot;copy-righted article&quot; on the lines as in the <a href=\"/doc/1937976/\" id=\"a_20\">Patents Act, 1970</a>. The\nterm &quot;copy-righted article&quot; is nowhere used in the Act or the Double Taxation Avoidance\nAgreement. The expression &quot;copyrighted article&quot; finds its origin in U. S. regulations and then in\nthe OECD commentary. The OECD commentary or IRS Regulations of the U. S. A. would not be\na safe or acceptable guide or aid for interpretation of provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_21\">Income-tax Act, 1961</a> or\nthe Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement between India and another country. The language\nused in Explanation 2 to <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_22\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the Act or <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_23\">article 12(3)</a> of the Double Taxation\nAvoidance Agreement defining the term &quot;royalty&quot; is not ambiguous. For the purposes of\ninterpretation of the term &quot;royalty&quot; in respect of computer software reliance cannot be placed on\nthe difference made in the OECD commentary or in the U. S. regulations between the expressions\n&quot;the transfer of a copyright right&quot; and &quot;the transfer of a copyrighted article&quot;. The question of\nroyalty in respect of computer software has to be decided on the basis of provisions of the Act\nand the Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement. Hence for the purposes of income-tax a\ncopyrighted article cannot be treated as product.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_45\">                                                  IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                        IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                 IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_16\">                                             Page 17 of 331</span>\n\nCIT v. P. V. A. L. Kulandagan Chettiar [2004] 267 ITR 654 (SC) applied.\nA computer programme is a literary work under the <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_24\">Copyright Act, 1957</a> and the consideration\nreceived will be in the nature of royalty if it is in respect of the transfer of all or any rights\n(including the granting of a licence) in respect thereof under clause (v) of Explanation 2 to\n<a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_25\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the 1961 Act. The definition of royalty in Explanation 2 to <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_26\">section 9(1)(vi)</a>\nand <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_27\">article 12(3)</a> of the Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement takes into its ambit both\nindustrial as well as copyright royalties. It will not be appropriate to restrict the scope of the\ndefinition of royalty under the income-tax law only on the ground that since computer\nprogramme has been provided protection under the <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_28\">Copyright Act</a>, the provisions of other\nintellectual property rights laws will not be applicable.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_46\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1671011\" id=\"span_17\">A computer programme is a process when it executes instructions lying in it in passive state. Therefore,\nany consideration made for the use of process would amount to royalty.\nExplanation 2 has to be read as part and parcel of <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_29\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the Act.\n<a href=\"/doc/1671011/\" id=\"a_30\">Keshavji Ravji and Co. v. CIT</a> [1990] 183 ITR 1 (SC) applied.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_47\">The second proviso to <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_31\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> carves out an exception from the main section exempting\nlump sum payment made by a resident for the transfer of all or any rights (including the granting\nof a licence) in respect of computer software supplied by a non-resident manufacturer along with\na computer or computer-based equipment under any scheme approved under the Policy on\nComputer Software Export, Software Export, Software Development and Training, 1986 of the\nGovernment of India. It cannot stand on its own and hence cannot be held a substantive provision.\nTherefore, <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_32\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> from the very inception included computer software for the purposes\nof royalty. If royalty income from the use or the right to use or transfer of all or any right\n(including the granting of the licence) in respect of copyright in computer programme was not\ntaxable under <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_33\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the Act, Parliament would not have prescribed a special rate of\nincome-tax in respect of royalty income in respect of any computer software under <a href=\"/doc/279820/\" id=\"a_34\">section\n115A(1A)</a>. The provisions of <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_35\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> have to be considered in the light of the provisions\nof <a href=\"/doc/279820/\" id=\"a_36\">section 115A(1A)</a> of the Act. The use of the expressions &quot;in respect of copyright in any book to\nan Indian concern&quot; or &quot;in respect of any computer software to a person resident in India&quot; in\n<a href=\"/doc/279820/\" id=\"a_37\">section 115A(1A)</a> shows that for the purposes of income-tax copyright in &quot;computer software&quot; is\n                                               IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                              IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_18\">                                          Page 18 of 331</span>\n\n\ndifferent from copyright in any &quot;book&quot; though both are literary works under the <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_38\">Copyright Act,\n1957</a>.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_48\">The Income-tax Act and the Copyright Act operate in different fields. The object of the <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_39\">Copyright\nAct</a> is to provide protection to the copyrights in various works of the authors whereas the purpose\nof income-tax is levy and collect tax on various types of incomes. The provisions of the Income-\ntax Act cannot be explained by resorting to various provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_40\">Copyright Act, 1957</a>.\nHowever, a limited reference to understand the meaning of term &quot;copyright&quot; can be made to the\n<a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_41\">Copyright Act, 1957</a> as this term has not been defined under the 1961 Act.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_49\">Under the Explanation inserted in <a href=\"/doc/874582/\" id=\"a_42\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the 1961 Act with effect from June 1, 1976\nby the <a href=\"/doc/61349371/\" id=\"a_43\">Finance Act, 2007</a> and again substituted by the <a href=\"/doc/75703920/\" id=\"a_44\">Finance Act, 2010</a> the income of a non-\nresident shall be deemed to accrue or arise in India under clause (v) or clause (vi) or clause\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_50\">(vii) irrespective of whether the non-resident has a residence or a place of business or business\nconnection in India or the non-resident has rendered services in India. Therefore, once the\nconsideration is received by a non-resident for the transfer or all or any rights including the\ngranting of a licence in respect of a patent, invention, model, design, secret formula or process\nor similar property or any copy-right literary, artistic or scientific work, the consideration\nreceived shall be deemed to accrue or arise in India and will be taxable in India. Hence, by\nvirtue of the amended Explanation to <a href=\"/doc/874582/\" id=\"a_45\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> royalty income will be taxable in India\nwhether or not the non- resident has a residence or place of business or business connection\nin India. The effect of an Agreement made pursuant to section 90 of the 1961 Act is that if no\ntax liability is imposed under this Act, the question of resorting to the Agreement would not\narise. No provision of the Agreement can fasten a tax liability when the liability is not imposed\nby this Act. If a tax liability is imposed by this Act, the Agreement may be resorted to for\nnegativing or reducing it. In case of difference between the provisions of the Act and of an\nAgreement under section 90, the provisions of the Agreement shall prevail over the provisions\nof the Act and can be enforced by an appellate authority or the court. However, as provided\nby sub-section (2) the provisions of this Act will apply to the assessee in the event they are\n                                                  IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                        IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                 IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_19\">                                             Page 19 of 331</span>\n\n\nmore beneficial to him. Where there is no specific provision in the Agreement, it is the basic\nlaw, i. e., the <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_46\">Income-tax Act</a> which will govern the taxation of income.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_51\">The definitions of term &quot;royalty&quot; in Explanation 2 to <a href=\"/doc/874582/\" id=\"a_47\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the 1961 Act and\nparagraph (3) of <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_48\">article 12</a> of the Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement are identical.\nAccording to paragraph (7)(a) royalties shall be deemed to arise in a Contracting State when\nthe payer is that State itself, a political sub-division, a local authority, or a resident of that\nState. If the person paying royalty has a permanent establishment or a fixed base there in\nconnection with which the liability to pay the royalties is incurred and the liability is borne by\nsuch establishment or base the royalty is deemed to arise in the State in which the permanent\nestablishment or fixed base is situated. According to paragraph (7)(b) royalties related to the\nuse of, or the right to use, the right or property, in one of the Contracting States, shall be\ndeemed to arise in that Contracting State. No conflict exists between the <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_49\">Income-tax Act</a> and\nthe Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_52\">The language of <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_50\">article 12(7)(b)</a> of the Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement between India\nand the United States of America is clear and unambiguous. The royalties or fees for included\nservices shall be deemed to arise in that Contracting State in which the use of, or the right to\nuse, the right or property, or the fees for included services is performed. The term &quot;right&quot; in\n<a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_51\">article 12(7)(b)</a> cannot be interpreted to mean &quot;copyright&quot; as used in the <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_52\">article 12(3)(a).</a>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_53\">The Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement with the United States of America was entered on\nDecember 20, 1990. By an amendment to <a href=\"/doc/874582/\" id=\"a_53\">section 9(1)</a> by insertion of Explanation with effect\nfrom June 1, 1976 the royalties will be deemed to accrue or arise in India whether or not the\nnon-resident has a residence or place of business or business connection in India. Therefore,\nby this amendment the income by way of royalty will be deemed to accrue or arise irrespective\nof a contrary provision in the Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement.&quot;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_54\"> 11.    In this backdrop, the issues raised are considered individually :-\n</p>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_22\"> Distribution fee payable under the agreement is a relation to use or right to use Intellectual property,\n knowhow, trademark &amp; other brand features :\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_23\">                                                 IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                       IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_20\">                                            Page 20 of 331</span>\n\n\n11.1. As is evident from the extracts reproduced from the assessment order earlier, the AO on\nexamination of agreements came to the conclusion that the Google Ireland holds the intellectual\nproperty, knowhow, trademark and other brand features with respect to products and services offered\nby the Adwords_program. And vide the distribution agreement, the assessee company has been granted\nrights to market and distribute Adwords program in India In this process, Google Ireland has permitted\nthe assessee company to access all intellectual property and confidential information which is utilized\nby the 1TES division of the assessee. He also observed that the job of the assessee does not end with\nmarking and distribution of Adword program but also has to support existing customers by providing\ncustomer services to the advertisers in consonance with standards maintained by Google. It is also\nrequired to process customer queries related to advertisement issues and escalate the same to Google\nIreland. The AO has also noted that before the advertiser ca-run his campaign, the same has to\nbe cleared as per editorial and trademark guidelines, hence the assessee had to monitor advertiser\nactivity and gather other information about advertisers as specified. The distribution agreement\ncannot be read without the service agreement and on combined reading, it is clear that the\nassessee company has been granted the right to use valuable business assets of Google Ireland\nwhich includes intellectual property in the products and services offered by Google Ireland.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_55\">Therefore, the distribution rights granted are itself IP rights covered by &quot;similar property used\nin <a href=\"/doc/874582/\" id=\"a_54\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the Act&quot;. Thereafter, he concluded since IP of Google resides in search\nengine technology, associated software and other features, logically the income received by it\nincludes royalty for its IP from all its non-retail users viz., Advertisers and Intermediaries.\nTherefore, the assessee company being the intermediary has obtained the right to use the\nintellectualproperty, knowhow, trademark and other brand features owned by Google\nlreland.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_56\">11.2. The AO after examining Clause-1.5 &amp; Clause-6 of the distribution agreement concluded\nthat the assessee company was permitted to use the trademarks of Google for the purposes of\nmarketing and distribution of Adwords program. Afterexamining Clause-3 of the agreement, he\nopined that Google Ireland is obliged toprovide advertising space through itsdistributor i.e. the\nassessee company and is also obliged to train the distributor, so that it can market and distribute\nthe Adwords program. The Adwords platform runs on servers located outside India and the assessee\n                                                    IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                          IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                   IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_21\">                                               Page 21 of 331</span>\n\n\ncompany cannot perform its activities of marketing and distribution without training which amounts\nto transfer of knowhow. The training relates to internal tools and is provided to the staff of the\nassessee company only. Thus, he was of the view that technical knowhow was imparted to the\nassessee company for the purpose of marketing, sale of the Adword program as also\nmaintenance of Adword accounts,after sales, service, billing, etc. Hence, the sums remitted to\nRoyalty within the meaning of the Act. I have examined the facts and circumstances of the case.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_57\">11.3. The relevant provisions of the Act to be examined are as under :-\n</p>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_24\">     &quot;In Sec.9(1)(vi) of the Income Tax Act, 1961, the term &#x27;royalty&#x27; has the head &#x27;capital\n     gains) for\n\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_25\">     (i)   the transfer of all or any rights (including the granting of a licence) in respect of a patent,\n data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_58\">           or\n                                                     IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n Intellectual property and trademark can be defined as under :-\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_31\">          &quot;Intellectual property (p) is a legal concept which refers to creations of the mind for which exclusive\n          rights are recognized. [1] Under intellectual property law, owners are granted certain exclusive\n          rights to a variety of intangible assets, such as musical, literary, and artistic works; discoveries\n          and inventions; and words, phrases, symbols, and designs. Common types of intellectual property\n          rights include copyright, trademarks,patents,industrial design rights,trade dress, and in some\n          jurisdictions trade secrets.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_32\">         A trademark, trade mark, or trade-mark[1] is a recognizable sign,design or expression which\n                                                                                                  [2][3][4][5]\n         identifies products or services of a particular source from those of others.                            The\n         trademark owner can be an individual, business organization, or any legal entity. A\n                                                    IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                          IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                   IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_23\">                                               Page 23 of 331</span>\n\n\n          trademark may be located on a package, a label, a voucher or on the product itself. For the\n          sake of corporate identity trademarks are also being displayed on company buildings.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_68\">   11.5. As per the service agreement Google Ireland shares the Confidential information\n   which is defined to be:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_33\">          &quot;Confidential Information&quot; shall mean all data and information of a confidential ;;Mature, including\n          know-holy and trade secrets, relating to the business, the affairs, the products, the development\n          projects or other products or services of Google Ireland or its suppliers or its\n          affiliate, including but not limited to intellectual Property.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_34\">11.6. Google Ireland as per the service agreement shares Confidential information in the following\nmanner:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_35\">       CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_36\">       Access and Use of Confidential Information. During the course of performance of this\n     Agreement; Google Ireland will disclose certain Confidential Information to Google India\n     solely to permit Google India to perform its obligations under this Agreement. Except as\n     otherwise provided in this Agreement, Google India agrees that such Confidential\n     Information shall be kept secret by Google India during the term of this Agreement and\n     after the expiration hereof Google India shall retrain from using or exploiting any and all\n     Confidential Information for any purposes or activities other than those contemplated in\n     this Agreement.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_37\">11.7. After considering the express terms in the service agreement, the finding of the AO with\nregard to right to use intellectual property as well as trademark is confirmed. The issue with\nrespect to transfer of technical knowhow will be dealt later under the head &quot;imparting of\ninformation&quot;.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_69\">Use or right to use the process in Adwords program was granted by Google Ireland &amp; That the fee\npayable is for granting of a licence in respect of a copy right in the Adwords program :-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_38\">These two issues are taken together as they are interrelated. I have studied he Adwords program in\ndetail and it is very clear to my mind that the Adwords program owned and developed by Google\nIreland is a Computer Program within the meaning of Explanations -- 3, 4 &amp; 5 to <a href=\"/doc/874582/\" id=\"a_55\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of\nthe Act. As per the Delhi Bench of the Hon&#x27;ble Tribunal (supra) the following principles emerge:-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_39\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1725672\" id=\"span_24\">A computer programme is a process when it executes instructions I in it in passive state.\nTherefore, any consideration made for the use of process would amount to royalty.\nThe term &quot;copyright&quot; is wide enough to include other works such as dramatic or musical work,\ncomputer programme,\nThe question of royalty in respect of computer software has to he decided on the basis of\nprovisions of the Act and the Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement. Hence for the purposes\nof income-tax a copyrighted article cannot be treated as product. <a href=\"/doc/1725672/\" id=\"a_56\">CIT v. P. V. A. L.\nKulandagan Chettiar</a> [2004] 267 ITR 654 (SC) applied.</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_40\">                                                   IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                         IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                  IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_25\">                                              Page 24 of 331</span>\n\nA computer programme is a literary work under the <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_57\">Copyright Act, 1957</a> and the\nconsideration received will be in the nature of royalty if it is in respect of the transfer of all\nor any rights (including the granting of a licence) in respect thereof under clause (v) of\nExplanation 2 to <a href=\"/doc/874582/\" id=\"a_58\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the 1961 Act. The definition of royalty in Explanation 2\nto <a href=\"/doc/874582/\" id=\"a_59\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_60\">article 12(3)</a> of the Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement takes into\nits ambit both industrial as well as copyright royalties. It will not be appropriate to restrict\nthe scope of the definition, of royalty under the income-tax law only on the ground that since\ncomputer programme has been provided protection under the <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_61\">Copyright Act</a>, the provisions\nof other intellectual property rights laws will not be applicable.\nTherefore, <a href=\"/doc/874582/\" id=\"a_62\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> from the very inception included computer software for the\npurposes of royalty. If royalty income from the use or the right to use or transfer of all or\nany right (including the granting of the licence) in respect of copyright in computer\nprogramme was not taxable under <a href=\"/doc/874582/\" id=\"a_63\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the Act, Parliament would not have\nprescribed a special rate of income-tax in respect of royalty income in espect of any\ncomputer software under section 115A(1A). The provisions of <a href=\"/doc/874582/\" id=\"a_64\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> have to be\nconsidered in the light of the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_65\">section 115A(1A)</a> of the Act. The use of the\nexpressions &quot;in respect of copyright in any book to an Indian concern&quot; or &quot;in respect of any\ncomputer software to a person resident in India&quot; in section 115A(1 A) shows that for the\npurposes of krIcome-tax copyright &quot;computer software&quot; is different from copyright in any\n&quot;book&quot; though both are literary works under the <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_66\">Copyright Act, 1957</a>.\nTherefore, once the consideration is received by a non-resident for the transfer or all or any\nrights including the granting of a licence in respect of a patent, invention, model, design,\nsecret formula or process or similar property or any copy-right literary, artistic or scientific\nwork, the consideration received shall be deemed to accrue or arise in India and will be\ntaxable in India. Hence, by virtue of the amended Explanation to <a href=\"/doc/874582/\" id=\"a_67\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> royalty\nincome will be taxable in India whether or not the non-resident has a residence or place of\nbusiness or business connection in India.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_41\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"41077886\" id=\"span_26\">11.9. The Jurisdictional High Court in <a href=\"/doc/41077886/\" id=\"a_68\">CIT Vs. Samsung Electronics</a> (345 ITR 494) (Kar) had\noccasion to examine the term &quot;royalty&quot; vis-\u00e0-vis &quot;computer software&quot;. The Hon&#x27;ble Court observed:-</span>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_42\">      &quot;In view of the abovesaid definition of &quot;royalty&quot;, it is clear that the necessary ingredient to be\n      satisfied to find out as to whether the payment would amount to &quot;royalty&quot; is as follows -\n      payment of any kind received as a consideration for the use of, or the right to use, any\n      copyright of literary, artistic or scientific work.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_43\">      It has been universally accepted that a literary work is entitled to copyright and wherefore, a\n      literary work is entitled to be registered as copyright. In India, the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/107053246/\" id=\"a_69\">section 2(o)</a> of\n      the Copyright Act, 1957, defines &quot;literary work&quot; as under:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_44\">&quot;&#x27;literary work&#x27; includes computer programmes, tables and compilations including computer\ndatabases&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_70\">Therefore, &quot;computer software&quot; has been recognized as copyright work is also.\nHaving regard to the above said definition of &quot;royalty&quot;, we have to consider\u2022 the contents of software\nlicence agreement entered into by the nonresident with Samsung Electronics and also the respondents\n                                                   IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                         IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                  IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_27\">                                              Page 25 of 331</span>\n\nin the case represented by Sri Ganesh, learned senior counsel and Sri Aravind Dattar, wherein it is a\ncase of purchase, sale or distribution or otherwise of the off-the-shelf software. It is described as a\n&quot;software licence agreement&quot;, wherein it is averred that customer accepts an individual, non-\ntransferable and non-exclusive licence to use the licenced software program(s) on the terms and\nconditions enumerated in the agreement. It is further averred that the customer-Samsung Electronics-\nshall protect confidential information and shall not remove any copyright, confidentiality or other\u2022\nproprietary rights provided by the nonresident. However, what is granted under the said licence is only\na licence to use the software for internal business without having any right for making any alteration\nor reverse engineering or creating sub-licences. What is. transferred under the said licence is the\nlicence to use the software and the copyright continue to be Jvith the non-resident as per the agreement.\nEven as per the agreement entered into with the other distributors as also the end-user licence\nagreement, it is clear that the distributor would get exclufive non-transferable licence within the\nterritory for\u2022 which he is appointed and he has got right to distribute via resellers the software, upon\npayment of the licences set forth in exhibit A to the agreement only to end users pursuant to a valid\nActuate shrinkwrap or other actuate licence agreement and except as expressly set forth in the said\nagreement, distributor may not rent, lease, loan, sell or\u2022 otherwise distribute the software the\ndocumentation or any derivative works based upon the software or documentation in whole or in part.\nDistributor shall not reverse engineer, decompile, or otherwise attempt to derive or modem the source\ncode for the software. The distributor shall have no rights to the software other than the rights expressly\nset forth in the agreement. The distributor shall not mods or\u2022 copy any part of the software or\ndocumentation. The distributor may not use subdistributors for firther distribution of the software and\ndocumentation without the prior consent of Actuate. What is charged is the licence fee to be paid by the\ndistributor of the software as enumerated in exhibit A to the agreement. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"41077886\" id=\"span_28\">Further, clause 6.01 of the\nagreement dealing with title states that the distributor acknowledges that actuate and its suppliers\nretain all right, title and interest in and to the original, and any copies (by whomever produced), of\nthe software or documentation and ownership of all patent copyright, trade mark, trade secret and\nother intellectual property rights pertaining thereto, shall be and remain the sole property of\nActuate. The distributor shall not be an owner of any copies of, or any interest in, the software, but\nrather is licenced pursuant to the agreement to use and distribute such copies. Actuate represents\nthat it has the right to enter into the agreement and grant the licences provided therein and\nconfidentiality is protected. Therefore, on reading the contents of the respective agreement entered\ninto by the respondents with the non-resident, it is clear that under the agreement, what is transferred\nis only a licence to use the copyright belonging to the non-resident subject to the terms and\nconditions of the agreement, as <a href=\"/doc/41077886/\" id=\"a_70\">referred to above</a>, and the nonresident supplier continues to be the\nowner of the copyright and all other intellectual property rights. It is well settled that copyright is a\nnegative right. It is an umbrella of many rights and licence is granted for making use of the copyright\nin respect of shrink wrapped software/off-the-shelf software under the respective agreement, which\nauthorizes the end user, i.e., the customer to make use of the copyright software contained in the\nsaid software, which is purchased off the shelf or imported as shrink wrapped software and the same\nwould amount to transfer of part of the copyright and transfer of right to use the copyright for\ninternal business as per the terms and conditions of the agreement. Therefore, the contention of the\nlearned senior counsel appearing for the respondents that there is no transfer of copyright or any\npart thereof under the agreements entered into by the respondent with the nonresident supplier of\nsoftware cannot be accepted ............</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_71\">                                                    IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                          IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                   IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_29\">                                               Page 26 of 331</span>\n\nIt is well settled that in the absence of any definition of &quot;copyright&quot; in the <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_71\">Income-tax Act</a> or the DTAA\nwith the respective countries, in view of <a href=\"/doc/1659104/\" id=\"a_72\">article 3</a> of the DTAA, reference is to be made to the respective\nlaw regarding the definition of &quot;copyright&quot;, namely, <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_73\">Copyright Act, 1957</a>, in India, wherein it is clearly\nstated that &quot;literary work&quot; includes computer programmes, tables and compilations including computer\n(databases). <a href=\"/doc/1454107/\" id=\"a_74\">Section 16</a> of the Copyright Act, 1957, states that no personshall be entitled to copyright\nor any similar right in any work, whether published or unpublished, otherwise than under and in\naccordance with the provisions of the said Act or of any other law for the time being in force, but nothing\nin this section shall be construed as abrogating any right or jurisdiction to restrain a breach of trust or\nconfidence. <a href=\"/doc/1129646/\" id=\"a_75\">Section 14</a> of the said Act dealing with the, meaning of &quot;copyright&quot; reads as follows :\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_45\">             &quot;14. Meaning of copyright.-For the purposes of this Act, &#x27;copyright&#x27; means the\n           exclusive right subject to the provisions of this Act, to do or authorise the doing of any\n           of the following acts in respect of a work or any substantial part thereof, namely\n           : (a) in the case of a literary, dramatic or musical work not being a computer\n           programme, (i) to reproduce the work in any material form including the storing of\n           it in any medium by electronic means ;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_46\">           (ii) to issue copies of the work to the public not being copies already in circulation ;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_47\">           (iii) to perform the work in public, or communicate it to the public ;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_48\">           (iv) to make any cinematograph film or sound recording in respect of the work ;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_49\">           (v) to make any translation of the work ;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_50\">           (vi) to make any adaptation of the work ;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_51\">           (vii) to do, in relation to a translation or an adaptation of the work, any of the acts\n           specified in relation to the work in sub-clauses (i) to (vi) ;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_52\">           (b) in the case of a computer programme, (i) to do any of the acts specified in clause (a);\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_53\">           (ii) to sell or give on commercial rental oil offer for sale or for commercial rental\n           any copy of the computer programme :\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_54\">     ...........Provided that such commercial rental does not apply in respect of computer\n     programmes where the programme itself is not the essential object of the rental.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_55\">
