public ;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_81\"> (e) in the case of a sound recording, (i) to make any other sound recording embodying it;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_82\"> (ii) to sell or give on hire, or offer for sale or hire, any copy of the sound recording regardless of\n whether such copy has been sold or given on hire on earlier occasions ;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_83\"> (iii) to communicate the sound recording to the public.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_84\"> Explanation. -For the purposes of this section, a copy which has been sold once shall be deemed to\n be a copy already in circulation.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_85\">                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_31\">                                        Page 28 of 331</span>\n\n  &quot;It may also be noted that under <a href=\"/doc/1038145/\" id=\"a_76\">section 51</a> of the Act dealing with &quot;When copyright -- infringed&quot;\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_57\">  states that copyright in a work shall be deemed to be infringed-when any person, without a\n  licence granted by the owner of the copyright or the RegisrrCopyrights under the Act or\n  in contravention of the conditions of a licence so green or of any condition imposed by\n  a competent authority under the Act does anytL the exclusive right to do which is by the\n  Act conferred upon the owner of the copyright. <a href=\"/doc/1013176/\" id=\"a_77\">Section 52</a> of the Act dealing with certain\n  acts not to be infringement of copyright states that the following acts shall not constitute\n  an infringement of copyright, namely :\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_58\">&quot;52.(I)(aa) the making of copies or adaptation of a computer programme by the lawfill possessor\nof a copy of such computer programme, Irvin such copy (i) in order to utilise the computer\nprogramme for the purpose for which it was supplied ; or\n\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_59\">(ii) to make back-up copies purely as a temporary protection against loss, destruction or damage\nin order only to utilise the computer programme for the purpose for which it was supplied.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_86\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"100867385\" id=\"span_32\">It is clear from the abovesaid provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_78\">Copyright Act</a> that the right to copyright work\nwould also constitute exclusive right of the copyright holder and any violation of the said right\nwould amount to infringement under <a href=\"/doc/1038145/\" id=\"a_79\">section 51</a> of the Act. However, if such copying of\ncomputer programme is done by a lawfid possessor of a copy of such computer programme,\nthe same would not constitute infringement of copyright and wherefore, but for the licence\ngranted in these cases to the respondent to make copy of the software contained in shrink-\nwrapped/off-the-shelf software into the hard disk of the designated computer and to take a\ncopy for back-up purposes, the end user has no other right and the said taking back-up would\nhave constituted an infringement, but for the licence. Therefore, licence is granted for taking\ncopy of the software and to store it in the hard disk and to take a back-up copy and right to\nmake a copy itself is a part of the copyright. Therefore, when licence to make use of the\nsoftware by making copy of the same and to store it in the hard disk of the designated computer\nand to take back-up copy of the software, it is clear. that what is transferred is right to use the\nsoftware, an exclusive right, which the owner of the copyright, i.e., the respondent-supplier\nowns and what is transferred is only right to use copy of the software for the internal business\nas per the terms and conditions of the agreement. The decision of the Delhi High Court in <a href=\"/doc/100867385/\" id=\"a_80\">CIT v.\nDynamic Vertical Software India P. Ltd.</a> [2011] 332 ITR 222 (Delhi) relied upon by Sri Aravind\n                                                   IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                         IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                  IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_33\">                                              Page 29 of 331</span>\n\n\n    Dattar, learned senior counsel appearing for the respondents in some of the cases in support of\n    his contention that by no stretch of imagination, payment made by the respondents to the non-\n    resident suppliers can be treated as &quot;royalty&quot; is not helpful to the respondents in the present cases\n    as in the said case, the Delhi High Court was considering the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/686617/\" id=\"a_81\">section 40(a)</a>(0 of the\n    Act and the order of the High Court reads as follows (page 223) :</span>\n</p>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_60\">&quot;What is found, as a matter of fact, is that the assessee has been purchasing the software from\nMicrosoft and sold it further in Indian market by no stretch of imagination, it would be termed as\nroyalty.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_87\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"100867385\" id=\"span_34\">Therefore, the contention of the learned senior counsel appearing for the respondents that there is no\ntransfer of any part of copyright or copyright under the impugned agreements or licences cannot be\naccepted. Accordingly, we hold that right to make a copy of the software and use it for internal business\nby making copy of the same and storing the same in the hard disk of the designated computer and taking\nback up copy would itself amount to copyright work under <a href=\"/doc/1129646/\" id=\"a_82\">section 14(1)</a> of the Act and licence is granted\nto use the software by making copies, which work, but for the licence granted would have constituted\ninfringement of copyright and the licencee is in possession of the legal copy of the software under the\nlicence. Therefore, the contention of the learned senior counsel appearing for the respondents that there\nis no transfer of any part of copyright or copyright and transaction only involves sale of copy of the\ncopyright software cannot be accepted It is also to be noted that what is supplied is the copy of the\nsoftware of which the respondent-supplier continues to be the owner of the copyright and what is granted\nunder the licence is only right to copy the software as per the terms of the agreement, which, but for the\nlicence would amount to infringement of copyright and in view of the licence granted the same would\nnot amount to infringement under <a href=\"/doc/1013176/\" id=\"a_83\">section 52</a> of the Copyright Act as <a href=\"/doc/100867385/\" id=\"a_84\">referred to above</a>. Therefore, the\namount paid to the non-resident supplier towards supply of shrink-wrapped software, or oche shelf\nsoftware is not the price of the C. D. alone nor software alone nor the price of licence granted. This is\na combination of all and in substance, unless licence is granted permitting the end user to copy and\ndownload the software, the dumb C. D. containing the software would not in any way be helpful to the end\nuser as software would become operative only if it is downloaded to the hardware of the designated\ncomputer as per the terms and conditions of the agreement and that makes the difference between the\ncomputer software and copyright in respect of books or prerecorded music software as book and\nprerecorded music C D. can be used once they are purchased, but so far as software stored in dumb C D.\nis concerned, the transfer of dumb C D. by itself would not confer any right upon the end user and the\npurpose of the C. D. is only to enable the end user to take a copy of the software and to store it in\nthe hard disk of the designated computer if licence is granted in that behalf and in the absence of\nlicence, the same would amount to infringement of copyright, which is exclusively owned by non-\nresident suppliers, who would continue to be the proprietor of copyright. Therefore, there is no\nsimilarity between the transaction of purchase of the book or prerecorded music C. D. or the C. D.\ncontaining software and in view of the same, the Legislature in its wisdom, has treated the literary work\nlike books and other articles separately from &quot;computer&quot; software within the meaning of the\n&quot;copyright&quot; as <a href=\"/doc/100867385/\" id=\"a_85\">referred to above</a> under <a href=\"/doc/1129646/\" id=\"a_86\">section 14</a> of the Copyright Act.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_88\">                                                     IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                      IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                           IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                    IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_35\">                                                Page 30 of 331</span>\n\n\n\nIt is also clear from the abovesaid analysis of the DTAA, the Income-tax Act, the Copyright Act that\nthe payment would constitute &quot;royalty&quot; within the meaning of <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_87\">article 12(3)</a> of the DTAA and even as\nper the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/874582/\" id=\"a_88\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the Act as the definition of &quot;royalty&quot; under clause 9(1)(vi) of\nthe Act is broader than the definition of &quot;royalty&quot; under the DTAA as the right that is transferred in\nthe present case is the transfer of copyright; including the right to Make copy of software for internal\nbusiness, and payment made in that regard would constitute &quot;royalty&quot; for imparting of any\ninformation concerning technical, industrial, commercial or scientific knowledge, experience or skill\nas per clause (iv) of Explanation 2 to section - 9(1)(vi) of the Act. In any view of the matter, in view\nof the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_89\">section 90</a> of the Act, agreements with foreign countries DTAA would override\nthe provisions of the Act. Once it is held that payment made by the respondents to the non-resident\ncompanies would amount to &quot;royalty&quot; within the meaning of <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_90\">article 12</a> of the DTAA with the\nrespective country, it is clear that the payment made by the respondents to the non-resident supplier\nwould amount to royalty. In view of the said finding, it is clear that there is obligation on the part of the\nrespondents to deduct tax at source under <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_91\">section 195</a> of the Act and consequences would follow as held\nby the hon&#x27;ble Supreme Court while remanding these appeals to this court. Accordingly, we answer the\nsubstantial question of law in favour of the Revenue and against the assessee by holding that on the facts\nand in the circumstances of the case, the Income-tax Appellate Tribunal was not justified in holding that\nthe amount(s) paid by the respondent(s) to the foreign software suppliers was not &quot;royalty&quot; and that the\nsame did not give rise to any &quot;income&quot; taxable in India and wherefore, the respondent(s) were not liable\nto deduct any tax at source and pass the following order:&quot;\n</p>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_61\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1562427\" id=\"span_36\">11.10. The Delhi Bench of the Hon&#x27;ble Tribunal in <a href=\"/doc/1562427/\" id=\"a_92\">Assistant Commissioner of Income-tax v. Amadeus India P. Ltd.</a>\n(257 ITR 23) (AT) had occasion to examine the term &quot;Computer Software&quot; &amp; &quot;Computer Program&quot; and defined the\nterms --</span>\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_62\">&quot;Computer software&quot; means &quot;a computer programme recorded on any disc, etc., and includes any such\nprogramme which was transmitted from India to a place outside India by any means&quot; ... .......\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_63\">............. But once the scope of the words computer programme was not explained in section 801111E, the\nglobal meaning of the word is to be taken into account. The doctrine of pari materia was relevant for this\npurpose. According to that doctrine, the meaning of a particular word could be imported if the same was\nnot contrary to the provisions of the Act to which it is being imported. Therefore, in the absence of a\nspecific definition of the word &quot;programme&quot; in the <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_93\">Income-tax Act</a>, the expression &quot;computer programme&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_89\">should be understood as defined in the <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_94\">Copyright Act</a> and should be interpreted broadly, liberally and\nconsistently with other legislations of Government to include export of &quot;data processing software&quot; as well\n                                                   IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                         IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                  IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_37\">                                              Page 31 of 331</span>\n\n\n.........The words &quot;computer programme&quot; has not been defined in the <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_95\">Income-tax Act</a> but it has been defined\nin the <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_96\">Copyright Act, 1957</a>, as amended in 1994. According to the definition, it means a set of instructions\nexpressed in words, codes, schemes orany other forms including a machine readable medium capable\nof causing a compiler to perform a particular task or achieve a particular result.&quot;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_90\">11.11. In Millennium IT Software Ltd., In re (338 ITR 391) (AAR), the Authoth ruled on the facts of\nthe case that :-\n</p>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_64\">&quot;The applicant entered into a software licence and maintenance agreement (agreement) with\nIndian Commodity and Exchange Limited (ICEL) on March 27, 2009. Under the agreement the\napplicant allowed ICEL to use the software product (the licenced programme) owned by it. The&#x27;\nlicenced programme was to be installed into the computer machines designated by ICEL. The\napplicant was to deploy its&#x27; personnel to the designated site to train the employees of ICEL. The\napplicant was required to provide at its own cost, maintenance and support services. For\ninstallation and implementation of the licenced programme, the applicant was to be paid Rs. 4\ncrore. The software became live on August 20, 2009. The licence to use the licenced programme\nwas for four years and thereafter its renewal was left to the discretion of ICEL. The agreement\nprovided for payment of licensing and maintenance fee from January 1, 2010 till December 31,\n2014. The mode of the payment was a fixed amount of Rs. 50 lakhs per quarter plus an additional\nfee based on the utilization of the licenced programme by ICEL, so that the total amount payable\nwould not exceed Rs. 150 lakhs per quarter. The licenced programme was developed by the\napplicant at its development centre in Sri Lanka. The rights granted&#x27; under the agreement were\nnon-excluSive, non-transferable, non-assignable, indivisible. The applicant had granted &#x27;GEL the\nright to make copies of the licenced programme to be installed on equipment only at designated\nsites of ICEL. Each copy was to carry copyright, trade mark and other notices relating to\nproprietary rights of the applicant. ICEL could not sell, distribute or disclose the licenced\nprogramme or associated documents to any third party., Use of the source code and reverse\nengineering of the licenced programme was strictly prohibited. On these facts the applicant sought\nan advance ruling on the questions whether the applicant was not taxable in India under the\n                                                   IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                         IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                  IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_38\">                                              Page 32 of 331</span>\n\n\n<a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_97\">Income-tax Act, 1961</a>, with respect to implementation and licence and maintenance fees paid by\nICEL to it under the agreement ; whether the applicant was not taxable under the Double Taxation\nAvoidance Agreement between India and Sri Lanka (DTAA) ; whether the applicant providing\nmaintenance service to ICEL could not be treated as having a permanent establishment in India ;\nwhether, if the applicant was not taxable in India for the fees paid by 10EL, it would later be required\nto withhold tax under <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_98\">section 195</a> of the Act on the fees, and if ICEL had deducted withholding tax, the\napplicant would be entitled to get refund ; and assuming that the applicant had no other taxable income\nin India, whether the applicant would be absolved from filing a tax return in India under the provisions\nof the Act with respect to the fees.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_65\">The Authority on the stated facts ruled :\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_66\">(i) That the fees paid by ICE&#x27;&#x27;, to the applicant were taxable as royalty under clause (v) of Explanation\n2 to <a href=\"/doc/874582/\" id=\"a_99\">section 9(1)</a><a href=\"/doc/134296/\" id=\"a_100\">6</a>,<a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_101\">0</a> of the Act.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_91\">(ii) That since the fees payable by ICEL to the applicant arose in India, they were taxable under\n<a href=\"/doc/1481519/\" id=\"a_102\">article 12(2)</a> of the DTAA in India.\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_92\">(iii) That the applicant did not have a permanent establishment in terms of <a href=\"/doc/1937835/\" id=\"a_103\">article 5</a> of the DTAA.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_93\">(iv) That the provision of withholding tax under section 195 would apply.\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_94\">(v) That as the applicant was liable to tax in India, it was required to .file a return of income under the\nprovisions of the Act.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_95\">Per V. K Shridhar (Member).-(i) Under Indian copyright law, computer programmes are considered\nto be literary works and accordingly entitled to copyright protection under <a href=\"/doc/1129646/\" id=\"a_104\">section 14</a> of the Copyright\nAct, 1957. The definition of computer programme has been worded in liberal language. It defines\ncomputer programme as a set of instructions expressed in words, codes, schemes or in any other form.\nThe use of this language signifies the legislative intent to award copyright protection to both the source\ncode and the object code of the computer programme. During the process of registration of a copyright\nin ti computer programme, the author also files the object code of the software with the Registrar of\nCopyrights. Thus authorship of both the source code and the object code are protected by the <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_105\">Copyright\nAct</a> as literary work.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_96\">  (ii) <a href=\"/doc/1013176/\" id=\"a_106\">Section 52</a> of the Copyright Act specifies the acts which do not constitute infringement of\n  copyright. <a href=\"/doc/1013176/\" id=\"a_107\">Section 52(aa)</a> uses the term &quot;lawfill possessor&quot;. It thus, carves out an exception for\n  lawful possessors of a computer programme as far as copies or adaptation for personal use is\n  concerned. The purchase of software from a reseller is payment for use of intellectual property and\n  not for the purchase of goods, unless the payment falls under the exception provided by the\n                                               IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                              IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_39\">                                          Page 33 of 331</span>\n\n\nsecond proviso to sec 9(1)(vi) of the 1961 Act. What had been licenced squarely fell within\nthe definition of computer programme under <a href=\"/doc/124202294/\" id=\"a_108\">section 2(ffc)</a> of the Copyright Act and was\ncopyrightable subject mailer.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_97\">(iii) The scope of the agreement extended beyond installation and implementation 07 -the\nlicenced programme. The maintenance service in respect of the licenced programme was an\nintegral part of agreement. In bifurcating the payments before and after installation and\nimplementation, the nature of the payments remained royally for the use of the licenced\nprogramme and would not change from royalty to business income. If it was to be in the\nnature of business income the agree/hent would have been for sale and service of licenced\nprogramme and the fee paid would have been taxable as business income under <a href=\"/doc/735354/\" id=\"a_109\">article 7</a> of\nthe DTAA, depending on whether or not the applicant had a permanent establishment in India\nunder <a href=\"/doc/1937835/\" id=\"a_110\">article 5</a> of the DTAA.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_98\">(iv) Therefore since without the agreement being in place, any usage of the computer programme\nby the customers would have amounted to copyright infringement, the payment made under the\nagreement was for Obtaining the right to use the&#x27;copyright lit the software and was taxable as\nroyalty under the DTAA and the 1961 Act.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_99\">         Per P. K. Balasubramanyan (Chairman).-(0 In terms of the <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_111\">InCome-tax Act, 1961</a>\n         royalty means consideration for the transfer of all or any right, including the granting\n         of a licence, in respect of any copyright or literary work. What the applicant had\n         granted to ICIEL, was a licence to use the computer programme developed by it and\n         owned by it and over which it had a copyright. This was done fora- consideration. This\n         was a licence recognized by the <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_112\">Copyright Act</a> and was &#x27;a known mode of exploitation\n         of a copyright. The applicant had not parted with its title over the copyright in the\n         software. It had conveyed to another a right to use the software over which it had a\n         copyright. The right of user of software, thus given, involved the right to use the\n         copyright. The user of a software created, over which a copyright is acquired cannot\n         be divorced from the use of the copyright itself. The right granted for use of a\n         copyrighted article for a consideration, would also be royalty. It was, therefore, not\n         possible to accept the argument that what was involved was the mere permitting of the\n         right to use a copyrighted article. Indian law does not diStinguish between copyrighted\n         article and the copyright. What is needed to attract liability is the farming out of the\n         right to use or of the right itself for consideration.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_100\">        (ii) The definition of royalty contained in <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_113\">article 12(3)</a> of the DTAA between India and Sri\n        Lanka shows that it is a payment of any kind received as consideration for the &quot;use of or the\n        right to any copyright&quot;. The definition in the DTAA is wider than the one found in the Act.\n        For, it takes in even the consideration received for permitting another to use a copyright.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_101\">                                                      IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                       IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                            IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                     IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_40\">                                                 Page 34 of 331</span>\n\n\n             Even a right to use need not be conferred. It is not necessary even to grant the right to use\n             the copyright if one were to look at it literally, though the grant of a right to use could be\n             said to be included in the grant of a right in the copyright. In the present case, not merely the\n             use was licenced but the licencee was given the right to copy it and use it wherever it needed\n             it for its business. The right given to ICEL for a consideration to copy the copyrighted\n             software and use it for its own purposes whenever and wherever needed by it, clearly\n             attracted the definition of royalty to the consideration paid by ICEL to the applicant, though\n             the right granted may be limited and did not take in a right to further transfer the right or its\n             use.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_102\">          Dassault Systems K K, In re [2010.1 322 ITR 125 (AAR) commented upon.&quot;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_103\">11.12. In Citrix Systems Asia Pacific Pty. Limited, In re (343 ITR 01)(AAR), the Authority ruled on\nthe facts of the case that :-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_67\">&quot;When a software is created by a person who acquires a copyright for it, he becomes the\nowner of that copyright. He can transfer or licence that right either by himself or through an\nagent. When he sells or licences the software for use, he also sells or licences the right to use\nthe copyright embedded therein. If a software is used without being lawfully acquired either\nby purchase or by licence, that would amount to an infringement of the copyright because of\nthe copyright embedded in the software. The software is a literary work and the copyright of\nthe creator over the software is an important and commercially valuable right. So, whenever\na software is assigned or licenced for use, there is involved an assignment of the right to use\nthe embedded copyright in the software or a licence to use the embedded copyright, the\nintellectual property right in the software. It is not possible to divorce the software from the\nintellectual property right of the creator of the software embedded therein. Even the right to\nsell or give on rental, would amount to a copyright and would be a right to be dealt with as a\ncopyright.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_68\">The definition of &quot;royalty&quot; in the <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_114\">Income-tax Act, 1961</a> is, consideration for transfer of all or any\nrights (including the granting of a licence) in respect of a pater: innovation, model, design, secret\n                                                    IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                          IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                   IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_41\">                                               Page 35 of 331</span>\n\n\nformula or process or trade mark or similar property. Consideration for grant of the use of any of\nthe above is also royalty. It also takes in the consideration for the transfer of all or any rights\n(including the granting of a licence) in respect of any copyright, literary, artistic or scientific work\nLicence is not confined to an exclusive licence. When a software, over which a copyright is acquired\nand thus owned, is licenced for use to another or sold to another for his own use, the licencee or\nthe purchaser gets the right to use the software without being held guilty of infringement of the\ncopyright. When the use of software, without anything more, would render the user liable for\ninfringement of the copyright embedded in the software, the sale or the licensing of the software\ninvolves the grant of a right to use the copyright in the software and right to use the intellectual property\nembedded in the software. &#x27;Therefore, the licensing of a softwaresfbr use by the end-use customer, is\nnot the mere sale of a copyrighted article.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_69\">The words within brackets, &quot;including the granting of a licence&quot; indicate an expansive&#x27;\ndefinition. The word &quot;includes&quot; is an inclusive definition and expands the meaning. Therefore,\nlicence cannot be restricted to transfer of a right dealt with earlier by the provision and should\nbe understood as taking in the grant of a licence simpliciter.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_70\"><a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_115\">Article 12</a> of the Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement between India and Australia (DTAA)\ndefines &quot;royalties&quot; to mean &quot;payment made as consideration for the use of or the right to use\nany copyright, patent, design or model, plan, secret formula or process, trade mark or other like\nproperty or right&quot;. When the DTAA speaks of royalty, and defines it, it must be understood as it\nis commonly understood. The article speaks of the use of or the right to use any copyright. Use\nof a copyright takes place when the copyright is used. This is distinct front the right to use a\ncopyright. The two expressions are used disjunctively and the expression used is &quot;or&quot;. The\ncontext does not warrant the reading of &quot;or&quot; as &quot;and&quot;. If so, the consideration received sfor\npermitting another to use a copyright is also royalty.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_71\">A copyrighted article is nothing but an article which incorporates the copyright of the owner, the\nassignee, the exclusive licencee or the licencee. So, when a copyrighted article is permitted or licenced\n                                                     IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                      IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                           IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                    IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_42\">                                                Page 36 of 331</span>\n\n\nto be used for a fee, the permission involves not only the physical or&#x27; electronic manifestation of a\nprogramme, but also the use of or the right to use the copyright embedded therein.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_72\">That apart, <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_116\">article 12(3)</a> of the DTAA defines royalties as payments, whether periodical or not and,\nhowever, described or computed, as consideration for &quot;the use of, or the right to use any copyright,\npatent, design or model, plan, secret formula or process, trade mark or other like property or right&quot;.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_104\">The definition is wider than that contained in the Act. It also ropes in payment of consideration for\nthe use of a copyright in addition to the consideration paid for the right to use a copyright, covered\nby the definition in the Act. Consideration paid for use of a copyrighted software is also payment for\nuse of the copyright embedded in the software. There cannot be a use of software, over which exists\na copyright, without a use of the copyright therein. The payment for such use can only be royalty.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_105\">The sale or licensing for use of a copyrighted software amounts to the grant of a right to use a\ncopyright.\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_106\">P. No. 30 of 1999, In re 11999) 238 JTR 296 (AAR) relied on.A ruling by the Authority is based on the\nfacts involved in the application leading to that ruling. The doctrine of precedent cannot be applied to a\nruling under <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_117\">section 245R(4)</a> of the Act. <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_118\">The Act</a> has itself made it clear that the ruling is binding on the\napplicant in the application and the Revenue, in respect of that application and the transaction involved\ntherein.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_107\">The applicant, a company incorporated in Australia and provider of software services, entered into\nagreements with Indian distributors for the distribution and sale of its software and hardware products\nin India. In the year 2006, the applicant entered into a distribution agreement with I, an Indian company\nengaged in the business of distribution of computer software and hardware, under which I was appointed\nnon-exclusive distributor of the pro-ducts of the applicant in India. For the software product, Citrix\nXenApp, while sale and collection was made through the distributor, no physical delivery of the product\nwas made to the distributor. On the basis of the demand of the customers, the distributor placed orders\nof purchase with the applicant and made payments to the applicant The applicant then di transmitted\na &quot;key&quot; to the end-user customer who, on receipt of the key, downloaded the software from the server\nof the applicant. In addition, I also facilitated :if execution of the Citrix subscription advantage programme\n                                                    IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                          IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                   IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_43\">                                               Page 37 of 331</span>\n\n\nbetween the applicant and its existing customers. The programme was a package of support services during\nthe period of the programme, including product version updates, sub&#x27;scription advantage news and updates\nand secure portal access. The applicant sought an advance ruling on the taxability in India of payments\nmade by I to it for the software product, Citrix XenApp and the subscription advantage programme. The\nquestions framed by the Authority were whether the payments received by the applicant from the\ndistributor for sale of the software product were in the nature of &quot;royalty&quot; within the meaning or the\nterm in Explanation 2 to clause (vi) of <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_119\">section 9(1)</a> of the Income-tax Act, 1961, or within the meaning\nof the term in <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_120\">article 12</a> of the Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement between India and Australia,\nwhether the payments received by the applicant from the distributor for the right to download/receive\nversion updates for the software products of the- applicant were in the nature of &quot;royalty&quot; within the\nmeaning of the term in Explanation 2 to clause (vi) of <a href=\"/doc/874582/\" id=\"a_121\">section 9(1)</a> of the Act, whether the payments\nreceived by the applicant from the distributor for the Citrix subscription advantage programme were in\nthe nature of &#x27;fees for technical services&quot; within the meaning of the term in Explanation 2 to clause (vii)\nof <a href=\"/doc/874582/\" id=\"a_122\">section 9(1)</a> of the Act, whether the payment received by the applicant from the distributor for the\nCitrix subscription advantage programme, was in the nature of &quot;royalty&quot; within the meaning of the term\nin <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_123\">article 12</a> of the DTAA and in the light of the declaration provided by.the applicant that it did not have\na permanent establishment in India in terms of <a href=\"/doc/1937835/\" id=\"a_124\">article 5</a> of the DTAA, whether the payment received by\nthe applicant was chargeable to tax in India and would the receipts by the applicant from the distributor\nsuffer withholding tax under <a href=\"/doc/1329583/\" id=\"a_125\">section 195</a> of the Income-tax Act, 1961, and at what rate. The Authority\non the stated facts, ruled :\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_73\">    (i) That the payments received by the applicant from the distributor for sales of the software\n         products were in the nature of royalty within the meaning of <a href=\"/doc/874582/\" id=\"a_126\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the Income-\n         tax Act, 1961.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_74\">(ii) That the payments concerned would be royalty as defined in <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_127\">article 12</a> of the DTAA between\nIndia and Australia.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_75\"> (iii) That the payment received by way of subscription for the updates would also be payment\n received for grant of a right to use the copyright embedded in the subscription advantage programme\n and it will be royalty.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_108\">                                                   IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                         IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                  IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_44\">                                              Page 38 of 331</span>\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_109\"> (iv) That it was not necessary to rule on the question whether the payment for subscription advantage\n programme would be in the nature of fees for technical services within the meaning of the Income-\n tax Act.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_110\"> ( ) That the payment received by the applicant from the distributor for the Citrix subscription\n advantage programme was royalty within the meaning of clause (a) of <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_128\">article 12(3)</a> of the DTAA\n between India and Australia. It was not necessary to consider the question whether it would fall under\n clause (g) of <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_129\">article 12(3)</a> of the DTAA.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_111\"> (i) Even accepting that the applicant did not have a permanent establishment in India, the amount\n was liable to be taxed in India under <a href=\"/doc/1481519/\" id=\"a_130\">article 12(2)</a> of the DTAA.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_112\"> (v) That the distributor I was required to withhold taxes in India at the time of making payments to\n the applicant in terms of <a href=\"/doc/1329583/\" id=\"a_131\">section 195</a> of the Income-tax Act at the rate of 10 per cent. of the gross\n amount of royalty, as provided under <a href=\"/doc/1481519/\" id=\"a_132\">article 12(2)</a> of -the DTAA.&quot;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_113\">11.13. In ThoughtBuzz Pvt. Ltd., In re (346 ITR 345) (AAR), the Authority ruled on the facts of the\ncase that :-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_76\">&quot;The applicant, a tax resident of Singapore, was engaged in providing social media monitoring service\nfor a company, brand or product, through a platform for users to hear and engage with their customers,\nbrand ambassadors, etc., across the internet. The applicant charged a subscription. Clients who\nsubscribed could log in to its website to do a search and the system operated by the applicant generated\na report with analytics with inputs provided by the clients. The information for generation of report was\nobtained from various websites through the applicant&#x27;s own computer programme for data extraction that\ngathered and categorized information on the internet The applicant sought an advance ruling on the\nquestions whether the amount received by offering subscription based service was taxable in India and\nwhether tax was required to be deducted from such amount by the subscribers who were resident in India.\nThe Authority on the stated facts ruled :\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_77\">        That the applicant was in the business of gathering, collating and making available imparting\n        information concerning industrial and commercial knowledge, experience and skill and\n        consequently the payment received from the subscriber would be royalty in terms of clause (iv)\n                                                     IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                      IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                           IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                    IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_45\">                                                Page 39 of 331</span>\n\n\n         of Explanation 2 to <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_133\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the Income-tax Act, 1961. The subscription received was\n         royalty liable to be taxed as such under the Act.Under the Double Taxation Avoidance\n         Agreement between India and Singapore (DMA), it would qualify as royalty since it was the\n         grant of the use for consideration or right to use for consideration, the process Or information\n         concerning industrial, commercial or scientific experience and under the D1 AA the subscription\n         received from the Indian subscriber would be taxable as royalty and taxable in India in view of\n         paragraph (2) of <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_134\">article 12</a> of the DTAA, subject to the bene-fit conferred therein on the applicant\n         on fulfilling the condition imposed by that paragraph. Tax was required\n         to be deducted in terms of <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_135\">section 195</a> of the Act from the payment made to it by the\n         subscribers who were resident in India.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_114\">11.14. After considering these views, the facts of the instant case are that Google Ireland has granted\nthe assessee company the right to use the Adwords program which is a complex computer program,\nwithout parting with the copyright, thus granting the licence to use the software in the garb of\nmarketing and distribution. On exploitation of the Adwords program, revenues are generated through\nadvertisers and collected by the assessee company. The assessee company in return pays the&quot;&#x27; share\nof these profits in the garb of distribution fee which is actually consideration on account of licence\nto use / right to use a computer program I process. The assessee company cannot hide behind cryptic\nclauses of the distribution agreement. The argument that Google Ireland has simply provided\nadvertising space on its website to the assessee company is not tenable. As discussed above, it has\nprovided the right to use the Adwords program which is a computer program for a consideration\nwhich squarely falls within the meaning of Royalty as per provisions of the Act.\n11.15. Ground Nos. 10 &amp; 11 relate to the action of AO in taxing&#x27; the distribution fee as &quot;Royalty&quot;\nunder <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_136\">Article 12</a> of the tax treaty. <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_137\">Article 12</a> of the DIM between India and Ireland is reproduced as\nunder:\n</p>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_78\">           &quot;The term &quot;Royalties&quot; as used in this Article means payments of any kind received as a\n          consideration for the use of or the right to use, any copyright of literary, artistic or scientific\n          work including cinematograph films or films or tapes for radio or television broadcasting,\n                                                   IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                         IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                  IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_46\">                                              Page 40 of 331</span>\n\n\n         any patent, trade mark, design or model, plan, secret formula or process or for the use of or\n         the right to use industrial, commercial or scientific equipment, other than an aircraft, or for\n         information concerning industrial, commercial or scientific experience.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_115\"> 11.16. There is no benefit available to the assessee from the provisions of the treaty, although\n treaty provisions will override the Act, as the mischief is covered by the provisions of the treaty.\n It covers right to use copyright of literary work which includes the computer program, trade\n mark and process. For reasons stated above, I have held that the right to use trade mark has\n been transferred as also the right to use copyrighted computer program and process. Therefore,\n the action of the AO in holding that the distribution fee is actually consideration received for\n the right to use / licence to use a computer program / process viz., Adwords program, is upheld.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_116\"> 11.17. The assessee&#x27;s representative strongly relied on two decisions of the Mumbai Bench of\n the Hon&#x27;ble Tribunal viz., (i) Pinstorm Technologies Pvt. Ltd. (ITA No. 4332/Mum/2009) &amp; (ii)\n Yahoo India Pvt. Ltd. Vs. DCIT (140 TTJ 195). I have carefully gone through both the orders.\n <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"89798900\" id=\"span_47\">The former blindly follows its decision in <a href=\"/doc/89798900/\" id=\"a_138\">Yahoo India</a> case. The facts of this case are that the\n Department of the Tourism, Government of India, hired the services of the assessee company\n to approach the foreign principal to provide uploading and display services for hosting the\n banner advertisement at Yahoo Hongkong portal. The assessee company hired the services of\n the principal for the same and made a payment without deducting tax at source. The Bench\n observed that banner advertisement hosting services did not involve use or right to use any\n industrial, commercial or scientific equipment and no such use was actually granted by the\n principal to the assessee company. Therefore, such payments did not amount Royalty. The\n facts and the issues in the case are completely different from the instant case. At no stage\n did the Mumbai Bench consider what exactly is the Adword program, nor did it have\n occasion to examine\n\nthe right to use trade mark and other intellectual property rights. Hence, the decision rendered <a href=\"/doc/89798900/\" id=\"a_139\">in that\ncase</a> is peculiar to the facts of its own and is not applicable in instant case.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_117\">                                                  IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                        IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                 IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_48\">                                             Page 41 of 331</span>\n\n\nThat the fee payable was for imparting_ information concerning technics! industrial, commercial\nor scientific knowledge and experience &amp; That the fee payable was on account of &quot;right to use\nindustrial, commercial &amp; scientific equipment&quot; :-\n</p>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_79\">11.18. The AO held that the Adwords platform is similar to a portal running on service and Adwords\nplatform is based on the search engine technology of Google. Henc4 Adword program in one way\nis also a commercial-scientific equipment and without having access to servers running the Adwords\nplatform. Without the use of the programme, the assessee company cannot perform its function as\nper distribution agreement. Hence, the distribution right also includes the right to use industrial,\ncommercial &amp; scientific equipments which are the servers.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_80\">11.19. I have already held that the Adwords program is a complex computer program and a process\nwithin the meaning of the provisions of the Act. The Adwords Program was developed by Google\nIreland and no information concerning the technical, industrial, commercial or scientific aspects\nhave been found to have been passed on to the assessee company. The assessee company simply\nhas the right to use the Adwords program and thereafter, is involved in collection of advertisement\nrevenue. It retains part of the collection on cost plus profit basis and the balance is remitted\nto Google Ireland. Hence, there is no question of any exchange of information nor was any\nequipment utilised by the assessee company. It cannot be said that the assessee&#x27; company gained\nthe right to use any kind of industrial, scientific equipment on the facts of the instant case. There\nhas been the instance, that, Google Ireland has provided training to the staff of the assessee\ncompany, but such training is restricted to use of the Adwords program and not on how to develop\nthe Adwords program. This is clear from the following observations of the AO:-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_81\"> Google Ireland provides training to the sales team of Google India on the following:\n \uf0b7 Training on the AdWords program and how the AdWords program may benefit the\n     advertisers in India\n \uf0b7 Training on the functionality of the tools available to optimize and follow an ad\n     campaign on the AdWords system;\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_82\">                                                 IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                       IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_49\">                                            Page 42 of 331</span>\n\n\n \uf0b7 The new features/ additions made to the AdWords program and the implications\n     for advertisers, etc.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_83\">11.20. The term &quot;Server&quot; in context of Information Technology has been described by &quot;VVikipedia&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_118\">as, a system (software and suitable computer hardware) that responds to requests across a computer\nnetwork to provide, or help to provide, a network service. Therefore, a server is nothing but\ncomputer software backed by necessary hardware. Thus, it cannot be said that the assessee\ngained right to use any scientific equipment. Since, Google Ireland has not parted with the\ncopyright it holds, in the Adwords program, it cannot be said that any kind of technical knowhow\nhas been transferred to the assessee company. Hence, without prejudice to my view in holding\nthat the remitted amount is Royalty on different grounds, I am not in an agreement with the AO\non these issues.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_119\"> The Google Ireland rendered services to enable the assessee company to market and\n distribute the Adwords program :\n</p>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_84\"> 11.21. The AO after examining the agreement opined that training is an inherent part of\n grant of distribution rights of the Adwords program. The training is given to the staff of the\n assessee company at all stages of marketing and distribution of Adwords program. The\n relevant extracts from the AO&#x27;s order are reproduced to highlight the nature of training :-\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_85\">     2.2 ..............\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_120\">Distributor will distribute AdWords Program in accordance with the training provided by Google.\nFailure to do so would constitute a material breach of this Agreement and shall be grounds for\ntermination under <a href=\"/doc/229141/\" id=\"a_140\">Section 9</a>.3.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_121\">        2.6         After-Sales Support. Distributor will provide after-sales services to Advertisers is\n        accordance with the broad instructions, training and standards of Google.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_122\">        EXHIBIT C           Service Level Agreement\n                                                    IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                          IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                   IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_50\">                                               Page 43 of 331</span>\n\n\n           Issues that cannot immediately be resolved by Distributor based on training and procedures\n           provided under the Agreement must be communicated promptly to Google by Distributor, and\n           Google will assist Distributor in determining the best solution.\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_86\">           &quot;Customer Queries&quot; shall mean e-mails sent from Distributor to Google related to Advertiser\n           issues, but excluding general communications between Distributor and Google (e.g. billing\n           questions, training information) and technical issues that only Google can solve.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_87\">2.1           Further in the submission dated 15th Feb 2013 it was stated that:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_88\">        Google Ireland provides training to the sales team of Google India on the following:\n      \uf0b7 Training on the AdWords program and how the AdWords program may benefit the advertisers\n         in India\n      \uf0b7 Training on the functionality of the tools available to optimize and follow an ad campaign on\n         the AdWords system;\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_89\">      \uf0b7 The new features/ additions made to the AdWords program and the implications for\n         advertisers, etc.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_123\">        11.22. From the above observations of the AO, it is very clear that the training has been provided\n        with respect to the Adwords program and it benefits the assessee who markets and distributes\n        the program to advertisers in India. Functionality of tools available on software and new\n        features made to the Adwords program have also been covered in the training process. This\n        clearly tantamounts to rendering of services to the assessee company. Therefore, the finding of\n        the AO on this point is confirmed.&quot;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_124\">15.      Before the CIT(A), the appellant has also challenged the AO&#x27;s finding that\n\nassessee is not a beneficial owner of sum paid/payable by the GIPL. The CIT(A) has\n\nextracted the observation of the AO on this issue and made a reference of certain judicial\n\npronouncements in his order and concluded that the GIL is the beneficial owner of the\n                                                     IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                      IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                           IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                    IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_51\">                                                Page 44 of 331</span>\n\nsum paid/payable by the GIPL, having relied upon the certificate of the Revenue\n\nauthorities of Ireland. The relevant observations are extracted hereunder for the sake of\n\nreference:\n</p>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_90\">  &quot;9.     In the instant case, the following facts are quite clear :-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_91\">(i)      The Google AdWords Programme distribution agreement is between GIL &amp; GIBL.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_92\">(ii)     The assessee Distributor has made payments for acquisition of distribution rights to GIL alone.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_93\">(iii)    The assessee has filed a notarized certificate of incorporation of GIL from the Irish Authorities to\nestablish that the entity is incorporated in the Company Acts of Ireland. Thereafter, the Revenue\nAuthorities of Ireland have certified that GIL files its corporation tax return in Ireland and is a resident\nof Ireland for tax purposes and duly is liable to Irish corporation tax on its worldwide income including\nthat from India.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_94\">(iv)      Although Circular No. 789 deals with the Indo Mauritius Treaty, the same was applied by the\nMumbai Bench to the lndo Netherland Treaty.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_125\">9.1.     Now, in this light, it will be relevant to analyze the AO&#x27;s attempt to distinguish the judgement\nof the Hon&#x27;ble High Court of Mumbai read with the decision of the Mumbai Bench of the Hon&#x27;ble\nTribunal.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_126\">        \uf0b7 Firstly, in the absence of agreement, it could not be ascertained whether the Dutch.\n          company acted or did not act as an &#x27;agent&#x27; or &#x27;nominee&#x27; of other group companies or was\n          a &#x27;conduit&#x27; between one of the group companies and the Indian company. Therefore, on the\n          given facts, the Tribunal could not have reached the legal finding which it did reach.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_127\">The AO has failed to establish that GIL was a conduit company for other group entities. It is very clear\nthat the amounts in question have reached the financial statements of GIL and the same is subject to\ntax under Irish Revenue Loss. Absence of agreements was not held to be crucial in the case analysed\nabove, The Apex Court in McDowell and Co. Ltd. v. CTO (154 ITR 148) was clearly of the view that\nTax planning may be legitimate provided it is within the framework of the law. Colourable devices\ncannot be part of tax planning and it is wrong to encourage or entertain the belief that it is\n                                                    IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                          IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                   IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_52\">                                               Page 45 of 331</span>\n\n\nhonourable to avoid the payment of tax by dubious methods. It is the obligation of every citizen to\npay the taxes honestly without resorting to subterfuges.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_128\">There is behind taxation laws as much moral sanction as is behind any other welfare legislation and it\nis a pretence to say that avoidance of taxation is not unethical and that it stands on no less a moral plane\nthan honest payment of taxation. The proper way to construe a taxing statute, while considering a device\nto avoid tax, is not to ask whether the provisions should be construed literally or liberally nor whether\nthe transaction is not unreal and not prohibited by the statute, but whether the transaction is a device\nto avoid tax and whether the transaction is such that the judicial process may accord its approval to\nit. It is neither fair nor desirable to expect the Legislature to intervene and take care of every device\nand scheme to avoid taxation. It is up to the court to take stock to determine the nature of the new and\nsophisticated legal devices to avoid tax and to expose the devices for what they really are and to refuse\nto give judicial benediction.\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_129\">By the Court: &quot;The evil consequences of tax avoidance are manifold. First, there is substantial loss of\nmuch needed public revenue, particularly in a welfare State like ours. Next, there is the serious\ndisturbance caused to the economy of the country by the piling up of mountains of black money, directly\ncausing inflation. Then there is &#x27;the large hidden loss&#x27; to the community by some of the best brains in the\ncountry being involved in the perpetual war waged between the tax avoider and his expert team of\nadvisers, lawyers and accountants on one side and the tax gatherer and his, perhaps not so skilful,\nadvisers, on the other side. Then again there is the &#x27;sense of injustice and inequality which tax avoidance\narouses in the breasts of those who are unwilling or unable to profit by it&#x27;. Last, but not the least, is the\nethics of transferring the burden of tax liability to the shoulders of the guideless, good citizens from\nthose of the &#x27;artful dodgers&#x27;.&quot;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_130\">    In the instant case, the AO has failed to demonstrate whether the transaction constituted a\n    colourable device so as to avoid tax. Hence, I cannot concur with him on this point at all.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_131\">\uf0b7 Secondly, the certificate of beneficial ownership furnished by the taxpayer is the interpretation\n    arrived at by the Netherlands authorities. Indian Courts may not necessarily agree with the\n    interpretation of the Netherlands authorities.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_132\">                                                     IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                      IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                           IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                    IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_53\">                                              Page 46 of 331</span>\n\nCertificate of residency has been filed before me by the assessee which bears the stamp and seal of the\nIrish Revenue Authorities stationed at Dublin and the Mumbai Bench has clearly held that this constitute\nsufficient evidence regarding status of assessee.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_133\">      \uf0b7 Thirdly, Circular 789 specifically deals with the India-Mauritius tax treaty. It cannot be\n         applied to India-Netherlands tax treaty.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_134\">         The wisdom of the Hon&#x27;ble High Court of this Country cannot be challenged by an AO.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_135\">      \uf0b7 Fourthly and most importantly, the Tax Residency Certificate (TRC), which is subject matter\n         of Circular 789 relied up on by the Court, has nothing to do with the beneficial ownership.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_136\">         The wisdom of the Hon&#x27;ble High Court of this Country cannot be challenged by an AO.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_137\">9.     For reasons mentioned, I am of the view that the case of the assessee is on `all fours&quot; vis-a-vis a\njudgement of the Hon&#x27;ble High Court as mentioned and the action of the AO cannot be sustained in first\nappeal. Hence, all grow ids are covered and in the result, appeal filed by the assessee is allowed in part.&quot;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_138\">16.    Being aggrieved with the order of the CIT(A), assessee as well as the Revenue are\n\nin appeal before the Tribunal.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_139\">17.    The learned Counsel for the assessee Shri. Percy Pardiwala, Sr. Advocate has\n\ninvited our attention to certain facts explained by the assessee through affidavit filed by\n\nits Director Mr. Hari Raju with the submission that assessee company was incorporated\n\non 16.12.2003 under the <a href=\"/doc/1353758/\" id=\"a_141\">Companies Act</a> and in 2004, assessee company set up its first\n\noffice at Bangalore to provide Information Technology (IT) Services to Google Inc., USA\n\n(hereinafter called as &quot;Google Inc&quot;). Vide agreement dated 01.04.2004 entered into\n\nbetween the appellant company and GIL, the appellant company was to provide IT enabled\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_54\">                                      Page 47 of 331</span>\n\nservices (ITES) to GIL. In any case, the distribution team of the\n\nAppellant has no access to data/personal information pertaining to end user of any of the\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_58\">                                       Page 51 of 331</span>\n\nGoogle properties such as the Google Search Engine etc; like his name, age, gender, etc.\n\nThe Appellant Company also does not even have access to the Internet Protocol (&quot;IP&quot;)\n\naddress of users of any Google service and therefore is not in a position to share that with\n\nanyone/advertisers (including Government and law enforcement agencies).\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_147\">24. It was further contended that Google AdWord Program is a &quot;self-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_149\">serve&quot;onlineadvertising    service   provided      by    Google       (Google      Inc./GIL)       for\n                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_59\">                                        Page 52 of 331</span>\n\nbusinesseswanting to display advertisement in relation to their websites and products on\n\nGoogle online property or a third party online property. Mr. Pardiwala further contended that the fundamental submissions of the assessee\n\nwhich has been completely missed by the AO is that the revenue arising from the\n\ndistribution agreement dated 12.12.2005 and superceded by the reseller agreement dated\n\n01.07.2012 whereby the distributor or reseller&#x27;s rights were granted to the assessee owing\n\nto the nature of the business income in the hands of the non-resident and in the absence of\n\nthe permanent establishment such non-resident in India no portion of such income could\n\nhave been brought to tax.
