CIT</a> 140 TTJ 195 (Mum.Trib.),in support of his contention that payment made\n\nto non-resident of India for purchase of online space to be in nature of business income or\n\nin the absence of permanent establishment (PE) of such non-resident it cannot be taxable\n\nin India.</span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_155\">29.   In support of above contention, reliance was also placed upon the order of the\n\nTribunal (Mumbai Bench) in case of Set India Pvt. Ltd., in ITA No.4372/Mum/2004 in\n\nwhich it has been held that right of distribution is purely a commercial right which is\n\ndistinct from right to use copyright and hence payment to non-resident under distribution\n\nagreement does not constitute royalty. Reliance was also placed upon the judgment of Taj\n\nTV Ltd., in ITA No. 4678/Mum/2007 wherein it was held that distribution income payable\n\nto the assessee company is a business profit and cannot be taxed in India in the absence of\n\npermanent establishment of the recipient in India. It was further contended by the learned\n\nCounsel for the assessee that it is not open for the Tribunal to be dissuaded with the Co-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_156\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"5905779\" id=\"span_66\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1867676\" id=\"span_67\">ordinate Bench&#x27;s decision and to form a independent opinion without referring the matter\n\nto the special bench in the light of judgment of the Apex Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/1867676/\" id=\"a_148\">UOI Vs.\n\nParas Laminates Pvt. Ltd.</a>, (1991) AIR 1991 SC - 696 and the order of the Tribunal in the\n\ncase of <a href=\"/doc/5905779/\" id=\"a_149\">DCIT Vs. Sumit Securities</a> (2011) 11 ITR (T) 88 (Mum.Trib. Special Bench).</span></span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_157\">                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_68\">                                       Page 56 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_158\">30.   The learned Counsel for the assessee further invited our attention to the assessment\n\norder for the assessment year 2008-09 where the appellant was constituted a dependent\n\nagency PE of GIL i.e., non-resident. In that case, there was no whisper by the AO that\n\nreceipt in the hands of non-resident par-took the character of royalty income. The\n\nabsurdity of the whole situation further gets highlighted by the AO not even adverting the\n\nposition of <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_150\">article 12</a> of the treaty where the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_151\">article 12(4)</a> specifically\n\nexcludes the application of <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_152\">article 12</a> where there is a permanent establishment of non-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_159\">resident in the house country. The learned Counsel for the assessee further contended that\n\ndepartment needs to make up its mind whether the non-resident has a permanent\n\nestablishment or not. It is only when certain determination is concluded, the <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_153\">article 12</a> can\n\nbe applied to determine whether receipts are in the nature of royalty income.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_160\">31.   The learned Counsel Mr. Pardiwala further invited our attention that from July\n\n2016, the legislature in its wisdom has introduced the equalization levy. The whole\n\npurpose of bringing this levy was to avoid protected litigation on the existence of\n\npermanent establishment and to capture the income of non-resident who on account of\n\nabsence of permanent establishment as per the existing provisions under treaty, escaped\n\nthe net of taxation in India. It was further contended that in assessment year 2017-18,\n\n2018-19, the department has taken a position that equalization levy is applicable on\n\npayment made by the advertiser to non-resident for the purchase of online advertisement\n                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_69\">                                        Page 57 of 331</span>\n\nspace. These oscillating views taken by the department reflects the confusion behind\n\ndepartment&#x27;s logic to bring to tax receipts under dispute tax in India and are thus devoid\n\nof any merit.    Therefore, the order CIT(A) treating the remittance to GIL as royalty,\n\ndeserves to be set aside.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_161\">32.   The learned Counsel for the assessee further invited our attention to provisions of\n\nFinance Bill, 2016 with the submission that the legislature has introduced the equalization\n\nlevy at 6% of the amount of consideration for specific service received/receivable by non-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_162\">resident not having permanent establishment (PE) in India, from the resident in India who\n\ncarries out business or profession or from a non-resident having permanent establishment\n\nin India with certain safe guards.      Keeping in view the object of introduction of\n\nequalization levy on specific payments to non-AE, the payment made by the assessee to\n\nits AE under Distributor AdWord Agreement should be considered as business profit in\n\nthe hands of non-resident.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_163\">33.   The learned Counsel for the assessee has also invited our attention to provisions of\n\n<a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_154\">article 12</a> of DTAA with Ireland with the submission that the term royalty used in this\n\narticle means the payment of any kind received as consideration for the use of or the right\n\nto use, any copyright of literary, artistic, scientific work including cinematography, films,\n\netc. As per the definition of word royalty as per DTAA, royalty can only be taxed on\n\nreceipt basis in the hands of the recipient i.e., non-resident. In the instant case, the AO\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_70\">                                       Page 58 of 331</span>\n\nhas treated the deemed income of royalty only on accrual basis. Therefore, the taxability\n\nof royalty in the impugned assessment year under <a href=\"/doc/874582/\" id=\"a_155\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> is improper. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"56225\" id=\"span_71\">In support\n\nof his contention, he <a href=\"/doc/5905779/\" id=\"a_156\">placed reliance upon</a> the order of the Tribunal in the case of <a href=\"/doc/56225/\" id=\"a_157\">Pizzahut\n\nInternational Inc., Vs. DCIT</a> (2012) Taxmann.com 111 Delhi, Booz.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"168097161\" id=\"span_72\"><a href=\"/doc/168097161/\" id=\"a_158\">Allen &amp; Hamilton\n\n(India) Ltd. &amp; Co. Kg.,Vs. ACIT</a> in ITA No.4502 to 4508/Mum/2003 and judgment of the\n\nBombay High Court in the case of DIT Vs. Siemens aktiengesellschaft in ITA\n\nNo.124/2010.</span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_164\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"117720131\" id=\"span_73\">34.   Mr. Percy Pardiwala had also placed a reliance upon the order of the Tribunal in the\n\ncase of CSC Technologies Singapore Pte. <a href=\"/doc/117720131/\" id=\"a_159\">Ltd., Vs. ADIT</a> (2012) 19 Taxmann.com 123,\n\nNational Organic Chemicals Industries Ltd., Vs. DCIT (2006) 5 SOT 317 Mumbai and\n\nSaira Asia Interiors Pvt. Ltd., ITA No. 673/2014 in support of his contentions that DTAA\n\ntax liability can arise only at the point of payment and not at the time of crediting the\n\namount. Therefore, at the time crediting the amount, there cannot be an occasion for\n\ndeduction or withholding the tax on such income.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_165\">35.   The learned Counsel for the assessee further placed a reliance upon the judgment\n\nof Delhi High Court in the case of Formula One World Championship (2016) 76\n\nTaxmann.com 6 (Del), and DIT Vs. Sherton International Inc., 313 ITR 267 in support of\n\nhis contention that use of intellectual property (Trade mark ) as incidental to main purpose\n                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_74\">                                        Page 59 of 331</span>\n\nof agreement, does not enable the payment thereof to qualify under the definition of\n\nRoyalty under the Act or by AO.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_166\">36.   Mr. Percy Pardiwala further contended that assessee had a bonafide belief for non-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_167\">deducting the TDS on the payment made on account of AdWord Program to the GIL, as\n\nthere was no disallowance at earlier occasion. Moreover, the GIL has no PE in India\n\ntherefore the assessee/appellant was under a bonafide belief that TDS is not required to be\n\ndeducted on the payment to GIL which in fact is a business receipt in its hand. In support\n\nof his contention he placed a reliance of the judgement in the case of CIT Vs. Kotex\n\nSecurities Ltd., 383 ITR 0001 (SC).\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_168\">37.   In oppugnation, learned Standing Counsel for the department, Shri. K. A. Arvind\n\ninvited our attention to the fact that in Google AdWord Program distribution agreement\n\ndated 12.12.2005, the GIPL was granted the marketing and distribution rights of AdWord\n\nProgram to the advertisers in India. The amount credited by GIPL to the account of GIL\n\ncould constitute sum chargeable under the provisions of the Act as the payment is in the\n\nnature of royalty for the purpose of licence to use intellectual property rights.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_169\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"586984\" id=\"span_75\">38.   Mr. Arvind has further contended that the AO has considered the judgment of the\n\nMadras High Court in the case of Consine IndiaPvt. <a href=\"/doc/586984/\" id=\"a_160\">LTd., Vs. Google India Pvt. Ltd.</a>,\n\ndated 30.09.2010 in which the concept of search engine and its operation was explained,\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_76\">                                       Page 60 of 331</span>\n\nwhile adjudicating the issue of nature of payment made by GIPL to GIL. It was further\n\ncontended that as per clause 2.6 of the AdWord Program Distribution Agreement, the\n\ndistributor (GIPL) will provide after sales services to advertisers in accordance with broad\n\ninstructions, training and standards of Google. Clause 3.1 of the AdWord agreement\n\nfurther provides for Google Ireland (GIL) to provide advertising space through the\n\nAdWord Programme for distribution by the distributor i.e., GIPL/Appellant to advertisers\n\nas set forth therein. As per clause 3.4, GILagrees to provide minimum level of service as\n\nspecified in exhibit &quot;C&quot;. As per exhibit &quot;C&quot; all advertiser shall be instructed by the\n\ndistributor to contact distributor directly for support and not to communicate directly with\n\nthe GIL. Mr. Arvind has further invited our attention to clause 7 of the Agreement\n\naccording to which disclosure of confidential and/or proprietary information disclosed\n\nhereunder, including the user data (including any aggregated user data), the existence and\n\ncontent of this agreement, and any information provided pursuant to this agreement shall\n\nbe governed by the Google standard mutual non-Disclosure Agreement executed by the\n\nparties prior to or concurrently with this agreement. He further invited our attention to\n\nclause 8 of the agreement, according to which the distributor shall maintain all user data\n\nin accordance with the local laws and regulation applicable to such data and shall\n\nimplement policies and procedures with respect to user data that are atleast as protective\n                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_77\">                                        Page 61 of 331</span>\n\nof the rights of the advertisers as is provided in the Google Privacy Policy set forth and all\n\nrelevant policies employed therein.</span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_170\">39.   The learned Standing Counsel further invited our attention to Services Agreement\n\ndated 01.04.2004 with the submission that from the reading of the Services Agreement it\n\nis clear that GIPL has been provided with confidential information as referred to in clause\n\n1.2 of the Services Agreement and as per clause 3.3, the GIPL shall perform the services\n\nonly for Google Ireland or its affiliates and consequently it shall not render any similar\n\nservices to any other person. It was further contended that as per clause 6.1 of Service\n\nAgreement, the GIL will disclose certain confidential information to Google India solely\n\nto permit Google India to perform its obligations under the agreement. It was also\n\nstipulated in the Service Agreement that the Appellant agrees that such confidential\n\ninformation shall be kept secret by the Appellant during the term of the Agreement and as\n\nper clause 12.5, on termination of the Service Agreement, Google India shall discontinue\n\nusing of confidential information, documentation, intellectual property and the software\n\ntechnology and shall have no further rights with respect thereto. On termination, the\n\nAppellant shall immediately return to Google Ireland or destroy all copies of confidential\n\ninformation, documentation and the software embodiments in its possession or control.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_171\">Upon termination of this agreement, GIL will have the option in its sole discretion of &quot;(1)\n\nelecting at any time to fulfil any unfinished service itself or by contracting with other\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_78\">                                       Page 62 of 331</span>\n\naffiliates or third parties and / or (ii) permitting Google India to continue to provide any\n\nunfinished services and continue to use confidential information/documentation,\n\nintellectual property and the software to the extent necessary to provide such services\n\npursuant to the limited licence agreement to be entered into between Google Ireland and\n\nGoogle India, following such termination or expiration.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_172\">40.   The learned Standing Counsel further contended that from the reading of all clauses\n\nof the service agreement in conjunction with Google AdWord Program Distribution\n\nAgreement, it is clear beyond doubt that Google India has been provided with thelicence\n\nto use IP for which Google India has agreed to make certain payments to Google Ireland\n\nand same is in the nature of royalty, as per provisions of <a href=\"/doc/874582/\" id=\"a_161\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the Act read\n\nwith DTAA. It was further contended that Google Ireland is allowing Google India to\n\naccess all intellectual properties and confidential information and the same is being used\n\nby the Appellant (Google India) for the activities related to distribution agreement. The\n\nobligation/distribution of the services agreement would make it clear that both the\n\ndocuments cannot be separated from each other and are to be read together. Therefore,\n\nfor the purpose of marketing and distribution activities, Google India is granted the right\n\nto use the valuable business asset of Google Ireland, which includes intellectual Property\n\nin the products and services offered by GIL.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_173\">                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_79\">                                        Page 63 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_174\">41.   The ld. Counsel for the Revenue, Mr. Aravind, has further contended that\n\ncontention of the assessee that Adwords Distribution agreement dated 12.12.2005 is\n\nentered into by the marketing &amp; distribution services for Adwords program division and\n\nthe service agreement dated 01.04.2004 is entered into by ITES division and they are not\n\ninterrelated, cannot be accepted. It is an admitted position that the appellant has an\n\nobligation to the advertiser to provide pre-sales and post-sales services and the pre-sale\n\nand post-sale services can only be provided through ITES division of the appellant with\n\naccess to the database and the confidential information owned by Google Ireland and\n\nlicenced to Google India, the appellant. In view of the agreement being entered by\n\nGoogle India with Google Ireland either on 12.12.2005 or on 01.04.2004 for the same\n\npurpose, the division of services by Google India by different divisions does not take\n\naway the impact of reading of both the agreements together, therefore both the\n\nagreements are inseparable. By reading of the obligations of Google India in both the\n\nagreements, it makes it clear that both the agreements has to be read together and cannot\n\nbe acted upon independent of each other. Hence the contention of the assessee that both\n\nthe agreements are different is an after-thought and liable to rejected.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_175\">42.   It was further submitted by Mr. Arvind that IP of Google resides in search engine\n\ntechnology, associated software and other features and hence right to use for performing\n\nvarious activities like accepting advertisements and providing after sales services would\n                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_80\">                                        Page 64 of 331</span>\n\nclearly fall within the ambit of &quot;Royalty&quot;.          As per the terms of the distribution\n\nagreement, the appellant has been authorized to sell or offer for sale the Adword program\n\nto the advertisers which is nothing but granting of licence to appellant to sell or offer for\n\nsale to advertisers with certain rights. By acquiring the distribution and marketing rights,\n\nthe appellant has been granted licence in respect of Adword&#x27;s program to sell or offer\n\nfor sale certain rights to the advertisrs and such rights or the right to use the Adword\n\nprogram. Therefore, the licence to use the Adword program by the copyright holder is\n\nlicenced to market and sell the Adword program. The licence to use the adword program\n\n(search engine) which is copyrighted by Google would amount to right to use the\n\ncopyright.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_176\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"15058786\" id=\"span_81\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"41077886\" id=\"span_82\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"39708190\" id=\"span_83\">43.   Mr. Aravind further <a href=\"/doc/586984/\" id=\"a_162\">placed reliance upon</a> the judgment of the jurisdictional High\n\nCourt in the case of <a href=\"/doc/39708190/\" id=\"a_163\">CIT v. Synopsis International Ltd.</a> (<a href=\"/doc/41077886/\" id=\"a_164\">2013) 2012 Taxman 454, CIT&amp;\n\nAnr. V. Samsung Electronics Ltd., &amp; Ors.</a> 345 <a href=\"/doc/15058786/\" id=\"a_165\">ITR 494, CIT v. CGI Information Systems\n\nand Management Consultants Pvt. Ltd.</a>, (2014) 226 Taxman 319 (Kar) and AAR\n\njudgment No.985/2010 dated 28.07.2015 in Skillsoft of Ireland Ltd., the order of the\n\nBangalore Bench of the Tribunal in the case of Vodafone South Ltd. v. DDIT(IT) (2015)\n\n53 taxmann.com 441 (Bang. Trib.) and ABB FZ-LCC (2017) 83 taxmann.com 86 (Bang.</span></span></span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_177\">ITAT) in support of his contention that the amount paid towards licence to use IPR is in\n\nthe nature of royalty and chargeable under <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_166\">section195</a> of the Act.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_178\">                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_84\">                                       Page 65 of 331</span>\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_179\">44.   It was further contended that licence to sell presupposes licence being granted to\n\nthe distributor (Google India) and licence to sell enables Google India to transaction the\n\nlicence/right to use the Adword program. Therefore the consideration paid is for the\n\nlicence to enable Google India to resell the licence/right to use to the advertisers would\n\namount to payment of a royalty, because without licence if sale of advertisement space\n\nto the advertiser by the appellant in the search engine of Google, which is an IP would\n\namount to infringement of the rights being enjoyed by Google Ireland.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_180\">45.   The ld. Standing counsel Mr. Arvind further contended that under the distribution\n\nagreement, the appellant has been granted the right to use trademarks and brand features\n\nfor the purpose of selling the advertisement space and the licence to use the IP being tool\n\nof the trade for the appellant, right to use Google trademarks and brand features without\n\nany ambiguity, would amount to licence to use IP and the consideration for its use\n\nconstitutes royalty chargeable<a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_167\"> u/s. 195</a> of the Act.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_181\">46.   It was further contended that under the distribution agreement, the appellant has\n\nbeen granted the distribution right involving the transfer of rights in process. The entire\n\nsearch engine technology on which licence has been granted to Google India for selling\n\nadvertisement space to the advertisers is a &quot;process&quot; and the search engine technology\n\nis an IP. Hence consequential payment for the licence to use the process being an IP is\n                                              IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                               IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                             IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_85\">                                         Page 66 of 331</span>\n\nroyalty. In view of clause 3.1 of the distribution agreement, the appellant was granted\n\ntransfer of know-how and has also been provided access to internal tools for performing\n\nthe obligations under the Adword agreement. The statements recorded from the persons\n\nconcerned of the Google India which is reproduced by the AO in his order, would make\n\nit clear that marketing, distribution and maintenance of the Adword program is a\n\nresponsibility of Google India involving knowledge of tools and training on its usage,\n\nknowledge of trademark policy, add content policy, access to database and access to\n\nconfidential information. Such knowledge has been imparted through extensive training\n\nby Google Ireland to the appellant which would amount to transfer of know-how and\n\nliable for tax<a href=\"/doc/874582/\" id=\"a_168\"> u/s. 9(1)(vi)</a> of the Act as royalty.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_182\">47.   The ld. Standing counsel further invited our attention to nondisclosure agreement\n\nwhich is Exhibit-B of the distribution agreement with the submission that it clearly\n\ndemonstrates that by virtue of the disclosure of the confidential information and access\n\nprovided to the confidential information to the appellant by Google Ireland, the sums\n\npayable by Google India to Google Ireland is for information, know-how and skill\n\nimparted to Google India, i.e., the appellant. It was further contended that grant of\n\ndistribution rights involves use of industrial, commercial and scientific equipment.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_183\">Adword platform is similar to portal running on servers and Adword&#x27;s platform is based\n\non search engine technology of Google. Hence Adword program is commercial and\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_86\">                                       Page 67 of 331</span>\n\nscientific equipment and without having access to the servers running on the Adword\n\nplatform, the appellant cannot perform its functions/exploit its rights as per the\n\ndistribution agreement. Hence the distribution rights includes the right to use the\n\nindustrial, commercial and scientific equipment which are the servers. He further placed\n\nreliance upon the judgment of the AAR in the case of Cargo Community Network Ltd.,\n\n159 Taxman 243 in which it has been held that portal and server together constitute\n\nintegrated commercial and scientific equipment and for obtaining internet access to\n\nairlines, the use of portal without server is unthinkable. Therefore it is a irresistible\n\nconclusion that payment for concurrent access to utilize the sophisticated services\n\noffered by the portal would be covered by the expression &quot;royalty&quot;.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_184\">48.   The ld. Standing counsel further contended that though the above aspect has not\n\nbeen agreed by the CIT(Appeals), the reasons and the findings recorded by the AO could\n\nbe relevant for considering the matter under the remaining heads like know-how,\n\ntrademark and process. It was further contended that from a reading of the definition of\n\nroyalty under the DTAA, it is clear that the amounts paid by Google India under the\n\ndistribution agreement is towards royalty.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_185\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"196789704\" id=\"span_87\">49.   Mr. Arvind further placed a reliance upon the judgment of Hon&#x27;ble Madras High\n\nCourt in the case of <a href=\"/doc/196789704/\" id=\"a_169\">Verizon Communications Singapore Private Ltd. v. ITO</a>, 361 ITR\n\n575 in which it has been held that payment for licence to use the IPR amounts to a\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_88\">                                       Page 68 of 331</span>\n\nroyalty. Reliance was also placed upon the judgment of AAR in the case of Citrix\n\nSystems Asia-Pacific Pvt. Ltd., 343 ITR 1 wherein it was held that payment from\n\ndistribution for sale/licensing of software product which is copyrighted amounts to\n\nlicence to use the IPR and is liable for tax under the Act as well as the DTAA as\n\nroyalty. Further reliance was also placed upon the judgment of AAR in the case of Cargo\n\nCommunity Network Ltd. reported at 289 ITR 355 in which it was held that portal is a\n\nscientific equipment and amounts paid towards right to use in industrial, commercial and\n\nscientific equipment would be covered by the expression royalty under the Act as well\n\nas the DTAA.</span> He further <a href=\"/doc/196789704/\" id=\"a_170\">placed reliance upon</a> the order of the Delhi Bench of the\n\nTribunal in the case of Gracemac Corporation v. ADIT (2010)41 DTR 65 in which it\n\nwas held that licence to use the copyright would fall within the ambit of royalty under\n\nthe Act as well as the DTAA.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_186\">50.   The ld. Standing counsel further invited our attention to the documents relating to\n\nfunctioning of Google Adword program from pages 416 to 528 of the Paper book filed\n\nby the appellant through which the assessee has tried to explain the functions of Google\n\nAdword program, with the submission that from the reading of the relevant material filed\n\nbefore the Tribunal, it is clear that the appellant has provided the following services in\n\nbrief to the advertisers:-\n</p>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_95\">          a. Contextual Targeting\n          h. Topic Targeting\n          i. Audience Targeting\n          j. Gender Targeting\n          k. Age-group Targeting\n          l. Manual and Automatic Bidding\n          m. Billing Information and backup credit card payment\n          n. Budget Targeting\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_187\">51.   These services cannot be provided without there being a licence or access to IPR\n\nand database. He further invited our attention to the agreement entered into with the\n\nappellant and customer/advertiser, according to which, the appellant has retained the\n\noption of accepting/rejecting the advertisement, continuation/termination of the\n\nadvertisement and also the nature of billing system. This would demonstrate further\n\naccess to the IPR which would amount to use the licence to use and the payment is in\n\nthe nature of royalty.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_188\">52.   The ld. Standing counsel further invited our attention to the statements of Shri\n\nArijit Sarkar and Shri Vikas Agnihotri, who made it clear that the appellant was provided\n\naccess to the tools which are in the nature of IPR for providing services in pursuance to\n\nthe agreement by Google India with Google Ireland and the advertisers. It was further\n\ncontended that Google India is providing AdSense services and generating revenue by\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_90\">                                       Page 70 of 331</span>\n\nutilizing licence to use IPR and hence payment for such use is royalty. He further invited\n\nour attention to the statements made by the assessee before the TPO dated 5.2.2013, copy\n\nof the same is placed at page 56 of paper book filed by the revenue, wherein the assessee\n\nhas admitted that data processing work and review of the advertisement as per the\n\neditorial guidelines is carried out by the appellant. These services cannot be rendered\n\nby the appellant without licence/access to database/IPRs.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_189\">53.   The ld. Standing counsel further invited our attention to the book of &#x27;Learning\n\nGoogle Adwords and Google Analytics&#x27; authored by Benjamin Goldman with the\n\nsubmission that Mr. Benjamin Goldman was a co-founder &amp; CEO at Loves Data and\n\nwas the first trainer certified by Google both for Google Adwords and Google\n\nAnalytics. As per the opinion of the author which can be considered an expert opinion,\n\nGoogle India in pursuance of distribution agreement and Adword sale agreement with\n\nthe advertiser would be rendering the following services:-\n</p>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_96\">         a.   Display Keyword Targeting\n         b.   Placement Targeting.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_97\">         c.   Topic Targeting.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_98\">         d.   Interest Categories.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_99\">         e.   Remarketing.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_100\">         f.   Gender Targeting.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_101\">         g.   Age Targeting.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_102\">         h.   Parent Status Targeting.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_103\">         i.   Display Campaign Optimiser\n         j.   Location Targeting, Network Targeting\n         k.   Physical Location Targeting\n                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_91\">                                        Page 71 of 331</span>\n\n          l. Schedule Targeting\n          m. YouTube Targeting\n          n. Device Targeting\n          o. AdSense\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_190\">54.   He further invited our attention to the reply of the assessee dated 28.01.2013 filed\n\nbefore the TPO for the AY 2009-10 in which it has been clearly admitted that the\n\npayment for rendering ITES (pre and post sale services) are on the basis of the percentage\n\nof distribution fee from India. The above aspect further makes it clear that distribution\n\nagreement and the service agreement is interdependent and has to be read together and\n\ninseparable.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_191\">55.   The ld. Standing counsel further contended that in view of the above position, it\n\nis clear that licence granted by Google Ireland in favour of the appellant is for\n\nexploitation of the search enginge being IPR and the IPR has been used by the appellant\n\nas tool of the trade and hence payment is in the nature of royalty liable to tax under the\n\nAct as well as the DTAA.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_192\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"35917315\" id=\"span_92\">56.   It was further contended that judgments relied upon by the assessee in the case of\n\n<a href=\"/doc/35917315/\" id=\"a_171\">Right Florist Pvt. Ltd.</a> (supra) is not applicable to the facts and circumstances of the case\n\nas <a href=\"/doc/35917315/\" id=\"a_172\">in that case</a> the advertisers had only placed advertisement in the search engine and no\n\nright in the IPR was conferred on the advertisers.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"89798900\" id=\"span_93\">Similar is the position with the\n\njudgment of <a href=\"/doc/89798900/\" id=\"a_173\">Yahoo India</a> (supra) on which heavy reliance is placed by the assessee. In\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_94\">                                       Page 72 of 331</span>\n\nthat case the transaction does not involve licence to use any IPRs and no access to the\n\nserver. The payment is only commission for procuring advertisement, whereas in the\n\npresent case right from review of the ad, acceptance of the ad and uploading the same\n\nincluding the post sale services are being provided by the appellantand without\n\ninvolving use or right to use IPR the said obligation cannot be performed. Similar is the\n\nposition with the decision of Pinstorm Technologies Pvt. Ltd. and Set India Pvt. Ltd.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_193\">(supra). In those cases, the facts are entirely different as nowhere the assessee either\n\ninvolved to provide post sales service or licence to use the IPRs.                 Therfore, the\n\njudgments cannot be relied in favour of the assessee.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_194\">57.   In so far as the reference made by the assessee to High Power Committee dated\n\n16.12.1999 and Tax Treaty dated 01.02.2001, it was contended that payment by the\n\nadvertiser directly to the owner of the search engine could not amount to royalty and\n\nthere is no dispute on these facts, but the facts of the impugned cases are entirely\n\ndifferent.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_195\">58.   With regard to taxability of royalty as per DTAA, the ld. Standing counsel has\n\ncontended that language of definition of royalty in DTAA does not suggest that amount\n\npaid to Google Ireland is taxable in the year of receipt and hence no obligation to deduct\n\ntax at source on credit basis. <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_174\">Section 195(1)</a> of the Act imposes obligation on the\n\nassessee to deduct tax at source in respect of payment made to non-resident where the\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_95\">                                       Page 73 of 331</span>\n\nsum is chargeable under the provisions of the Act. It is held by the Apex Court in the\n\ncase of GE India reported at (2010) 193 Taxman 234 (SC) and Ellie Lilly reported at\n\n(2009)178 Taxman 505 (SC) that provisions of section 195 has to be read along with\n\ncharging provisions i.e., <a href=\"/doc/42009/\" id=\"a_175\">section 4</a>, <a href=\"/doc/648260/\" id=\"a_176\">5</a>, <a href=\"/doc/229141/\" id=\"a_177\">9</a> &amp; <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_178\">90</a> of the Act. On conjoint reading of the above\n\nprovisions, it is clear that the amounts paid by the assessee to Google Ireland are\n\nchargeable under the Act on accrual basis. Hence contention of assessee that amount is\n\nchargeable in the hands of Google Ireland on receipt basis is misplaced.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_196\">59.   It was further contended that if the language of definition of royalty under DTAA\n\nwith Ireland under <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_179\">article 12(3)(a)</a> is read, the wordings of the term &quot;royalties&quot; as used\n\nin this article means payment of any kind received as consideration for the use .....&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_197\">would clearly and unambiguously makes it clear that payment received as consideration\n\nfor the use would alone be considered as royalty. The words &quot;payments of any kind\n\nreceived as a consideration for the use of&quot; has to be read together and it would only mean\n\nthe classification of the income and not the method of accounting. Hence the contention\n\nof the assessee is misplaced.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_198\">60.   The ld. Standing counsel further invited our attention to the fact that the assessee\n\nhas provided IT services IT enabled services to Google Ireland in addition to marketing\n\nand distribution services for the adverts program. The assessee will be receiving\n\namounts for IT services and IT enabled services from Google Ireland according to\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_96\">                                       Page 74 of 331</span>\n\nservices agreement and assessee will be paying Google Ireland in view of the marketing\n\nand distribution services for Adword program. It is an undisputed fact that the assessee\n\nis wholly owned subsidiary of Google. In view of the close connection between Google\n\nIndia and Google Ireland, the payments to be received by the assessee for IT services\n\nand IT enabled services from GIL can be adjusted towards payment made to GIL towards\n\nmarketing and distribution services for Adword program.The fact that the assessee has\n\nnot reflected the amounts paid to Google Ireland in the P&amp;L account would further\n\njustify the above aspect.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_199\">61.   In the above context if the language employed in <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_180\">article 12(3)(a)</a> of the DTAA\n\nspecifically with the words &quot;means payment of any kind received&quot; would mean the\n\nreceipt of amount by virtue of adjustment/set off in the books of account of the assessee.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_200\">Further &quot;payment of any kind received&quot; has to be read as any mode of payment either\n\nby book adjustment/credit or actual payment. Any other meaning would read the\n\nlanguage redundant in view of the provisons of <a href=\"/doc/42009/\" id=\"a_181\">section 4</a>, <a href=\"/doc/648260/\" id=\"a_182\">5</a>, <a href=\"/doc/229141/\" id=\"a_183\">9</a>, <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_184\">90</a> &amp; <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_185\">195</a> of the Act. The\n\nDTAA does not determine the method of accounting and the year of taxability in respect\n\nof parties to the agreement. What the DTAA provides for is the extent of taxability of\n\nincome and the percentage of the tax on the income liable for tax and the distribution of\n\ntax among the countries party to the DTAA. Hence the language employed in defining\n\nthe meaning of royalty cannot be read to mean the method of accounting. The DTAA\n                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_97\">                                        Page 75 of 331</span>\n\ndoes not deal with the year of taxability or the method of accounting of either parties.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_201\">The only section which has imposed obligation on the assessee is <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_186\">section 195(1)</a> of the\n\nAct which obligates the assessee to deduct tax at source in respect of the income\n\nchargeable under the Act. The section does not empower the payer to examine the\n\napplicability of the DTAA to the payee. The language of <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_187\">section 90(2)</a> of the Act makes\n\nit very clear beyond any doubt that option to exercise the benefit of either the Act or the\n\nDTAA is conferred on the non-resident. Hence at the stage of payment without there\n\nbeing any indication by the recipient, the payer cannot step into the shoes of recipient to\n\nexercise the option provided u/s. 90(2) and claim the benefit of DTAA. Therefore the\n\ncontention of the assessee that receipt in the hands of Google Ireland is liable to be taxed\n\non cash basis is completely baseless, also for the reason that Google Ireland itself has\n\nfiled return of income for the AY 2007-08 and 2008-09 and has declared its method\n\nfollowed for accounting as mercantile system of accounting. It was further contended\n\nthat in the case of Vodafone South Ltd., the Tribunal has categorically held that\n\napplicability of DTAA is not automatic and what is to be considered at the time of\n\npayment by the assessee is only regarding the chargeability of income under the Act and\n\nthe assessee cannot be permitted to take shelter under the DTAA as the benefit of DTAA\n\nis conferred only on the non-resident recipient. He further <a href=\"/doc/89798900/\" id=\"a_188\">placed reliance upon</a> the\n\njudgment of the Apex Court in the case of Standard Triumph Motor, 201 ITR 391\n                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_98\">                                        Page 76 of 331</span>\n\nwherein it has been held that method of accounting adopted by the recipient is irrelevant\n\nand credit in the books of account of the payer is sufficient to attract taxability. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"49006086\" id=\"span_99\">He also\n\n<a href=\"/doc/89798900/\" id=\"a_189\">placed reliance upon</a> the judgment of the Apex Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/49006086/\" id=\"a_190\">Palam Gas Service\n\nv. CIT</a> reported at (2017) 81 taxmann.com 43 (SC).</span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_202\">62.   The judgments relied upon by the assessee in support of his contention in the case\n\nof Siemens, Booz Allen, National Organic Chemicals, Pizza Hut, CSC Technology and\n\nSaira Asia are not applicable to the present case as the scope of <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_191\">section 90(2)</a> of the Act\n\nand the effect of charging provisions of <a href=\"/doc/42009/\" id=\"a_192\">section 4</a>, <a href=\"/doc/648260/\" id=\"a_193\">5</a> &amp; <a href=\"/doc/229141/\" id=\"a_194\">9</a> have not been examined. In the\n\nabsence of any authority in favour of the assessee, the assessee cannot presume that the\n\nnon-resident would be claiming benefit under the DTAA.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_203\">63.   If the terms of the payment as agreed between the parties is examined, it is clear\n\nthat on periodic intervals the amounts are liable to be paid and in the light of these facts,\n\nthe contention of the assessee are contrary to the claim that amount is taxable in the\n\nhands of the non-resident on receipt basis. He further placed heavy reliance upon the\n\njudgment of the Apex Court in the case of Vodafone International Holdings BV, 341\n\nITR 1 (SC) at para 67 &amp; 68 with the submission that in order to examine the real\n\ntransaction, the corporate veil is required to be lifted. If the agreement entered into\n\nbetween the assessee and Google Ireland is examined by bearing in mind the subsidiary\n\nrelationship between the two, especially to Exhibit-A dealing with the payment terms &amp;\n                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_100\">                                        Page 77 of 331</span>\n\nconditions at para 6.1, distributor has to make the payments at mutually agreed intervals\n\nduring the year and the final payment to be on the basis of duly audited accounts. Further\n\nas per clause 1 of the said terms &amp; conditions, the payment is on the percentage agreed\n\nupon on the basis of the books of accounts of the distributor maintained in accordance\n\nwith the Accounting Standards. In view of the above aspects, if the corporate veil is\n\nlifted and transactions are examined, it is clear that the contention of the assessee that\n\nliability to deduct tax at source would arise in the year of payment is contrary to the\n\nscheme of the Act and also to the agreements entered into by Google India with Google\n\nIreland. In view of the above, the non-payment to Google Ireland by Google India\n\nutmost would be deferment of payment in view of the close relationship and will not\n\nhave any bearing on the taxability in the year of accrual.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_204\">64.   With regard to incidental use of trademark, the ld. Standing counsel has contended\n\nthat the assessee has acquired a right under distribution agreement to sell the space in\n\nthe search engine which is an IPR including the trademark. The assessee is using the\n\ndistribution agreement coupled with IPR as tool of the trade and hence payment towards\n\nuse of trademark is also in the nature of royalty and liable to tax under the Act as well as\n\nthe DTAA.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_205\">65.   With regard to judgment of the Hon&#x27;ble Delhi High Court in the case of Sheraton\n\nInternational 313 ITR 267, Formula One World Championship (2016) 76 taxmann.com\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_101\">                                       Page 78 of 331</span>\n\n6 (Del) and the other judgments referred to by the assessee, the ld. Standing counsel has\n\nsubmitted that in those cases the main services provided was for the advertisement and\n\nno licence to use the IPR was involved, whereas in the present case, the use of IPR is\n\ninvolved. Therefore, the Hon&#x27;ble Court has held that use of trademark was incidental to\n\nthe main purpose of the agreement which was referred to as advertisement, publicity and\n\nsales promotion, whereas in the instant case, the assessee was selling the Adword space\n\nto the advertisers with the use of IPRs for the said purpose.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_206\">66.   With regard to equalization levy, the ld. Standing counsel has submitted that\n\nsection 164 and 165 are introduced by <a href=\"/doc/75703920/\" id=\"a_195\">Finance Act, 2016</a>. The Parliament has proposed\n\nthe equalization levy on the amount to be paid to the non-resident on digital advertising\n\nspace and the same has to be considered as business income.                    First of all, the\n\namendments are not applicable for the AY 2013-14 as the same are prospective. The\n\nequalization levy does not determine the classification of payment between royalty and\n\nbusiness income. Equalisation levy is only a charge on the payment made towards digital\n\nadvertisement space by the advertisers.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_207\">67.   With regard to the affidavit filed by the assessee explaining the functioning of the\n\nGoogle Adword program, the ld. Standing counsel has submitted that it is mere repetition\n\nof the statements made before the lower authorities. The assessee has not come out with\n\na specific functioning of the Google Adword program. Therefore, the functioning of\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_102\">                                       Page 79 of 331</span>\n\nGoogle Adword program can be ascertained from the relevant material placed during the\n\ncourse of hearing of these appeals.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_208\">68.   The ld. Standing counsel further contended that in the light of the detailed\n\nevidence on record, the nature of payment under the distribution agreement and the\n\nservice agreement is a royalty and the AO has rightly held the assessee to be in default\n\non non-deduction of tax on such payments.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_209\">69.   With regard to the contentions of the bonafide belief for non-deduction of TDS,\n\nthe learned Standing Counsel has contended that the assessee has divided the entire\n\nAdWord Program into two agreements 1) Distributor Agreement and 2) Service\n\nAgreement, knowing fully well that the payment made under the AdWord Program on\n\naccount of use of technology, access to IPRs, intangibles, derivative works as per the\n\nagreement, is a payment of royalty to GIL for which assessee / appellant is required to\n\ndeduct the TDS. Therefore, it cannot be said that appellant was under a bonafide belief\n\nthat he was not liable to deduct the TDS on such payment to GIL as it is a business profit\n\nin his hands.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_210\">70.   In rebuttal, the ld. Counsel for the assessee, Mr. Percy Pardiwalla, has contended\n\nthat the contentions of the revenue rather substantiate the averments of the appellant that\n\nnature of receipts are in the hands of non-resident was business income and in the\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_103\">                                      Page 80 of 331</span>\n\nabsence of any determination of Permanent Establishment of the non-resident in India\n\nin the order of the AO, there was no question of applicability of section 195 and\n\nconsequently <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_196\">section 201</a> of the Act. For the AYs 2017-18 and 2018-19, the department\n\nhas taken a position that equalization levy is applicable on payments made by the\n\nappellant to non-resident for the purchase of online advertising space. All these views\n\nundertaken by the department reflect the confusion with the department&#x27;s logic to bring\n\nto tax receipts under dispute to tax in India and are thus devoid of any merit. It was\n\nfurther contended that Adword is a registered trademark in the name of Google Inc., the\n\nAdword program is an auction based advertising program that lets advertisers deliver\n\nrelevant advertisement targeting to such queries on web content across Google site and\n\nthrough the Google network. To clarify, Google Adword programmit was contended\n\nthat there are following 5 parties involved as far as transaction involving Indian\n\nadvertisers are concerned:-\n</p>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_104\">      1. Google Inc. USA - The program owner of the Adword and legal owner of\n         Google web properties like Google search eninge.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_105\">      2. GIL has got licence to exploit rates in Google web properties and\n         commercially exploit Google Adword program globally, except America&#x27;s\n         region.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_106\">      3. GIPL - The appellant has got distribution rights under distribution\n         agreement to sell online advertisement space to advertisers in India.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_107\">      4. The advertisers and business owner wanting to advertise its product\n         or services; and\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_104\">                                       Page 81 of 331</span>\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_108\">      5. The viewer/user who views the advertisement while using Google web\n         properties like search engine.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_211\">71.   The Indian advertiser who enters into contract with the appellant has to pay the\n\nappellant an amount fixed by the advertiser for every click the viewer makes on its\n\nadvertisement.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_212\">72.   With regard to the judgments of the jurisdictional High Court referred to by the\n\nrevenue, the ld. Sr. Consel, Mr. Pardiwalla has contended that use of software necessarily\n\nresults in payment of royalty is distinguishable because in all those cases, what was being\n\nexamined by the Hon&#x27;ble High Court was taxability of the payment made by\n\n*shrinkwrap software and has no relevance or bearing on the issue at hand. Thus\n\nreliance placed on the decision does not support the case of the revenue. Nowhere the\n\nappellant has been granted licence to use the software. The ld. Counsel reiterated his\n\ncontentions that ITES segment was set up in the year 2004 for providing back office\n\nsupport services which include ad review and review of the advertisement process is\n\nautomatic process and the systems itself directs any flagged advertisement for manual\n\nreview. There is no human intervention in the selection process as to where ad will get\n\nreviewed since, there are multiple locations providing this review. These ad review\n\nservices are provided to GIL not to the advertisers. The ld. Counsel further contended\n\nthat the argument of the revenue that right to modify the ads is not possible without the\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_105\">                                       Page 82 of 331</span>\n\nlicence to use IPR is clearly baseless because the whole process of the advertising is that\n\nthe advertiser chooses the keywords, form of advertisements, advertising contents that\n\nis reflective of the business to be advertised. Under the terms &amp; conditions of the\n\ncontract between the advertisers and the appellant, the advertisers grant all the\n\nintellectual property rights inherent in such advertisement whether a form or content of\n\nthe appellant for which no consideration is paid by the appellant to the advertiser. Be\n\nthat as it may, it is neither relevant nor determinative of the issue at hand i.e., payment\n\nmade by the appellant to GIL.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_213\">73.   With regard to the contention of the department that Google India has right to use\n\nGoogle brand features and therefore the payment made by the appellant is in the nature\n\nof royalty, the ld. Counsel for the assessee has submitted that the argument of the\n\ndepartment is contrary to the precedent set by the Hon&#x27;ble High Court of Delhi in the\n\ncase of Formula One World Championship (2016) 76 taxmann.com 6 (Del) and DIT Vs.\n\nSheraton International Inc. 313 ITR 267 wherein the Hon&#x27;ble Delhi High Court has held\n\nthat use of intellectual property that is incidental to the main purpose of agreement does\n\nnot enable the payment made thereof to qualify under the definition of royalty under the\n\nAct or DTAA. It was further contended that use of Google brand feature is probably\n\nthe only intellectual property granted to the appellant under distribution agreement,\n\nhowever, the same has been granted to the appellant for the purpose of appellant\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_106\">                                       Page 83 of 331</span>\n\nachieving its obligation under the said agreement. Therefore, use of Google brand\n\nfeature under distribution agreement would not render payment of royalty under the Act\n\nor under India-Ireland DTAA in the light of judgments of Formula One World\n\nChampionship (supra) and Sheraton International Inc. (supra).\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_214\">74.   With regard to contentions of the revenue that GIPL has access to database of\n\nGIL, the ld. Counsel has submitted that the appellant has no access to database of Google\n\nand in support thereof, he placed some documents to demonstrate that wherever\n\ninvestigating agencies have sought information regarding user of such products, the\n\nappellant has maintained a position of not being able to comply with such requests as it\n\ndoes not have such access. It is only GIL or Google Inc. USA which can comply with\n\nsuch requests. Therefore, the averment of the revenue is completely unsupported and\n\nfactually incorrect.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_215\">75.   The ld. Counsel for the assessee has also placed reliance upon the commentaries\n\nof The Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) and UN\n\nmodel as a guide for interpretation of treaty with the submission that this OECD\n\ncommentary has been relied upon on by the Hon&#x27;ble Supreme Court and the various\n\nHigh Courts while deciding a number of cases. The OECD has set up a Technical\n\nAdvisory Group (TAG) to examine the issue arising in characterization of e-commerce\n\npayment. TAG has categorizede-commerce transaction into 28 types including internet\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_107\">                                       Page 84 of 331</span>\n\nadvertising. TAG has concluded that payment arising from the advertisement would\n\nconstitute business profits falling under <a href=\"/doc/735354/\" id=\"a_197\">Article 7</a> rather than royalties. He also further\n\nplaced reliance upon the High Power Committee report on &#x27;Electronics, Commerce and\n\nTaxation&#x27; constituted by CBDT vide order dated 16.12.1999, wherein and the\n\nCommittee has also held that payment arising from the advertisement would constitute\n\nprofits and gains from the business or profession.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_216\">76.   It was further contended that prior to introduction of equalization levy, the\n\namounts receivable by non-residents was considered to be business income and not\n\ntaxable in India in the absence of Permanent Establishment in India. Considering the\n\ngrowth of the online advertisement industry and in line with OECD&#x27;s Action Plan-1 of\n\nthe base erosion and proper shifting, the Govt. introduced equalization levy to tax the\n\nreceipt which was not subject to tax in India in the absence of Permanent\n\nEstablishment. The Memorandum of Finance Bill, 2016 proposed to insert a new\n\nchapter titled &#x27;Equalisation Levy&#x27; in the Finance Bill. 2016 to provide for equalization\n\nlevy of 6% of the amount of consideration for specified services received or receivable\n\nby the non-resident not having Permanent Establishment in India from a resident in India\n\nwho carries out business or profession or form non-resident having Permanent\n\nEstablishment in India.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_217\">                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_108\">                                       Page 85 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_218\">77.   The ld. Counsel for the assessee further contended that grant of distribution right\n\nin Adword program does not involve transfer of right in copyright. The appellant is a\n\nmere non-exclusive distributor or reseller of ad space through the Adword programe in\n\nIndia. The appellant merely purchases the advertisement space under the Adword\n\nprogram from GIL and distribute the same to advertisers in India. He quoted an example\n\nby saying that for advertisements in other mediums like newspaper magazine, the\n\ncustomer approaches an advertisement agency to have the advertisement public in one\n\nor more newspaper. The advertisement agency in turn approaches the respective\n\nnewspaper entity or an entity that has bought media space for publishing of the\n\nadvertisement of their customers.       The consideration paid by the customer to\n\nadvertisement agencies is in the nature of advertisement fees and the consideration paid\n\nby the advertisement agencies to the respective newspaper entity is also in the nature of\n\nadvertisement fees, inspite of the fact that the advertisement may be displayed by\n\nconsiderable use of technology.      Similarly, in the instant case, the consideration\n\nreceived by the appellant from the advertisers in India is in the nature of advertisement\n\nfees and consideration paid by the appellant to Google Ireland for purchase of\n\nadvertisement space is also in the nature of advertisement fees, even though the\n\nadvertisement would be displayed as a consequence of use of technology.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_219\">                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_109\">                                        Page 86 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_220\">78.    It was further contended that Adword program is not a computer program, but a\n\ntitle givento the facility/services provided by Google to the advertisers to advertise their\n\nproducts/services by displaying links/contents on the Google search engine. In fact\n\nAdword is a trademark registered by Google Inc. and not a copyright patent. The AO\n\nhas placed reliance on the description of Adword program in Wikipedia and other articles\n\nappearing in worldwide web to hold Adword is complex computer program. The\n\nHon&#x27;ble Supreme Court in various cases has observed that the information on Wikipedia\n\ncan be entered by any person, as such it may not be authority and accordingly should not\n\nbe relied upon for judicial decisions. Therefore the information available on Wikipedia\n\nshould not be taken into account. The present non-exclusive right to distribute the ad\n\nspace is a commercial space and not an intellectual property right, therefore the\n\nprovisions of IT Enabled Services by the appellant is independent of the\n\ndistribution/reseller of advertisement space under the Adword program by the appellant\n\nto advertisers in India. ITES division of the appellant is a separate outsourcing business\n\nsegment for which it earns revenue under a separate outsourcing service agreement with\n\nGIL.   The appellant was providing ITES services prior to the commencement of the\n\ndistribution or resale activities.   Further the amount receivable under the service\n\nagreement is not set off against the amount payable by the company under the\n\ndistribution/resale agreement. Role of distribution/reseller function is limited to\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_110\">                                       Page 87 of 331</span>\n\ndistribution/resale activities, whereas ITES performed functions inter alia are to ensure\n\nthe advertisement placed by advertiser globally in conformity with the Google editorial\n\nguidelines/local Government regulations of the country, from where ad is to be\n\ndisplayed. These ad review services are not provided to the advertisers since the\n\nresponsibility is of the appellantthat ads do not violate Government regulation in the web\n\nplatform operator i.e., Google Ireland. There is no professional interaction between the\n\ndistribution team and the ITES team since these are separate functions performed\n\nindependent of each other. In fact they operate from separate location. ITES division\n\noperates out of units situated at Hyderabad and Gurgaon, whilstthe distribution division\n\noperates out of Mumbai and Gurgaon. It was further contended that even under ITES\n\nagreement, only limited rights to use intellectual property of Google Ireland is granted\n\nto the appellant i.e., to carry out only specific services under the said agreement. Under\n\nthe ITES agreement, there are no rights that are granted to Google India for commercial\n\nexploitation of such intellectual property. Under the ITES segment, providing such\n\nlimited right to use intellectual property to Google Ireland is entered in April 1, 2004\n\nwhich is much before the agreement for Adword program on 12.12.2005. The ld.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_221\">Counsel for the assessee has further quoted example of Indian distributor of luxury car.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_222\">                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_111\">                                       Page 88 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_223\">79.   With regard to the judgments referred to by the revenue, the ld. Counsel for the\n\nassessee has contended that all the judgments are distinguishable on facts, therefore they\n\ncannot be relied on.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_224\">80.   The ld. Counsel for the assessee further contended that since the lower authorities\n\nhave not examined the issue in the light of various judicial pronouncements, the order of\n\nthe CIT(Appeals) deserves to be set aside.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_225\">81.   Having carefully examined the orders of lower authorities in the light of rival\n\nsubmissions and the material available on record, we find that in these appeals, the sole\n\ncontroversy revolves around an issue as to what is the nature of payment made by the\n\nappellant to Google Ireland Ltd. under the Google Adworddistribution/reseller\n\nagreement. The AO has examined the distribution/ reseller agreement along with the\n\nservice agreement executed between the appellant and GIL in order to arrive at a\n\nconclusion with regard to the nature of payment made by the appellant to GIL. Therefore\n\nit has become necessary for us to adjudicate as to whether the distribution/ reseller\n\nagreement and service agreements are two independent and separable agreements or they\n\nare interconnected and interdependent agreements, without which the obligation under\n\nthe distributor agreement cannot be discharged. Therefore we have to examine both the\n\nagreements minutely.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_226\">                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_112\">                                      Page 89 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_227\">82.   Undisputedly the service agreement was executed between the appellant and GIL\n\non 01.04.2004 under which the appellant was required to render ITES services for GIL.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_228\">Under the service agreement, GIL desired to avail services relating to information\n\ntechnology, information technology enabled services and software development related\n\nservices from the appellant as per terms &amp; conditions of this agreement. Under the head\n\n&#x27;Services&#x27;, the appellant agreed to perform certain information technology enabled\n\nservices and software development services as requested by GIL from time to time with\n\nrespect to products, utilizing GIL software technology and other appropriate technology\n\nfrom GIL or third parties which may be properly used for this purposes.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_229\">83.   Under the head &#x27;Ownership&#x27;, the intellectual property shall be owned by GIL and\n\nGoogle India acknowledges and agrees that all such confidential information,\n\nintellectual property, software technology and documentation shall remain the exclusive\n\nproperty of Google Ireland. Similar is the position with regard to derivative works and\n\nsoftware embodiment. OWNERSHIP.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_243\">5.1 Intellectual Property.
