The amended and restated Google Adword program distribution agreement was\n\nexecuted on 12.12.2005 between the appellant and Google Ireland through which the\n\nappellant was appointed as a Adword Program distributor. This distributor agreement\n\nwas further renewed under different title of agreement i.e., Google Reseller agreement\n\non 01.07.2012. Though nomenclature of agreement is changed, but the functions and\n\nobligations of the appellant almost remain the same. In distributor agreement the\n\nappellant was called as a &#x27;distributor&#x27;, and in reseller agreement the appellant was called\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_119\">                                       Page 96 of 331</span>\n\na &#x27;reseller&#x27;. Besides change of nomenclature, the obligation upon the appellant remained\n\nthe same. Therefore we discuss the clauses of distribution agreement in order to\n\nunderstand the Google Adword program and duties and obligations cast upon the\n\nappellant in discharge of its functions. In this distribution agreement, a reference was\n\nmade that the GIL and distributor desire to terminate the prior agreement and further\n\ndesired that this agreement supersedes and replace the prior agreement in its entirety.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_258\">But before us, a copy of the prior agreement which was superseded by this agreement\n\nhas not been filed. But this fact is clear that before this agreement, there was some other\n\nagreement under which the appellant was performing certain functions. Through this\n\nagreement, the appellant was appointed as non-exclusive authorized distributor of\n\nGoogle Adword program to advertisers in accordance with the terms &amp; conditions set\n\nforth in this agreement. It has been clarified that distributor shall conduct its business\n\nfor its own account, in its own name, and not as an agent, employee, partner or franchisee\n\nof Google i.e., Google Ireland. It was also made clear that distributor may not solicit\n\nbusiness from and shall not distribute Adword program to (even if unsolicited) any entity\n\nthat does not have a principal place of business within the territory, meaning thereby the\n\nappellant was appointed as distributor of Google Adword program to conduct its\n\nbusiness relating to the said Google Adword program within the territory of India within\n\nthe broad guidelines provided by Google with its reasonable commercial expertise, sale\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_120\">                                       Page 97 of 331</span>\n\nforce and customer service infrastructure. We have also examined the statement of Shri Arijit Sarker, Director, India\n\nOperation, Head of Information Technology Enabled Services, GIPL, and he explains\n\nthe functions of outsource ad reviews by stating that advertisers upload the ads which\n\nresides in data servers outside of India and these ads are reviewed in accordance with\n\nGoogle policies. 94% of these ads are automatically reviewed by the system, 6% of ad\n\ncomes from manual review which is done through proprietary web application (approval\n\nbin) by the ad review team. Here the dispute is raised with regard to the 6% of ads which\n\ncomes for the manual review to the appellant.           The ad review team consists of 100\n\nGoogle employees and 200 vendor employees, they are graduates from various colleges\n\nin India.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_311\">94. This division\nof GIPL is involved in performing back office functions as described below:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_166\">          \uf0b7   Response to the queries of client&#x27;s customers: The queries generated by the\n              customers of Google entities flow from customers through a workflow based\n              on pre-determined parameters. On receiving the query, the team responds to\n              the query. The response to is routed through Google Ireland; and\n\n          \uf0b7   Reviewing advertisements for the purpose of running the same on the Google\n              website: These services mainly involve IT enabled data processing work. The\n              data comes from the customer and is routed to GIPL through a workflow\n              based on language and tier. On receiving the data, the team reviews the\n              advertisement to ensure that the advertisement conforms with the Google\n              editorial guidelines. If the advertisement does conform, the customer&#x27;s\n              advertisement can then be viewed on the websites of Google or its partners\n              located outside India.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_167\">          \uf0b7   GIPL reviews the advertisement on accordance with the company law and\n              the local law regulation, through various software. These are automated\n              software which provides assistance by filtering the content of the ads with\n              specified keywords and helps GIPL in reviewing the content of the ads.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_168\">Further, we wish to submit that the provision of IT enabled services is independent of the\ndistribution of the AdWords program to the advertisers in India, since the IT enabled\nservices rendered by GIPL include the following:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_169\">          \uf0b7   Review of the search results to ensure the outcome of the desired results or\n              to improve upon the quality of search results;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_170\">          \uf0b7   Evaluation of Google services and tools for efficiency, bugs, performance,\n              failure, etc.;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_171\">          \uf0b7   Data processing work i.e. once the advertiser registers for the AdWords\n              through an online process, the advertisement is sent to the IT enabled\n              services team to review for conformation with the editorial guidelines. These\n              services are primarily carried out for AdWords services of other group\n              entities outside India and not only for the advertisement from India.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_172\">Thus, the IT enabled services rendered by GIPL are to ensure that the advertisements\nconform with the editorial guidelines and is not linked to the distribution services.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_173\">                                              IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                               IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                             IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_140\">                                         Page 117 of 331</span>\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_315\">3. On receiving the data, the Adword team reviews the advertisement\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_143\">                                       Page 120 of 331</span>\n\naccording to company policy and thereafter advertisement can be viewed on websites\n\nof Google or its partners located outside India. The complex functioning of the online advertisement was examined by the\n\nTribunal in the case of <a href=\"/doc/35917315/\" id=\"a_211\">ITO v. Right Florists Pvt. Ltd.</a> (supra) and for the sake of\n\nreference, we extract the relevant portion as under:-</span>\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_181\">      <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"586984\" id=\"span_145\">&quot;5. To adjudicate on the issue in appeal, we will have to first understand the\n      nature of services rendered by the Google and Yahoo. Both these companies\n      own popular search engine websites, namely Google and Yahoo, and the\n      advertising is done in the results generated by the search results against\n      agreed keywords or by placing the advertising banners on websites. Yahoo\n      and Google, as internet search engines, have brought about an information\n      revolution and the entire conventional business model of businesses\n      reaching out to the potential customers has undergone paradigm shift.\n      Nicholas Negroponte, in his classic book &#x27;Being Digital&#x27; published in\n      January 1995, had said that &quot;Information about information is more\n      important than information&quot; and illustrated this with an example, which\n                                     IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                      IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                           IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                    IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_146\">                                Page 121 of 331</span>\n\nlooks too simplistic today, that &quot;the fact that TV Guide makes more money\nthan all the four major networks combined&quot;. It was around this time that the\nsearch engines &quot;Jerry and David&#x27;s Guide to the World Wide Web&quot; and\n&quot;backrub&quot;, former avatars of Yahoo and Google respectively, made the\nbusiness of information about information, which is what these search\nengines are really involved in- with sophisticated technology and process of\ngreat value proposition, a reality. The services offered by Yahoo and Google\nare of the same nature, but, for the sake of convenience, we will take up\n<a href=\"/doc/586984/\" id=\"a_212\">Google</a>&#x27;s case as an illustration for the nature of services. Google is a web\nsearch engine. The way it works is this. A web search engine is basically\nsoftware code that is designed to search for information on the World Wide\nWeb. When an internet user needs information about, inter alia, a product\nor service, he visits the website of the search engine and keys in the search\nwords. The software and database embedded in the search engine than\nproduces a large number of results, such as addresses of the related websites\nso that internet user can then visit those website for further information on\nthat product or service. This query need not be about a service or product\nonly, it may relate to virtually anything under the sun. These search engine\nresults are sometimes in thousands, are generally presented in a web pages,\nimages, information and other types of files. While producing these results,\nthere are sponsored search results also, which is de facto advertising, and\nthese sponsored search results help those advertisers visibility of their\nrespective websites. Unlike web directories, which are maintained only by\nhuman editors, search engines also maintain realtime information by\nrunning an algorithm on a web crawler, and, therefore, the advertising\nservices offered by these search engines are practically without any human\ntouch and entirely automated. When we use these search engines, the search\nresults are produced which are termed as &#x27;search engine result pages&#x27;, and,\nas we have seen a little while ago, these SERPs also include sponsored\nsearch results or online advertising. The online advertising on SERPs is a\npart of the business of search engines like Google. To an advertiser, it is\ngenerally a three step process - advertiser creates advertisement and\nchooses keywords, which are words or phrases relating to his business.\nWhen people search on Google using one of the assigned keywords, the\nadvertisement may appear next to the search results. The advertisements are\nthen before an audience which is already interested in the related product or\nservice. People can simply click on these advertisements and they will reach\nthe related website so as to make a purchase or learn more about the product\n                                      IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                       IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                     IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_147\">                                 Page 122 of 331</span>\n\nor service. The functioning of this form of online advertising can indeed be\nexplained in such simple terms, but actual rendering of this service is a\nhighly technical process. To search engines, providing this online\nadvertising service, is a complex technical activity and rendering of a highly\ntechnical service which works with the use of software code that is designed\nto search for information on the World Wide Web, and generate, alongwith\nthe search results, sponsored search results - the service which is paid for\nby the sponsors of those results, like this assessee before us. On one hand, a\nweb search engine like Google, as we have noted earlier also, maintains\nreal-time information by running an algorithm on a web crawler, and, on the\nother hand, produces, in the search results to the internet users,\nadvertisement sponsored. It is primarily for this service that the Google is\npaid for. An online advertising could also be by way of a web banner or\nbanner advertisement is a form of advertising on the World Wide Web\ndelivered by an adserver, an expression which refers to technology and\nservice that places advertisements on web sites. The content of the adserver\nis constantly updated so that the website or webpage on which the ads are\ndisplayed contains new advertisements, when the site or page is visited or\nrefreshed by a user. The purpose of ad serving is to deliver targeted ads that\nmatch the website visitor&#x27;s interest. This form of online advertising entails\nembedding an advertisement into a web page. It is intended to attract traffic\nto a website by linking to the website of the advertiser. An ad server, which\nis at the core of this form of online advertising, is a computer server,\nspecifically a web server, that stores advertisements used in online\nmarketing and delivers them to website visitors. Whichever be the form of\nadvertising service, whether by sponsored results or by web banner or by\nany other similar mode, there can hardly be any dispute on the proposition\nthat these search engines or ad servers, which are patented and provide\nvaluable services, which are essentially technical in nature. It is this kind of\nadvertising service for which the payments were made by the assessee. Let\nus, for example, take the case of a florist in Shillong who intends to use these\nservices. His potential customer is a person looking for a florist in Shillong.\nWhen such a person actually uses the search words &#x27;florist&#x27; and &#x27;Shillong&#x27;\nin Google, the search engine result page could be like this:</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_182\">                                          IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                           IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                         IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_148\">                                     Page 123 of 331</span>\n\n\n\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_183\">99.   The above search engine result page shows several sponsored results, termed and\nmarked as &quot;ads&quot; and it is for these sponsored results being shown on the search engine\nresult page that the Google charges a fees. The mechanism for this online advertising\ncould be further appreciated by the following screenshots:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_184\">                                         IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                          IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                               IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                        IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_149\">                                    Page 124 of 331</span>\n\n\n\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_185\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"586984\" id=\"span_150\">100. But no specific reply was furnished by the learned Counsel for the assessee.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_187\">It was simply contended that the service agreement was executed prior to the Adword\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_152\">                                       Page 126 of 331</span>\n\nDistribution Agreement. Now in the light of material available on the record in the form of literature,\n\nsubmissions and orders of the authorities below we will try to understand as to how the\n\nAdword program works. The Google AdWord Program is a complex program and has\n\nvarious benefits viz., a) relevant ads of people b) target of select audience c) it causes\n\nminimum advertisisng expenses d) it is only payable when people are engaged. It gives\n\nadvertiser the access to the tools of the AdWord Program which can be accessed through\n\nthe gateway of Google India/Appellant through the use of patent technology. AdWord\n\nProgram gives the advertiser to choose the preferred time, season of the year when the\n\nads are to be shown. Once advertiser accepts the terms, the appellant gives access to the\n\nvarious tools of AdWords Program. The time zone and display time of the advertisement\n\nis identified and allocated by the appellant to the advertiser with the help of Google\n\nAdWord Program. AdWord Program is more focused and targeted in advertisement\n\ncampaign which results into more attention, engagement, delivery and conversion which\n\nis only possible on the Google network with the access of tools of search engine and\n\ngoogle analytics. Appellant is having the access to IP address at desktop or laptop or IP\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_153\">                                      Page 127 of 331</span>\n\naddress of the tablet, photographs, time spent on website, eating habits, wearing\n\npreferences. With the help of IP address, Google search engine is having access to\n\nvarious information and data pertaining to the user of the website in the form of name,\n\nsex, city, state, country, phone number, religion, etc.           Besides the above basic\n\ninformation, Google is also having the access of the history of the user as well as the\n\nbehaviour of the person searching in google engine. Based on various inputs mentioned\n\nabove and contents of more than 2 million website, the Appellant was able to provide\n\nthe effective focus ad campaign to the advertiser. The AdWord Program and tools\n\ntherein gives the advertiser to pick up the keywords, phrases which are similar in nature\n\nand germane, and in a digitalised and tabulated form and grouped together. The\n\nadvertiser is having the access to this Google Analytics Program through the appellant.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_189\">Whenever one particular keyword is searched, the targeted ad will be shown ot the\n\nconsumer and by clicking on the ad, the consumer will be landed on a webpage. The\n\nselection and display of the keyword, play a pivotal role in the advertising campaign and\n\nfor this purpose appellant has provided the optimization and technique to the advertiser.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_190\">The appellant being a service provider under the agreement uses its expertise and the\n\ninformation within its domain and control, to suggest the keywords based on recent\n\nmarketing material and need of the advertiser. The appellant also suggests a periodical\n\nreview of the website homepage, product and services which can be bundled together.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_191\">                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_154\">                                       Page 128 of 331</span>\n\nIt also suggests the traffic forecast of the list of keywords, multiple keywords placed to\n\nget the new keyword ideas. Based on the initial keywords the advertiser enters, the tools\n\nshows various keywords suggestion automatically grouped into different adgroups. This\n\nis only possible if the Appellant is permitted to use information, data and keyplanner to\n\nthe advertiser which is patent and protected software of the Google. The keyword\n\nplanner also suggests the suitability of the keywords which are useful in the particular\n\nmonth of the year. The advertiser is able to plan its campaign for optimization or for the\n\npurpose of getting more information and conversion based on keyword planners. Based\n\non this information and forecast, the advertiser is able to build on the keywords.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_192\">103. The display of the advertisement based on the keywords, is dependent upon the\n\nauction price paid by the advertiser. The keywords bid at the highest rate by the\n\nadvertiser would be shown at the top of search result and therefore, is likely to fetch\n\nmore visibility and attention. With the help of tools of Google, the advertiser as well as\n\nthe appellant have an access to the impact of change of keywords on the likely\n\nimpressions of the advertisement.      With the help of keywords matching, various\n\napproaches are being adopted by the Google AdWord Program i.e., broad match, phrase\n\nmatch and exact match. The exact match for example allows the advertiser to focus on\n\nthe optimization phrase on the individual keywords and it yields the best results possible.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_193\">                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_155\">                                      Page 129 of 331</span>\n\nWhereas, the phrase match is more processing than broad match and the broad match\n\nprovides the greatest possibility of coverage of the advertisement.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_194\">104. The appellant facilitates the advertisers to start the campaign of advertising\n\ninitially with the help of broad match thereafter with the phrase match and thereafter\n\nwith the exact match. With the help of keyword management, the Google AdWord\n\nProgram takes care of misspelling, singular, plural, abbreviation, achronynms,\n\nstemming, etc. For example, if the advertisement shows the formal shoes, then the\n\nkeywords are the formal plus shoes. If it is broad match keywords then the advertisement\n\nwill show formal shoes, sports shoes, black shoes, party shoes, etc. However, if the\n\nadvertiser had only opted for exact word match, then search result will show only a\n\nformal shoe.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_195\">105. The appellant helps the advertiser with the help of tools of AdWord Program to\n\ninclude or delete various variation of keywords in the realm of advertisement compaign\n\nand similarly the advertiser may with the help of Google tool can avoid the unnecessary\n\ntraffic on its website. For example, if an advertiser does not want the visit of a surfer\n\nwho is searching the service apartment on rent basis and only wants the person surfing\n\nto buy the apartment, then the Appellant can help him by putting negative words of rent\n\nin the keyword search. Therefore, the only person who is searching for service apartment\n                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_156\">                                        Page 130 of 331</span>\n\nwould be lending on the advertisers website and the person who is searching for rented\n\nservice apartment, would not be visiting the website of the advertiser i.e., advertisement\n\nwould not be displayed to him in the searched result. Likewise, if the advertiser is selling\n\nleather cover for iPhone, then the advertiser may not like that the person who is looking\n\nfor leather cover for another brand or item may visit the website of the advertiser. For\n\nthat, negative words can be used to avoid to improve the CTR (clicked through rate) with\n\nthe help of tools of Google. By using these tools, the Appellant has been giving various\n\nsuggestions to the advertisers to improve various keywords.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_196\">106. The Google AdWord Program is also having Google Analytics which is connected\n\nwith the Google AdWord Program and which is a potential patented tool to target the\n\nkeywords and the negative keywords. This is the USP of the Google AdWord Program,\n\nwhich is maintaining thousands of different keywords used by people to search the\n\nwebsite and based on this user behaviour, the Google Analytics suggests the appropriate\n\nkeywords to be used by the advertiser for encouraging the traffic on the website.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_197\">Similarly, the Google Analytics also uses the same data to filter out the negative\n\nkeywords on the basis of which an unattended or unwarranted person have landed on the\n\nwebsite of the advertiser. The Appellant is using all these tools in conjucture with the\n\nadvertisers at the time of granting the backhand services to advertisers, as the Appellant\n\nis having access to all these data, information, etc.\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_157\">                                       Page 131 of 331</span>\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_198\">107. On the basis of the above, we are of the view that the agreement between the\n\nassessee and GIL is not only in the nature of providing the space for advertisement and\n\ndisplay the advertisement to the consumers. If we look into the advertisement module\n\nof the AdWord Program, we will come to an irrestitable conclusion that it is not merely\n\nan agreement to provide advertisement space but it is an agreement for facilitating the\n\ndisplay and publishing of an advertisement to the targeted customers.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_199\">108. We have also examined the obligations cast upon the appellant under the\n\nagreements and found the obligation cast upon the appellant under the Google Adword\n\ndistribution agreement can only be discharged with the help of the ITES\n\ndivision. Therefore, the Google Adword distributor agreement and the service\n\nagreement are to be read together as they are interconnected with the navel cord and\n\nwithout resorting to the service agreement the terms and conditions under the Google\n\nAdWord Distribution Agreement cannot be complied with. Therefore, in order to\n\nunderstand the function of Google Adword program, we have to read both the\n\nagreements together.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_200\">109. Now the question comes as to whether the payment made by the appellant to\n\nGoogle Ireland is in the nature of payment of royalty or a business profit in the hands of\n\nGoogle Ireland. In this regard, our attention was invited to the fact that Google Ireland\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_158\">                                       Page 132 of 331</span>\n\nis a non-resident and does not have any Permanent Establishment in India, therefore the\n\npayment made to Google Ireland is in the form of business profit and not taxable in\n\nIndia. But the revenue has treated the payment made by the appellant to Google Ireland\n\nafter retaining a particular percentage of advertisement receipts as a payment of royalty\n\nand is chargeable to tax u/.s 9(1)(vi) of the Act. Since the appellant has not deducted tax\n\nat source on such payments<a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_214\"> u/s. 195</a> of the Act, the AO had treated the assessee to be in\n\ndefault and raised the demand under <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_215\">section 201</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_216\">201A</a> of the Act.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_201\">110. The AO has also held that the GIL is not the beneficial owner of the sum\n\npaid/payable by GIPL and therefore the rate of tax as provided under India-Ireland\n\nDTAA @ 10% cannot be applied. He further held that GIL which is claimed to be the\n\nbeneficial owner of the sum paid/payable by GIPL is controlled and managed from\n\nBermuda with whom India has not entered into DTAA. Therefore, rate of tax on royalty\n\npaid/payable by GIPL under the Act will have to applied. Accordingly, royalty was\n\ntaxed @ 10.556% under <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_217\">section 115A</a> of the Act.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_202\">111. During the course of hearing, the learned Counsel for the assessee has invited our\n\nattention to various judicial pronouncements with the submissions that similar type of\n\npayments made by the advertisers to the non-residents, who owns the search engine was\n\nconsidered to be the business profit of the non-resident and in the absence of their\n                                              IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                               IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                             IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_159\">                                         Page 133 of 331</span>\n\npermanent establishment in India, the business profit was not liable to be taxed in India.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_203\">Therefore, we prefer to examine the judgments referred to by the assessee in support of\n\nhis contention as to whether the judgments apply to the present facts of the case or not.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_204\">Heavy reliance was placed upon the order of the Tribunal in the case of ITO Vs. Right\n\nFlorist (supra) in which the Tribunal has held that the payments made by the advertiser\n\nto Google and Yahoo is a business profit in the hands of the recipient and in the absence\n\nof the permanent establishment of the non-resident, the business income cannot be taxed\n\nin India. In that case, the facts culled out by the Tribunal are that the assessee is a florist\n\nwho uses such advertising with search engine through Google and Yahoo to generate\n\nbusiness. Whenever, anyone does a web search on a respective search engine in looking\n\nfor a particular website and uses certain keywords, the advertisement of the assessee is\n\nshown along with the search results. The assessee has made certain payments in respect\n\nof online advertising to US based entities viz., Google Ireland Ltd., and overture service\n\nInc., USA (Yahoo USA). No taxes were withheld from these payments and during the\n\ncourse of assessment proceedings, the AO required the assessee to show cause as to why\n\nthese payments are not to be disallowed as deduction in computation of its income under\n\n<a href=\"/doc/686617/\" id=\"a_218\">section 40(a)(ia)</a> of the Act. The stand of the assessee was that these payments are made\n\nto foreign entities who did not have any permanent establishment in India. Therefore,\n\nthe same were not taxable in India. The AO rejected the claim of the assessee. The\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_160\">                                      Page 134 of 331</span>\n\nassessee&#x27;s contention that even under <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_219\">section 9(1)</a> of the Income Tax Act, only so much\n\nof the income of the non-resident can be brought to tax as reasonably attributable to the\n\noperations carried out in India were rejected by the AO and he observed that irrespective\n\nof whether or not, in assessee&#x27;s opinion, the income was taxable in India, the assessee\n\nought to have approached the AO under section 195 prior to making the foreign\n\nremittance. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1594163\" id=\"span_161\">Assessee&#x27;s failure to do so, according to the AO, was contrary to the law\n\nlaid down by the Hon&#x27;ble Supreme Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/1594163/\" id=\"a_220\">Transmission Corporation of AP\n\nLtd., Vs. CIT</a> 239 ITR 587. With these observations, AO has disallowed the payment\n\nmade by the assessee under <a href=\"/doc/686617/\" id=\"a_221\">section 40(a)(ia)</a> of the Act. Assessee carried the matter\n\nbefore the CIT(A) but did not find favour with him and finally the matter travelled to the\n\nTribunal. The Tribunal, having examined the nature of the advertisement programmes\n\nand the services rendered by the non-resident, was of the view that receipt in respect of\n\nonline advertisement on Google and Yahoo cannot be brought to tax in India under the\n\nprovisions of the <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_222\">Income Tax Act</a> and also in the absence of their permanent\n\nestablishment in India, under the provisions of India-USA and India-Ireland tax treaty.</span>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_205\">The relevant observations of the Tribunal are as under:\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_206\">&quot;28. In view of the above discussions, we are of the considered view, on the limited\nfacts of the case as produced before us\u201e the receipts in respect of online advertising on\nGoogle and Yahoo cannot be brought to tax in India under the provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_223\">Income\nTax Act</a>, as also under the provisions of India US and India Ireland tax treaty. This\n                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_162\">                                        Page 135 of 331</span>\n\nobservation is subject to the rider that so far as the PE issue is concerned, we have\nexamined the existence of PE only on the basis of website simplicitor, and on no other\nadditional basis, as no case was made out for the same. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"19107927\" id=\"span_163\">In any case, revenue has not\nbrought anything on record, either at assessment stage or even before us, to suggest that\nGoogle or Yahoo had a PE in India, and as held by a Special Bench of this Tribunal in\nthe case of <a href=\"/doc/19107927/\" id=\"a_224\">Motorola Inc Vs DCIT</a> (95 ITD 269 SB), &quot;DTAA is only an alternate tax\nregime and not an exemption regime&quot; and, therefore, &quot;the burden is first on the Revenue\nto show that the assessee has a taxable income under the DTAA, and then the burden is\non the assessee to show that that its income is exempt under DTAA&quot;. No such burden is\ndischarged by the Revenue. Accordingly, there is no material before us to come to the\nconclusion that Google or Yahoo had a PE in India, which, in turn, could constitute the\nbasis of their taxability in India.</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_207\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"30875803\" id=\"span_164\">29. Now that we have come to the conclusion that Google and Yahoo did not have any\ntax liability in India, in respect of the advertising revenues in question, the next question\nthat we need to deal is whether or not assessee still had a tax witholding obligation in\nrespect remittances for these online advertising payments, and whether assesse&#x27;s failure\nto withhold taxes even when the recipient did not have tax liability could be visited with\ndisallowance under <a href=\"/doc/686617/\" id=\"a_225\">section 40(a)(i)</a>. It is only elementary that when recipient of an\nincome does not have the primary tax liability in respect of an income, the payer cannot\nhave vicarious tax withholding liability either. This position is independent of the payer\nhaving moved an application under section 195 or not, or on the payer or the payee\nhaving obtained an advance ruling in their favour or not. The law is now very well settled\nin this regard by Hon&#x27;ble Supreme Court&#x27;s judgment in the case of <a href=\"/doc/30875803/\" id=\"a_226\">GE India Technology\nCentre Pvt Ltd Vs CIT</a> (327 ITR 456) wherein Their Lordships have categorically held\nthat, &quot;where a person responsible for deduction is fairly certain, then he can make his\nown determination as to whether the tax was deductible at source and, if so, what should\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_165\">                                      Page 136 of 331</span>\n\nbe the amount thereof&quot;.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1594163\" id=\"span_166\">In the said case, Their Lordships have, rejecting revenue&#x27;s\nreliance on Hon&#x27;ble Supreme Court&#x27;s judgment in <a href=\"/doc/1594163/\" id=\"a_227\">Transmission</a>&#x27;s case (supra) - which\nhas also been referred to by the Assessing Officer, observed as follows:</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_208\">     <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1594163\" id=\"span_167\">......<a href=\"/doc/1594163/\" id=\"a_228\">.In Transmission Corporation</a> case (supra) a non-resident had entered into\n     a composite contract with the resident party making the payments. The said\n     composite contract not only comprised supply of plant, machinery and\n     equipment in India, but also comprised the installation and commissioning of\n     the same in India. It was admitted that the erection and commissioning of plant\n     and machinery in India gave rise to income taxable in India. It was, therefore,\n     clear even to the payer that payments required to be made by him to the non-\n     resident included an element of income which was exigilble to tax in India. The\n     only issue raised <a href=\"/doc/1594163/\" id=\"a_229\">in that case</a> was whether TDS was applicable only to pure\n     income payments and not to composite payments which had an element of\n     income embedded or incorporated in them. The controversy before us in this\n     batch of cases is, therefore, quite different.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1594163\" id=\"span_168\">In <a href=\"/doc/1594163/\" id=\"a_230\">Transmission Corporation</a> case\n     (supra) it was held that TDS was liable to be deducted by the payer on the gross\n     amount if such payment included in it an amount which was exigible to tax in\n     India. It was held that if the payer wanted to deduct TDS not on the gross\n     amount but on the lesser amount, on the footing that only a portion of the\n     payment made represented &quot;income chargeable to tax in India&quot;, then it was\n     necessary for him to make an application under <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_231\">Section 195(2)</a> of the Act to the\n     ITO(TDS) and obtain his permission for deducting TDS at lesser amount. Thus,\n     it was held by this Court that if the payer had a doubt as to the amount to be\n     deducted as TDS he could approach the ITO(TDS) to compute the amount\n     which was liable to be deducted at source. In our view, Section 195(2) is based\n     on the &quot;principle of proportionality&quot;. The said sub-Section gets attracted only\n                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_169\">                                        Page 137 of 331</span>\n\n      in cases where the payment made is a composite payment in which a certain\n      proportion of payment has an element of &quot;income&quot; chargeable to tax in India.</span>\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_330\">      It is in this context that the Supreme Court stated, &quot;If no such application is\n      filed, income-tax on such sum is to be deducted and it is the statutory obligation\n      of the person responsible for paying such &#x27;sum&#x27; to deduct tax thereon before\n      making payment. He has to discharge the obligation to TDS&quot;. If one reads the\n      observation of the Supreme Court, the words &quot;such sum&quot; clearly indicate that\n      the observation refers to a case of composite payment where the payer has a\n      doubt regarding the inclusion of an amount in such payment which is exigible\n      to tax in India. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1594163\" id=\"span_170\">In our view, the above observations of this Court in\n      <a href=\"/doc/1594163/\" id=\"a_232\">Transmission Corporation</a> case (supra) which is put in italics has been\n      completely, with respect, misunderstood by the Karnataka High Court to mean\n      that it is not open for the payer to contend that if the amount paid by him to the\n      non-resident is not at all &quot;chargeable to tax in India&quot;, then no TDS is required\n      to be deducted from such payment. This interpretation of the High Court\n      completely loses sight of the plain words of Section 195(1) which in clear terms\n      lays down that tax at source is deductible only from &quot;sums chargeable&quot; under\n      the provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_233\">I.T. Act</a>, i.e., chargeable under <a href=\"/doc/339978/\" id=\"a_234\">Sections 4</a>, <a href=\"/doc/65305/\" id=\"a_235\">5</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1369261/\" id=\"a_236\">9</a> of the\n      I.T. Act.</span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_331\">30.    In view of the above discussions, as also bearing in mind entirety of the case, we\nare of the considered view that there was no failure in deduction of tax at source by the\nassessee before us inasmuch as the assessee did not have any obligation to deduct tax at\nsource under <a href=\"/doc/1329583/\" id=\"a_237\">section 195</a> for the simple reason that income embedded in impugned\npayments was not exigible to tax in India. Accordingly, the disallowance under <a href=\"/doc/420239/\" id=\"a_238\">section\n40(a)(i)</a> was uncalled for. Learned CIT(A) rightly deleted the impugned disallowance.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_332\">                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_171\">                                       Page 138 of 331</span>\n\nWe uphold the conclusions arrived at by the CIT(A) and decline to interfere in the\nmatter.&quot;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_333\">112. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"131633874\" id=\"span_172\">In this order, the Tribunal has placed a reliance upon the other order of the\nTribunal in the case of <a href=\"/doc/131633874/\" id=\"a_239\">Pinstorm Technologies Pvt. Ltd., Vs. ITO</a> (supra).</span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_334\">113. In the case of Pinstorm Technologies Pvt. Ltd., the facts culled out by the AO are\nthat the assessee company was engaged in the business of digital advertising and internet\nmarketing. It utilizes the internet search engine such as Google and Yahoo, etc., to buy\nspace in advertising on the internet on behalf of its client. The search engine carries out\nits own programme whereby the assessee books certain words called &quot;keywords&quot;.\nWhenever any person searches through the net of specific keywords, the advertisement\nof the assessee or its client is displayed. The price charged for such bookings depends\non type of its phrase, its popularity, usage, etc. The search engine renders the services\nworld wide basis and thus is not relatable to any specific country. The entire transaction\ntakes place through the internet and even the invoice is raised and payment is made\nthrough internet. During the year under consideration, the assessee company had made\na payment of Rs.1,09,35,108/- to Google Ireland Limited and the said payment was\nclaimed as advertisement expenses. While making the said payment, no tax at source\nwas deducted by the assessee on the ground that amount paid to Google Ireland Limited\nconstituted business profit of the said company and since the said company did not have\na permanent establishment (PE) in India, the amount payable was not taxable in India.\nAccording to AO, the services rendered by the Ireland company to the assessee was in\nthe nature of technical services and hence the assessee company was liable to deduct tax\nat source from the payment made against the said services. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"89798900\" id=\"span_173\">Since no such tax at source\nwas deducted by the assessee, the deduction claimed by the assessee on account of\n                                              IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                               IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                             IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_174\">                                         Page 139 of 331</span>\n\nexpenditure incurred on payment of advertisement charges to M/s. Google Ireland was\ndisallowed by the AO by invoking the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_240\">section 40(a)(ia)</a> of the Act. The\ndisallowance was challenged before the CIT(A) and the CIT(A), while confirming the\ndisallowance has held that payment made by the assessee to Google Ireland for the\nservices rendered was in the nature of royalty, chargeable to tax in India and assessee is\ntherefore liable to deduct tax at source from the said payment. The matter went to the\nTribunal and the Tribunal, relying upon the order of the Tribunal in the case of <a href=\"/doc/89798900/\" id=\"a_241\">Yahoo\nIndia Pvt. Ltd.</a>, (supra) has held that the amount payable by the assessee to Google\nIreland for the services rendered for uploading and display of banner of advertisement\non its portal was in the nature of business on which no tax was deductible at source since\nthe same was not chargeable in India in the absence of any permanent establishment\n(PE) of Google Ireland Ltd., in India.</span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_335\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"89798900\" id=\"span_175\">114. Since this case was adjudicated by the Tribunal in the light of order of the Tribunal\nin the case of <a href=\"/doc/89798900/\" id=\"a_242\">Yahoo India Pvt. Ltd.</a>, (supra) therefore, it has become necessary for us to\nexamine the facts in the case of Yahoo India Pvt. Ltd. In the case of Yahoo India Pvt.\nLtd., the facts culled by the Tribunal in its order are that the assessee company is fully\nowned subsidiary of Yahoo Inc., USA which is engaged in the business of providing\nconsumer services such as search engine content and information on wide spectrum of\ntopics, email, chat, etc. During the course of assessment proceedings, it was noticed by\nthe AO that assessee has made a payment of Rs.34,86,947 to Yahoo Holdings (Hong\nKong) Ltd., being cost of services/research material/ advertisement media. Yahoo\nHolding (Hong Kong) Ltd., is engaged in the business of providing internet services,\ntechnological tools, marketing solutions for business to customers in Hong Kong. It\nprovides banner advertisement and Microsoft hosting services on Yahoo Hong Kong\nportal. The banner advertisement is also known as web banner which is a form of\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_176\">                                       Page 140 of 331</span>\n\nadvertisement on the world wide web page. During the year under consideration, the\nDepartment of Tourism of India, through an advertisement agency media turf world wide\nintended to display a banner advertisement during the period from 18.02.2004 to\n15.03.2004 on the portal owned by Yahoo Holding (Hong Kong) Ltd. For this purpose,\nit hired the services of assessee company to approach Yahoo Holding (Hong Kong) Ltd.,\nto provide uploading and display services for hosting the banner advertisements at\nYahoo Hong Kong portal. Accordingly, the assessee company entered into the contract\nin the form of media insertion order for display of impressions with the Department of\nTourism. The total consideration for the same was agreed for Rs.65,11,500/- out of\nwhich assessee company agreed for granting agency discount to media turf of 25%. The\nassessee company in turn hired the services of Yahoo Holdings (Hong Kong) Ltd., for\nuploading and display of banner advertisement on its portal and the consideration for the\nsaid services was agreed at US$75,464, equivalent to Indian Rs.34,86,947/-. The said\npayment to M/s. Yahoo Holding (Hong Kong) Ltd., was made by the assessee during\nthe year under consideration without deducting tax at source. The stand of the assessee,\nas taken before the AO was that since the services/operation performed by the Yahoo\nHoldings (Hong Kong) Ltd., was entirely outside India and since Yahoo Holdings (Hong\nKong) Ltd., had no presence in India, the amount paid by them for the services rendered\noutside India was not taxable in India and no tax therefore was required to be deducted\nat source from the payment of the said amount. According to the AO, the income\nattributable to the services claimed to be rendered outside India has accrued in India as\nper provisions of <a href=\"/doc/1369261/\" id=\"a_243\">section 9</a> and the same is being taxable in India. Therefore, the assessee\nis required to deduct the tax at source before remitting the said amount to Yahoo\nHoldings (Hong Kong) Ltd. Since no tax was deducted by assessee company from the\npayment remitted to Yahoo Holdings (Hong Kong) Ltd., deduction claimed by the\n                                               IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                              IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_177\">                                       Page 141 of 331</span>\n\nassessee on account of the said payment was disallowed by the AO by invoking the\nprovisions of <a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_244\">section 40(a)(ia)</a> of the Act.</span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_336\">115. Aggrieved, the assessee preferred an appeal before the CIT(A) but did not find\nany favour with him and ultimately the matter reached the Tribunal and the Tribunal\nheld that since the banner advertisements hosting service did not involve use or right to\nuse by the assessee of any industrial, commercial or scientific equipment and no such\nuse was actually granted by Yahoo Holdings (Hong Kong) Ltd., limited to the assessee\ncompany and uploading and display of the banner advertisement on its portal was\nentirely the responsibility of the Yahoo Holdings (Hong Kong) Ltd., the assessee\ncompany was only required to provide the banner advertisement to Yahoo Holdings\n(Hong Kong) Ltd., for uploading the same on its portal. Thus the assessee had no right\nto access the portal of Yahoo Holdings and there is nothing to show any positive act of\nutilization or employment of the portal of the Yahoo Holdings by the assessee company.\nIn the light of these facts, the Tribunal came to the conclusion that payments made by\nthe assessee to Yahoo Holding (Hong Kong) Ltd., for the services rendered for uploading\nand display of banner advertisement to the Department of Tourism on its portal was not\nin the nature of royalty and the same was in the nature of business profit and in the\nabsence of any permanent establishment (PE) of Yahoo Holdings (Hong Kong) Ltd., in\nIndia, it was not chargeable to tax in India. Therefore, the assessee was not required to\ndeduct TDS from the payment made to Yahoo Holdings (Hong Kong) Ltd., for services\nrendered. Therefore, the payment so made cannot be disallowed by invoking the\nprovisions of <a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_245\">section 40(a)(ia)</a> for non-deduction of TDS.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_337\">116. In all these cases, the assessee was either an advertiser or act on behalf of some\nother advertiser and has purchased space from the owner of search engine to display its\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_178\">                                       Page 142 of 331</span>\n\nadvertisements online. Therefore, the payment made by the assessee to the owner of the\nsearch engine was considered to be business receipt / business profit in the hands of the\nowner of search engine who is non-resident and in the absence of permanent\nestablishment (PE) in India, the business profits / business receipts received by them\nwere not chargeable to tax in India. But in the instant case, appellant has not purchased\nthe advertisement space for putting its advertisement online from the GIL. The assessee\nhas been duly appointed a distributor under the Google Adword Distribution Agreement\nto distribute and sell the advertisement space obtained from the GIL under the\nDistribution Agreement.      Under the distribution agreement, appellant was under\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_209\">obligation to provide pre-sale and after sale services with the help of ITES division.\nWhile providing after sales services / technical services, the assessee had access to the\nintellectual property rights and tools and informatives, derivative works owned by the\nGIL. In the instant case, assessee is not a simpliciter buyer of AdWord Space for putting\nthe advertisement either for himself or for others which was the position in the aforesaid\ncases referred to by the assessee. Therefore, we are of the view that the facts of the case\nreferred to by the assessee i.e., Pinstorm Technologies Ltd., Right Florist and Yahoo\nIndia Ltd., are different than the facts of the instant case. Thus the <a href=\"/doc/89798900/\" id=\"a_246\">ratio <a href=\"/doc/89798900/\" id=\"a_247\">laid down in</a></a>\nthose cases would not be applicable to the present facts of the case.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_338\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"89798900\" id=\"span_179\">117. The learned Counsel for the assessee has also emphatically argued that the issue\ninvolved in these appeals is squarely covered by the aforesaid judgments in the case of\nRight Florist, Pinstorm Technologies Ltd., Yahoo India Ltd., Therefore, issue may be\ndecided following the <a href=\"/doc/89798900/\" id=\"a_248\">ratio <a href=\"/doc/89798900/\" id=\"a_249\">laid down in</a></a> the aforesaid cases. If the Tribunal takes a view\ncontrary to the view taken in the aforesaid cases, the matter may be referred to larger\nbench. In this regard, we have carefully perused the facts of the aforesaid cases and we\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_180\">                                       Page 143 of 331</span>\n\nfind the facts of those cases are different and therefore the ratio laid down therein cannot\nbe followed in the instant case. Hence we do not find any merit in the contention of the\nassessee for making a reference to larger bench if contrary view is required to be taken.\nWe therefore reject these contentions of the assessee.</span>\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_339\">118. A reference was also made upon the order of the Tribunal in the case of Taj TV\nLtd., (Supra), and on its perusal, we find in para 3, the Tribunal has recorded the facts of\nthe case according to which Taj TV Ltd., has appointed Taj India as distributor vide\nagreement dated 01.03.2002 to distribute an encrypted advertiser and/or subscription\nsupportive television programme services known as Ten Sports for cable systemsolely\nfor exhibitions to subscribers in India. As per the said agreement, the distribution\nrevenue collected by Taj India will be split in the ratio of 60:40 between Taj TV and Taj\nIndia. The amount payable by Taj India to Taj TV shall be net of Indian Income Tax.\nTaj TV is registered in Mauritius and also does not have any branch office in India.\nFurther telecasting is being done from outside India. It was contended in that case that\nTaj TV does not have permanent establishment in India and the transaction entered into\nby Taj TV are on principal to principal basis at arm&#x27;s length price. Taj India does not\nhave any authority to enter into any contract on behalf of Taj TV and all the\nadvertisements, sale contracts are entered into between the advertisers and the Taj TV.\nThe commission of 10% in respect of advertisement revenue is fair and consistent with\nthe industry practice. The Tribunal examined the Revenue&#x27;s contention for treating the\npayment to be royalty and came to the conclusion that the assessee only makes available\nthe content to the cable operator which are transmitted by them to the ultimate\ncustomer/viewer and under the Distribution Agreement, assessee has not been granted\nany licence to use any copyright to the distributor or to the cable operator. The rights\n                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_181\">                                        Page 144 of 331</span>\n\nover the content at all times lies with the assessee company and are never made available\nwith the distributor or cable operator. Therefore, impugned income will not constitute\nroyalty. The relevant observation of the Tribunal in this regard is extracted hereunder\nfor the sake of reference:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_210\"> &quot;So far as ground No.3 is concerned, that the distribution of income should\n be taxable as &#x27;royalty&#x27; under <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_250\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> up to 12th July, 2002, we are\n unable to concur with the divergent stand taken by the AO that for three\n months e payment will constitute &#x27;royalty&#x27; and for balance nine months, the payment\n will constitute &#x27;business income&#x27;. It has also been brought to our knowledge\n that in the subsequent years the AO has treated &#x27;distribution income&#x27; as\n business income and not as royalty. Thus, prior to period 12th July, 2002,\n also when assessee was not registered under the Laws of Mauritius then also it will\n not affect the nature of income. In any case, as stated earlier, under the distribution\n agreement, the assessee company has not granted any license to use any\n copyright to the distributor or to the cabreoperators. The assessee only\n makes available the content to the cable operators. operators which are transmitted\n by them to the ultimate customer/viewers. Further, rights over the content at all times\n lies with the Assessee Company and are never made available with the distributors\n or cable operators. Thus, the finding of the CIT(A) on this score is also confirmed\n that even for the first period 01.04.2002 to 12th July, 2002 the said income will not\n constitute `royalty&#x27;.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_340\">119. Whereas in the instant case, assesse was appointed as distributor under the\nAdWord Distributor Agreement with the condition that assesse would provide on sale\nand after sale service which required the involvement of technical know-how, IPRs,\nderivative workss, etc. In the case of Taj TV, the Taj TV has simply appointed the\ndistributor and no licence was given to the distributor to use the technology developed\nby Taj TV. The distributor was simply allowed to further transmit the programs to the\nviewers/customers. Therefore, facts of Taj TV Ltd., and the facts of the assesse are\nentirely different and the ratio laid down in this case cannot be applied to the facts of the\npresent case.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_341\">                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_182\">                                       Page 145 of 331</span>\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_342\">120. A reference was also made in the case of DDIT Vs. Set India Pvt. Ltd., (supra)\nand on its perusal, we find that a consideration was paid by the assesse to Set Satellite\nSingapore Pte. Ltd., for acquiring non-exclusive right to distribute set and set max\nchannels. The Revenue has treated it to be in the nature of royalty taxable at 15% on the\ngross basis under <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_251\">article 12</a> of India-Signapore DTAA whereas the assesse treated it to\nbe the business profit. The facts of the case are that Set Satellite Singapore Pte. Ltd., is\na company registered in Singapore and the said non-resident company operates\ntelevision channels viz., Set and Setmax in India. The said non-resident company also\nconverts ad air times for the said channels and also entered into agreement for\ndistribution of TV channels in India. As per the agreement, 70% distribution revenue is\nreceived by non-resident company and 30% of distribution revenue is taken by assesse\ncompany as consideration for the distribution of non-resident TV channels in India. The\nsaid non-resident company does not have any permanent establishment in India and as\nper <a href=\"/doc/1937835/\" id=\"a_252\">article 5</a> DTAA its business profit is not taxable in India. While adjudicating the\nissue, the Tribunal has come to the conclusion that non-resident company has granted\nnon-exclusive distribution agreement rights to the channels to the assessee or has not\ngiven any right to use or exploit any copyright. The assessee no way concerned whether\nprograms broadcast by the non-resident are copyrighted or not. The Tribunal further\nobserved that said distribution is purely a commercial right which is distinct from the\nright to use copyright. The Tribunal has placed the reliance upon the clause 6.3 of\ndistribution agreement entered between the assessee company and non-resident which\nstates as right granted to the assessee under the agreement is not at all and shall not be\nconstrued to grant of any licence or transfer of any right or any copyright. The Tribunal\nfurther observed that cable operator only re-transmits the TV signals transmitted to it by\nbroadcaster without any editing delays additions and therefore payment made by the\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_183\">                                       Page 146 of 331</span>\n\nassessee to the non-resident company is not for use of any copyright and cannot be\ncharacterized as royalty. But in the instant case, assesseee was not appointed as a\ndistributor simpliciter. The obligation was also cast upon the appllant under the\ndistribution agreement to provide onsale and after sale services and these services could\nnot be provided without resorting to technical support of the ITES division including the\naccess to technical know-how, IPRs, copyright, derivative works etc. Therefore, facts\nof the case of Set India Pvt.Ltd., and facts of the instant case are not similar. Thus, the\nratio laid down in that case will not apply to the present case.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_343\">121. A further reliance was also placed upon the judgment of the Delhi High Court in\nthe case of DIT Vs. New Sky Satellite (2016) 68 Taxmann.com 8 Delhi and on its\nperusal we find that the assessee is a company incorporated in Thailand was engaged in\nthe business providing digital broadcasting services as well as consultancy services to\nits customers who consisted of both residents of India and non-residents. The assessee\ncompany provided its services through its satellite T. In the course of assessment\nproceedings, the AO opined that income earned by assessee in India was taxable under\n9(1)(vi) and the assessee would not get benefit of <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_253\">article 12</a> of India-Thailand DTAA.\n<span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"110502981\" id=\"span_184\">The Tribunal, relying upon the judgment of Delhi High Court in the case <a href=\"/doc/110502981/\" id=\"a_254\">Asia Satellite\nTelecommunication Company Ltd., Vs. DIT</a> 332 ITR 340 in which it has been held that\nreceipts earned from providing data transmission services to space segment capacity on\nsatellite did not constitute royalty within the meaning of <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_255\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> and while\nproviding transmission services to its customers, the control of satellite always remained\nwith the satellite operator and the customers were only given access to the transponder\ncapacity, held that the receipt of the customers earned from providing data transmission\nservice did not fall within the term of royality under <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_256\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the act. The\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_185\">                                       Page 147 of 331</span>\n\nmatter travelled to the Hon&quot;ble High Court and the Hon&#x27;ble High Court, while\nadjudicating the issue has taken the cognizance of the amendments brought in <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_257\">section\n9(1)(vi)</a> of the Act and has held &quot;that it is fallacious to assume that any change made to\ndomestic law to rectify a situation of mistaken interpretation can spontaneously farther\ntheir case in an international treaty. Therefore mere amendment to <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_258\">section 9(1)(vi)</a>\ncannot result in change. It is imperative that such amendment brought about in the\nagreement as well. Any attempt short of this even if it is evidence of state&#x27;s of discomfort\nat letting data broadcast revenue&#x27;s ship will be insufficient to persuade this court to hold\nthat such amendments are applicable to DTAAs&quot;. Their Lordship further held that &quot;since\nthe <a href=\"/doc/75703920/\" id=\"a_259\">Finance Act</a> 2012 will not effect <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_260\">article 12</a> of DTAA, it would follow that the first\ndeterminative interpretation given to the word &#x27;royalty&#x27; in Asia Satellite,when the\ndefinition were in fact pari-materia (in the absence of any contouring explanation), will\ncontinue to hold the field for the purpose of assessment year precedent to the <a href=\"/doc/26112980/\" id=\"a_261\">Finance\nAct, 2012</a> and in all cases which involve DTAA, unless the said DTAA are amended\njointly amended by both the parties to incorporate income from data transmission\nservices as par-taking the nature of royalty. In the instant case, assessee was appointed\nas a distributor with certain obligations and the obligations cannot be fulfilled without\nhaving access to technical know-how, trade mark, derivative works, brand features, etc.,\nof the GIL. Therefore, the <a href=\"/doc/110502981/\" id=\"a_262\">ratio <a href=\"/doc/110502981/\" id=\"a_263\">laid down in</a></a> this case will not apply to the present case.\nWe have also examined the other judgments referred to by the assessee during the course\nof hearing and we find that facts of those cases are different from the facts of the instant\ncase. Therefore, no assistance from those cases in favour of the assessee.</span>\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_344\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"39708190\" id=\"span_186\">122. We have also examined the judgements referred to by the Revenue and in the case\nof <a href=\"/doc/39708190/\" id=\"a_264\">CIT Vs. Synopsys International Old Ltd.</a>, (supra). We find that Synopsys US, was\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_187\">                                      Page 148 of 331</span>\n\nthe owner of the copyright of electronic design and automation (EDA), Tool/software in\ncertain geographies. It granted a licence to Synopsys International Ltd., Ireland, the\nassessee. The technical licence agreement is for a consideration to enable the assessee\nto use and commercially exploit the IP in EDA, tools and software in certain\ngeographies. Synopsys US specially required the assessee to enter into end user software\nlicence agreement (EULA) with the customers. The EULA between the assessee and\nthe Indian customers has elaborate and restrictive clause, primarily with the object of\nprotecting the owner&#x27;s right in the product, documentation and IP in the software. The\nreading of the agreement shows that Indian customers had purchased the licenced\nproduct i.e., electronic design automation (EDA), tools /software from the assessee for\nits use as a tool on application software. The EDA tool support the design and production\nof integrated circuit (ICs).As fabrication process is an extensive process, IC\nmanufactures meticulously follow the design flow process before fabrication of the I.C\nis taken up. EDA tools are used in this design flow process. Design flow is a procedure\nwhich contain several sub-procedures.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_345\">123. The assessee received US $9,98,098, US $ 33, 53, 253 and US $ 20,97,003\nrespectively for 3 assessment years i.e., 2001-02, 2002-03 and 2003-04 from Indian\ncustomers. The notice under <a href=\"/doc/1837761/\" id=\"a_265\">section 147</a> was issued. The assessee filed a return for all\n3 years declaring Nil income but applied refund. The AO however held that assessee is\nliable to pay tax under <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_266\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/123806724/\" id=\"a_267\">20</a>% on gross basis for first 2 sassessment\nyear and 10% for the third assessment year. An appeal was preferred before the CIT(A).\nThe CIT(A) has held that the software given in use to Indian customers was named in\nthe agreement as &#x27;Synopsys software&#x27;. Had this software been itself got through licence\nfrom other companies, the name of software company would have borne the name of\nthat company from which it was received.
