The CIT(A) further observed that what is\n                                              IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                               IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                             IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_188\">                                         Page 149 of 331</span>\n\ntransferred through the agreement was a licence to use the synopsys software i.e. a\ncopyright owned by the appellant itself and not the copyright of the copyrighted article.\nHence the receipt squarely fell under definition of royalty and therefore taxable in India.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_346\">124. Aggrieved, assessee preferred an appeal before the Tribunal with the contention\nthat it is goods and not by way of licence agreement, the software only was allowed to\nuse. In other words a copyrighted article was transferred and no transfer was made in\nrespect of copyright. Being not convinced with the contention of the assessee, the\nTribunal held that fees received by the assessee could not be treated as royalty as defined\nunder <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_268\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the Act. Revenue preferred an appeal before the Hon&#x27;ble High\nCourt and before the Hon&#x27;ble High Court the following question was raised:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_211\">1. Whether consideration paid by Indian customers or end users, to the assessee for\ntransfer of right to use the software/computer programme is in respect of the copyright\nand falls within the mischief of royalty as defined under sub-clause (v) to explanation 2\nto clause (vi) of <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_269\">section 9(1)</a> of the Act.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_347\">125. The Hon&#x27;ble High Court extensively dealt with the issue in the light of various\njudicial pronouncements. The Hon&#x27;ble High Court has observed that the licence is a\npermission to do something that would otherwise be unlawful and the question arise\ntherefore as to what legal permission is granted by the software licence. For transfer of\nsuch right, if consideration is paid, it is not a consideration for transfer of copyright but\nfor use of IP embedded in the copyright and therefore it is for the transfer of one of those\nrights of the owner of the copyright. It is not a copyright but it is in respect of a copyright.\nWhen copyrighted article is sold and the end user gets the right to use the intellectual\nproperty embedded therein, it is not a right in the copyright as such. Therefore, the mode\n                                              IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                               IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                             IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_189\">                                         Page 150 of 331</span>\n\nadopted or the terminology given is not decisive to decide the nature of transfer,\nultimately it is substance which is to be looked into. The relevant observation of the\nHon&#x27;ble High Court in this regard is extracted hereunder for the sake of reference:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_212\">&quot;43. A licence is a permission to do something that would otherwise be unlawful. The\nquestion arises, therefore, as to what legal permission is granted by a software licence.\nThe answer is, briefly, that in some cases the licence will be a permission to use\nconfidential information, and in virtually in all cases it will be a permission to copy a\ncopyright work. If the software has been kept secret by the producer, or only supplied\non conditions of confidentiality and has not been published too widely, then the software\nlicence will be akin to a licence of confidential information or know-how. The owner or\nlicensor of a copyright, has a right to t permission to use the software or a computer\nprogramme, in respect of which they have a copyright, without transferring the right in\ncopyright. It is one of the right of a copyright. owner or licensor. Without such right\nbeing transferred, the end-user has no right to use the software or computer programme.\nIf he uses it, it amounts to infringement of copyright. For transfer of such right if\nconsideration is paid, it is not a consideration for transfer of a copyright but for use of\nintellectual property embedded in the copyright, and therefore it is for transfer of one of\nthose rights of the owner of the copyright. It is not a right in copyright but it is in respect\nof a copyright. When a copyrighted article is sold also, the end-user gets the right to use\ntile intellectual property embedded in the copyright and not a right in the copyright as\nsuch. Therefore the mode adopted or the. terminology given is not decisive to decide the\nnature of transfer. Ultimately, it is the substance which has to be looked into.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_213\">44. Therefore, it is necessary to look into the terms of the agreement entered into between\nthe parties, as it would be purely question of fact to be decided on the basis of the\nintention of the parties as could be gathered from the written words used in the\nagreement.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_348\">126. Having examined the contents and substance of end user software licence\n\nagreement executed between Synopsys International Ltd., and Athena Semiconductor\n\nPvt. Ltd., Bangalore, India (end user), the Hon&#x27;ble High Court has come to the\n\nconclusion that from the description of the agreement, it is an end user software licence\n                                               IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                              IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_190\">                                          Page 151 of 331</span>\n\nagreement, according to which software licence synopsys grant licencee a non-exclusive\n\nnon-transferrable licence without right of sub-licence to use the licenced software in\n\ndesign techniques only in the quantity of authorized by licencee in accordance to\n\ndocumentation in the use area. Their Lordship further held that even if it is not transfer\n\nof exclusive right of copyright, the right to use the confidential information embedded\n\nin the software in terms of aforesaid licence makes it abundantly clear that there is\n\ntransfer of certain rights which the owner of a copyright possess in the said computer\n\nsoftware programme in respect of copyright owned. In terms of the DTAA, the\n\nconsideration paid for the use or the right to use the said confidential information in the\n\nform of computer programme software itself constitutes royalty and attract taxes. The\n\nrelevant observation of the Hon&#x27;ble High Court is extracted hereunder for the sake of\n\nreference:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_214\">&quot;As is clear from the description of the agreement it is an end-user software licence agreement.\nClause 2.1 deals with grant of rights. It provides, Software License Synopsys hereby grants\nlicencee a non-exclusive, non-transferable license, without right of sub-licence of use the\nlicensed software and design techniques onlyin the quantity authorized by a licensee in\naccordance with the documentation in the use area. Licensee may make a reasonable\nnumber of copies of the licensed software for backup and/or archival purposes only. Merely\nbecause the words non-exclusive and non-transferable is used in the said licence it does not\ntake away the software out of the definition of the copyright. The word licenced software\nhas been defined. Similarly, the words design, design technique is also defined. The word\ndocumentation is also defined and it is not in dispute what is granted is a license. Even if it is\n                                               IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                              IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_191\">                                          Page 152 of 331</span>\n\nnot transfer of exclusive right in the copyright, the right to use the confidential information\nembedded in the software in terms of the aforesaid licence makes itabundantly clear that there\nis transfer of certain rights which the owner of copyright possess in the said\ncomputer sotware /programme in respect of the copyright owned. In terms of the DTAA the\nconsideration paid for the use or right to use the said confidential information in the form of\ncomputer programme software itself constitutes royalty and attracts tax. It is not necessary that\nthere should be a transfer of exclusive right in the copyright as contended by the assessee. The\nconsideration paid is for rights in respect of the copyright and for the user of the confidential\ninformation embedded in the software/computer programme. Therefore, it falls within the\nmischief of Explanation (2) of clause (vi) of sub-section (1) of <a href=\"/doc/1369261/\" id=\"a_270\">section 9</a> of the Act and there is\na liability to pay the tax.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_215\">46. If there was any doubt regarding the taxability of this income the parliament by\n<a href=\"/doc/75703920/\" id=\"a_271\">Finance Act, 2010</a> has substituted the explanation to <a href=\"/doc/1369261/\" id=\"a_272\">section 9</a> which gives a clear\nintention of the legislature insofar as the liability of tax under this provision is\nconcerned. A perusal of the said explanation makes it clear that as there was a doubt\nearlier, they want to remove the doubts by introducing this explanation. By the\nexplanation they have declared that for the purpose of <a href=\"/doc/1369261/\" id=\"a_273\">section 9</a> which deals with income\ndeemed to accrue or arise in India, under clauses (v), (vi) and (vii) of sub-section (1),\nsuch income shall be included in the total income of the nonresident, whether or not (i)\nthe non-resident has a residence or place of business or business connection in India,\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_216\">(ii) the non-resident has rendered services in India. Therefore, the object is to levy tax\non the income of a non-resident, if it has accrued or arisen in India and one such income\nis the income from royalty.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_349\">127. In the instant case, under the AdWord Distribution Agreement, certain obligations\nwere cast upon assessee in order to provide efficient on sale and after sale services which\nare only possible with the aid of ITES division. Under this AdWord Agreement and the\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_192\">                                       Page 153 of 331</span>\n\nService Agreement, the assessee was given licence to use the confidential information,\ntechnical know-how, trade mark, brand features, derivative works, etc. Though the\nownership of these intangibles remains with the GIL or the Google Inc., but the assessee\nwas given the licence to use it in order to provide better service. Therefore, the facts of\nthis case are similar to that case of Synopsys International Ltd. Hence the ratio <a href=\"/doc/39708190/\" id=\"a_274\">laid\ndown by</a> the jurisdictional High Court <a href=\"/doc/39708190/\" id=\"a_275\">in that case</a> apply to the present case.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_350\">128. It was emphatically argued by the learned Counsel for the assessee that the\npayments made by the assessee under the AdWord Distributor Agreement was in fact a\nbusiness profit in the hands of the GIL as it was paid on account of purchase of AdWord\nspace,though the AdWord Advertisement space was further sold to different advertisers.\nThe nature of payment remains the same i.e., the payment for purchase of AdWord\nspace. Thus it should be the business profit in the hands of the GIL. It was further\nargued that there was no transfer of technical know how or trade mark, intellectual\nproperty rights or any process in favour of the appellant under the AdWord Distribution\nAgreement. Therefore, the payment made under AdWord Distribution Agreement\ncannot be termed to be payment of royalty and is chargeable to tax under <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_276\">section 9(1)(vi)</a>\nof the Act as deemed income accrued in India. Whereas, according to Revenue, it was\nnot a payment simpliciter towards the purchase of AdWord space for its resale to\nadvertisers. The payment was made after deducting a particular percentage form the\nadvertisement receipts,received from the advertisers, though as per services agreement,\nthe assessee has rendered certain technical services but that too for the AdWord\nPrograms only, for which assessee has to receive certain payments as per terms of service\nagreement from GIL. But the entire aspect is to be looked into in the light of both the\ndistribution agreement and service agreement and the obligations cast upon the appellant\n                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_193\">                                        Page 154 of 331</span>\n\nunder Adword distribution agreement.According to Revenue, the after sales services and\nthe Ad review are not possible without the aid of ITES division. Therefore, the payment\nmade to GIL by the assessee was on account of usage of their trade mark, intellectual\nproperty rights, process, derivative workss, etc., and thus nature of payment is royalty.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_351\">129. In order to adjudicate the nature of payment, we are required to examine the\nAdWord Distributor Agreement and the Service Agreement. In foregoing paras, we\nhave already concluded that the AdWord Distributor Program and Services Agreement\nare interconnected with the navel cord and they cannot be segregated or separated.\nWithout resorting to service agreement, the obligation cast upon the assessee under the\nAdWord Distribution Agreement cannot be fulfilled. Therefore, we have to examine the\nrelevant clauses of the AdWord Distributor Agreement and the Service Agreement.\nUnder the AdWord Distributor Agreement, the appellant was responsible for uploading\nall advertisers&#x27; information that is required by Google for participation in the AdWord\nProgram. As per clause 2.3, the distributor shall provide after sales service to the\nadvertisers in accordance with the broad instructions, training and standards of Google.\nThe appellant&#x27;s employees were also required to be technically trained by the Google.\nAs per clause 9.4, upon the termination or the expiry of the agreement, all rights and\nlicences granted by one party to other including but not limited to use the other parties\nbrand features shall seize immediately and each party shall promptly return to the other\nparty or destroy and certify the destruction of all confidential information as defined in\nthe non-disclosure agreement and <a href=\"/doc/1849490/\" id=\"a_277\">section 7</a> of this agreement on the other party. The\nbrand features and the confidential information has been defined in this agreement and\nthe same were also extracted in the foregoing paras of this order according to which the\nbrand features includes the title, interest and intellectual property right relating to brand\nfeatures and the confidential information means the confidential information shared by\n                                               IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                              IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_194\">                                          Page 155 of 331</span>\n\none party to other as per this agreement meaning thereby under the AdWord Distributor\nAgreement, the appellant has access to the confidential information, intellectual property\nrights and the brand features of the GIL in order to provide effective services under the\nAdWord Distributor Agreement. The technical services are required to be provided by\nthe appellant under the AdWord Distributor Agreement as per services agreement,\naccording to which the confidential information means all data and information of\nconfidential in nature including technical know how and trade secrets relating to the\nbusiness, the affairs, the product, the development projects or the products or services of\nGoogle Ireland or its suppliers or its affiliates including but not limited to intellectual\nproperty rights andconfidential information be communicated orally in writing or in any\nother recorded electronic or tangible forms.As per the service agreement data and\ninformation shall be considered to be confidential information.                     In the Service\nAgreement, derivative works is also defined and it means for copy writable or\ncopyrighted material, any modifications, derivative works, translation, abridgment,\nrevision of the form in which such material may be recast, transmitted or adopted. The\nintellectual property rights was also defined in the Services Agreement according to\nwhich it means all intellectual, proprietaries and/or intangible property right consulting,\nembodied in, pertaining to, used in or in respect of business of Google Ireland, the\nproducts or the provision related to services and all tangible embodiments thereof\nwherever located including but not limited to the followings :\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_217\">(1) all trade marks, trade names, service mark, logos or trades data bases including all\nregistration and applications therefor.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_218\">(2) All copy rights, nodal rights as defined therein and other rights in works of authorship\nincluding all registrations and applications therefor\n(3) All patents and patent application patentable ideas, invention, innovations and\nimplements\n                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_195\">                                        Page 156 of 331</span>\n\n(4) All know-how, trade secrets\n(5) All designs and code documentation, methodologies, process, design information,\netc.\n\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_352\">130. We have already reproduced relevant clauses of the service agreement in the\nforegoing paras for reference. Therefore, we do no prefer to extract the same again here\nwhile dealing with the issue. Under this service agreement, ownership of intellectual\nproperty, confidential information, software technologies, documentation shall remain\nthe exclusive property of the Google Ireland but the Google India, the appellant, was\nallowed under the licence to use it in order to fulfil the obligations cast upon it under the\nAdWord Distributor Agreement. Upon termination, either of the parties are required to\neither destroy all copies of information, information, documentations and other software\nembodiments in its possession or control and not to use the same after the expiration or\nthe termination of the agreement.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_353\">131. From the careful perusal of this AdWord Distribution Agreement and the Service\nAgreement, we find that appellant has not only purchased the advertisement space from\nthe GIL and resold it to the advertisers against certain advertisement charges but the\nappellant was required to provide on sale and post sale technical services to the advertiser\nand the GIL, which are not possible without resorting the service agreement. In fact, the\nassessee obtained the advertisement space under the AdWord Distribution Agreement\nand resell it to different advertisers along with on sale and after sale services. With the\naid of ITES division, the appellant is required to execute the programs and also provide\nall technical support to the advertisers and GIL. As per the agreement with the\nadvertisers, the advertisers are required to approach only the appellant and not the GIL.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_354\">                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_196\">                                       Page 157 of 331</span>\n\nAppellant is in fact required to sort out all glitches to be faced by the advertisers while\nputting the ads on the AdWord Program. Having examined functioning of the appellant\nfrom the submission on behalf of the appellant and the revenue, the literatures in the\nform of book viz., Learning AdWord and Google Analytics by Benjamin Mangold and\nthe snap shots placed before us, we find that Google AdWord Program is a complex\nprogram or rather it can be called to be a portal or software developed by Google Inc.\nWhile discharging its obligation under the Google AdWord Programs and Service\nAgreement the appellant has an access to the trade marks, IPRs, derivative works, brand\nfeatures and the confidential information of the GIL. Therefore, it cannot be called that\nwhatever payments were made by the appellant to GIL was simpliciter a payment\ntowards the purchase of AdWord space from the GIL for its resale to advertisers.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_355\">132. In order to examine whether this payment is a business profit of GIL or it was a\npayment of royalty to GIL, we have to examine the definition of royalty given in\nexplanation below <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_278\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the Act, according to which the royalty means the\nconsideration received for transfer of any rights including the granting of licence in\nrespect of patent, invention, model, designs, secret formula or process or trade mark or\nsimilar property or for imparting of any information concerning the working of or the\nuse of patent, invention, model, design, secret formula or process or trade mark or similar\nproperty or use of any patent, invention, model, secret formula or process or trade of\nmark or similar property, etc. For the sake of reference, we extract the explanations\nbelow <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_279\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the act as under:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_219\">   &quot;Explanation
