As per explanation 3 below, <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_281\">section 9(1)(vi)</a>, the computer software means any\ncomputer program recorded in disk, tape, perforated media or the information storage,\ndevice and includes any such programme or customized electronic data and as per\nexplanation 5, the royalty includes the consideration in respect of any right, property or\ninformation whether or not the possession or control of such right, property or\ninformation is with the payer, such right or property, information is used directly by the\npayer, location of such property or information is in India. As per explanation 6, the\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_201\">                                       Page 160 of 331</span>\n\nword process is also defined and shall be deemed to have included transmission of\nsatellite.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_365\">134. Royalty is also defined under <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_282\">article 12</a> of Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement\n(DTAA) with Ireland. According to which, the term royalty means payment of any kind\nreceived by consideration for use or right to use any copyright of literary, artistic or\nscientific works including cinematograph films or tapes used for video or television\nbroadcasting, any patent, trade mark, design or model plan, secret formula, process or\nfor the use of or the right to use industrial, commercial or scientific equipment. For the\nsake of reference, we extract <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_283\">article 12</a> as under:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_225\">&quot;<a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_284\">ARTICLE 12 :</a>ROYALTIES AND FEES FOR TECHNICAL SERVICES\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_226\">1. Royalties or fees for technical services arising in a Contracting State and paid to a\nresident of the other Contracting State may be taxed in that other State.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_227\">2. However, such royalties or fees for technical services may also be taxed in the\nContracting State in which they arise, and according to the laws of that State, but if the\nrecipient is the beneficial owner of the royalties or fees for technical services, the tax\nso charged shall not exceed 10 per cent of the gross amount of the royalties or fees for\ntechnical services.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_228\">3. (a) The term &quot;royalties&quot; as used in this Article means payments of any kind received\nas a consideration for the use of, or the right to use, any copyright of literary, artistic\nor scientific work including cinematograph film or films or tapes for radio or television\nbroadcasting, any patent, trade mark, design or model, plan, secret formula or process\nor for the use of or the right to use industrial, commercial or scientific equipment, other\nthan an aircraft, or for information concerning industrial, commercial or scientific\nexperience;\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_229\">                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_202\">                                       Page 161 of 331</span>\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_230\">(b) The term &quot;fees for technical services&quot; means payment of any kind in consideration\nfor the rendering of any managerial, technical or consultancy services including the\nprovision of services by technical or other personnel but does not include payments for\nservices mentioned in <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_285\">Articles 14</a> and <a href=\"/doc/609295/\" id=\"a_286\">15</a> of this Convention.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_231\">4. The provisions of paragraphs 1 and 2 shall not apply if the beneficial owner of the\nroyalties or fees for technical services, being a resident of a Contracting State, carries\non business in the other Contracting State in which the royalties or fees for technical\nservices arise through a permanent establishment situated therein, or performs in that\nother State independent personal services from a fixed base situated therein, and the\nright or property in respect of which the royalties or fees for technical services are paid\nis effectively connected with such permanent establishment or fixed base. In such case\nthe provisions of <a href=\"/doc/735354/\" id=\"a_287\">Article 7</a> or <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_288\">Article 14</a>, as the case may be, shall apply.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_232\">5. Royalties or fees for technical services shall be deemed to arise in a Contracting\nState when the payer is that State itself, a political sub-division, a local authority or a\nresident of that State. Where, however, the person paying the royalties or fees for\ntechnical services, whether he is a resident of a Contracting State or not, has in a\nContracting State a permanent establishment or a fixed base in connection with which\nthe liability to pay the royalties or fees for technical services was incurred, and such\nroyalties or fees for technical services are borne by such permanent establishment or\nfixed base, then such royalties or fees for technical services shall be deemed to arise in\nthe State in which the permanent establishment or fixed base is situated.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_233\">6. Where, by reason of a special relationship between the payer and the beneficial\nowner or between both of them and some other person, the amount of the royalties or\nfees for technical services, having regard to the use, right or information for which they\nare paid, exceeds the amount which would have been agreed upon by the payer and the\nbeneficial owner in the absence of such relationship, the provisions of this Article shall\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_203\">                                       Page 162 of 331</span>\n\napply only to the last-mentioned amount. In such case, the excess part of the payments\nshall remain taxable according to the laws of each Contracting State, due regard being\nhad to the other provisions of this Convention.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_366\">135. If we examine the nature of services performed by the assesse with the aid of ITES\ndivision we would find that the assessee has an access to patent, technical know-how,\nIPRs, trade mark, the process, derivative works, brand features, etc., of the GIL, therefore\nthe payments of advertisement fees made by the assesse after retaining a particular part\nof it to GIL is not the payment simpliciter towards the purchase of AdWord space which\nmay be treated as business profit in the hands of the recipient but it is a payment of\nroyalty to the GIL in the light of definition of royalty given under the Act and the DTAA.\nTherefore, we conclude that the payment made by the assesse to GIL is a payment of\nroyalty and as per provisions of <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_289\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the Act, it is an income deemed to\naccrue or arise in India.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_367\">136. Ld counsel for the asseessee has also raised an argument that Royality can only\nbe taxed on receipt basis in the light of definition given in <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_290\">Article 12</a> of DTAA and not\nas per domestic law. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"49006086\" id=\"span_204\">In this regard.We have examined the judgment of the Apex Court\nin the case of <a href=\"/doc/49006086/\" id=\"a_291\">Palam Gas Service Vs. CIT</a> [2017] 81 taxmann.com 43 (SC) referred to\nby the Revenue in which their Lordship have examined the scope of provisions of <a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_292\">section\n40(a)(ia)</a> in respect of word payable occurring therein. Their Lordship having examined\nthe various provisions and have held that <a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_293\">section 40(a)(ia)</a> covers not only those cases\nwhere the amount is payable but also when it is paid. In this behalf one has to keep in\nmind the purpose with which <a href=\"/doc/1486104/\" id=\"a_294\">section 40</a> was enacted and the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/1195538/\" id=\"a_295\">section 194C</a>\nand <a href=\"/doc/609896/\" id=\"a_296\">200</a> of the Act. Once it is found that the aforesaid section mandates the person to\ndeduct tax at source not only the amount payable but also when the sums are actually\n                                               IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                              IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_205\">                                          Page 163 of 331</span>\n\npaid to the contractor, any person who does not adhere to the statutory obligation has to\nsuffer the consequences which are stipulated in the Act itself. The relevant observation\nof their Lordship is extracted hereunder for the sake of reference:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_234\">&quot;15. We approve the aforesaid view as well. As a fortiorari, it follows that <a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_297\">Section 40(a)(ia)</a>\ncovers not only those cases where the amount is payable but also when it is paid. In this behalf,\none has to keep in mind the purpose with which <a href=\"/doc/1486104/\" id=\"a_298\">Section 40</a> was enacted and that has already\nbeen noted above. We have also to keep in mind the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/1195538/\" id=\"a_299\">Sections 194C</a> and <a href=\"/doc/609896/\" id=\"a_300\">200</a>. Once\nit is found that the aforesaid Sections mandate a person to deduct tax at source not only on the\namounts payable but also when the sums are actually paid to the contractor, any person who\ndoes not adhere to this statutory obligation has to suffer the consequences which are stipulated\nin the Act itself. Certain consequences of failure to deduct tax at source from the payments\nmade, where tax was to be deducted at source or failure to pay the same to the credit of the\nCentral Government, are stipulated in <a href=\"/doc/726238/\" id=\"a_301\">Section 201</a> of the Act. This Section provides that in that\ncontingency, such a person would be deemed to be an assessee in default in respect of such tax.\nWhile stipulating this consequence, <a href=\"/doc/726238/\" id=\"a_302\">Section 201</a> categorically states that the aforesaid Sections\nwould be without prejudice to any other consequences which that defaulter may incur. Other\nconsequences are provided under <a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_303\">Section 40(a)(ia)</a> of the Act, namely, payments made by such\na person to a contractor shall not be treated as deductible expenditure. When read in this\ncontext, it is clear that <a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_304\">Section 40(a)(ia)</a> deals with the nature of default and the consequences\nthereof. Default is relatable to Chapter XVIIB (in the instant case <a href=\"/doc/1195538/\" id=\"a_305\">Sections 194C</a> and <a href=\"/doc/609896/\" id=\"a_306\">200</a>, which\nprovisions are in the aforesaid Chapter). When the entire scheme of obligation to deduct the\ntax at source and paying it over to the Central Government is read holistically, it cannot be\nheld that the word &#x27;payable&#x27; occurring in <a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_307\">Section 40(a)(ia)</a> refers to only those cases where the\namount is yet to be paid and does not cover the cases where the amount is actually paid. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"49006086\" id=\"span_206\">If the\nprovision is interpreted in the manner suggested by the appellant herein, then even when it is\nfound that a person, like the appellant, has violated the provisions of Chapter XVIIB (or\nspecifically <a href=\"/doc/1195538/\" id=\"a_308\">Sections 194C</a> and <a href=\"/doc/609896/\" id=\"a_309\">200</a> in the instant case), he would still go scot free, without\n                                               IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                              IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_207\">                                         Page 164 of 331</span>\n\nsuffering the consequences of such monetary default in spite of specific provisions laying down\nthese consequences. The Punjab &amp; Haryana High Court has exhaustively interpreted <a href=\"/doc/1486104/\" id=\"a_310\">Section\n40</a>(a(ia) keeping in mind different aspects. We would again quote the following paragraphs\nfrom <a href=\"/doc/49006086/\" id=\"a_311\">the said judgment</a>, with our complete approval thereto:</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_235\">      &quot;26. Further, the mere incurring of a liability does not require an assessee to deduct\n      the tax at source even if such payments, if made, would require an assessee to deduct\n      the tax at source. The liability to deduct tax at source under Chapter XVII-B arises\n      only upon payments being made or where so specified under the sections in Chapter\n      XVII, the amount is credited to the account of the payee. In other words, the liability\n      to deduct tax at source arises not on account of the assessee being liable to the payee\n      but only upon the liability being discharged in the case of an assessee following the\n      cash system and upon credit being given by an assessee following the mercantile\n      system. This is clear from every section in Chapter XVII.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_236\">      27. Take for instance, the case of an assessee, who follows the cash system of\n      accounting and where the assessee who though liable to pay the contractor, fails to do\n      so for any reason. The assessee is not then liable to deduct tax at source. Take also the\n      case of an assessee, who follows the mercantile system. Such an assessee may have\n      incurred the liability to pay amounts to a party. Such an assessee is also not bound to\n      deduct tax at source unless he credits such sums to the account of the party/payee, such\n      as, a contractor. This is clear from <a href=\"/doc/1195538/\" id=\"a_312\">Section 194C</a> set out earlier. The liability to deduct\n      tax at source, in the case of an assessee following the cash system, arises only when\n      the payment is made and in the case of an assessee following the mercantile system,\n      when he credits such sum to the account of the party entitled to receive the payment.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_237\">      28. The government has nothing to do with the dispute between the assessee and the\n      payee such as a contractor. The provisions of the Act including <a href=\"/doc/1486104/\" id=\"a_313\">Section 40</a> and the\n      provisions of Chapter XVII do not entitle the tax authorities to adjudicate the liability of\n      an assessee to make payment to the payee/other contracting party. The appellant&#x27;s\n      submission, if accepted, would require an adjudication by the tax authorities as to the\n                                               IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                              IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_208\">                                          Page 165 of 331</span>\n\n       liability of the assessee to make payment. They would then be required to investigate all\n       the records of an assessee to ascertain its liability to third parties. This could in many\n       cases be an extremely complicated task especially in the absence of the third party. The\n       third party may not press the claim. The parties may settle the dispute, if any. This is an\n       exercise not even remotely required or even contemplated by the section.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_368\">16. As mentioned above, the Punjab &amp; Haryana High Court found support from the judgments\nof the Madras and Calcutta High Courts taking identical view and by extensively quoting from\nthe said judgments.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_369\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"20720737\" id=\"span_209\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1208473\" id=\"span_210\">17. Insofar as judgment of the Allahabad High Court is concerned, reading thereof would\nreflect that the High Court, after noticing the fact that since the amounts had already been paid,\nit straightaway concluded, without any discussion, that <a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_314\">Section 40(a)(ia)</a> would apply only\nwhen the amount is &#x27;payable&#x27; and dismissed the appeal of the Department stating that the\nquestion of law framed did not arise for consideration. No doubt, the Special Leave Petition\nthereagainst was dismissed by this Court in limine. However, that would not amount to\nconfirming the view of the Allahabad High Court (See V.M. Salgaocar &amp; Bros. (<a href=\"/doc/1208473/\" id=\"a_315\">P) Ltd. v.\nCommissioner of Income Tax</a>, (2000) 243 ITR 383 and Supreme Court <a href=\"/doc/20720737/\" id=\"a_316\">Employees Welfare\nAssociation v. Union of India</a>, (1989) 4 SCC 187.</span></span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_370\">18. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"196157087\" id=\"span_211\">In view of the aforesaid discussion, we hold that the view taken by the High Courts of\nPunjab &amp; Haryana, Madras and Calcutta is the correct view and the judgment of the Allahabad\nHigh Court in <a href=\"/doc/196157087/\" id=\"a_317\">CIT v. Vector Shipping Services (P) Ltd.</a>, (2013) 357 ITR 642 did not decide the\nquestion of law correctly. Thus, insofar as the judgment of the Allahabad High Court is\nconcerned, we overrule the same. Consequences of the aforesaid discussion will be to answer\nthe question against the appellant/assessee thereby approving the view taken by the High\nCourt&quot;.</span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_371\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"21107386\" id=\"span_212\">137. Our attention was also invited to judgment of the Bombay High Court in the case\nof <a href=\"/doc/21107386/\" id=\"a_318\">Director (International Taxation) Vs. Siemens</a> aktiengesellschaft (supra) in ITA\n                                              IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                               IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                             IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_213\">                                         Page 166 of 331</span>\n\nNo. 124/2000 in which the Hon&#x27;ble High Court has upheld the view taken by the\nTribunal holding that the royalty and fees of technical services should be taxed on receipt\nbasis.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"162725073\" id=\"span_214\">We have also examined the order of the Tribunal in the case of <a href=\"/doc/162725073/\" id=\"a_319\">Saira Asia Interiors\nPvt. Ltd., Vs. ITO</a> (supra) and Naitonal Organic Chemicals Vs. DCIT in which it was\nheld that under the related DTAA, tax liability can only arise at the point of payment and\nthe income embedded in the payment is not taxable at the point of time of crediting the\namount.</span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_372\">138. In order to adjudicate an issue of chargeability of tax on royalty we have examined\nthe provisions of <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_320\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the Act according to which, it shall be deemed to\naccrue or arise in India when it has become payable by a person who is resident of India.\nWhereas as per <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_321\">article 12</a> of DTAA, the royalty means payment of any kind received as\nconsideration for the use of or the right to use any copyright of literary, artistic scientific\nwork including cinematograph films etc. It was emphatically argued by Sr. Advocate\nMr. Percy Pardiwala that wherever there is a conflict between the domestic law and\nDTAA, the provisions of DTAA will only prevail. Therefore, royalty only means when\nit is received as consideration for the use of or the right to use any copyright of literary,\nartistic, etc. We have also examined the judgment referred to by the parties on this issue\nand we find that it has to be taxed on receipt basis but in the instant case, the nature of\nAdWord Agreement and the Service Agreement is quite complexed. Under the Service\nAgreement, assessee would be paid by the GIL whereas under AdWord Agreement, the\nappellant is required to make payment of advertisement charges after deducting a\nparticular percentage to the GIL. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"39708190\" id=\"span_215\">Though the assessee has contended that ITES division\nis exclusively involved in providing technical services to GIL as per the Services\nAgreement and its marketing division is involved in providing services as per AdWord\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_216\">                                       Page 167 of 331</span>\n\nProgram but in order the examine the actual services rendered by the appellant under the\nAdWord Program, one has to read both the agreements together. In the foregoing paras,\nwe have already adjudicated and concluded that both these agreements are connected\nwith the navel cord and they cannot be segregated. Therefore, both the agreements are\nto be read together in order to find out the actual functioning of the appellant, as held by\njurisdictional High Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/39708190/\" id=\"a_322\">Synopsys International Ltd.</a>, (supra) that it is\nnecessary to look into the terms of the agreement entered into between the parties as it\nwould be purely question of fact to be decided on the basis of the intention of the parties\nas could be gathered from the written words used in the agreement. During the course\nof hearing, our attention was also invited to return filed by the GIL for the assessment\nyear 2009-10 in which the GIL has shown its method of accounting as mercantile system\nof accounting. Once the GIL has itself has declared the method of accounting as\nmercantile system it cannot claim that for the purpose of royalty, system of accounting\nshould be on cash/reciept basis. Moreover, the adjustment entries are to made in the\nbooks of accounts of the appellant as well as GIL with regard to the payment under\nService Agreement and AdWord Program Agreement. It is also an admitted fact that in\nview of clause 6.1 of EXHIBIT-A attached to Distributor agreement, payments are to be\nmade during the year on short intervals and the final trued-up payment on the basis of\naudited accounts of GIL. The appellant could not demonstrate that the royalty was not\npaid to or received by the GIL and it was simply credited to its accounts in the books of\naccounts of the appellant.     Under these situations, we do not find any merit in the\ncontentions of the assessee that royalty cannot be taxed in the impugned assessment year\nas it was not received by the GIL and therefore is not chargeable to tax in India as a\ndeemed income accrued. Since the payment made under the distribution agreement to\nGIL by the appellant is held to be the payment of royalty, appellant is required to deduct\nthe TDS on such payment.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_373\">                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_217\">                                       Page 168 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_374\">139. On the point of equalization levy, we find that by the <a href=\"/doc/75703920/\" id=\"a_323\">Finance Act, 2016</a>,\nequalization levy was introduced and as per its definition, equalization levy means tax\nleviable on consideration received or receivable for any services specified under the\nprovisions of the chapter. They have also defined specified service which means online\nadvertisement, any provision for digital advertisement space or any other facility of\nservice for the purpose of online advertisement and includes any other services as may\nbe notified by the Central Government in this behalf. It was further clarified that from\nthe date of the commencement there shall be charge on equalization levy @ 6% on the\namount of consideration for any specified services received or receivable by a person\nbeing a non-resident from a person resident in India carried on business or profession or\nnon-resident having a permanent establishment in India.                Meaning thereby, the\nLegislature has introduced the equalization levy to tax the considerations paid by\nresident to a non-resident who is not having a permanent establishment in India. No\ndoubt, it may be business profit in the hands of the recipient and the Legislature intend\nto tax the business profit as it has arisen out of the activities performed in India. But it\ndoes not effect other kind of services in which technology,the know-how, copyrights or\nIPRs are involved. From this argument, the learned Counsel for the assessee intend to\ndemonstrate that since the intention of the Legislature to treat the payment by the\nadvertisers to the non-resident is a business profit in the hands of the recipient the same\nshould be treated in other cases also where the payments are made by the residents to\nnon-residents for a consideration of use of technical know-how, IPRs, copyrights,\nderivative workss and other intangibles. But we do not find any merit in this argument\nas the equilization levy is to charged only on consideration for specified services and not\nothers where there is use of IPR, copyright and other intangibles. In the instant case,\nunder AdWord Distribution and Service Agreement, the appellant has acquired licence\nto use IPRs, copyright and other intangibles to provide better services either to GIL or\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_218\">                                       Page 169 of 331</span>\n\nto advertisers. Therefore, the introduction of equilisation levy would not convert the\nnature of payment made by the appellant to GIL.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_375\">140. With regard to another limb of argument of the learned Counsel for the assessee\nthat in providing a space on Google AdWord Program to the advertisers, the trade mark\nor the GIL was not involved therefore consideration paid by the appellant to GIL is not\nroyalty, we find that the AdWord was registered with the Registrar of Trade Marks,\nMumbai in the name Google Inc., but the programme is known as Google AdWord\nProgram owned by the Google Inc., under which GIL has provided space to appellant\nfor its sale to the various advertisers. The learned Counsel for the assessee in this regard\nhas contended that if the trade mark is used as incidental to the main activity, it cannot\nbe called that there was a transfer of trade mark or licence to use the trade mark. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"163959178\" id=\"span_219\">In\nsupport of his contention, he has invited our attention to the judgment of Delhi High\nCourt in the case of <a href=\"/doc/163959178/\" id=\"a_324\">Director of Income Tax Vs. Sherton Internaitonal Inc.</a>, (2009) 178\nTaxmann 84 Delhi and on its perusal we find that assessee company is incorporated in\nUSA and non-resident under the Indian Tax Laws. The assessee is engaged in providing\nservices to hotels in various parts of the world. On 27.01.1979 the assessee entered into\none such agreement with ITC Ltd., for providing services to 3 of its hotels i.e., Welcome\nGroup Mourya Sherton, New Delhi, Welcome Group Mugal Sherton, Agra and\nWelcome Group, Chhola, Sherton Madras. The scope of services envisaged in the\nagreement was publicity, advertisement and sales including reservation services. In\nconsideration of the services which the assessee was required to render, ITC Ltd., agreed\nto pay a fee @ 3% of the room sales to the assessee. According to Revenue what the\nassessee was making available to the ITC hotels was technical and consultancy service;\nprovision of training to its employees&#x27; the use of its trade mark (even though they were\nfree of cost, these were services according to him linked to the trade mark), making\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_220\">                                      Page 170 of 331</span>\n\navailable the technical know-how, documentation and manuals for which while the\nassessee was not charging a lumpsum fees the consideration received by the assessee\nwas relatable to the business concluded by its client-hotels and the reservation network.\nBased on these observations, the AO has concluded that assessee had a business\nconnection with India and hence fees received on account of services rendered by the\nassessee were deemed to accrue or arise in India and therefore assessee&#x27;s case was\ncovered under <a href=\"/doc/1369261/\" id=\"a_325\">section 9</a> of the Act. While adjudicating the issue CIT(A) has classified\nservices rendered by the assessee into three categories. According to the CIT(A), the fee\nreceived by the assessee for the followingthree categories constituted payment of royalty\nunder <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_326\">article 12(3)(a)</a> of the DTAA; &quot; (1) use of trade mark, trade name and the stylized\nS service mark, even though the agreement allowed its use at no cost it being a\ncolourable device the payment received by the assessee had to be attributed to the user\nof the said intangible assets ;2) Payments towards reservation service and &quot;(3) lastly\npayments towards maintenance of high international standards.&quot; In respect of last\ncategory of service which were in the nature of publicity, marketing and promotion\nactivities and had been rendered outside India, according to CIT(A), they constitute a\ncommercial income and in the absence of PE in India the said payments could not be\nbrought to tax.</span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_376\">141. The matter was carried to the Tribunal and the Tribunal examined the arguments\nand the related nature of services and came to the conclusion that the main service\nrendered by the assessee to its client was advertisement, publicity and sales\npromotion,keeping in mind the mutual interest and in that context, the use of trade mark,\ntrade name or the stylized &#x27;S&#x27; or other illuminated sereivces referred in the agreement\nwith the assessee were incidental to the main service. Accordingly, Tribunal has held\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_221\">                                      Page 171 of 331</span>\n\nthat payment received was neither in the nature of royalty under <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_327\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> read\nwith explanation 2 nor in the nature of fee for technical service under <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_328\">article 12</a> of the\nDTAA. The Revenue went in appeal before the Hon&#x27;ble High Court and the Hon&#x27;ble\nHigh Court has confirmed the view of the Tribunal.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_377\">142. In the instant case, appellant was not engaged in the advertisement, publicity of\nany particular activities of the GIL. Appellant was appointed as a distributor under the\nAdWord Distribution Agreement with certain obligations towards the advertisers and\nthe GIL and that obligations cannot be fulfilled without the aid of ITES division and\nhaving access to the IPRs, technical know-how, trade mark, derivative workss and other\nintangibles of the GIL. Thus the facts of the aforesaid case and that of the appellant&#x27;s\ncase are entirely different. Therefore the <a href=\"/doc/163959178/\" id=\"a_329\">ratio <a href=\"/doc/163959178/\" id=\"a_330\">laid down in</a></a> that case will not be\napplicable in the present case.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_378\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"23544710\" id=\"span_222\">143. We have also been carried through other judgments of Delhi High Court in the\ncase of <a href=\"/doc/23544710/\" id=\"a_331\">Formula One World Championship Ltd., Vs. CIT (Supra</a>) and from its perusal\nwe find that assessee i.e., Formula One World Championship Ltd., was a UK tax resident\ncompany.     Consequent to the agreement entered into between the Federation\nInternationale Automobile (FIA an International Motor Sports Event Regulating\nAssociation) Formula One Asset Management Ltd., (FOAM) and FOWC, FOAM\nlicenced all commercial rights in FIA Formula One World Championship\n(Championship)to FOWC for hundred years terms effecting from 01.01.2011. Assessee\nentered into risk promotion contract (RPC) dated 13.09.2011 by which it granted to\nJaypee Sports right (Right) to host, stage and promote formula one, Grand Prix of India,\nevent foraconsideration of USD 40 millions and art work licence agreement (ALA)\ncontemplated in RPC was also entered into between the FOWC and the Jaypee the same\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_223\">                                      Page 172 of 331</span>\n\nday i.e., 13.09.2011 permitting the use of certain marks and intellectual property\nbelonging to FOWC for a consideration of USD 1. Every F1 racing event is hosted,\npromoted and staged by a promoter with whom FOWC as the a right holder enters into\ncontract and whose event is nominated by the CRH (a contract right holder which in\neffect FOWC) to the FIA for inclusion in the official F1 racing calendar. In other words\nFOWC an exclusive nominating body at whose instance the event promoter is permitted\nparticipation.</span>\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_379\">144. The FOWC and Jaypee Sports both approached the AAR on issue whether the\npayment of consideration receivable by FOWC outside India in terms of RPC from\nJaypee was or was not royalty as defined in <a href=\"/doc/134715/\" id=\"a_332\">article 13</a> India-UK DTAA. The AAR\nhaving examined the contents of the agreement in the light of rival submissions have\nconcluded that firstly the amount paid was royalty secondly FOWC had no fixed place\nof business nor was doing any business activity in India and had not authorized any\norganization or entity to conclude contracts on their behalf and therefore had no PE in\nIndia. The assessee moved to the High Court against both the observations of the AAR\nand with regard to question whether the payment made by Jaypee to FOWC is in the\nnature of royalty under DTAA for the use of later&#x27;s trade mark. The Hon&#x27;ble High Court\nhas examined the facts of the case and have come to the conclusion that from the tenure\nof the agreement, the main purpose of the RPC is not for grant of trade mark rights or\nprivilege or licence to use, and the payment made to it under RPC are not the royalty\neither in the Act or the DTAA, they most certainly are not for the use of the trade mark\nor the IP rights, but rather for the grant of privilege of staging, hosting and promoting\nthe event at the promoters racing circuit in Noida. The relevant observation of the\nHon&#x27;ble High Court are extracted hereunder for the sake of reference:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_238\">                                               IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                              IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_224\">                                          Page 173 of 331</span>\n\n&quot; 65. FOWC and Jaypee argue that the rights given to the latter under the RPC was the grant\nof a bundle of rights the basic objective of which were to enable it, as the promoter to stage a\nmotor racing event in India at its circuit, the Buddh International Circuit, that would be an\nofficial round of the FIA Formula One World Championship, in which all participant teams\nand drivers in that Championship would therefore compete. The permission to use the F1 marks\nfor purposes limited to advertising the Event was incidental to the grant of that principal right\nto enable hosting of the event. They also argue that under the terms of the RPC and the ALA\nJaypee had no independent right of commercial exploitation of the marks. Therefore, the two\nindependent contracting parties agreed that no consideration was payable to FOWC in relation\nto the use of the marks. Though the right included the right to name the event specifically, it\nwas predominantly in the nature of a pure commercial right. It was argued that the payment to\nFOWC was the dominant purpose of securing the right to &quot;put on&quot; (i.e. host, stage and promote)\nat its circuit and as its own commercial venture, an official round of a globally popular\ninternational sporting series. The consideration was not paid for the use of any copyright,\ntrademark or other intellectual property mentioned in the definition of &#x27;royalty&#x27;, whether\nW.P.(C) 10307/2016, W.P.(C) 9509/2016 &amp; W.P.(C) 10145/2016 Page 56 under second\nExplanation to <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_333\">Section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the Act or <a href=\"/doc/654960/\" id=\"a_334\">Article 13(3)</a> of the India-UK DTAA. The ALA was\nincidental to and a mere consequence of the RPC. Its purpose was to ensure that the use of Fl\nand related marks would be strictly controlled and limited to the sole purpose of promoting the\nevent(s), and that Jaypee and other third parties would not be permitted to use the marks in\nconnection with any other unauthorized activity or commercial venture that could damage or\ndilute the reputation and prestige of the Championship. FOWC and Jaypee therefore urge that\nthe AAR\u201fs finding that the payment amounted to &quot;royalty&quot; is contrary to law. They argue that\nthe AAR also erred in distinguishing and not following Sheraton(supra). The revenue on the\nother hand, supports the AAR\u201fs conclusions and findings; it urges that the terms of the ALA\nand the RPC clearly show that the payment of US$ 40 million was to enable Jaypee the use of\nthe F1 trademark and logo; they point out that these marks figured prominently in the trackside\nadvertisements and the tickets that were printed. The use of the logo, to promote the event,\n                                                IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                      IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                               IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_225\">                                          Page 174 of 331</span>\n\ntherefore meant use of the F1 marks. The nomenclature or terms of an agreement are not\nalways determinative of the true nature of the transaction; in this case, it clearly was to permit\nthe use of the F1 mark. The character of the event would have been different had the F1 marks\nnot been used by Jaypee. It was held that the popularity of the event is a result only of the use\nof the Marks, and that without those marks it would not enjoy its popularity otherwise.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_239\">66. Under trademark law, particularly in India, trademark use even for advertisement purposes\nis to be preceded by prior consent of the proprietor and any unauthorized use of the trademark\nwithout such prior permission of the proprietor could lead to an infringement of the trademark\n(in India, under <a href=\"/doc/84096/\" id=\"a_335\">section 29</a> of the Trade Marks Act,1999). To secure registration for the marks\nin India from the Trademark Registry, FOWC and Jaypee entered into the ALA. The function\nof the ALA was (a) to provide for a strictly limited usage of the marks i.e. only for advertisement\nand promotion of the Indian Grand Prix Event; (b) to provide for restrictions on usage of such\nmarks, i.e. not for any commercial purposes such as use on merchandise, etc.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_240\">67. The grant of a right, in the form of license to use the trademark is primarily to be utilized\nin the licensee\u201fs product. In a typical case of licensing, the trademark proprietor W.P.(C)\n10307/2016, W.P.(C) 9509/2016 &amp; W.P.(C) 10145/2016 Page 57 does not wish to mark its\nproducts in an area; it licenses the mark, to be used by the licensee\u201fs products, subject to\nlimitations. The licensee has no right to initiate legal proceedings, in the event of infringement,\nunless specifically authorized; the property in the mark always vests with the owner. Even the\nuse of the mark by the licensee inures to the owner, as the latter\u201fs continuous use, in terms of\n<a href=\"/doc/641855/\" id=\"a_336\">Section 48</a> of the Trademarks Act. Typically, therefore when a trade vendor or establishment\nsells branded goods, it is incorrect to state that the income earned by the vendor is generated\nfrom the brand or mark, associated with the product, rather than from sale of the product itself.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_380\">It would be incorrect therefore, to conclude that income earned is not from the sale of the\nproduct, but from marketing intangibles connected with the reputation of the mark, though that\nreputation guarantees a high demand for the product, from which the seller benefits. Likewise,\nin the case of distribution, a distribution agent is under an agreement with the manufacturer to\nsell its good; it also possesses the right to advertise the goods and brands of the manufacturer.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_381\">                                                IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                      IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                               IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_226\">                                           Page 175 of 331</span>\n\nThis implies a license of the manufacturer\u201fs trademark. In such an event, the distributor need\nnot pay for the right to use the intellectual property under which the goods are sold; he merely\npays for obtaining the exclusive commercial right to sell the goods he buys from the\nmanufacturer for enabling onward sale.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_382\">68. Para 10.1 of OECD commentary on <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_337\">Article 12</a> of the Model Convention states that\npayments solely made in consideration for obtaining the exclusive distribution rights of a\nproduct or service in a given territory are not royalty, since the resident distributor does not\npay for the right to use the trade name or trade mark under which the products are sold but\nmerely obtains the exclusive right to sell in his state of residence the product that he is agreeing\nto buy from the manufacturer; such payments will be characterized as business income.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_383\">69. It is relevant here to consider the conditions in the RPC and the ALA, which FOWC entered\ninto with Jaypee. Under recital B to the RPC, FOWC clearly has the exclusive right to exploit\nthe commercial rights in the championship and to award Jaypee the right to host, stage and\npromote F1 Grand Prix events. Clause 17, states that FOWC would through reasonable\nendeavors ensure that at least sixteen cars participate in the Event. Clause 18 forbids Jaypee\nfrom making any audio or visual image of the event;\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_384\">W.P.(C) 10307/2016, W.P.(C) 9509/2016 &amp; W.P.(C) 10145/2016 Page 58 likewise there are\nrestrictions in clause 19 of the RPC. Clause 23.2 permits FOWC to make incidental use of I.P.\nrights solely for the limited purpose of facilitating Jaypee to promote the event. Under the RPC\nJaypee remains liable to pay the full contractual amount in the year of termination and in the\nsubsequent year, the right to use trademarks, logos and IP Rights ceases instantly, the moment\ntermination takes place. This is a strong pointer to the fact that the amounts payable by Jaypee\nto FOWC under the RPC are really for the privilege of hosting and staging the championship\nrace and not for the IP rights, which in any event, could be utilized by it only to promote the\nrace and for no other purpose.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_385\">70. Clauses 23.3 and 23.4 of the RPC are to be read in conjunction with the ALA. The ALA\ndoes not confer any additional rights, neither was a license nor any form of right to use the\n                                                IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                      IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                               IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_227\">                                          Page 176 of 331</span>\n\ntrademark given to Jaypee by FOWC which resulted in royalty payment within the meaning of\n<a href=\"/doc/134715/\" id=\"a_338\">Article 13</a> of the DTAA.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_386\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"95221080\" id=\"span_228\">71. The judgment in <a href=\"/doc/95221080/\" id=\"a_339\">Director of Income Tax v. Ericsson A.B.</a> [2012] 343 ITR 470 (Del), held\nthat a lump-sum payment which is not based on or connected with the extent of the user of the\nIP rights would not constitute &quot;royalties&quot; within the meaning of the DTAA. In the present case,\nthe payments made under the RPC were separate lump-sum amounts in respect of the three\nseparate race events held in each of the three (3) years 2011 to 2013. It is not a payment, which\nis based on either the number of tickets sold or the total amount of revenue earned by Jaypee\nin each of the said years or indeed on any other measure. In the present case, the lump sum\npayable to FOWC by Jaypee would likewise, not constitute royalties. Importantly, recital (B)\nof the ALA specifically stated that FOWC wished to grant a license to Jaypee permitting only\nthe incidental use of certain IP rights and artwork &quot;solely for the limited purpose of facilitating\nthe hosting, staging and promotion of the event&quot;. The definition of &quot;permitted use&quot; in clause\n1.1 of the ALA states that it means only the incidental use of the licensed marks and materials\n&quot;for the purpose of hosting, staging or promoting the event, but for the avoidance of doubt, not\nto include use for any merchandising or other products or services whatsoever, whether\ndistributed free of charge or for sale&quot;. Clauses 2.2 and 6.2 of the ALA provide that the ALA will\ncontinue only until the RPC terminates or expires; Clause 2.3 of the ALA W.P.(C) 10307/2016,\nW.P.(C) 9509/2016 &amp; W.P.(C) 10145/2016 Page 59 prohibits Jaypee from using any of the\nlicensed marks, or as part of the name of the circuit, any corporate name, any domain name,\nwebsite address or other URL identification or equivalent used in association with Jaypee.\nJaypee thus has no IP rights whatsoever independently of the staging and hosting of the event.\nThe undertakings given by Jaypee, set out in Clauses 3.1(e) and 3.6 of the ALA too reinforce\nthis conclusion. These are strong indications that the parties did not intend, through the RPC\nand the ALA, to license the trademark. Thus, it does not amount to royalty under the DTAA.\nThe court is in agreement with FOWC\u201fs position on the definition of &quot;royalty&quot; as set out in the\nsecond Explanation to <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_340\">Section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the Act; it is significantly broader than the definition\nof &quot;royalties&quot; set out in <a href=\"/doc/654960/\" id=\"a_341\">Article 13(3)</a> of the India-UK DTAA. The definition in the Act\n                                               IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                              IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_229\">                                          Page 177 of 331</span>\n\nspecifically covers and includes lump-sum payments, whereas <a href=\"/doc/654960/\" id=\"a_342\">Article 13(3)</a> of the DTAA only\nrefers to payments.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_387\">72. The impugned order neither contains any discussion nor finding whatsoever on this crucial\nissue even though it goes to the root of the entire case. The entire tenor of the agreement- even\nthe main purpose of the RPC, is not for the grant of trademark rights or privilege or license to\nuse. The payments made to it under the RPC are not &quot;royalty&quot; either under the Act or the DTAA,\nthey most certainly are not for the use of trademarks or IP rights, but rather for the grant of\nthe privilege of staging, hosting and promoting the Event at the promoter&#x27;s racing circuit in\nNoida(NCR). FOWC under the RPC, made available to Jaypee all of the elements which\nconstitute the event. In particular, this includes nominating (to FIA) the promoter&#x27;s event for\ninclusion in the official F1 racing calendar; after such inclusion the F1 racing teams with their\nF1 cars and drivers were bound to participate in Jaypee&#x27;s event held at the Promoter&#x27;s racing\ncircuit, strictly in conformity with the requirements of the F1 Sporting and Technical\nRegulations and the FIA Sporting Code. Therefore, the grant of F1 rights by the FOWC to\nJaypee is merely incidental to the hosting and staging of the event by it, and this is clear from\nthe fact that the use of rights by Jaypee has been strictly confined and limited to use only for\nthe promotion of the event, and for no other purpose and in no other manner whatsoever. &quot;\n</p>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_241\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"95221080\" id=\"span_230\">145. But in the instant case, assessee is not involved in advertisement activities of GIL\n\nand for same reasons as discussed in the foregoing para, we are of the view that since\n\nthe facts are not similar to the facts of the appellant&#x27;s case, the <a href=\"/doc/95221080/\" id=\"a_343\">ratio <a href=\"/doc/95221080/\" id=\"a_344\">laid down in</a></a> this\n\ncase would not apply to the present case.</span>\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_242\">146. With regard to the bonafide belief of the appellant we find that the agreements\n\nrelating to Google AdWord Program i.e., Google AdWord Distributor Agreement and\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_231\">                                       Page 178 of 331</span>\n\nService Agreement were prepared in such a way to give a different colour of transaction,\n\nwhereas under the Google AdWord Program, the Appellant is required to sell the ad\n\nspace to the Advertiser with the technical support onsale and post sale and the technical\n\nsupport to the Advertisers are not possible without the use of technical know-how, IPR,\n\nderivative workss, intangibles, information and copyrights owned by the GIL or its\n\nparent holdings. Since the appellant has access to technical know-how, IPRs, derivative\n\nworkss, intangibles, information and copyrights of the GIL or its parent holdings, the\n\npayment made in lieu of that is certainly a payment of royalty on which appellant is\n\nrequired to deduct TDS. These facts were known to the appellant since the execution of\n\nboth the agreements. Therefore, it cannot be said that appellant was under the bonafide\n\nbelief for non-dedcution of TDS. The facts in the case of Kotak Securities Ltd.(supra),\n\nare different as in that case the appellant was under bonafide belief with regard to nature\n\nof payments as no dispute was ever raised by the Revenue in earlier years. But in the\n\ninstant case, the appellant was aware of the nature of payments since the execution of\n\nagreements at different points of time as to what kind of services were required to be\n\nprovided by the appellant to the GIL under the Google AdWord Program and the\n\nRevenue has disputed the nature of claim raised by the appellant since beginning.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_243\">Therefore, the ratio laid down in the case of Kotak Securities will not apply to the present\n\nfacts of the case.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_244\">                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_232\">                                       Page 179 of 331</span>\n\n\n\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_245\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1594163\" id=\"span_233\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"26945525\" id=\"span_234\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"319514\" id=\"span_235\">147. We have also carefully examined the judgment of the Apex Court in the case of\n\nGE India Technology Cen. <a href=\"/doc/319514/\" id=\"a_345\">Pvt. Ltd., Vs. CIT</a> 327 <a href=\"/doc/26945525/\" id=\"a_346\">ITR 456, CIT Vs. Eli Lilly and Co.,\n\n(India) Pvt. Ltd.</a>, (<a href=\"/doc/1594163/\" id=\"a_347\">supra) and Transmission Corporation of AP Ltd., Vs. CIT</a> (supra), in\n\nwhich it has been repeatedly held that liability of deducting tax at source is in the nature\n\nof vicarious liability which pre-supposes existence of primary liability, the said liability\n\nis a vicarious liability and the principal liability is of the person who is\n\ntaxable. It was also observed in those judgments that the plea of bonafide\n\nbelief cannot be taken for non-deducting of TDS when it is clear that TDS is required\n\nto be deducted as per <a href=\"/doc/1329583/\" id=\"a_348\">section 195</a> of the Act. The plea of bonafide belief can only be\n\nconsidered while adjudicating the issue of penalty to be levied under <a href=\"/doc/18164608/\" id=\"a_349\">section 271C</a> of the\n\nAct.</span></span></span>\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_246\">148. In the case of GE IndiaTechnology Cen. Pvt. Ltd., (supra), their Lordship of Apex\n\nCourt have held that <a href=\"/doc/1329583/\" id=\"a_350\">section 195</a> imposes a statutory obligation on any person\n\nresponsible for paying to a non-resident any interest (not being interest on securities) or\n\nany other sum (not being dividend) chargeable under the provisions of the Act to deduct\n\nincome tax at the rate in force unless is liable to pay income tax thereon as an agent. It\n\nwas further held that payment to non-resdients by way of royalty and payment for\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_236\">                                       Page 180 of 331</span>\n\ntechnical services rendered in India are common examples for sums chargeable under\n\nthe provisions of <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_351\">Income Tax Act</a>, to which the aforesaid requirement of tax deduction\n\nat source applies. The tax so collected and deducted is required to be paid to the credit\n\nof Central Government in terms of <a href=\"/doc/609896/\" id=\"a_352\">section 200</a> r.w.r. 30 of the Income Tax Rules, 1962.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_388\">We have carefully examined the facts of the instant case in the light of various judicial\n\npronouncements referred here in above and the AdWord Distributor Agreement and the\n\nService Agreement together and we find that under these agreements assessee was\n\nlicenced to use the trade marks, IPRs, brand features, derivative workss and other\n\nintangibles etc, though it may not be transferred in favour of the appellant.Therefore the\n\nconsideration paid by the assessee is on account of usage of all these intangibles in order\n\nto provide better services to the GIL or to the advertisers is certainly in the nature of\n\npayment of royalty and is chargeable to tax under <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_353\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> of the Act and under\n\n<a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_354\">article 12</a> of the DTAA. Since the assessee has not deducted the tax at source as per\n\nprovisions of <a href=\"/doc/1329583/\" id=\"a_355\">section 195</a> of the Act, assessee was rightly held to be in default under\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1049794/\" id=\"a_356\">section 201(1)</a> of the Act. We therefore confirm the order of the CIT(A) upholding the\n\nordert of the AO passed under <a href=\"/doc/1049794/\" id=\"a_357\">section 201(1)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/613881/\" id=\"a_358\">201(1A)</a> of the Act in this regard.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_389\">149. IT(IT) A No. 1295/Bang/2014\n\n\n      Through this appeal, the revenue has assailed the order of CIT(Appeals) inter alia\n\non the following grounds:-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_247\">                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_237\">                                        Page 181 of 331</span>\n\n      &quot;1. The learned CIT (Appeals) has erred in partly allowing the appeal of the\n          assessee which is opposed to law, equity, facts and circumstances of the case.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_248\">      2. The learned CIT (A) erred in fact by not appreciating the Assessing Officer&#x27;s\n          conclusion based on the material available on the record that the Google\n          Ireland Limited (GIL) is not the beneficial owner of the Royalties paid by the\n          Google India Private Ltd(GIPL)\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_249\">      3. The learned CIT (A) erred in fact and law by holding that the M/s.Google\n          Ireland Limited (GIL) is the beneficial owner of the royalties paid by the GIPL\n          and the rate of TDS is at 10.556%.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_250\">      4. The learned CIT (A) erred in not appreciating the reliance of the AO in the\n          statement made under Oath before the Public Accounts Committee, House of\n          Commons, British Parliament by Mr. Matt Brittin of Google UK Ltd and the\n          various articles appearing in WWW,which shows that the transaction of\n          assessee company constitutes a colourable device.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_251\">      5. The learned CIT (A) erred by not considering the fact that onus is on the\n          assessee to establish with the reliable documentary evidence that the recipient\n          (GIL) is the beneficial owner of royalty.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_252\">      6. The learned CIT (A) has not appreciated the Assessing Officer&#x27;s reliance on the\n          agreements entered between the entities to decide the beneficial owner of the\n          Royalties which were neither produced before the AO nor the Ld.CIT(A).&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_390\">150. Though various grounds are raised, but they all relate to the issue as to whether\n\nGIL is the beneficial owner of the payment received in the form of royalty.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_391\">150. We have carefully examined the orders of the lower authorities and we find that\n\nthe AO has taxed the royalty @ 10.56% (including education cess and surcharge) under\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_238\">                                       Page 182 of 331</span>\n\n<a href=\"/doc/279820/\" id=\"a_359\">section 115A</a> of the Act having rejected the contention of the assessee that GIL is the\n\nbeneficial owner of the royalty and royalty will be taxed at 10% as per <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_360\">Article 12</a> of\n\nDTAA.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_392\">151. The assessment order was challenged before the CIT(Appeals) in AY 2013-14\n\nand the CIT(Appeals) has accepted the contention of the assessee and has held that GIL,\n\nthe recipient of royalty, is the beneficial owner, therefore royalty will be taxed at 10%\n\nonly; and this order of CIT(Appeals) is challenged before us.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_393\">152. In other succeeding years i.e. AYs 2014-15 &amp; 2015-16, the assessment order\n\ndenying the benefit of beneficial ownership to GIL was approved by the CIT(Appeals)\n\nand the assessee is in appeal before us in ITA no 949&amp; 958 /Bang/17 against the\n\nconfirmation of the order of the AO. Therefore, we have to adjudicate the issue in the\n\nlight of evidence placed before the lower authorities and also before us.                    While\n\ndisallowing the claim of assessee, the AO has examined the concept of beneficial\n\nownership and the surrounding circumstances and also in the light of tax residency\n\ncertificate issued by the revenue authorities to GIL. The AO has examined the concept\n\nof beneficial ownership in the light of UK Trust of Laws and UN Model of Convention\n\nand was of the view that the object of this concept is to identify the true owner of income\n\nor asset with ultimate rights of enjoyment. There is no definition available either under\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_239\">                                      Page 183 of 331</span>\n\nUN Model of Convention or under domestic tax laws of India. The AO further observed\n\nthat the concept of beneficial ownership was introduced in the tax treaties as a counter\n\nmeasure against the treaty shopping to confine bargain only to the contracting states\n\nwhich were intended to benefit from the treaty. The concept of beneficial ownership is\n\na tool to protect the bargain between the two contracting states. None of the country\n\nwould intend to relinquish its tax base without getting something back for it. The\n\nbeneficial owner is used to confine the bargain to the parties who were intended to\n\nbenefit from it.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_394\">153. The AO further noticed in India-Ireland treaty that the treaty does not use the\n\ngeneral term &#x27;owner&#x27;, but uses specific term &#x27;beneficial owner&#x27;. Therefore treaty intends\n\nto give benefit of withholding tax at the reduced rates only to the person who can be\n\nloosely described as a &#x27;final&#x27; owner of income. The concept of final owner of income\n\ncan be elaborated with the help of attributes of ownership of income. The AO further\n\nobserved that income ownership has several attributes such as right to possess, use or\n\nmanage income, power to alienate and ability to consume waste or destroy, the risk of\n\ndepreciation and hope of appreciation. It is possible to split these attributes among\n\ndifferent persons by entering into a legal or contractual arrangement to avail benefit of\n\nthe favourable treaty without losing ownership of income. Therefore, beneficial owner\n\nis the one who has more attributes of ownership of income than others. He further placed\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_240\">                                       Page 184 of 331</span>\n\nreliance upon the explanation by Charles Du Toit describing that beneficial owner is the\n\nperson whose ownership attributes outweighs those of any other person. Besides he has\n\nalso placed a reliance on the Bulletin for International Taxation, Vol. 64 (2010) no.10,\n\npages 500 - 509.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_395\">154. During the course of assessment proceedings, the AO has asked the assessee to\n\nfurnish evidence in support of its claim that GIL is the beneficial owner of the royalties\n\npayable by GIPL. In response thereto, the assessee has filed a Tax Residency\n\nCertification (TRC) issued by the revenue authorities of Ireland and the financial\n\nstatements of GIL for the year ending 31.12.2012. Reliance was also placed upon the\n\nstatement of Finance Minister on 01.03.2013 in which it was clarified that TRC produced\n\nby the resident of the contracting state will be accepted as evidence that the person is a\n\nresident of that contracting state and the income-tax authorities will not go behind the\n\nTRC to question the resident status of that person. The AO was not convinced with the\n\nexplanation furnished by the assessee and he observed that TRC of GIL has never been\n\nquestioned. The GIPL was asked to prove whether GIPL is the beneficial owner of\n\namounts payable by GIPL under <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_361\">Article 12</a> of the treaty. The AO further observed that\n\nTRC is essential to prove that person is a resident of a contracting state in order to claim\n\nthe treaty benefits. He has also examined the statement of Hon&#x27;ble Finance Minister on\n\nthe proposed insertion of <a href=\"/doc/110992633/\" id=\"a_362\">section 90A(5)</a> and noted that TRC certifies that you are a\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_241\">                                      Page 185 of 331</span>\n\nresident, but it does not certify you are a beneficial owner. The AO has also examined\n\nthe different layers of holdings involved in these transactions. The legal owner of\n\nIntellectual Property (IP) in the Adword program is Google Inc. USA , which has in turn\n\ngiven the rights to use the IP in the Adwords program to the group entity Google Ireland\n\nHoldings (GIH). GIH in turn by license agreement given the rights to use the Adwords\n\nprogram to Google Netherlands Holding, BV (GNHBV), a Netherlands based\n\nsubsidiary, for which GNHBV pays royalty to GIH. GNHBV in turn by license\n\nagreement has given the rights to use the Adwords program to GIL for which GIL pays\n\nroyalty to GNHBV. The AO has depicted the relationship diagram in his order and for\n\nthe sake of reference, we extract the same hereunder:-\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_253\">                                   Google In. USA\n\n\n                               Google Ireland Holdings\n\n\n                             Google Netherlands Holdings\n                                         BV\n\n\n\n                               Google Ireland Limited\n\n\n                               Google India Private Ltd\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_242\">                                      Page 186 of 331</span>\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_396\">155. The AO accordingly asked the assessee to furnish (1) the agreement between\n\nGNHBV and GIL, (2) the agreement between GIH and GNHBV, and (3) the agreement\n\nbetween Google Inc. and GIH; but the assessee did not furnish the copies of agreement\n\nbefore the AO and the AO observed that it would be difficult to accept the claim that\n\nGIL is the beneficial owner of royalties payable by GIPL to GIL as the terms and\n\nconditions under which GIL was given the licensing rights over Adwords program is not\n\nknown. He further observed that GIL is an operational company, but this fact alone is\n\nnot sufficient to state that GIL is the beneficial owner of the amounts paid/payable by\n\nGIPL. In the absence of these agreements, the AO has examined the various material on\n\nthe Google group in the World Wide Web in order to understand the relationship\n\nbetween different entities of the Google group. He has also examined the Nineteenth\n\nreport of Session 2012-13 of House of Commons, Committee of Public Accounts in\n\nwhich the Committee has recorded the explanation furnished by Google and the\n\nstatement of Mr. Matt Brittin, Google&#x27;s Vice President for Sales &amp; Operations in\n\nNorthern &amp; Central Europe, summoned by Public Accounts Committee and took note\n\nthat profits derived by GIL are transferred to entity GIH which is controlled and managed\n\nfrom Bermuda, a tax haven. The relevant observations of the AO are extracted hereunder\n\nfor the sake of reference:-\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_254\">      &quot;The transactions of Google UK Ltd in relation to distribution of Adword program\n      in UK were analysed by the Public Accounts Committee, House of Common of the\n                                       IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                        IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                      IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_243\">                                  Page 187 of 331</span>\n\nUnited Kingdom. Mr. Matt Brittin, Google&#x27;s Vice President for Sales and\nOperations in Northern and Central Europe, was summoned by the Public Account\nCommittee and his statements were recorded under Oath. The deposition of the Chie\nExecutive Officer of Google UK Ltd forms part of the report of the House of\nCommons Committee of Public Accounts, FIMRC Annual Report 2011-12,\nNineteenth Report of Session 2012-13, dated 28th Nov 2012 which is available on\nthe WWW. The relevant part of the report are reproduced as under:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_255\">   10. Google explained in its responses that it minimised tax within the letter of\n   the law and that low tax areas or tax havens influenced where it located its\n   group companies. Goodie makes money from business to business advertising, adverts\n   which can be targeted to the UK website and to UK Google users. Goodie\n   also confirmed that it had an entity based in          Bermuda to protect its\n   intellectual property. We consider that the company undermined its own\n   argument since it remits its non-USA profits (including from the UK) not to\n   the USA but to Bermuda and therefore may be depriving the USA of legitimate\n   tax revenue as well as the UK. Subsequently, Google told us that there were\n   no outstanding issues with HMRC about Google UK&#x27;s accounts. HMRC is\n                                       IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                        IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                      IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_244\">                                  Page 188 of 331</span>\n\ncurrently carrying out a review of the tax returns filed by Google.
