How much is\nsitting in Bermuda?\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_405\">Matt Brittin: I do not know the number, but it is true that Bermuda is part of\nour operations, and the reason is that when an international company sets up\noperations outside your domestic market, which in our case is the US, you\nlook for where to locate your operations. Within the European Union, we\nchose to locate in Ireland, for the reasons I have explained, but you also need\nto protect your intellectual property, and to set up operations in countries\naround the world to do that. We have an entity in Bermuda to do that.\n..........................\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_406\">                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_245\">                                        Page 189 of 331</span>\n\n      Q545 Austin Mitchell: What cannot be fiddled through is paid at 12.5%, but\n      the 12.5% is not paid on most of it. As Fiona said, it does not benefit them--they cannot get\n      their hands on it because 30% tax would have to be paid for it to be\n      repatriated to America, to the shareholders. Could you provide information about how your shareholders benefit from\n      the Bermuda holdings? The relevant parts of the report are reproduced as under:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_257\">      ...............\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_258\">      Q143 Stephen Barclay: On that point, where is the intellectual property\n      owned on the work those engineers are doing?\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_259\">                                    IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                     IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                          IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                   IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_246\">                               Page 190 of 331</span>\n\nMatt Brittin: I think it depends on the specific projects that they are working\non. The intellectual property for Google is licensed, as you know and referred\nto earlier, to Google Ireland, in order that Google Ireland can sell that aspect\nof the product that is saleable--the advertising platform.\nQ144 Stephen Barclay: So some of the intellectual property is owned in\nIreland on that work?\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_260\">Matt Brittin: Ireland have the right to sell those products.\nQ145 Stephen Barclay: Is some of it also owned in Bermuda?\nMatt Brittin: Yes.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_261\">.......................\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_262\">Q150 Stephen Barclay: Right. What tax rate do you pay on transfers from\nIreland to Bermuda?\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_263\">Matt Brittin: I don&#x27;t think we pay a tax rate on Ireland to Bermuda. We talked\nabout Bermuda in the last hearing, and I confirmed that we do use Bermuda.\nObviously, Bermuda is a low-tax environment.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_264\">Q151 Stephen Barclay: Sure. So, what, you just transfer from Ireland to\nBermuda without any tax implications?\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_265\">Matt Brittin: I would need to check, but I think that is my understanding.\n.........................\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_266\">Q150, 151. What tax rate do you pay on transfers from Ireland to\nBermuda/Do you just transfer from Ireland to Bermuda without any tax\nimplications?\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_267\">We do not make direct transfers of royalty payments or other payments\nsubject to withholding tax from Ireland (ie, Google Ireland Ltd. or &quot;GIL&quot;) to\nBermuda (ie, Google Ireland Holdings or &quot;GIH&quot;). Rather, we make transfers\nfrom GIL to Google Netherlands Holdings BV (&quot;GNBV&quot;), and from GNBV to\nGIH. No withholding taxes are payable on these transfers.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_268\">                                          IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                           IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                         IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_247\">                                     Page 191 of 331</span>\n\n      Our use of GNBV as part of our corporate structure is a legacy from when\n      we set up our international business over a decade ago. We structured it that\n      way because of concerns about potential Irish withholding taxes. As\n      mentioned in our previous testimony we do not believe that our use of GNBV\n      has a material effect on royalty payments or other payments subject to\n      withholding tax from GIL, primarily because of changes made in 2010 to Irish\n      tax laws and regulations.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_269\">      .....................\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_270\">      Q154 Stephen Barclay: The crux of my point is that you are depriving the US\n      taxpayer of significant funds, because this is economic activity in the US, and\n      technology developed in the US, which I presume was then sold to an\n      operation in Bermuda, probably in 2001 or 2002. The tax you save on that\n      deprives the US taxpayer of significant sums. The significance to us in the UK\n      is that the tax you save on those non-US profits is then used for a competitive\n      distortion to allow you, in essence, to compete against UK companies,\n      because you have a huge amount of profit on which you are not paying tax.\n      Matt Brittin: No, I don&#x27;t think that is right. The money that goes to Bermuda\n      can be used for funding our growth outside the US, so that money can be used\n      for R and D expenses, acquisitions and investment in data centres or\n      buildings. In fact, since we met last time, we have committed \u00a31 billion to\n      investment in King&#x27;s Cross to increase our presence in the UK, and we could\n      fund that from Bermuda. Thousands of companies, including many British\n      companies, use arrangements such as ours in Bermuda, which allow us to\n      invest in the growth of our business outside the US. Large sums are spent on\n      R and D, acquisitions, investments in buildings and, as I have mentioned, we\n      have just committed to King&#x27;s Cross.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_271\">6.2      It is evident from the Report of the Public Accounts Committee of the House\nof Commons that the profits derived by GIL are transferred to the entity GIH which\n                                          IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                           IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                         IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_248\">                                     Page 192 of 331</span>\n\nis controlled and managed in Bermuda, a tax haven. Even in the case of India the\nrevenue earned by GIPL on sale of advertising space are routed through the same\nstructure and the profits finally end up with GIH which is controlled and managed\nin Bermuda. The structure has been used by Google in order to avoid tax.\n6.3      The fact that the profits from advertising services end in the tax haven\nBermuda also appears in the discussion between the one of the Directors of OECD\nand the Irish Legislators     in the WVVW in           the   link                      http:/\noireachtasdebates.oireachtas.ie/debates%20authoring/DebatesWebPack.nsf/comm\nitteetakes/FI52013072300005?opendocument. This link is regarding Global\nTaxation Architecture: Discussion with Director of the OECD Centre for Tax Policy\nand Administration Mr. Pascal Saint-Amans and the Irish legislators. At page 9 in\nthe link, the Irish Legislator Deputy Richard Boyd Barett describes the term\n&quot;Double Irish&quot; sandwich in relation to Google which is reproduced below for\nreference:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_272\">      Deputy Richard Boyd Barrett: Yes; it is double Dutch. The only explanation\n      Mr. Saint-Amans seems to be giving is that our low corporate tax rate seems\n      to attract companies involved in this aggressive tax avoidance, For the\n      benefit of peopletrying to understand this situation, is the following\n      briefdescription, given to me by former Google workers, an accurate\n      description of how Ireland functions as a facilitator of tax avoidance?\n      Google&#x27;s advertising is a digital product. They sell that product to customers\n      in Europe, the Middle East, and Africa from their Dublin HQ - and as such\n      don&#x27;t have to pay taxes on that anywhere except Ireland because the sale of\n      that good is deemedunderinternationallawtohavetakenplaceinIreland.\n      The profit they make from all those advertising sales is eaten up by a charge\n      levied by Google Ireland Limited&#x27;s holding company - which (conveniently)\n      tends to be almost the exact amount of profit they make. The little bit that is\n      left is charged at the standard 12.5%.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_273\">                                          IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                           IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                         IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_249\">                                     Page 193 of 331</span>\n\n      The holding company&#x27;s profits aren&#x27;t taxed because of Irish law waiving\n      corporation tax on certain profits made from intellectual property royalties\n      and/or because the company isn&#x27;t managed or controlled in Ireland. That\n      money then tends to be routed through further subsidiaries in Holland before\n      moving to the Caribbean.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_274\">6.4      Relevant part of the article Ireland&#x27;s For Sale Sign: The 12.5% Corporation\nTax Rate by Donald Brennan appearing in the Irish Left Review Journal, January\n2013 wherein the Double Irish structure used by Google is explained, is reproduced\nbelow forreference:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_275\">      In effect it requires the creation of two Irish holding companies. A US\n      corporation has the ability to own &#x27;non-US&#x27; intellectual property, in an Irish\n      company which is not an Irish tax resident. As the company is incorporated\n      in Ireland, it&#x27;s seen as Irish from a US point view, but from Irish Revenue&#x27;s\n      perspective it is a foreign company and therefore not subject to withholding\n      tax. This conveniently ignores the fact that the non-resident Irish company is\n      in a tax haven like the Cayman Islands or Bermuda. It then enters into a\n      licensing agreement with an Irish incorporated and Irish tax-resident\n      company (the second holding company), which from Irish revenue&#x27;s point of\n      view is subject to corporation tax. This second holding company acts as the\n      EMEA hub where, nominally,pass through. However, for the money to flow\n      to the Cayman Islands or Bermuda it requires the use of the Netherlands. In\n      the Netherland&#x27;s another company is created called Netherlands Holdings\n      B.V, for example.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_276\">      The case of Google is perhaps the best known, although the structure exists\n      for many, if not potentially all companies. We know, for example, that the two\n      &#x27;Irish&#x27; holding companies are Google Ireland Holdings and Google Ireland\n      Limited. So, when a French, Egyptian or Indian customer buys services from\n      Google (such as Adwords or Adsense) their credit card is debited to a bank\n                                    IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                     IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                          IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                   IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_250\">                               Page 194 of 331</span>\n\naccount located in Dublin and appears as a sale in Google Ireland Limited.\nWhile Google Ireland Limited takes the sale, it has to &#x27;buy&#x27; the rights for the\nGoogle algorithm or Google trademark from Google Ireland Holdings, which\nis nominally in Bermuda. However, rather than the money moving directly to\nBermuda it goes to a holding company in the Netherlands, Google\nNetherlands Holdings B.V, from which it is passed hack to Google Ireland\nHoldings. The Netherland&#x27;s entity, according to Bloomberg, &quot;pays out about\n99.8 percent of what it collects to the Bermuda entity, company filings\nshow.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_410\">This odd structure and the way it is treated by Irish revenue is put in place\nbecause of the perspective of US tax law. As Prof Edward Kleinbard put it in\na 2071 academic paper published in the Florida Tax Review:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_277\">     &quot;...from a US tax point of view, neither Ireland Limited nor Google\n     BV exists at all. The United States sees only an Irish (not Bermuda)\n     company (Irish Holdings) with a Bermuda branch, where most of its\n     net income comes to rest.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_411\">It&#x27;s important to remember that while we are mentioning geographical places\nno physical movement actually occurs. As the Bloomberg report showed the\nAmsterdam-based subsidiary lists no employees. Similarly, Goggle Ireland\nHoldings has no employees.. It&#x27;s a registered company in Bermuda: number\n4531144 and is owned by Google Bermuda Unlimited. According to the\nFrench investigative magazine OWNI, &quot;Google Bermuda only consists of a\nmailbox held by Conyers Dill &amp; Pearman, a company which specializes in\noffshore arrangements. The firm is composed of several local business\nlawyers, working on behalf of multinational companies interested in this tax\nhaven&#x27;s benefits&quot; 45. Its unlimited status is important, as in Irish law this\nmeans that it doesn&#x27;t have to publish its accounts. Google Bermuda Unlimited\nowns Google Ireland Holdings also an unlimited company, which owns\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_251\">                                     Page 195 of 331</span>\n\n      Google Ireland Limited and Google Europe. However, the &#x27;Bermuda&#x27;\n      company Google Ireland Holdings and Irish company Google Ireland\n      Limited are both registered at the office of a certain Dublin solicitor. Google\n      Ireland Holdings has to be registered in Ireland for it to be considered under\n      US tax law as an &#x27;Irish company&#x27;.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_412\">6.5      Reference is drawn to the Article by Jesse Drucker dated Oct 21, 2010\nappearing in the website Bloomberg Technology, the extracts of which are\nreproduced below: Google Inc. cut its taxes by $3.1 billion (in the last three years\nusing a technique that moves most of its foreign profits through Ireland and the\nNetherlands to Bermuda.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_413\">Google&#x27;s income shifting -- involving strategies known to lawyers as the &quot;Double\nIrish&quot; and the &quot;Dutch Sandwich&quot; -- helped reduce its overseas tax rate to 2.4\npercent, the lowest of the top five U.S. technology companies by market\ncapitalization, according to regulatory filings in six countries.\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_278\">&quot;It&#x27;s remarkable that Google&#x27;s effective rate is that low,&quot; said Martin A. Sullivan, a\ntax economist who formerly worked for the U.S. Treasury Department. &quot;We know\nthis company operates throughout the world mostly in high-tax countries where the\naverage corporate rate is well over 20 percent.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_414\">      ..................\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_415\">The earnings wind up in island havens that levy no corporate income taxes at all.\nCompanies that use the Double Irish arrangement avoid taxes at home and abroad\nas the U.S. government struggles to close a projected $1.4 trillion budget gap and\nEuropean Union countries face a collective projected deficit of 868 billion euros.\nCountless Companies\n.................\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_416\">Transfer Pricing\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_252\">                                       Page 196 of 331</span>\n\nThe tactics of Google and Facebook depend on &quot;transfer pricing,&quot; paper\ntransactions among corporate subsidiaries that allow for allocating income to tax\nhavens while attributing expenses to higher-tax countries              ...........\n..............\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_417\">The Double Irish\nAs a strategy for limiting taxes, the Double Irish method is &quot;very common at the\nmoment, particularly with companies with intellectual property,&quot; said Richard\nMurphy, director of U.K.- based Tax Research LLP. Murphy, who has worked on\nsimilar transactions, estimates that hundreds of multinationals use some version of\nthe method.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_418\">....................\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_419\">Boosting Earnings\n..................\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_420\">Taxpayer Funding\n...................\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_421\">Arm&#x27;s Length\nIncome shifting commonly begins when companies like Google sell or license the\nforeign rights to intellectual property developed in the U.S. to a subsidiary in a low-\ntax country. That means foreign profits based on the technology get attributed to the\noffshore unit, not the parent. Under U.S. tax rules, subsidiaries must pay &quot;arm&#x27;s\nlength&quot; prices for the rights -- or the amount an unrelated company would.\nBecause the payments contribute to taxable income, the parent company has an\nincentive to set them as low as possible. Cutting the foreign subsidiary&#x27;s expenses\neffectively shifts profits overseas.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_422\">After three years of negotiations, Google received approval from the IRS in 2006\nfor its transfer pricing arrangement, according to filings with the Securities and\nExchange Commission.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_423\">                                       IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                        IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                      IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_253\">                                  Page 197 of 331</span>\n\nThe IRS gave its consent in a secret pact known as an advanced pricing agreement.\nGoogle wouldn&#x27;t discuss the price set under the arrangement, which licensed the\nrights to its search and advertising technology and other intangible property for\nEurope, the Middle East and Africa to a unit called Google Ireland Holdings,\naccording to a person familiar with the matter.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_424\">Dublin Office\nThat licensee in turn owns Google Ireland Limited, which employs almost 2,000\npeople in silvery glass office building in central Dublin, a block from the city&#x27;s\nGrand Canal. The Dublin subsidiary sells advertising globally and was credited by\nGoogle with 88 percent o, its $12.5 billion in non-U.S. sales in 2009.\nAllocating the revenue to Ireland helps Google avoid income taxes in the U.S.,\nwhere most of its technology was developed. The arrangement also reduces the\ncompany&#x27;s liabilities in relatively high-tax European countries where many of its\ncustomers are located.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_425\">The profits don&#x27;t stay with the Dublin subsidiary, which reported pretax income of\nless than 1 percent of sales in 2008, according to Irish records. That&#x27;s largely\nbecause it paid $5.4 billion in royalties to Google Ireland Holdings, which has its\n&quot;effective centre of management&quot; in Bermuda, according to company filings.\nLaw Firm Directors\n7 This Bermuda-managed entity is owned by a pair of Google subsidiaries that list\nas their directors two attorneys and a manager at Conyers Dill &amp; Pearman, a\nHamilton, Bermuda law firm.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_426\">Tax planners call such an arrangement a Double Irish because it relies on two Irish\ncompanies. One pays royalties to use intellectual property, generating expenses that\nreduce Irish taxable income. The second collects the royalties in a tax haven like\nBermuda, avoiding Irish taxes.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_427\">To steer clear of an Irish withholding tax, payments from Google&#x27;s Dublin unit don&#x27;t\ngo directly to Bermuda. A brief detour to the Netherlands avoids that liability,\n                                        IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                         IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                       IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_254\">                                   Page 198 of 331</span>\n\nbecause Irish tax law exempts certain royalties to companies in other EU- member\nnations. The fees first go to a Dutch unit, Google Netherlands Holdings B. V., which\npays out about 99.8 percent of what it collects to the Bermuda entity, company\nfilings show. The Amsterdam-based subsidiary lists no employees.\nThe Dutch Sandwich\nInserting the Netherlands stopover between two other units gives rise to the &quot;Dutch\nSandwich&quot; nickname.\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_279\">&quot;The sandwich leaves no tax behind to taste,&quot; said Murphy of Tax Research LLP.\n......................\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_280\">   Getting Profits Out\n   &quot;You accumulate profits within Ireland, but then you get them out of the\n   country relatively easily,&quot; Stewart said. &quot;And you do it by using Bermuda.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_428\">   ...........................\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_429\">   Once Google&#x27;s non-U.S. profits hit Bermuda, they become difficult to track.\n   The subsidiary managed there changed its legal form of organization in 2006\n   to became a so-called unlimited liability company. Under Irish rules, that\n   means it&#x27;s not required to disclose such financial information as income\n   statements or balance sheets.\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_281\">   &quot;Sticking an unlimited company in the group structure has become more\n   common in Ireland, largely to prevent disclosure,&quot; Stewart said.\n   Deferred Indefinitely\n   ................\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_282\">   Administration Concerned\n   ...................\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_283\">   To contact the reporter on this story: Jesse Drucker in New York at\n   jdrucker4@bloomberg.net.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_284\">                                       IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                        IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                      IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_255\">                                  Page 199 of 331</span>\n\n6.6    the above Article describes that Google uses the &quot;Double Irish sandwich&quot; in\norder to transfer the profits earned from advertising services to Bermuda which is\na tax haven. Google Ireland Holdings (&quot;GIH&quot;) which though registered in Ireland\nis controlled and managed from Bermuda and therefore the profits from sale of\nadvertising space through the Google Adwords Program lands up in Bermuda.\n6.7    It is apparent that Double Irish structure is used by Google to transfer the\nprofits from its advertising services arising in the different parts of the world,\nincluding India, to the tax-haven Bermuda. GIPL has not been able to substantiate\nthat GIL is the beneficial owner of the amounts payable even after numerous\nopportunities were given. GIPL was given an opportunity to defend its claim by\ngiving it an opportunity to furnish all relevant documents and agreements. These\nagreements would have helped in understanding whether GIL is the beneficial\nowner. However GIPL declined to furnish the same. Accordingly GIL cannot avail\nthe India-Ireland Treaty benefits and the Treaty rate of 10% cannot be applied in\nthe instant case.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_285\">6.8    The Judiciary in India has addressed the issue of beneficial ownership by\nfocusing on the issue of substance of the transaction and the entities involved. In a\nlandmark judgment in Aditya Birla Nuvo Ltd. V, Dy. DIT (International Taxation)\n[2012] 342 ITR 308, the Hon&#x27;ble Bombay High Court held that a Mauritius entity\nholding shares in the Indian company is not the &quot;beneficial owner&quot; but it was only\nthe US parent which is the &quot;beneficial owner&quot; of such shares. Accordingly benefits\nunder Indo-Mauritus tax treaty were denied on the basis of applying look through\napproach. The Court observed that the voting rights, rights of management and\nrights of sale or alienation of shares were vested with the US entity and hence the\nsame is the &quot;beneficial owner&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_430\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"368282\" id=\"span_256\">6.9    The Hon`ble AAR dealt with the issue of beneficial ownership in the case of\n<a href=\"/doc/368282/\" id=\"a_363\">Nat West Securities B.V. v. Dy. CIT</a> [2005] 1 SOT 503 (Mum.), X Ltd., In re [1996]\n220 ITR 377/86 Taxman 252 (AAR-New Delhi). A British bank incorporated its\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_257\">                                      Page 200 of 331</span>\n\nsubsidiaries in Mauritius and routed investments into India through those\nsubsidiaries and claimed benefits of Indo Mauritius tax treaty against dividend\nincome etc. AAR observed that Mauritius subsidiaries are not beneficial owners of\nthe shares, AAR rejected application on the ground that transaction was designed\nto avoid taxes. The facts placed before the AAR led to the inference that the purpose\nof investment through subsidiaries was only for avoidance of taxes and availing\nlower tax rates through Indo-Mauritus tax treaty. As the substance of Mauritus\nentities could not be established, the same were not treated as beneficial owners of\nthe investments.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_431\">6.10 Therefore the Indian Judiciary has adopted the substance over form approach\nin relation to beneficial ownership. Based on this approach and relying on the\ntransfer pricing documentation for AY 2009-10, the statement made under Oath\nbefore the Public Accounts Committee, House of Commons, British Parliament by\nMr. Matt Brittin of Google UK Ltd and the various articles appearing in WWW it is\nseen that GIL is not the beneficial owner of the amounts paid/payable by GIPL. The\namounts paid/payable by GIPL to GIL end up in Gill which is controlled and\nmanaged in the tax haven Bermuda. Thus GIH is the beneficial owner of the amounts\npaid/payable by GIPL.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_432\">7       India does not have a Tax Treaty with Bermuda. Hence the rates under the\nAct will apply. <a href=\"/doc/149656697/\" id=\"a_364\">Section 206AA</a> of the Act requires the deductee to furnish the\nPermanent Account Number (&quot;PAN&quot;) to the deductor failing which the deductor\nshall deduct tax at source at higher of the following rates:\n</p><pre id=\"pre_4\">(i)     the rate prescribed in the Act;\n(ii)    at the rate in force i.e., the rate mentioned in the <a href=\"/doc/75703920/\" id=\"a_365\">Finance Act</a>; or\n(iii)   at the rate of 20 per cent.\n7.1     In the notice dated 21st Dec 2013 the assessee company GIPL was given an\n</pre><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_433\">opportunity to submit the Permanent Account Number (&quot;PAN&quot;) of GIH. In the\nsubmission dated 26th Dec 2013 it was stated by GIPL, that <a href=\"/doc/149656697/\" id=\"a_366\">Section 206AA</a> makes\n                                       IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                        IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                      IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_258\">                                  Page 201 of 331</span>\n\nit mandatory for the person who is entitled to receive the amount and not the\nbeneficial owner of the amount to furnish PAN to the deductor. It was further stated\nthat GIL is the person that is entitled to receive the amount as per the Distribution\nAgreement and since GIL has already obtained a PAN the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/149656697/\" id=\"a_367\">Section\n206AA</a> are not applicable.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_434\">7.2    According to GIPL the person referred in <a href=\"/doc/15081352/\" id=\"a_368\">Section 206AA(1)</a> &quot;any person\nentitled to receive any sum or amount on which tax is deductible ........&quot; cannot be\nequated with person who is the beneficial owner of the sum or amounts. However\nsuch understanding is based on the incorrect interpretation of the person referred\nin <a href=\"/doc/15081352/\" id=\"a_369\">Section 206AA(1)</a>.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_435\">7.3    In the instant case GIPL has not established that GIL is the person entitled\nto receive any sum or amount since as explained in the preceding paragraphs the\nsums received by GIL are transferred to the group entity GUI which is controlled\nand managed in Bermuda.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_436\">7.4    GIPL did not avail the opportunities given to furnish the agreement between\nGIL and GNHBV, agreement between GNHBV and GIH in order to substantiate its\nclaims that GIL has all the attributes of ownership over the sums paid/payable by\nGIPL. The Assessee company, GIPL has merely furnished the Financial statements\nof GIL to claim that GIL is the actual beneficiary of the sums paid/payable to it but\nthe agreements between GIL and GNHBV and GNHBV and GIH have not been\nfurnished. These agreements contain the terms and conditions under which GIL has\nbeen given the licensing rights over the Adwords program. These agreements also\ncontain the stipulations that decide the royalty payments that have to be made by\nGIL on the sale of Adwords programs. GIL under the agreement may be required to\ntransfer all the revenues from sale of Adwords programs to GNHBV after keeping\na small sum to meet the administrative expenses. Therefore the submissions of the\nfinancial statements of GIL the year ending December 2012 only shows that GIL is\nan operational company but it does not say anything with respect to the ownership\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_259\">                                       Page 202 of 331</span>\n\n      and control over the sums paid/payable by GIPL. Hence the claim of GIPL that GIL\n      is the beneficial owner of the sums paid/payable, is not acceptable and it is GIH\n      which is entitled to receive the sums from GIPL.&quot;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_437\">156. The AO has also examined the information available in public domain in this\n\nregard and finally concluded that GIL is not the beneficial owner of the royalty received\n\nfrom GIPL and accordingly taxed the royalty at 10.556%.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_438\">157. Aggrieved, the assessee preferred an appeal before the CIT(Appeals) and the\n\nCIT(A) being convinced with the contentions of the assessee, accepted the claim of\n\nassessee and held that GIL is the beneficial owner of the royalty received.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_439\">158. Now the revenue is before us and during the course of hearing, the ld. Standing\n\nCounsel has invited our attention to the Report of House of Commons, Committee of\n\nPublic Accounts, who have recorded the statement of Mr. Mett Brittin, VP of Google\n\nSales &amp; Operations, Northern &amp; Central Europe. The ld. Standing Counsel has also\n\ncontended that assessee was asked to furnish the agreements executed between GNHBV\n\n&amp; GIL, GIH &amp; GNHBV and Google Inc. and GIH, in order to understand that how much\n\nright was conferred upon the other companies through license agreement. Undisputedly,\n\nGoogle Adwords program is owned by Google Inc. USA. It was licensed by Google\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_260\">                                       Page 203 of 331</span>\n\nInc. to Google Ireland Holdings (GIH) which in turn was further licensed to Google\n\nNetherlands Holding BV (GNHBV) and in turn GNHBV licensed it to Google Ireland\n\nLtd. (GIL). Since there are four layers of holdings of this Adwords program, it is not\n\nclear how much right in license were conferred to different holdings and how the revenue\n\ncollected on Adwords program is to be distributed amongst the above holdings. The\n\nassessee has claimed that GIL is the beneficial owner of the payment of royalty, therefore\n\nonus is upon it to place evidence on record that out of the total revenue collected on\n\naccount of Adwords program, major share goes to GIL. But the assessee did not\n\ndischarge its obligation and raised a vague argument.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_440\">159. The ld. Standing Counsel further <a href=\"/doc/368282/\" id=\"a_370\">placed reliance upon</a> the Hon&#x27;ble Finance\n\nMinister&#x27;s clarification on Tax Residency Certificate (TRC) dated 01.03.2013 on which\n\nthe assessee has placed heavy reliance with the submission that through this clarification\n\nit has been made clear that TRC produced by a resident of contracting state will be\n\naccepted as evidence that he is a resident of that contracting state and income-tax\n\nauthorities in India will not go behind the TRC and question the resident status. He also\n\ninvited our attention to the clarification regarding the taxation of income from dividends\n\nand capital gain under Indo-Mauritius Double Tax Avoidance Convention (DTAC) with\n\nthe submission that the clarification was issued with regard to taxation from dividends\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_261\">                                       Page 204 of 331</span>\n\nand capital gains under Indo-Mauritius DTAC. Though it has been clarified that\n\nwherever certificate of residence is issued by Mauritius authorities, such certificate will\n\nconstitute sufficient evidence for accepting the status of residence as well as beneficial\n\nownership for applying the DTAC accordingly. Therefore the clarification issued would\n\nnot apply to the income received in the form of royalty. Moreover, the AO has made\n\nout a case that different layers of holdings were involved in the Adwords program and\n\nthe revenue has to be shared amongst them, therefore it cannot be presumed only on the\n\nbasis of TRC that GIL is the beneficial owner. Therefore, the CIT(Appeals) has wrongly\n\nconcluded that GIL is the beneficial owner under the facts and circumstances. In\n\nsucceeding year, the CIT(A) has concurred with the view of the AO. The ld. Standing\n\nCounsel further placed a reliance upon the judgment of High Court of Australia in the\n\ncase of Google Inc. v. Australian Competition and Consumers Commission[2013]\n\nHCA 1 249 CLR 435 in which it was held that Google is the owner of the search engine\n\nand the Adwords programs which were later on given to license to other holdings.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_441\">160. The ld. Counsel for the assessee, Mr. Percy Pardiwalla, Sr. Advocate, has placed\n\nheavy reliance upon the clarifications given with regard to Indo-Mauritius DTAC with\n\nthe submission that the Circular No.789 dated 13.04.2000 in this regard continued to be\n\nin force at the time when the press release was issued on 01.03.2013 by Finance Ministry\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_262\">                                       Page 205 of 331</span>\n\nwith regard to clarification on tax residency certificates. Therefore, in the light of this\n\nclarification, the TRC in respect of GIL submitted by the assessee is sufficient to hold\n\nthat GIL is the beneficial owner of the royalty received. Therefore, it should be charged\n\nto tax only @ 10%.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_442\">161. The learned Counsel for the assessee further contended that the GIL was\n\ncontrolling the AdWord Program and has given a distributor right to the Appellant under\n\nthe Google AdWord Program. Whatever revenue is generated under this Google\n\nAdWord Program it was remitted to GIL by the Appellant after deducting its share as\n\nper the Distribution Agreement. It was further contended that it has been repeatedly held\n\nthat the residency certificate is a beneficial ownership certificate and on its filing the\n\nRevenue will not make any further enquiry with regard to the residential status of the\n\nnon-resident. In support of his contention, he has placed reliance upon the following\n\njudgments:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_286\">   \uf0b7      ADIT Vs. Universal International Music BV reported at (2011) 45 SOT 219\n\n          (Mum.)\n\n   \uf0b7      Authority of Advanced Ruling, New Delhi P.No.13 of 1995 (1997) 94 Taxman\n\n          171 (AAR-New Delhi)\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"74045814\" id=\"span_263\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"115852355\" id=\"span_264\">69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_265\">                                      Page 206 of 331</span>\n\n   \uf0b7      Alibaba.com ecommerce         Pvt.    Ltd., Vs.       ADIT      ITA      No.3332       to\n\n          3334/Mum/2015 dated 30.11.2016\n\n   \uf0b7      Authority of Advanced Ruling, New Delhi Ex.Ltd., in re. (1996) 86 Taxman\n\n          292 (AAR-New Delhi)\n\n   \uf0b7      Velcro Canada Inc., Vs. R reported at 2012 TCC 57 Tax court of Canada\n\n   \uf0b7      <a href=\"/doc/115852355/\" id=\"a_371\">Vodafone International Holding BV Vs. UOI</a> 341 ITR 1 (SC)\n\n   \uf0b7      <a href=\"/doc/74045814/\" id=\"a_372\">Director of Income Tax Vs. Universal International Music BV</a> (2013) 31\n\n          taxmann.com 223 (Bombay)</span></span>\n\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_443\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"74045814\" id=\"span_266\">162. With regard to the report of House of Commons, Committee of Public Accounts,\n\nthe ld. Counsel for the assessee has contended that report was prepared in different\n\ncontext, therefore it cannot be relied on while examining the beneficial ownership\n\ncharacter of GIL. He however <a href=\"/doc/74045814/\" id=\"a_373\">placed reliance upon</a> the order of the CIT(Appeals) for\n\nthe AY 2013-14.</span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_444\">163. Having carefully examined the orders of lower authorities in the light of rival\n\nsubmissions, we find that in AY 2013-14, the CIT(Appeals) has accepted the contention\n\nof the assessee that GIL is the beneficial owner of the royalty received, whereas in the\n\nsucceeding years, the CIT(Appeals) has concurred with the view of the AO that GIL is\n\nnot the beneficial owner. Now the assessee and revenue are before us on this issue.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_445\">                                              IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                               IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                             IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_267\">                                         Page 207 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_446\">164. During the course of hearing, we have directed the assessee to produce the\n\nagreements executed between Google Inc. USA and GIH, GIH &amp; GNHBV and GNHBV\n\n&amp; GIL. In response thereto, the ld. Counsel for the assessee has stated that they have no\n\naccess to these agreements. He however filed the license agreement executed between\n\nGIL and GNHBV. It is also noticed from the record that GIPL is a 100% subsidiary of\n\nGoogle International LLC and GIL is the subsidiary of GNHBV.                         GNHBV is a\n\nsubsidiary of Google Ireland Holdings and Google Ireland Holdings is a subsidiary of\n\nGoogle Inc. USA. Therefore, all these companies are interconnected and inter-related,\n\ntherefore contention of assessee cannot be accepted that it has no access to license\n\nagreements executed between different holdings with regard to Adwords program.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_447\">From a careful reading of operating license agreement executed between GNHBV and\n\nGIL, we find that GIL has acquired certain right to use certain proprietary technology as\n\nwell as certain trademark and intangible including those described in Exhibit-A and\n\nExhibit-B. Through this license agreement, the license to use, license trademarks,\n\nlicense technology, license improvements was given by GNHBV to Google Ireland Ltd.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_448\">It is also clear from a reading of various clauses that the licensee i.e., GIL is not the legal\n\nowner of the license trade marks and the license technology, meaning thereby that GIL\n\nis not the owner of license technology and license trademark which include Adwords\n\nprogram and GIL has only acquired certain rights in intangibles from GNHBV. Though\n                                                 IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                       IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_268\">                                         Page 208 of 331</span>\n\nthere is a clause for license fee statement, it is not clear as to how much license fees is\n\nto be paid by GIL to GNHBV on account of sub-license of Adword program. If we\n\ncompare this agreement with the distributor agreement executed between GIPL and GIL,\n\nwe would find that in both the agreements, the licensee have admitted that IPRs and the\n\nintangibles are owned by licensor. For the sake of reference, we extract the relevant\n\nclauses of the aforesaid agreement as under:-\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_287\">                           &quot;OPERATING LICENSE AGREEMENT\n      THIS LICENSE AGREEMENT (this &quot;Agreement-) is effective as of January 1,\n      2004, by and between Google Netherlands Holdings B.V., a company organized\n      under the law, of the Netherlands (the &quot;Licensor&quot;), and Google Ireland Limited, a\n      company organized under the laws of Ireland (the &quot;Licensee&quot;).\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_288\">                                           RECITALS\n      A. Intellectual Property Management.\n</p><pre id=\"pre_6\">      a.     Patent Prosecution And Maintenance.\n             (i)    Licensor shall be solely responsible for the preparation, filing,\n</pre><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_463\">      prosecution and maintenance of patents or patent applications included in the\n      Licensed Technology, including all payment obligations, provided that Licensor\n      shall not be required to prosecute or maintain any such patents.&quot;\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_464\">165. Other agreements executed between Google Inc and GIH and GIH &amp; GNHBV\n\nwere not placed before us. Therefore, in the absence of these agreements, it is very\n\ndifficult for us to decide as to how much share GIL will get out of the revenue collected\n\nin the form of royalty under the Adwords Distribution agreement and services agreement\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_273\">                                       Page 213 of 331</span>\n\nfrom the GIPL. Since the onus is upon the assessee to establish that GIL is the beneficial\n\nowner, assessee could not place any evidence except the oral submission.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_465\">166. We have also examined the clarification issued by the Ministry of Finance through\n\nCircular No.789 dated 13.04.2000 and the press release dated 01.03.2013. Through\n\nCircular No.789, it has been made clear that wherever certification of registration is\n\nissued by Mauritius authorities, such certificate will constitute sufficient evidence for\n\naccepting the status of residence as well as beneficial ownership for applying the DTAC.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_466\">Through Press Release dated 01.03.2013, it has been clarified that the Tax Residency\n\nCertificate (TRC) produced by a resident of a contracting state shall be accepted as\n\nevidence that he is a resident of that contracting state and income-tax authorities in India\n\nwill not go behind the TRC and question the resident status, meaning thereby, through\n\nthis Press Release, it has been made clear that Tax Residency Certificate (TRC) is the\n\nfinal word with regard to resident status of the party and the Revenue cannot go beyond\n\nit unless and until they have some other evidence.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_467\">167. We have also examined the various judgments referred by the learned Counsel\n\nfor the assesse. In the case of ADIT Vs. Universal International Music BV (supra), the\n\nTribunal has held that once the resident certificate issued by the Netherland Tax authority\n\nis not doubted by the AO, the assessee has to be treated beneficial owner of royalty in\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_274\">                                       Page 214 of 331</span>\n\nview of such certificate even if no agreement with repartiore companies regarding\n\nacquisition of rights by assessee has been filed.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_468\">168. The Authority of Advanced Ruling in P.13 of 1995 (supra) has observed while\n\ndealing with the issue of beneficial ownership that &quot;the agreements require ABC to\n\nutilize all the expertise at its disposal - whether its own or acquired from others - in the\n\nexecution of the contract and for these services it receives payments which are in the\n\nnature of royalties or fee for technical services. The circumstances that, for discharging\n\nits functionsunder the contracts, the ABC in turn may have to seek similar help from\n\nothers and pay for it cannot detract from the beneficial ownership of the ABC over the\n\nroyalties and technical fees it receives from XY. On the other hand, it is because the\n\nABC has such beneficial ownership that it is able to utilize the payments received from\n\nXY for making payments to its suppliers if not already made. In fact, ABC may or may\n\nnot be passing on to its suppliers the same amounts which it receives from XY. Nor may\n\nit be passing on the expertise acquired by it from the various suppliers in the same form\n\nto XY as it has to modify, synthesise and integrate them so as to suit the needs of the\n\nmanufacturing plant and industrial complex and supply the technologies to XY as a\n\nsingle source. Hence, unless the processes and expertise referred to are supplied directly\n\nto XY by the affiliates and third parties - which is not envisaged under the agreements\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_275\">                                       Page 215 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_469\">- royalties and technical fees paid by XY to the ABC will belong to the latter and be in\n\nits beneficial ownership. Question Nos. 5 and 6 modified as indicated above are\n\nanswered accordingly.&quot;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_470\">169. In the case of Alibaba.com E-Commerce Vs. ADIT (supra), the Tribunal has\n\nobserved that tax residency certificate is sufficient to determine the proof of residency\n\nand the Income Tax Authorities cannot ignore the valid tax residency certificate issued\n\nby the Government authority of the other contacting state. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"115852355\" id=\"span_276\">The Tribunal has also taken\n\na cognizance of the judgment of the Apex Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/115852355/\" id=\"a_375\">Vodafone International\n\nHolding BV Vs. UOI</a> (supra) in which the court has held that the tax residency certificate\n\n(TRC) does not prevent enquiry into a tax fraud, for example where an OCB is used by\n\nIndian resident for wrong tripping or any other illegal activities and nothing prevents the\n\nRevenue from looking into special agreements, contracts or arrangements made or\n\neffected by Indian resident or the role of OCB in the entire transaction and held that\n\nwithout any evidence the Revenue cannot treat the assessee merely conduit of\n\nAlibaba.com, Hong Kong contrary to the material on record.</span>\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_471\">170. The Authority of Advanced Rules in X Ltd., (supra) did not answer the questions\n\nposed before them and restored the matter with the direction that more factual data\n\nshould be obtained before the assessee companies are held not to be beneficial owners\n                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_277\">                                        Page 216 of 331</span>\n\nof shares held by them. In the case of Velcro Canada Inc., Vs. R (supra), the tax court\n\nof Canada has held that Velcro Netherlands to be the beneficial owners of the royalties\n\nbecause it had the possession, use, risk and control of the funds. It was also contractually\n\nbound to pay an amount equal to 90% of the royalties to Velcro Antilles but entitled to\n\ndeal with the actual payment received from Velcro Canada as its profit. They further\n\nheld that if Velcro Antilles choose to enforce the license agreement, the payment was\n\nstill made to Velcro Netherlands and Velcro Netherland had no power to bind Velcro\n\nAntilles and was therefore not its agent.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_472\">171. We have carefully examined the facts of the instant case in the light of ratio laid\n\ndown through various judgments by different judicial authorities and the Hon&#x27;ble\n\nSupreme Court and the High Court and we find that in the instant case the tax residency\n\ncertificate was filed at page No.586 to 587 of the compilation filed by the assessee in\n\nwhich it has been mentioned that the Google Ireland files its corporation tax return in\n\nIreland on the basis that it is resident for tax purposes in Ireland. As such the company\n\nis liable to Irish Corporation Tax on its world-wide income, profit and gains including\n\nany income, profits and gains from its sales in India. In the certificate there is no mention\n\nwith regard to the beneficial ownership of the Google AdWord Program of which\n\ndistributorship was given to the Appellant.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_473\">                                          IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                           IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                         IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_278\">                                     Page 217 of 331</span>\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_474\">172. In the instant case, the AO has made out a case that different layers of holdings\n\nwere involved in the receipts received by the GIL from GIPL, the Appellant, under the\n\nGoogle AdWord Program. Undisputedly, the search engine and Adwords program\n\nbelong to GIL as is clear from the judgment of High Court of Australia in which it has\n\nbeen held that Google Inc. operates the well known internet search engine &quot;Google&quot;. In\n\nthat judgment, they have also described how the Google engine functions and how the\n\nsearch results are obtained. In that judgment, it was also held that Adwords Program is\n\na program which allows advertiser to create, change and monitor the performance of\n\nsponsored links. Google provides advertisers with access to the Adwords program\n\nthrough Adwords accounts. Worldwide, hundreds or thousands of advertisers use the\n\nAdwords program. It was also observed that Google derives most of its revenue from\n\nits online advertising business, which involves publishing or displaying advertisements\n\nas sponsored links on its search results pages. It was also made clear in that judgment\n\nthat participation in the Adwords program is subject to Google&#x27;s Terms of Service, the\n\nAdwords Program Terms and applicable Google policies, etc. From the reading of the\n\njudgment, it is clear that Google Inc. is the ultimate body which controls the Google\n\nAdwords program and they also share some part of revenue collected on account of\n\nAdwords program. It is also undisputed fact that Google Inc. has licensed the Adwords\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_279\">                                       Page 218 of 331</span>\n\nprogram and other intangibles to Google Ireland Holdings (GIH), who in turn licensed\n\nto Google Netherlands Holdings B.V. (GNHBV) and in turn GNHBV further licensed\n\nthe Adwords program and intangibles to Google Ireland Ltd. (GIL), who finally has\n\ngiven the distributor rights of the assessee for selling the Adwords space to advertisers,\n\nmeaning thereby, whatever revenue is generated on account of sale of Adwords space to\n\ndifferent advertisers, the revenue is to be distributed amongst all the holdings as they\n\nhave some beneficial interests therein. But before us, the assessee has not filed license\n\nagreements executed between Google Inc. USA and Google Ireland Holdings (GIH),\n\nGIH and GNHBV. Only one license agreement between GNHBV and GIL was filed\n\nand from its careful perusal, we find that out of the rights acquired in the Adwords\n\nprogram and intangibles, GNHBV has sub-licensed certain rights to GIL for Adwords\n\nprogram. On the basis of that license, GIL executed Adwords Distributor agreement\n\nwith the appellant for selling the Adwords space to advertisers. Since other parent\n\nholdings of GIL were involved in the Adwords programs directly or indirectly, they have\n\na right to share the revenue generated under the Adwords Distributor Agreement.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_475\">Though the onus is cast upon the appellant to place the relevant evidence in order to\n\nestablish that GIL is the beneficial owner of the royalty received, but it could not place\n\nany evidence on record in this regard, except the oral submissions. Whereas, the AO\n\nhas made out a case that so many holdings are involved in this Adwords program,\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_280\">                                       Page 219 of 331</span>\n\ntherefore the revenue collected on account of royalty payment is to be shared amongst\n\nthe other holdings. The assessee rather could not place the agreements executed between\n\nGoogle Inc. &amp; GIH and GIH &amp; GNHBV and GNHBV and GIL before the AO despite\n\nhis repeated requests. He has simply filed the operating license agreement executed\n\nbetween GNHBV and GIL berore us and for the remaining he has contended that he has\n\nno access to it. This contention of the assessee cannot be accepted inasmuch as all\n\nGoogle companies are inter-related and subsidiary of others. From a reading of this\n\nagreement, it is not clear as to how much revenue is shared by different holdings. In the\n\nabsence of the relevant evidence either in the form of agreement executed between the\n\nvarious holding companies or otherwise, it is not clear as to whether the GIL has full\n\ncontrol over the receipt received under Google AdWord Program or the GIL was acting\n\nas a conduit of its parent holdings. We therefore are of the view that this aspect requires\n\na fresh look by the AO in the light of all the relevant evidences. We accordingly restore\n\nthe matter to the AO after setting aside the order of the CIT(A) in this regard in all these\n\nappeals to readjudicate the issue of beneficial ownership in the light of the license\n\nagreements executed between the parent holdings of the GIL. We accordingly direct the\n\nappellant to extend all sort of cooperation by producing the agreement executed between\n\nthe various parent holdings of the GIL. Thus the matter is restored to the file of the AO\n\nto readjudicate the issue of beneficial ownership in terms indicated above. Accordingly\n                                                   IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                         IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                  IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_281\">                                              Page 220 of 331</span>\n\nappeal No. 1190/Bang/2014 is dismissed and appeal Nos. 949 &amp; 950/Bang/2017 are\n\npartly allowed for statistical purposes and appeal No. 1295/Bang/2014 is allowed for\n\nstatistical purposes.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_476\">173. Ltd. as\n   well as M/s Google Ireland Ltd.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_514\">12.
