Ltd and the differential amount of\n   Rs. 44,71,75,626/- has been held to be the escaped profit of the enterprise in India, considering the\n   fact that Google Ireland Ltd. has neither filed its return if income nor is willing to admit any\n   taxability in India.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_515\">14. For these and such other grounds that may be urged at the time of hearing, it is humbly prayed that\n   the order of the CIT(A) be reversed and that of the Assessing Officer be restored.\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_516\">15. The appellant craves leave to add, to alter, to amend or delete any of the grounds that may be urged\n   at the time of hearing of the appeal.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_517\">175. The brief facts on the impugned issues are that the AO has rejected the books of\naccounts as per the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/488246/\" id=\"a_386\">section 145</a> of the Act and calculated the disallowance\nof certain payments made to GIL by the appellant on account of business profit under\n<a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_387\">section 40(a)(ia)</a> of the Act on non-deduction of TDS. The assessee has challenged the\nrejection of books of accounts and also the disallowance made under <a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_388\">section 40(a)(ia)</a> of\nthe Act before the CIT(A) but did not find favour with him. Now the assessee is before\nus and his leanred Counsel Mr. Percy Pardiwala has invited our attention to provisions\nof <a href=\"/doc/488246/\" id=\"a_389\">section 145</a> r.w.s. 144 of the Act with the submission that once the books of accounts\nare rejected, the AO has to estimate the assessee&#x27;s income and no disallowance can be\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_286\">                                       Page 225 of 331</span>\n\nmade under different heads. The assessee had prepared its financial statements for the\nyear in accordance with law and the prescribed accounting standards issued by ICAI and\ncomplete and consistent disclosures including the notes to accounts have been made in\nthe financial statements. There had been no change in the financial policy followed by\nthe assessee during the year under consideration from the earlier years and the financial\nstatements of the assessee had been duly audited by an independent statutory auditor.\nThe AO has rejected the books of accounts having observed that the form of reporting\nof the advertisement revenues in the profit and loss account is not proper. There has\nbeen no finding of the AO that books of accounts were unreliable or that entire revenue\nwas not credited in the books of accounts and it was not possible to compute the actual\nprofit on the basis of the books of accounts. The learned Counsel for the assessee further\ninvited our attention that after rejecting the books of accounts, the AO recasted the profit\nand loss account without making any change in the revenue.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_518\">      The learned Standing Counsel for the Revenue has placed a reliance upon the\norder of the CIT(A).\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_519\">176. Having carefully examined the orders of lower authorities on this issue, we find\nthat AO has not pointed out the specific defect in maintenance of books of accounts.\nMoreover, after recasting the profit and loss account, there was no change in the profit\noriginally reported by the assessee in its financial statements. It is also noticed that\nassessee has been following the same method of accounting in earlier years but it was\nnever rejected by the AO. We also find force in the contention of the learned Counsel\nfor the assessee that the lower authorities have not identified the accounting standards\nthat has to be followed by the assessees. In the light of these facts, we are of the\nconsidered opinion that rejection of the books of accounts by the AO is not proper. We\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_287\">                                      Page 226 of 331</span>\n\ntherefore set aside his order and direct the AO to accept the books of accounts prepared\nby the assessee.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_520\">177. So far as the disallowance on payments made by the assessee to GIL on account\nof business profit under <a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_390\">section 40(a)(ia)</a> on non-deduction of TDS is concerned, we find\nthat during the impugned assessment year i.e., 2008-09, the Tribunal has adjudicated the\nissue of the characterization of payments made by the appellant and held it to be\npayment of royalty. Though the Hon&#x27;ble High Court directed the Tribunal to adjudicate\nthe issue of characterization of the payments made by GIPL the appellant, to GIL, in\nother appeals of different assessement years, but in the impugned assessment year i.e.,\n2008-09 the Tribunal has taken a particular view with regard to characterization of\npayment under the AdWord Distribution Agreement, we cannot ignore these facts.\nMoreover, in foregoing appeals, we have re-examined the issue of characterization of\nsuch payment to GIL and held it to be royalty. The claim of bonafide for non-deduction\nof TDS on such pyment has already been examined by us in the foregoing paras while\nadjudicating an issue as to wheather the asseessee is to be held in default on non\ndeduction of TDS and we did not find any merit in the contention of the appellant that\nunder a bonafide belief it did not deduct the TDS on such payment. Therefore, we do\nnot find any justification to readjudicate the same issue again in this appeal. Hence, we\ndo not any infirmity in the order of the CIT(A) and we accordingly confirm the same.\nAccordingly ground Nos. 5 to 16 in the assessee&#x27;s appeal and ground\nNos. 5 to 13 in the Revenue&#x27;s appeal are disposed off.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_521\">178. So far as the ground Nos. 10 to 12 in IT(TP)A No. 374/Bang/2013 and ground\nNos. 5 to 7 and 12 in IT(TP)A No. 466/Bang/2013 relating to disallowance under <a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_391\">section\n40(a)(ia)</a> with regard to additional profit computed by the AO on the basis of transfer\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_288\">                                       Page 227 of 331</span>\n\npricing adjustment are concerned, we are of the view that since in the transfer pricing\nappeals the issue of computation of arm&#x27;s length price of payment made under Google\nAdWord Program by GIPL to GIL has been restored back to the AO / TPO for\nrecomputation of arm&#x27;s length price in terms of directions issued by Tribunal, the\ndisallowance of additional profit calculated on the basis of transfer pricing adjustment,\ncannot be made under <a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_392\">section 40(a)(ia)</a> of the Act. We therefore set aside the order of\nCIT(A) and delete the disallowance made in this regard.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_522\">Ground No. 18 of assesse&#x27;s appeal relating to initiation of penalty proceedings under\n<a href=\"/doc/144487481/\" id=\"a_393\">section 271(1)(c)</a> is premature and we dismiss the same.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_523\">179. During the course of hearing of the appeal, the Revenue has also moved an\napplication for the admission of additional grounds with the submission that assessee\nhas accepted in the written submissions filled before the Tribunal that it is providing\nservices through ITES division in connection with the distribution of the advertisement\nby way of Ad- review in compliance of the local laws. The additional grounds raised\nare extracted as under:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_299\">     1. The assessee is appointed as non-exclusive authorised distributor of adword\n       program to the advertisers in India by Google Ireland. Under the Google Adword\n       Program Distribution Agreement dated 12/12/2005, the assessee has been granted\n       marketing and distribution rights of adword program to the advertisers in India.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_524\">       Clause 3.4 of the distribution agreement provides for minimum level of service as\n       specified in Exhibit - C. Exhibit -C to the adword agreement refers to service level\n       agreement and imposes responsibility for providing services to the customers\n       (advertisers). All advertisers are instructed to contact the assessee directly for\n       support. In view of the specific obligation to provide presale and Post sale support\n       by the assessee to the advertisers, the assessee is providing the same as per\n       service agreement dated 1/4/2004.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_525\">                                              IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                               IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                             IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_289\">                                         Page 228 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_526\">     2. The above aspect has been admitted by the assessee in its written submissions filed in\n       IT(TP)A.No.1511 to 1516/B/2013 arising out of, proceedings under <a href=\"/doc/726238/\" id=\"a_394\">section 201</a> of the\n       Act.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_527\">     1. The assessee in the written submissions has accepted that it is providing services\n        through ITES division in connection with the distribution of advertisement by way of\n        review with respect to the compliance of the local laws. It is further admitted that the\n        assessee performs ad review services under ITES agreement with Google Ireland for\n        which assessee is separately compensated. It is the specific contention of the revenue\n        that this amount received by the assessee from Google Ireland for rendering services\n        the in India to the advertisers is claimed as export under <a href=\"/doc/1904729/\" id=\"a_395\">section 10A</a> of the Act.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_528\">     2. The above aspect is a question of law which can be considered by this Hon&#x27;ble tribunal\n       at this stage. Further the above activities were not disclosed by the assessee in the\n       course of any proceedings except before this Hon&#x27;ble tribunal by way of written\n       submissions in the appeal involving applicability of <a href=\"/doc/1329583/\" id=\"a_396\">section 195</a> of the Act.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_529\">     3. As held by the Apex Court, additional ground which is question of law can be raised\n       and entertained at any stage. The application of <a href=\"/doc/1904729/\" id=\"a_397\">section 10A</a> of the Act without there\n       being any export of article or thing or computer software is question of law.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_530\">180. In support of request for admission of the additional grounds, the learned Standing\nCounsel further contended that it was admitted by the assessee that it performs ad review\nservices under ITES division with Google Ireland for which the assessee was separately\ncompensated. Therefore in the light of specific contentions that the amount received by\nthe assessee from Google Ireland for rendering services in India to the advertisers cannot\nbe claimed as exemption under <a href=\"/doc/1904729/\" id=\"a_398\">section 10A</a> of the Act. Since this aspect was not\nexamined by the AO, the additional grounds now may be admitted and matter be restored\nto the AO for its adjudication in accordance with law.             The learned Counsel for the\nassessee strongly objected the application for the admission of the additional grounds\nwith the submissions that this ground was never raised either before the CIT(A) or before\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_290\">                                       Page 229 of 331</span>\n\nthe Tribunal at the time of filing of the appeal. Moreover, this ground was also not raised\nbefore the Tribunal at the time of hearing of the appeal by different bench. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"125770855\" id=\"span_291\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"91542\" id=\"span_292\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"1617513\" id=\"span_293\">Now this\nground has been raised at the advanced stage of the hearing of the appeal. The learned\nCounsel Mr. Percy Pardiwala further contended that AO has not raised the dispute in\nthis regard during the course of assessment proceedings. Therefore, these grounds\ncannot be raised at this stage. He further placed reliance upon the judgments in the case\nof <a href=\"/doc/1617513/\" id=\"a_399\">Hukumchand Mills Ltd., Vs. CIT</a> 63 <a href=\"/doc/91542/\" id=\"a_400\">ITR 232 (SC) and M Corp Global Pvt. Ltd., Vs.\nCIT</a> 178 taxmann.com 347 (<a href=\"/doc/125770855/\" id=\"a_401\">SC), Instrade Corporation Ltd., Vs. ACIT</a> 62 taxmann.com\n239 (Karnataka High Court) in support of his contention that Tribunal has no power to\nenhance the income of the assessee. If the additional grounds are admitted and matter is\nrestored to the AO, the AO will recompute the deduction under <a href=\"/doc/1904729/\" id=\"a_402\">section 10A</a> resulting\ninto enhancement of the Income. Therefore, the additional grounds cannot be admitted.</span></span></span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_531\">181. Having carefully examined the order of authorities below in this regard, we find\nthat AO has accepted the claim of deduction under <a href=\"/doc/1904729/\" id=\"a_403\">section 10A</a> of the Act and he has\nnot raised any dispute with regard to amount received by the assessee from Google\nIreland for rendering services in India to the advertisers which was claimed as export\nproceeds under <a href=\"/doc/1904729/\" id=\"a_404\">section 10A</a> of the Act. It is also a settled position of law that at the\nsecond appellate stage, the Revenue cannot improve upon the case made out by the AO.\nThere are various provisions under the <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_405\">Income Tax Act</a> where the Revenue can rectify\nits mistakes but before the Tribunal no additional grounds can be raised which was not\na subject matter of appeal or dispute before the lower authorities. The Tribunal, being a\nsecond appellate authority, is supposed to adjudicate those issues which were raised\nbefore it and also was of subject matter of dispute before the lower authorities. The\nRevenue cannot improve upon its case. Therefore, we are of the view that additional\ngrounds raised by the Revenue are not admissible and we accordingly reject the same.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_532\">                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_294\">                                       Page 230 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_533\">182. Ground Nos. 2 to 4 in the Revenue&#x27;s appeal relate to the computation of deduction\nunder <a href=\"/doc/1904729/\" id=\"a_406\">section 10A</a> and in this regard, the learned Counsel for the assessee has contended\nthat this ground is covered by the judgment of jurisdictional High Court in the case of\nTata Elxsi 349 ITR 98 and the view taken by jurisdictional High Court has also been\nconfirmed by the Hon&#x27;ble Apex Court. Therefore, no interference in the order of the\nCIT(A) is called for.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_534\">      The learned Standing Counsel placed reliance upon the order of the AO.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_535\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"26146277\" id=\"span_295\">183. Having carefully examined the order of authorities below, we find that CIT(A)\nhas adjudicated the issue in the light of judgment of jurisdictional High Court in the case\nof <a href=\"/doc/26146277/\" id=\"a_407\">CIT Vs. Tata Elxsi Ltd.</a>, (supra) and the judgment of Tata Elxsi has been approved by\nthe Hon&#x27;ble Apex Court. Since the CIT(A) has adjudicated the issue in the light of\njudgment of jurisdictional High Court, we find no infirmity in his order and accordingly\nwe confirm the same in this regard. Accordingly, the appeal of the assessee is partly\nallowed and that of the Revenue is dimissed.</span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_536\">184. (154 TTJ\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_556\">   <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"131633874\" id=\"span_302\">142) and Hon&#x27;ble Mumbai Tribunal in the case of <a href=\"/doc/131633874/\" id=\"a_421\">Pinstorm Technologies vs ITO</a>\n   (54 SOT 78) squarely applicable to the facts of the present case.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_557\">16. On the facts and in the circumstances of the case and in law, the Ld. AO has erred\n   in relying on the decision of Delhi Tribunal in the case of Microsoft (134 TTJ\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_559\">   257) and holding that there is no distinction between the provisions of the Act\n   and the DTAA with respect to the taxation of Royalty.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_560\">18. On the facts and in the circumstances of the case and in law, the Ld. AO/ Hon&#x27;ble\n   DRP has erred at one side holding that the above mentioned decisions are not\n   applicable to the facts of the Appellant by stating that in Appellant&#x27;s case the\n   issue pertains to grant of rights by appellant to Google India and on other hand\n   not following the Tribunal decisions in case of direct advertisers. (140 TTJ 195) without appreciating that the similar facts\n   are involved in the case of the Appellant.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_561\">19. On the facts and in the circumstances of the case and in law. the Ld. AO has\n   erred in inter-alia holdingas under:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_309\">   a) That the AdWords Program is a complex computer software and\n     consideration paid by Google India to Appellant is for granting of license to\n     use the copyright in the Ad Words program as specified in <a href=\"/doc/588056/\" id=\"a_422\">section 14(a)(iii)</a>\n                                         IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                          IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                               IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                        IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_303\">                                    Page 235 of 331</span>\n\n     and 14(b)(ii), without appreciating that the said consideration was payable\n     by Google India in the subject year and no licensing of copyright is involved\n     in the case of Appellant and only a sale of online advertisement space under\n     the said AdWords Program.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_310\">   b) That the consideration paid by Google India to Appellant is for granting of\n     license to use the copyright in the AdWords program without appreciating\n     that that the said consideration was payable by Google India in the subject\n     year and that Google India is only a distributor engaged in purchase and\n     sale of an online Ad space and not engaged in exploitation of any copyright\n     or other intangible property subsists in it, which would result in any royalty\n     payment to the Appellant.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_311\">   c) That non-exclusive right is also covered under the <a href=\"/doc/1136195/\" id=\"a_423\">Copyright Act</a> while the\n     Act specifically provides that the Copyright means the exclusive rights to do\n     or authorise the doing of any of the acts mentioned in <a href=\"/doc/1129646/\" id=\"a_424\">section 14</a> of the\n     Copyright Act.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_312\">   d) That the Ld. AO in para 5.18 of the impugned order has mentioned that the\n     very nature of software is that it, being a computer programme. cannot be\n     sold without the transaction involving some transfer of copyright.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_562\">20. Without prejudice to the grounds above, the Ld. AO/ Hon&#x27;ble DRP has erred\n   in not appreciating that the clauses of Explanation 2 to <a href=\"/doc/492579/\" id=\"a_425\">section 9(1)(vi)</a> are\n   mutually exclusive and same amount receivable by the Appellant from Google\n   India towards sale of online advertisement space under the AdWords Program\n   cannot be taxed under multiple clauses of the said Explanation.\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_563\">21. On the facts and in the circumstances of the case and in law, the Ld. AO/\n   Hon&#x27;ble DRP has erred in disregarding the judicial precedents squarely\n   applicable to the facts of the Appellant and not considering the reliance\n   placed by the Appellant on the High Powered Committee constituted by the\n   Central Board of Direct Taxes vide Notification No. F No 500/ 122/ 99 dated\n   December 16, 1999.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_564\">                                          IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                           IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                         IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_304\">                                    Page 236 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_565\">22. On the facts and in the circumstances of the case and in law, the Ld. AO/\n   Hon&#x27;ble DRP has erred in placing reliance on the judicial precedents which\n   are not applicable to the case of the Appellant.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_566\">23. On the facts and circumstances of the case and in law, the Ld. AO/ DRP erred\n   in stating that the reliance placed by the Appellant on various case laws is\n   distinguishable without pointing out the distinction, particularly in decided\n   cases where the Appellant was the payee in respect of transaction of sale of\n   online advertisement space online to the Indian advertisers under the\n   AdWords Program.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_567\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1798885\" id=\"span_305\">24. On the facts and circumstances of the case and in law, the Ld. AO/ Hon&#x27;ble\n   DRP has erred in misconstruing the distribution agreement between the\n   Appellant and Google India dated 12 December 2005 by stating that the said\n   agreement is for use of software and other IP of the Appellant, which\n   tantamounts to re-writing of the agreement which is not permissible under the\n   law as laid down by the Supreme Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/1798885/\" id=\"a_426\">CIT vs Motor &amp; General\n   Stores Pvt. Ltd.</a> (66 ITR 692) (SC).</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_568\">25. On the facts and circumstances of the case and in law, the Ld. AO has erred\n   in holding that the distribution agreement are complemented by the IT\n   Enabled Services agreement entered into by the Appellant with Google India\n   dated 1.4.2004 CITES agreement&#x27;) and therefore, amounts receivable by the\n   Appellant from sale of online advertisement space on AdWords Program was\n   in the nature of Royalty.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_569\">26. On the facts and circumstances of the case and in law, the Ld. AO has erred\n   in not appreciating that the ITES agreement has no connection whatsoever\n   with the distribution agreement under which the Appellant distributes\n   advertisement space to Google India and which has nothing to do with the\n   ITES agreement. The linkage between the two agreements is erroneous given\n   that the Google India started providing ITES agreement related services in\n   the year 2004 and the distribution activity started only in the end of 2005.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_570\">                                         IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                          IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                               IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                        IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_306\">                                    Page 237 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_571\">27. On the facts and circumstances of the case and in law, the Ld. AO has erred\n   in concluding that Appellant&#x27;s obligation under the Distribution Agreement\n   was nterconnected with the obligation of the Appellant under the ITES\n   segment.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_572\">28. Without prejudice to the above grounds and on the facts and circumstances\n   of the case and in law, the Ld. AO erred in holding that by virtue of ITES\n   agreement dated 01.04.2004 for rendering of Ad-review services by the ITES\n   division results in use/ access to IP tools, confidential information, data etc.\n   Ld. AO failed to appreciate that the Ad review process done by the ITES\n   division comprises of approximately 6% of the total Ads that are reviewed\n   and consequently 94% of the Ads which are reviewed automatically, without\n   any involvement of the ITES division and therefore by no stretch of any\n   imagination there could be any access to or use of IP tools, confidential\n   information. data etc. for majority of the Ads.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_573\">29. On the facts and circumstances of the case and in law, the Ld. AO has erred\n   in holding that grant of right to use Google brand features and trademark and\n   Intellectual Property provided to Google India under the ITES agreement is\n   independent of the distribution agreement.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_574\">30. On the facts and circumstances of the case and in law, the Ld. AO has erred\n   in not appreciating that the right to use Google brand features and trademark\n   provided to Google India under the Distribution agreement was not the main\n   purpose of the Distribution agreement and was rather incidental to the main\n   purpose of distribution of online advertisement space and marketing of\n   AdWords Program in India.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_575\">31. On the facts and circumstances of the case and in law, the Ld. AO has erred\n   in not appreciating that the right to use Google brand features and trademark\n   was granted merely for identification of Google India as the distributor of\n   online advertisement space and marketer of AdWords Program.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_576\">32. On the facts and in the circumstances of the case and in law. the Ld. AO has\n   erred in contradicting with the order of Ld. Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO&#x27;)\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_307\">                                       Page 238 of 331</span>\n\n      and construing that the Appellant has given Copyright/ Copyright rights for\n      further exploitation to Google India while the TPO has accepted the said\n      transaction in the nature of distribution and marketing of the AdWords\n      Program for the purpose of computing the Arm&#x27;s Length Price.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_577\">   33. Without prejudice to the principal ground that the amount receivable is not\n      &#x27;Royalty&#x27;, the Ld. AO/ Hon&#x27;ble DRP has erred in not accepting that the\n      amounts receivable by the Appellant from Google India will be taxable as\n      *Royalty only in the year of receipt of actual amount i.e. in the Financial Year\n      2014-15 as per the beneficial provisions of DTAA. Further the Ld. AO has\n      erred in not adjudicating on the said ground in the impugned order.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_578\">   34. Without prejudice to the above grounds and on the facts and circumstances\n      of the case and in law, the Ld. AO has erred in levying interest<a href=\"/doc/380949/\" id=\"a_427\"> u/s 234B</a> of\n      the Act without appreciating that any taxes payable by the Appellant were\n      deductible by the payers at source in India and therefore no such interest is\n      leviable on the Appellant which is in consonance with the judicial precedents\n      on this issue, including the decision of Hon&#x27;ble Special Bench of Delhi\n      Tribunal in the case of Motorola Inc. (96 TTJ 1).\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_579\">   35. On the facts and in the circumstances of the case and in law, the Ld. AO has\n      erred in levying interest under <a href=\"/doc/633848/\" id=\"a_428\">section 234A</a> of the Act.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_580\">185. Though various grounds are raised by the assessee but they all relate to the validity\n\nof the reopening of the assessment, characterization of payment made by the GIPL to\n\nGIL under AdWord Program and chargeability of interest under <a href=\"/doc/633848/\" id=\"a_429\">section 234A</a> and <a href=\"/doc/380949/\" id=\"a_430\">234B</a>\n\nof the Act.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_581\">                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_308\">                                      Page 239 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_582\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"1801435\" id=\"span_309\">186. With regard to validity of the reopening of the assessment, the learned Counsel\n\nfor the assessee has contended that AO has initiated the proceedings for the assessment\n\nyear 2007-08 under <a href=\"/doc/1837761/\" id=\"a_431\">section 147</a> of the Income Tax Act (hereinafter called as an &quot;Act&quot;),\n\nand issued a notice under <a href=\"/doc/1888237/\" id=\"a_432\">section 148</a> dated 29.03.2014. In compliance thereof, the\n\nappellant, Google Ireland Ltd., furnished the return of income and objected the reasons\n\nfurnished by the AO. Although appellant was not served with the notice under <a href=\"/doc/95929751/\" id=\"a_433\">section\n\n143(2)</a> of the Act, but the objection in this regard has been raised before the AO and\n\nthereafter transfer pricing proceedings was initiated by the TPO. The learned Counsel\n\nfor the assessee has further submitted that the reassessment proceedings under <a href=\"/doc/1837761/\" id=\"a_434\">section\n\n147</a> was initiated by the AO without having any jurisdiction. The learned Counsel for\n\nthe assesse further contended that as per judgment of the Hon&#x27;ble Supreme Court in the\n\ncase of <a href=\"/doc/1801435/\" id=\"a_435\">GKN Driveshafts (India) Ltd. Vs. ITO</a> 259 ITR 19 the AO has to dispose off\n\nthe objections filed against the reasons for reopening the assessment by passing a\n\nspeaking order before proceeding with the assessment. It was further contended that\n\nbefore disposing of the objections filed by the assessee, the AO cannot proceed with the\n\nassessment. Therefore notice under <a href=\"/doc/95929751/\" id=\"a_436\">section 143(2)</a> cannot be issued before disposing of\n\nthe objections against the reasons for reopening filed by the assessee. In the present\n\ncase, notice under <a href=\"/doc/95929751/\" id=\"a_437\">section 143(2)</a> is stated to have been issued prior to providing the\n\nreasons for reassessment and for this reason alone the reassessment proceeding should\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_310\">                                      Page 240 of 331</span>\n\nbe held invalid. The learned Counsel for the assessee further contended that notice under\n\n<a href=\"/doc/95929751/\" id=\"a_438\">section 143(2)</a> was issued on 16.02.2016 along with notice under <a href=\"/doc/1650322/\" id=\"a_439\">section 142(1)</a> of the\n\nAct. Thus the notice is time barred as it was issued beyond the prescribed period.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_583\">Therefore, assessment framed consequent thereto deserves to be quashed. It was further\n\ncontended that while exercising jurisdiction under <a href=\"/doc/1837761/\" id=\"a_440\">section 147</a> of the Act, the AO should\n\nhave reason to believe that income has escaped assessment and secondly appropriate\n\nsanction in terms of <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_441\">section 151of</a> the Act must be obtained. It was further contended\n\nthat the notice was issued by ACIT without having a proper approval from the JCIT.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_584\">Therefore, the assumption of jurisdiction is not proper.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_585\">187. The learned Standing Counsel, on the other hand, has contended that the assessee,\n\nGoogle Ireland is a non-resident and has not filed any return of income in India. The\n\nAO on the basis of the assessment framed in the case of the payer i.e., GIPL, formed a\n\nbelief that income chargeable to tax in the hands of the appellant has escaped\n\nassessment.The learned Standing Counsel further contended that AO is not required to\n\nwait for filing of the objections against the reasons recorded for reopening of the\n\nassessment, for issuing a notice under <a href=\"/doc/95929751/\" id=\"a_442\">section 143(2)</a> of the Act. Once the returns are\n\nfiled in response to notice under <a href=\"/doc/1888237/\" id=\"a_443\">section 148</a> of the Act, the AO is at liberty to issue a\n\nnotice under <a href=\"/doc/95929751/\" id=\"a_444\">section 143(2)</a> of the Act within the prescribed period. The learned\n\nStanding Counsel further contended that due approval was obtained by the JCIT and the\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_311\">                                       Page 241 of 331</span>\n\ncopy of the approval is also placed on record by the learned Standing Counsel. He has\n\nalso invited our attention to the order sheets with the submission that assessee has filed\n\nthe return of income on 20.08.2014 in response to notice under <a href=\"/doc/1888237/\" id=\"a_445\">section 148</a> of the Act\n\nand on the same day the AO issued a notice under <a href=\"/doc/95929751/\" id=\"a_446\">section 143(2)</a> of the Act. In support\n\nof his contention he has filed the copy of the order sheets. The learned Standing Counsel\n\nfor the Revenue further contended that order sheet recorded by the AO in the course of\n\ndischarge of official duty is presumed to be correct unless contrary is proved. In the\n\norder sheet, it is clearly mentioned that notice under <a href=\"/doc/95929751/\" id=\"a_447\">section 143(2)</a> was issued on\n\n28.08.2014, therefore contention of the assessee cannot be accepted that notice was not\n\nissued in time. The learned Standing Counsel has placed the reliance on the judgment of\n\nthe Apex Court in the case of Rajesh Javeri Stock Broker 291 ITR 500 in support of his\n\ncontention that the prima facie belief is required to be formed by the AO to reopen the\n\nassessment.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_586\">188. Having carefully examined the orders of authorities below, in the light of rival\n\nsubmission in this regard, we find that the assessee has disputed the reopening of the\n\nassessment mainly on two aspects, one is that the AO has reopened the assessment\n\nwithout forming a belief that income chargeable to tax has escaped the assessment and\n\nsecondly the notice under <a href=\"/doc/95929751/\" id=\"a_448\">section 143(2)</a> was not issued within the prescribed period.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_587\">                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_312\">                                       Page 242 of 331</span>\n\nBefore proceeding to deal with the issues on merit, we want to record certain facts\n\nhereunder:\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_588\">189. Notice under <a href=\"/doc/1888237/\" id=\"a_449\">section 148</a> was issued on 16.04.2014 according to the assessee. The\n\nassessee has filed the return of income on 14.08.2014 in response to notice under <a href=\"/doc/1888237/\" id=\"a_450\">section\n\n148</a> of the Act. On 20.08.2014, the AO has taken a note that assessee has filed the return\n\nof income in response to notice under <a href=\"/doc/1888237/\" id=\"a_451\">section 148</a> of the Act and the case was selected\n\nfor scrutiny.He accordingly issued notice under <a href=\"/doc/95929751/\" id=\"a_452\">section 143(2)</a> of the Act. Thereafter\n\nthe proceedings were commenced on 24.07.2015 and reasons for reopening the\n\nassessment was communicated to the assessee on 24.07.2015 and on 16.09.2015,\n\nassessee filed the objections. The objections filed by the assessee were disposed off by\n\nthe AO and thereafter again a notice under <a href=\"/doc/95929751/\" id=\"a_453\">section 143(2)</a> was issued to the assessee\n\nalong with questionnaire under <a href=\"/doc/1650322/\" id=\"a_454\">section 142(1)</a> of the Act. While issuing second time\n\nnotice under <a href=\"/doc/95929751/\" id=\"a_455\">section 143(2)</a>, the AO has categorically recorded in the order sheet that\n\nfresh notice under <a href=\"/doc/511102/\" id=\"a_456\">section 142</a> is issued to the assessee; meaning thereby that earlier\n\nnotice was also issued on 28.08.2004. Nothing has been placed on record on behalf of\n\nthe assessee to dispute the minutes of the proceedings recorded by the AO during the\n\ncourse of assessment except the oral contentions. Copy of the proceedings recordedby\n\nthe AO are placed on record. We, therefore, find no merit in the oral submissions with\n\nregard to the non-issuance of the notice under <a href=\"/doc/95929751/\" id=\"a_457\">section 143(2)</a> within the prescribed period\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_313\">                                       Page 243 of 331</span>\n\nas it is a settled position of law that the minutes or the order sheet recorded in discharge\n\nof official duty are correct unless and until contrary is proved in view of <a href=\"/doc/731516/\" id=\"a_458\">section 114</a> of\n\nthe Indian Evidence Act, 1872. Therefore, we are of the view that notice under <a href=\"/doc/95929751/\" id=\"a_459\">section\n\n143(2)</a> was issued in time. Moreover, we do not find any procedure either in the Act or\n\nin Income Tax Rules wherein it has been laid down that after issuance of notice under\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1888237/\" id=\"a_460\">section 148</a>, the returns are to be filed and thereafter assessee has to seek the reasons\n\nrecorded for reopening of the assessment and thereafter file the objection thereto. After\n\ndisposal of the objections the AO can only issue the notice under <a href=\"/doc/95929751/\" id=\"a_461\">section 143(2)</a> of the\n\nAct. For completing the assessment under <a href=\"/doc/1837761/\" id=\"a_462\">section 147</a> of the Act, the procedure laid\n\ndown under <a href=\"/doc/164072874/\" id=\"a_463\">section 143</a> of the Act is to be followed. Nowhere it has been mentioned\n\neither under <a href=\"/doc/164072874/\" id=\"a_464\">section 143</a> or under <a href=\"/doc/1837761/\" id=\"a_465\">section 147</a> of the Act that the notice under <a href=\"/doc/95929751/\" id=\"a_466\">section\n\n143(2)</a> cannot be issued before disposing of the objections filed by the assessee against\n\nthe reasons recorded for reopening the assessment. Accordingly, we find no merit in the\n\ncontention of the assessee that the notice under <a href=\"/doc/95929751/\" id=\"a_467\">section 143(2)</a> is bad in law.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_589\">With regard to other arguments of the asseessee that AO has not formed a proper belief\n\nthat income chargeable to tax has escaped assessment, we find that the asseessee has not\n\nfiled any return of income and in the hands of payer, the AO treated the payments made\n\nto the payee ie. the asseessee to be royality though the payer claimed it to be business\n\nprofit in the hands of the asseessee. In view of the judgement of the Apex court in the\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_314\">                                      Page 244 of 331</span>\n\ncase of Rajesh Javeri Stock Brokers(Pvt) Ltd (supra) the AO is required to form a prima\n\nfacie belief that income chargeable to tax has escaped asseesseement and not to make\n\nout a full proof case. From a carefull reading of the reasons recorded for reopening the\n\nasseesseement we are of the view that the AO has formed a primafacie belief that income\n\nchargeable to tax has escaped asseesseement. and we therefore hold that reopening is\n\nvalid.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_590\">190. So far as assessment on merit is concerned, we find that Revenue has assessed the\n\nappellant on business profit received by it after treating the GIPL as its Permanent\n\nEstablishment in India. The issue of characterization of the payment made by the GIPL\n\nto GIL has already been adjudicated by us in the appeals filed by GIPL and we have\n\ntaken a view that the payments made by GIPL to GIL under the Google AdWord\n\nDistribution Agreement is payment of royalty, therefore the receipts in the hands of GIL\n\ncannot be treated to be business profit in the hands of the GIL. The GIL and GIPL were\n\ndealing on principal to principal basis. Therefore, GIPL cannot be called to be the\n\nPermanent Establishment of the GIL. In the light of these facts, we are of the view that\n\nRevenue has wrongly assessed the GIL for business profit received by it under AdWord\n\nDistribution Agreement. Moreover, during the course of hearing, the learned Standing\n\nCounsel has candidly admitted that now the Revenue&#x27;s stand is very clear that the\n\npayment made by the GIPL to GIL is in the nature of royalty and not the business profit\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_315\">                                       Page 245 of 331</span>\n\nreceived by the GIL. Therefore, we are of the considered opinion that assessment of\n\nbusiness profit in the hands of GIL after treating the GIPL as Permanent Establishment\n\nof the GIL is not proper. Even if the royalty is to be paid by the GIPL to GIL the tax is\n\nto deducted on its payment and benefit of the same will be given to GIL while completing\n\nthe assessment in its hands. Therefore, we set aside the order of CIT(A) and restore the\n\nmatter to the AO with a direction to reframe the assessment in the light of the\n\nobservations of the Tribunal in the foregoing paras and also in accordance with law.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_591\">Accordingly, this appeal is partly allowed for statistical purposes.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_592\">191. IT(TP)A Nos. 69/Bang/2014, 191/Bang/2014, 68/Bang/2014, 205/Bang/2015,\n\n559/Bang/2016, 881/Bang/2016, 387/Bang/2017, ITA No.1299/Bang/2015\n\n\n\n      These are cross appeals filed by the assessee-company as well as the revenue\n\ndirected against respective final assessment orders passed<a href=\"/doc/144280341/\" id=\"a_468\"> u/s.143(3)</a> r.w.s.144C of the\n\nAct, for the assessment years 2009-10, 2010-11 and 2011-12,.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_593\">192. For the sake of clarity and convenience, facts relevant to the assessment year\n\n2009-10 in IT(TP)A No.69/Bang/2014 (filed by the assessee) and IT(TP)A\n\nNo.191/Bang/2014 (filed by the Revenue) are stated herein.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_594\">                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_316\">                                      Page 246 of 331</span>\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_595\">193. Briefly the facts of the case are as under. The assessee is a company duly\n\nincorporated under the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/1353758/\" id=\"a_469\">Companies Act, 1956</a>. It is a wholly owned\n\nsubsidiary of Google International LLC, USA. It has STPI units at Hyderabad and\n\nGurgaon.    It is engaged in the business of providing IT services as well as IT enabled\n\nServices (ITes) and also Marketing and Distribution of AdWords programme to its\n\ngroup companies including Google Inc and Google Ireland and other group of\n\ncompanies. It filed return of income for the assessment year 2009-10 on 30.09.2009 and\n\nlater revised declaring total income of Rs.16,57,21,800/- after claiming deduction of\n\nRs.184,32,46,295/- u/s.10<a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_470\">A of the Income-tax Act</a>. /computed by the assessee having regard to arms\n                                                   length price on application of the Comparable\n
