of the Income-tax Act, 1961\n                                                IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                      IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                               IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_323\">                                           Page 253 of 331</span>\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_604\">194. The Assessing Officer (AO) referred the matter to the TPO with the approval of\nthe learned CIT, Bengaluru-1 for the purpose of determining the ALP of the above\ninternational transactions. The TPO, vide order dt.29.01.2013, passed<a href=\"/doc/179109058/\" id=\"a_487\"> u/s 92CA(3)</a> of\nthe Act, suggested ALP adjustment of Rs.83,50,87,934/- in respect of international\ntransactions with Google Ireland on aggregate basis and of Rs.44,84,24,329/- in respect\nof IT services to Google Inc, USA. While doing so, the TPO had analysed the TP study\nsubmitted by the assesse-company, in para 2.2 of his order as under:\n</p>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_314\">       &quot;2.2 On perusal of group profile given on page 13 and 14 of the TP study it is\n       observed that Google Inc. USA is the ultimate holding company of the group. Google\n       Inc is a public and profitable company focused on search services. Google Inc is the\n       legal owner of AdWords Software product, Brand Names, and all other related\n       Intellectual Property Rights (IPRs). Google Inc. has given rights to use intangible\n       assets to Google Ireland Holdings which is an Ireland based subsidiary of Google\n       Inc. Google Ireland Holdings in turn has by license agreement given rights to use\n       the same intangible assets to Google Netherlands Holdings BV; a Netherlands based\n       subsidiary. Google Netherlands Holdings pays royalty to Google Ireland Holdings\n       for the same. Google Netherlands Holdings again in turn by license agreement has\n       given the rights to use these intangible assets to Google Ireland Limited; an Ireland\n       based subsidiary. Google Ireland Limited pays royalty to Google Netherlands\n       Holdings BV. Google Ireland Limited has entered into an agreement with Google\n       India Limited on December 12, 2005 for distribution of AdWords program. In the TP\n       Report the assessee has mentioned on page 13 that as per the agreement Google\n       India has to market and distribute Ad Words Program to advertisers in the\n       designated Territory, within the broad guidelines provided by Google Ireland\n       Limited. It is mentioned on page 14 of the TP Report that Ad Words is an automated\n       system under which advertisers purchase enhanced visibility in the Google website\n       and manage their own accounts. Advertisers independently develop a set of\n       Keywords that are related to their business and bid on a <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_488\">CPC</a> basis. Advertisers\n       submit bids for the dollar amount they are willing to pay to display their\n       advertisement when those key words are queried by an internet user.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_605\">195.     The AO observed that in the TP study made by the assessee-company, an attempt\nhas been made to show that it is only a low-end service provider and it had been suitably\ncompensated by its AE. But the TPO had contradicted this characterisation and observed\n                                               IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                              IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_324\">                                         Page 254 of 331</span>\n\nthat the assessee should have furnished the FAR analysis of each service rendered to its\nAE, instead of FAR analysis of the whole group and observed as under:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_315\">     &quot;3.0 TPO&#x27;s finding: In depth study of transfer Pricing Study submitted by the\n     assessee, salient features of which have been mentioned above, has convinced the\n     undersigned that in the TP Study the attempt has been made to declare Google India\n     as low end service provider and that it has been suitably compensated by the\n     Associated Enterprises (AEs). But the facts brought on record in the TP study do not\n     support this conclusion. Therefore, the conclusion that Google India is a low end\n     service provider is in utter contradiction to what I have gathered on analysis of facts\n     given in the TP Study. In this connection, please refer to the 3CEB Report dated 30th\n     September 2009, which the assessee has filed in the Office of Additional\n     Commissioner of Income Tax 30&#x27; September2009. In the report, I find that in respect\n     to each international transaction name and address of associated enterprise with\n     whom the international transaction has been entered into has been mentioned. The\n     assessee has rendered to or taken from specific associated enterprise the specific\n     service. Accordingly, as mentioned in the 3CEB report, Information Technology\n     Services have been rendered by the assessee to Google Inc USA and the assessee\n     has determined arm&#x27;s length price (in short ALP) of the services at Rs 2,483,571,117.\n     Likewise, Information Technology Services have been rendered by the assessee to\n     Google Ireland Limited and ALP of the services has been determined at Rs\n     2,892,019,907. Payment of fees for distribution rights have been made by the\n     assessee to Google Ireland Limited and the ALP has been determined by the\n     assessee at Rs 1,681,852,823. This being the factual position in respect to services\n     rendered or taken by the assessee, the assessee should have furnished for each\n     service the FAR analysis of the assessee and the Associated Enterprise concerned\n     to whom the services have been rendered. As against it, in the TP Study FAR\n     analysis has been given of assessee on one hand and the &#x27;whole group&#x27; on the\n     other hand. FAR analysis furnished by the assessee in the TP study, therefore, is\n     not considered proper. How can the functions performed, assets used and the risks\n     incurred by the whole group can be taken in to consideration with the functions\n     performed, assets used and the risks incurred by the assessee in respect to specific\n     international transactions undertaken by the assessee with specific Associated\n     Enterprise. To be precise Google India has under taken the international\n     transactions of rendering IT and ITES with Google Inc USA and Google Ireland\n     Limited respectively, then the FAR analysis should be made of Google India and\n     Google Inc for IT services, and Google India and Google Ireland Limited for\n     ITES. Similarly in respect to distribution rights in AdWords product the FAR\n     analysis should be between Google India and Google Ireland Limited. If in FAR\n     analysis of Google India on one hand and the whole group on the other hand is\n                                       IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                        IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                      IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_325\">                                  Page 255 of 331</span>\n\nmade, Google India will always be found inferior in terms of functions performed,\nassets used and the risks incurred, as one enterprise or the other in the whole\ngroup would be performing one function or the other, using asset and incurring\ndifferent risks and thus in unison group companies as a whole would be presenting\nvery impressive picture as compared to Google India. On page 48 of TP study in\nrespect to Marketing &amp; Distribution of AdWords Program while comparative\nstudy of functions, in respect to &#x27;Product Intangible&#x27; it has been mentioned that\nfunctions related to Development of research Program, Conduct of research,\nConduct of research for improving the existing products, testing of products, and\ndevelopment of product have been performed by AE and Google India has not\nperformed these functions. It is wrong analysis. Ad Words product related rights\nhave been given to Google India by Google Ireland Limited. Google Ireland is\nnot the legal owner of AdWords Software Product. All IP rights in it belong to\nGoogle Inc.USA. Google Ireland Limited, as discussed in para 2.2 through an\narrangement which involves four layers of license agreements amongst group\ncompanies, has given distribution rights in the Ad Words Software related\ndistribution rights to Google India. These four layers at the expense of\nrepetition are shown below-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_316\">         Therefore, in this relationship Google India and Google Ireland\nLimited are relevant and between the two Google India has performed functions\nrelated to intangible product. Rather, Google India has performed significant\nfunctions whereas Google Ireland Limited has not performed any function at\nall in this regard. I find that Google India has performed IT services to\nGoolgeInc USA almost every year. Position of IT services rendered by Google\nIndia to Google Inc USA in this year and in two preceding years is as follows-\n</blockquote>\n\n<pre id=\"pre_7\">         Financial IT         services IT services Amount\n         Year      rendered by         rendered to\n          2006-07 Google India         Google Inc 1,181,826,53\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_326\">                                       USA          9</span>\n         2007-08 Google India          Google Inc 1,981,054,354\n                                       USA\n          2008-09 Google India         Google Inc   2,483,571,117\n\n</pre><blockquote id=\"blockquote_317\">Likewise Business strategy and conceptualization function has not been performed\nby Google Ireland Limited. Some group company must have performed this\nfunction but in respect to relevant international transactions in this case Google\nIndia and Google Ireland Limited are relevant Companies and their functions\nshould be performed. Therefore, it cannot be said that Google Ireland Limited has\nperformed this function and Google India has not performed this function. Market\n                                              IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                               IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                             IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_327\">                                         Page 256 of 331</span>\n\n       Research activity Google India has performed but nothing can be said about\n       Google Ireland Limited as in the study on page 48 comparisons has been made\n       with the `Whole Group&#x27; which vitiates the comparison and makes it unreliable.\n       Likewise Development of Global Marketing Strategy cannot be considered on the\n       basis of TP Study as the function of Google Ireland Limited as the study is not\n       reliable due to comparison with the whole group and not with Google India Limited\n       alone. In respect to assets, once again intangible assets on page 51 of TP study\n       have been shown as belonging to AE. But in this connection I repeat that\n       Intangibles are legally owned by Google Inc USA, whereas in respect to\n       transactions related to distribution of AdWords Program only Goolge Ireland\n       Limited is relevant and I find that these assets do not belong to Ireland Google\n       Limited. Service level Quality risk mentioned on page 52 may be incurred by either\n       the Google India which is providing services to the customers and who has direct\n       interaction with the customers or by Google Inc USA which legally owns the\n       intangible assets. As google Ireland Limited has taken the rights to distribute the\n       software based services under licensed agreement from Google Netherlands\n       HoldingsBV the risk is incurred by Google India and not by Google Ireland\n       Limited because Google Ireland Limited will shift the risk to Google Netherlands\n       Holdings BV. Likewise, ultimate deliverable quality risk lies with Google Inc USA\n       and not with Google Ireland Limited. I also hold that Software Technology risk is\n       not incurred by Google Ireland Limited as software product is legally owned by\n       Google Inc; USA. On the basis of FAR analysis dissected by me, I find that\n       characterization of Google India as a low risk service provider , who assumes very\n       less risk, performs very limited functions and does not own any capital asset or\n       valuable intangible assets as concluded on page 53 in TP study is wrong\n       conclusion and cannot be relied upon. Rather when I compare Google India with\n       Google Ireland Limited I find in terms of functions performed, assets used and\n       risks assumed, Google India is a far more active participant compared to Google\n       Ireland Limited and therefore cannot be called as low end and low risk service\n       provider.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_606\">196.      The TPO, after perusal of the agreement of Google AdWords programme\ndt.12.12.2005 entered into by the assessee-company and Google Ireland, had concluded\nthat the assessee-company is not marketing/distribution agent for Google Ireland, but\ndoing the business on its own account as it employed all the assets and assumed the risks\nassociated with the business. The TPO further observed that Google Ireland is not a\nlegal owner of AdWords Software programme and the same were acquired by it through\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_328\">                                       Page 257 of 331</span>\n\nan arrangement among the group companies and therefore in order to find out the\neconomic substance of the transaction, the TPO also analysed the functions performed\nby different units of the assessee-company and finally concluded that the ITeS business\nis closely connected with the AdWords programme as they are not separable from each\nother. The relevant finding of the TPO as given in para 3.2 of TPO&#x27;s order is reproduced\nherein below:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_318\">         &quot;3.2 In para 2.1 above brief description has been made of the kind of\n     work which has been done by different units of Google India. The\n     description is based on the discussion given in the TP study submitted by\n     the assessee. A part of it related to ITES Services and AdWords services\n     are repeated here. STPI Units at Hyderabad and Gurgaon also perform\n     (apart from IT Services) certain IT enabled services, which involve approving\n     and administering of the advertisement. Hyderabad and Gurgaon Units are\n     involved in performing back office functions for the Adwords Business such\n     as approval of advertisements for the purpose of running the same on the\n     google website. On page 13 it is mentioned that Google India has entered into\n     an agreement with Google Ireland Ltd, Ireland for distribution of Adwords\n     Program with its reasonable commercial expertise, own sales force and\n     customer service infrastructure. The Delhi and Mumbai offices consist of\n     direct sales teams (approximately 250 people in totality) which approach\n     corporate and midsized advertisers in order to sell as apace for the Ad Words\n     business. Therefore, ITES and AdWords business are not separable from each\n     other. Further, in so far Intangibles are concerned it is not the use of\n     AdWords Software Program that has contributed in earning of supernormal\n     profit but also it is assessee&#x27;s ITES efforts, dedicated sales team of 250 people\n     in Delhi and Mumbai Offices, commercial expertise of employees of Google\n     India, Customer service infrastructure established by Google India and the\n     customer base which have committed to use Google Software to give their\n     advertisements that has made equally salutary contribution in earning super\n     profit. Google India&#x27;s STPI Units at Bangalore and Hyderabad act as R&amp;D\n     Centers wherein certain Information Technology Services are performed. In\n     Bangalore contracted R&amp;D services in the nature of Google&#x27;s Product\n     development, product upgrades, product support, testing of products and\n     product improvement are performed. In Bangalore Unit search results\n     appearing on Google&#x27;s website are reviewed; it enables Google to improve\n                                             IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                              IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                            IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_329\">                                        Page 258 of 331</span>\n\n       the quality of search results for end users across the world. (Please refer to\n       search quality evaluation on page 11&amp;12). Hyderabad facility also performs\n       certain software development tasks in relations development of web-based\n       language translator. Evaluation of Google Services and tools for efficiency,\n       bugs, performance, failure and other aspects is also done in Bangalore. The\n       Software is tested by the Hyderabad unit of Google India before it is ready\n       for release. (please refer to QA Engineering on page 12). These IT services\n       rendered by Google India to Google Inc. USA have also its bearing on\n       total revenue earned by Google India. These Services cannot be called as\n       low end routine services. These services do contribute towards the success\n       of AdWords Software Program, which is legally owned by Google Inc\n       USA. Therefore, in AdWords Marketing and distribution business the\n       contribution of Google India is not routine or low end. Google India has\n       apart from rendering IT and ITES Services to Google Inc USA and Google\n       Ireland Limited respectively has contributed Intangible assets in earning\n       super profits in Adwords Business. Therefore, I find the Cost Plus Mark\n       up method used to evaluate the IT, ITES and AdWords Business efforts of\n       Google India is absolutely improper. It is not considered as acceptable.\n       This method ignores the extraordinary contribution made by Google India\n       in providing IT, ITES Services, doing Marketing and distribution business\n       and contributing Intangible assets to earn super profit. Therefore, I\n       propose to also determine the Arm&#x27;s Length Price of Intangible Assets\n       contributed by Google India.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_607\">197.     Finally the TPO concluded that the entire transactions of the assessee-company\nwith its AEs cannot be considered in isolation since the assessee also contributed towards\nthe development of intangibles, namely, market intangibles to Google Ireland Ltd., and\nTechnology intangibles to Google Inc and gives the possession of this valuable non-\nroutine intangibles. All the transactions are inter-related and inter-linked therefore, the\nTPO proposed to adopt residual profit split method as the most appropriate method.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_608\">198.      In response to the show-cause notice issued by the TPO on the above lines, the\nassessee-company made submissions vide its letter dt.04.12.2012. After considering the\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_330\">                                       Page 259 of 331</span>\n\nreply, another show-cause notice was issued by the TPO on 04.12.2012 proposing that\nthe assessee had also rendered R &amp; D services to Google Inc, USA and these services\nare high-end services and it cannot be treated as a low-end service provider. Another\nshow-cause notice dt.18.01.2013 was issued directing the assessee-company to furnish\nthe FAR analysis of the company performed by the assessee in respect of international\ntransactions disclosed in form 3 CEB report. On account of failure of the assessee-\ncompany to furnish the FAR analysis of the functions performed by the assessee-\ncompany in respect of international transactions with the AE, the TPO proposed to reject\nthe TP study submitted by the assessee-company. The TPO also proposed to apply the\nresidual profit split method, as the transactions between the Google India and Google\nIreland cannot be considered in isolation because it deployed all routine valuable\nintangibles i.e. marketing intangibles by Google India and Technology Intangible by\nGoogle Ireland Ltd., In TPO&#x27;s view, because of presence of this valuable non-routine\nintangibles and transactions being inter-related and inter-linked, had proceeded to apply\nresidual profit-split method after referring to the Guidelines of OECD on this method.\nAccordingly the TPO had proceeded to arrive at arm&#x27;s length price based on the residual\nprofit split method as under:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_319\">     &quot;6.2 Profit Split Method\n         6.2.1 the OECD Guidelines provide ,for two different profit .split methods\n     to determine taxable income associated with intercompany transactions: the\n     &#x27;comparable&#x27; profit split method and the &#x27;residual&#x27; profit split method.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_320\">               Under the comparable profit split method the split used in comparable\n     uncontrolled transaction is applied to the controlled transactions. The\n     comparable profit split compares the division of operating profit among the\n     controlled taxpayers to the division of operating profit among the uncontrolled\n     taxpayers engaged in similar activities under similar circumstances. Because of\n     the difficulty I obtaining the uncontrolled profit split information, including the\n     iulbrmation needed for consisting accounting treatment, it as likely that the\n     comparable profit split method will seldom be a viable approach.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_321\">                                         IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                          IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                               IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                        IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_331\">                                    Page 260 of 331</span>\n\n    The residual profit split method allocates operating profit or losses from\ncontrolled transactions in proportion to the relative contributions made by each\nparty in creating the combined profits or losses. Relative contributions may be\ndetermined by functions peiforined, risks assumed, resources employed, costs\nincurred and value drivers contributed to the overall business.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_322\">     According to paragraph 3.5 of the 1995 OECD report, &quot; the profit split\nmethod .first identifies the profit to be split for the associated enterprises from?,\nthe controlled transactions in which the associated enterprises are engaged. It\nthen splits those profits between the associated enterprises on an economically\nvalid basis that approximates the division of profits that would have been\nanticipated and reflected in an agreement made at arm&#x27;s length&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_609\">     The Method is generally used where multiple entities, operating in an integrated\nmanner, contribute to a large extent, to the creation of valuable business drivers that\nadd to future growth prospects of the company as a whole. Applying the profit split\nwould mean splitting any (residual) profit between the entities on all sides of\ntransaction.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_610\">     Paragraph 3.5 and 3.7 of the OECD guidelines mention that where transactions\nare very interrelated, it might be that they cannot be evaluated on a separate basis.\nIndependent companies might under similar circumstances, decide to set up a\npartnership&#x27; and agree to a form of profit split. The strength of the profit split\nmethod is that it is less likely that either party to the controlled transaction will be\nleft with an extreme and improbable profit result since all the parties to the\ntransaction are evaluated. This multi-sided approach may also be used to achieve a\ndivision of profits from economies of scale or other joint efficiencies that satisfied\nboth the taxpayer and tax administration.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_611\">     Paragraph 3.11 of the OECD guidelines stresses that if the profit split method\nwere to be used by the associated enterprises to establish transfer pricing in controlled\ntransactions, ten each associated enterprise would seek to achieve the division of prof\nthat independently would have expected to realize in a joint relationship. Generally,\nconditions established in this manner would have to be based upon projected profits\nrather\u2022 than actual profits, because it is not possible for the taxpayer to know what the\nprofits of the business activity would be at time the conditions are established.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_612\">     6.2.2 in the instant case the international transactions between Google India\nand Google Ireland are the fees paid by Google India for import of distribution\nrights, Information Technology Enabled Services rendered by Google India to\nGoogle Ireland and Provision of sales and marketing services by Google India to\nGoogle Ireland. However, the above transactions cannot be considered in isolation\nsince the parties viz Google India and Google Ireland deploy valuable intangibles\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_332\">                                     Page 261 of 331</span>\n\nviz the customer base of Google India and Technology IPR of Google Ireland. Due\nto presence of valuable non-routine intangibles and also as the transactions\n(routine transactions such as provision of contract 1TE Services and marketing\nservices and the non-routine transactions involving intangibles) are all interrelated\nand inter-linked., the residual profit split (RPSM) is selected as the most\nappropriate method.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_613\">     The residual profit split method provides a routine return for routine services\nand also accommodates a compensation for non-routine services and investment in\nnon-routine assets; hence it is considered to be the most reliable method to evaluate\nthe license fees received for Ad-words by Google Ireland, and it also provides the\nbest level of conformity to the OECD Guidelines.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_614\">                           Identification of total cost for the different activities\n                 Step 1\n Adjustment in respect to international\ntransactions with Google Ireland therefore is proposed for Rs 937,714,990.&quot;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_633\">  7.0 The Assessee Company has considered its markup in respect to ITES at arm&#x27;s length on\nthe basis of comparison of its profit margin (operating profit/operating cost) with the profit margin\nof uncontrolled comparables computed using TNMM as the most appropriate method. TPO finds\nthat the identification of uncontrolled comparables&#x27; profit margin is not proper. Therefore, the\nTPO rejects the ALP computed by the Assessee U/s 92 C (3) (c) of the <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_489\">Income Tax Act</a> 1961 and\nproceeds to compute the PLI of Uncontrolled Comparables identified by me (TPO). The detailed\ndiscussion thereon is given in annexure &#x27;A&#x27; to this show cause. It is computed at 25.04 percent.\nAccordingly, markup on ITES is computed by applying the mark up percentage of 25.04 percent\non cost-base computed by the Assessee at Rs 2,430,651,096\n\n  8.0 theAssessee Company has also entered into international transaction of IT Services. This\ninternational transaction has been entered by the Assessee with Google Inc USA. TheAssessee\nhas considered the markup of 21.25 % on cost base of Rs 2,048,315,417 as at arm&#x27;s length\nbycomparing the same with PLI of Uncontrolled Comparables using TNMM as the most appropriate\nmethod.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_634\">  8.1 In this connection on careful reading of TP Study it emerges that Google India&#x27;s STPI\nUnits at Bangalore and Hyderabad act as R&amp;D Centers wherein certain Information Technology\nServices are performed. In Bangalore contracted R&amp;D services in the nature of Google &#x27;s Product\ndevelopment, product upgrades, product support, testing of products and product improvement\nare performed. Thus R&amp;D\nishigher value added service. It cannot be considered as low end service. It is considered as\ncomparable to companies which are engaged in Knowledge Process Outsourcing Services.\nTherefore, the TPO considers that the data used to determine the ALP of IT Services is incorrect\nand not reliable. I have used Profit split method\nasmost appropriate method, which is one of the recognized method prescribed in <a href=\"/doc/20412593/\" id=\"a_492\">Section 92 C (1)</a> of the Act\n1961 and to arrive at ALP other methods used world over is permissible. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"183975450\" id=\"span_343\">In this connection it is\nrelevant to cite relevant paragraph form the landmark decision of Hon. ITAT &quot;C&quot; Bench Chennai\nin the case of <a href=\"/doc/183975450/\" id=\"a_493\">Ascendas (hdia) Private Limited Vs Dy CIT Circle</a> 1(1) Chennal (ITANo 1736/ Mds/\n201 1 AY 2007-08).</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_337\">                   <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"183975450\" id=\"span_344\">&quot;It thus appears that following one of the methods mentioned in (a) to (/)\n     above are mandatory. However, in our opinion the purpose of enactment of chapter x, is to\n     benchmark an international transaction with the Fair Market Value of such transaction, so as to\n     ensure that there are no profit transfer- between parties in different jurisdiction effectually\n     circumventing taxes. Thus, purpose of transfer pricing rules, is to verify whether the\n     prices at which an international transactions has been carried out is comparable with\n     the market value of the underlying asset or commodity or service. It may be true\n     that difficulties might arise in ascertaining the fair market value but such difficulties\n     should not be a reason for not adapting the rules and methods prescribed in t h i s\n     regard. This might require some szihte adjustments in the m e t h o d o l og y\n     prescribed for evaluation of an international transaction. A water\n     t i g h t a t t i t u d e o f i n t e r p r e t a t i o n o f t h e pres cribed met h ods will defeat th e\n     ver y p urpos e of enactment of transfer pricing rules and regulations and also\n     detrimentally affect the effective and fair administration of an international tax regime.\n     T h e i n t e r p r e t a t i o n o f t h e w o r d &#x27; s h a l l &#x27; n e e d n o t a l w a y s b e mandatory and\n     could also be read as &#x27;may&#x27;, is a rule <a href=\"/doc/183975450/\" id=\"a_494\">laid down by</a> the on. Gujarat High Court in the\n     case of CIT V Gujarat Oil &amp; Allied Industires (201 JTR 325). This is more or less the\n     same view taken by the Hon Apex C ourt in the cas e of Di r ector of Inspecti on of\n     Income Tax (Investigation) V Pooran mal &amp; Sons (96 ITR 390) and in the case of Sainik\n     Motors VS State of Rajasthan (air 1961 SC 1480). Hence, while finding the most\n     appropriate method it is not that n&#x27;aodernvaluation methods fitting the type of\n     underlyingservice or commodities have to be ignored. Fixing enterprise value based on\n     discounted value of future profits or cash is a method used worldwide. Endea vor\n     is only to arri ve at a value whichwould give a comparable uncontrolled price for\n     shares sold. If viewed from this angle, we cannot say that the discounted cash flow\n     method adopted by the TPO was not in accordance with Sec 92 C (1).</span>\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_338\">   3. However, Hon&#x27;ble DRP held\nthat the activities of ITeS and AdWords distribution programme segment should be\nbenchmarked separately. However, upheld appropriateness of various filters applied by\nthe TPO in the selection of comparables in ITeS segment and also upheld the\ncomparables selected by the TPO.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_644\">204. AdWords Segment:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_346\">      Hon&#x27;ble DRP held that profit split method should be adopted as most appropriate\nmethod. However, for the purpose of arriving at over-all profits earned by the group,\nexpenses incurred by Google Ireland should be factored. Hon&#x27;ble DRP granted relief in\nrespect of 10A deduction by directing the AO to reduce telecommunication expenses\nincurred in foreign currency both from export turnover as well as total turnover.\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_645\">205. In respect of disallowance<a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_500\"> u/s 40(a)(ia)</a> of the Act, Hon&#x27;ble DRP directed AO/TPO\nto restrict disallowance by reducing the amount of TP adjustment. Hon&#x27;ble DRP also\ngranted relief in respect of addition made on account of accretion of additional income\nto AdWords programme by holding that the transactions in relation to distribution of\nAdWord programme is also bench marked.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_646\">                                                 IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                       IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_348\">                                            Page 275 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_647\">206. After receipt of the Hon&#x27;ble DRP&#x27;s order, final assessment order was passed vide\norder dated 31/01/2014<a href=\"/doc/144280341/\" id=\"a_501\"> u/s 143(3)</a> r.w.s. 144C of the Act. Ground Nos.1 to 8 are general in nature relating to TP adjustment and were not\npressed during the course of hearing, hence dismissed as such.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_690\">210.        Ground No.9 challenges FAR analysis and functions of the assessee-company\nundertaken by the TPO and re-characterising activities of the assessee-company as only\nKnowledge Process Outsourcing (KPO) instead of ITeS. It is submitted that the\nassessee-company had entered into service agreement with Google Ireland on\n01.01.2006 whereby the assessee-company had agreed to provide IT services to Google\nInc. USA. Copy of the agreement is placed at pages 1 to 14 of the paper book.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_691\">211. It is submitted that the units at Bangalore and Hyderabad act as R&amp;D centre for\nGoogle Ireland from where certain IT services are provided. It was further submitted\nthat this service involved evaluation of Google services and tools for efficiency, bugs,\nperformance, failure and other aspects. The software is tested by the Hyderabad unit of\nGIPL before it is ready for release. The results of evaluation are reported to the\ndevelopment team abroad in the form of reports, mails, oral/non documented forms and\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_353\">                                       Page 280 of 331</span>\n\nother modes of technical communication. In consideration for the said services, assessee-\ncompany is compensated on a cost plus 17.50% mark-up basis.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_692\">212.     With regard to the above international transaction, the assessee-company has\ncarried out detailed functional analysis covering functions performed, assets utilized and\nthe risks assumed by assessee-company and its AE i.e. Google Inc. and the same has\nbeen submitted to the TPO as part of TP documentation.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_693\">213. Based on the FAR analysis, TNMM was selected as the most appropriate method\nfor the international transaction relating to rendering of IT services. The economic\nanalysis was conducted by the assessee-company to identify external comparable\ncompanies engaged in similar services. Based on the comparability analysis, a set of 16\ncomparable companies was identified.          The three year&#x27;s average OP/TC of the\ncomparable companies was computed at 16.46% as compared to OP/TC of 21.25%\nearned by the assessee-company. Hence, the assessee-company concluded that the\ninternational transaction in IT services segment was at arm&#x27;s length.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_694\">214. The TPO had rejected the FAR analysis conducted by the assessee-company in\nthe TP study and re-characterised the activities/functions and performance of the\nassessee-company as KPO service instead of IT services. The TPO observed that\nBengaluru and Hyderabad STP units of the assessee-company act as R&amp;D centres, which\nare in the nature of product development, product upgrades, product support, testing of\nproducts and product improvement. This approach of the TPO was confirmed by the\nHon&#x27;ble DRP.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_695\">                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_354\">                                      Page 281 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_696\">215. Now before us, it is contended that the assessee-company provides IT services\nwhich involve evaluation of Google products and tools for efficiency, bugs,\nperformance, failures and other aspects, software development tasks in relation to\ndevelopment of web based language translator, etc. The TPO failed to understand that\nthe assessee-company performs IT services in the role of a contract developer wherein\nthe assessee-company is not exposed to any market risk or commercial risk for the\nproduct and accordingly, remunerated as assured return on a cost plus mark-up basis.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_697\">216. It was further submitted that the functions of KPO fall under category of ITeS. It\nis further submitted that the modules worked upon by the assessee-company form very\ninsignificant part of Google products and major significant functions are done by Google\nIreland, USA for development of end product and there was no-end product developed\nin India and Google patent search engine was developed much before setting up of the\nassessee-company in India. It was further         submitted that in the earlier years i.e.\nassessment years 2005-06 to 2008-09, these activities have been characterised by the\nTribunal as IT services and the same was confirmed by this Tribunal and there was no\nchange in the functional profile of the assessee-company during the previous year\nrelevant to assessment year under consideration from earlier assessment year. Thus it\nwas contended that the TPO was not justified in re-characterising the functions of the\nassessee-company as KPO services. Further, learned AR of the assessee-company also\ndrawn our attention to the definition of the term &#x27;KPO&#x27; and &#x27;software development&#x27; as\ndefined under rule 10T(a)(g) of the Safe Harbour Rules.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_698\">217. On the issue of selection of comparable by the TPO, it was submitted that the\ncompany M/s.Acropetal Ltd. cannot be compared with that of the assessee-company as\nit is into the health-care software and allied products and services and also into\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_355\">                                       Page 282 of 331</span>\n\ndevelopment of products. The company has got very high intangibles and no segmental\ninformation is available. Therefore, this company cannot be compared with that of the\nassessee-company.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_699\">218. As regards M/s.Coral Hub Ltd., it is submitted that this company is into services\nlike sale of books, preparation of financial statement, liquidity monitoring etc., which\ncannot be compared with that of the assessee-company and it was also submitted that\nthis company outsources segmental portion of its work to outside people. Therefore, this\ncompany cannot be compared with that of the assessee-company.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_700\">219. Similarly, as regards M/s.Eclerx Services Ltd. this company was into business of\nconsultancy and advisory services and provide analytic services and no segmental\ninformation is available. This company also cannot be compared. Therefore, this\ncompany cannot be compared with that of the assessee-company.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_701\">220. Regarding ITeS Services, learned AR of the assessee-company submitted that the\nservices rendered in terms of agreement entered into by the assessee-company with\nGoogle Ireland on 01.04.2004 and in consideration of such services, the assessee-\ncompany was remunerated at cost + mark up of 15.5%. The nature of ITeS was\nsubmitted as under:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_359\">     Information Technology Services and Information Technology Enabled\n     Services:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_360\">     Google India has registered 3 units (Bangalore, Hyderabad and Gurgaon) with the\n     Software Technology Parks of India (&#x27;STPI&#x27;) and from these units, the company\n     provides Information Technology services and IT enabled services. Google India\n     has entered into agreements with Google Ireland Limited and Google Inc to provide\n                                         IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                          IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                               IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                        IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_356\">                                    Page 283 of 331</span>\n\nIT and ITeS services.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_361\">Google India&#x27;s STPI units at Bangalore and Hyderabad act as R&amp;D centers for\nGoogle group wherein certain Information Technology services are performed.\nThe Bangalore facility started with low-level routine software development tasks\nrelated to various Google products like Google Maps, Google Earth, Scheduler,\nwhich extended t\u00e7nxia\u00e7d R&amp;D services in the nature of Google&#x27;s product\ndevelopment, product upgrades, product support, testing of products and product\nimprovements. The services are:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_362\">Search quality evaluation :\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_363\">This service involves the review of search results appearing on Google&#x27;s website,\nwhich enables Google to make decisions that are designed to improve the quality of\nsearch results for end users across the world.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_364\">QA engineering\n\nThis service involves the evaluation of Google services and tools for efficiency,\nbugs, performance, failure and other aspects. The software is tested by the\nHyderabad unit of Google India before it is ready for release. The results of\nevaluation are reported to the development team abroad in the form of reports, mails,\noral/non-documented forms and other modes of technical communication.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_365\">The STPI units at Hyderabad and Gurgaon also perform certain IT enabled services,\nprimarily relate to performance of all such activities which involve approving and\nadministering of the Advertisements in line with certain editorial and other relevant\nguidelines.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_366\">These units are involved in performing back office functions for the AdWords\nbusiness, as described below:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_367\">Approvals for advertisements for the purpose of running the same on the Google\nwebsite\n\nThese services mainly involve IT-enabled data processing work. The data comes\nfrom the customer and is routed to the Hyderabad unit through a workflow based\non language and tier. On receiving the data, the AdWords team reviews\nthe advertisement according to company policy. The resulting advertisement\napproval means that a customer&#x27;s advertisement can now be viewed on the web sites\n                                              IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                               IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                             IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_357\">                                         Page 284 of 331</span>\n\n     of Google and or its partners located outside India.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_368\">     Further, the Hyderabad facility also performs certain software development tasks\n     in relation to development of a web-based language translator. As regards ground Nos.16 to 23 raised by the assessee-company, it challenges the\nresidual profit split method applied by the TPO by rejecting the TNMM selected by the\nassessee company. It was further contended that, the mandatory\nconditions for application of profit split method, are not satisfied in the present case as\nthere is no transfer of unique intangibles is involved, and multiple international\ntransactions which are so inter-related that they cannot be evaluated separately for the\npurpose of determining the ALP of any one transaction.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_717\">233. It was further submitted that the assessee company had submitted the &#x27;note&#x27;\non the nature of activities carried out by the assessee-company in IT segment under the\nsearch quality evaluation and QA engineering. The evaluation\nresults are reported to development wing abroad in the form of reports, e-mails etc.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_718\">234.
