As regards the characterisation done in the\nearlier years, it was submitted that, in the earlier assessment years, there was no detailed\ndiscussion by the TPOs in respect of the nature of services rendered by the assessee-\ncompany in this segment. Therefore, in the absence of these discussions one cannot\ncome to a conclusion on the characterisation of the functions undertaken by the assessee-\ncompany and the principle of res-judicata has no application to the facts of the case as\neach year is separate and distinct.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_719\">                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_364\">                                      Page 290 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_720\">235. ITeS :\n</p>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_376\">      The learned CIT(DR) submitted that the services rendered in ITeS were concerned\nwith AdWords Distribution Programme and therefore the activities of ITeS services and\nAdWords Distribution Programme are inter-linked, inter-related and in-separable.\nTherefore, it was argued that the Hon&#x27;ble DRP was not justified in directing the AO/TPO\nto bench mark the activities under the ITeS segment and AdWords Distribution\nProgramme. It was further submitted that there was inconsistency in the approach of\nHon&#x27;ble DRP while directing the TPO to aggregate the international transactions relating\nto the distribution of AdWords programme and ITeS. However, the Hon&#x27;ble DRP took\na contrary stand by holding that ITeS and AdWords segment are to be bench marked\nseparately. In this connection, learned CIT(DR) had invited our attention to clauses 2.3,\n2.5, 2.6 and 3.4 of the Agreement entered into by the assessee-company with Google\nIreland in respect of AdWords Distribution Programme, dt.12.12.2005 to demonstrate\nthat the activities of ITES are closely inter-linked with the activity of AdWords\nDistribution Programme. It is submitted that the assessee-company set up STP units at\nGurgaon and Hyderabad which involved services like administration, advertisement and\nother back-office functions such as after sales support for AdWords business. The ITeS\nperformed at STPI units at Bengaluru and Hyderabad added value addition to Google\nInc. Through its operations the assessee company had contributed intangible assets of\ncustomer base, marketing team to Google Inc and Google Ireland. Google Ireland has\nonly contributed to AdWords business is not correct. It has generated intangibles i.e.,\nAdWords programme, which was in fact, not owned by it, but was only a sub-licensee.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_721\">Based on this analysis of functions performed by the assessee-company the TPO had\narrived at the conclusion that the assessee-company was not compensated adequately for\nits contribution to marketing intangibles. Whereas the AE i.e., Google Ireland was paid\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_365\">                                       Page 291 of 331</span>\n\ndistribution fee of more than what would have otherwise been paid in the uncontrolled\nsituation. Thus it was submitted that the TPO was justified in adopting residual profit\nsplit method, wherein the profits from the routine activities like ITeS was reduced from\nthe net revenue earned by the assessee-company on AdWords Distribution Programme\nand thereafter divide the profit between the assessee company and Google Ireland in the\nratio of 50 : 50.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_722\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"194564509\" id=\"span_366\">236. It was further submitted by the learned CIT(DR) that the Hon&#x27;ble DRP also has\nconfirmed this method. However, the Hon&#x27;ble DRP directed the TPO to apply the profit\nsplit method of residual profit split method. He argued that having regard to the FAR\nanalysis all the functions performed by the assessee, it is clear that the assessee company\nalso contributed towards the advertisement of marketing intangibles which, in turn,\ncontributed to the development of AdWords business profits and the methodology\nadopted to compensate the assessee company on cost-plus basis, is not proper. In such\ncircumstances, profit split method is the most appropriate method for determining the\nALP of the distribution fees. He further submitted that the profit split method is one of\nthe recognised methods for determination of ALP where the assessee generates revenue\nout of the operations that are highly integrated and where there is a transfer of technology\nintangibles. Reliance was also placed on the judgment of the Hon&#x27;ble Delhi High Court\nin the case of <a href=\"/doc/194564509/\" id=\"a_517\">Global One India P. Ltd v. ACIT</a> (44 taxmann.com 100). He also disputed\nthe submission made by the Ld. AR that only twenty persons were employed for\nmarketing the AdWords Distribution Programme.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_723\">237. We heard rival submissions and perused material on record. As regards TP\nadjustment, the TPO had rejected the TP study report submitted by the assessee-\ncompany on the ground that there was no separate FAR analysis of each function\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_367\">                                      Page 292 of 331</span>\n\nperformed with AEs. The TP study contains FAR analysis of the functions performed.\nNeedless to say the TP analysis should be ideally made on a transaction by transaction\nbasis except in cases where transactions are so closely inter-related or continuous that\napplication of arm&#x27;s length principle on a transaction by transaction basis would be\nunreliable or cumbersome. Only in those cases the transactions are aggregated.\nTherefore, the TPO was justified in rejecting the TP study submitted by the assessee-\ncompany. However, from perusal of TPO order it is clear that the TPO made TP analysis\nwithout referring to any evidence or actual conduct of the parties. Thus, in our\nconsidered opinion neither the assessee-company nor the TPO had approached the issue\nin proper perspective and therefore in the absence of proper TP analysis in proper\nperspective, the comparability of international transaction cannot be judged. Therefore,\nin fitness of things, we remand the entire TP issues to the file of the TPO/AO for\nundertaking the exercise of TP analysis afresh after affording opportunity to the\nassessee-company to furnish TP study report covering the entire functions performed,\nassets employed and risks undertaken transaction-wise in respect of international\ntransaction with AE.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_724\">238. After the TP analysis on the above lines, the TPO shall aggregate the functions\nwhich has a direct nexus with core business activity i.e. AdWords distribution\nprogramme. We had already held in the appeal against order passed<a href=\"/doc/1049794/\" id=\"a_518\"> u/s 201(1)</a> that the\nactivities under ITeS services are integral part of AdWords distribution programme.\nFurther the TPO shall examine whether the functions of the assessee-company resulted\nin creation of intangibles i.e.   marketing intangibles or technological intangibles.\nWhether these intangibles so created are owned and used by the assessee-company itself\nor transferred to any other entity and whether the assessee-company is properly\ncompensated for transferring such intangibles. The findings of the TPO shall be based\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_368\">                                       Page 293 of 331</span>\n\non the actual conduct of the parties and evidence produced by the assessee-company.\nThe characterisation of functions cannot be based on not merely terms of contract or\ndescription of the services given by the assessee-company. It has to be determined\nhaving regard to the actual conduct of the parties. It is pertinent to state that the AO/TPO\nshall have regard to the guidelines issued by the CBDT vide circular No.6 of 2013 dated\n29th June 2013 in deciding the functional profile of the assessee-company. In respect of\ntransactions independent of AdWords distribution programme, the characterisation of\nthe functions performed shall be based on the actual conduct of the parties and actual\nactivities performed. The comparability of all these independent transactions shall be\njudged with reference to uncontrolled similar transaction by adopting most appropriate\nmethod and comparability factors. In respect of transactions aggregated with AdWords\nbusiness transaction, the TPO shall bench-mark the transaction by adopting Profit Split\nMethod as most appropriate method, as the transaction of business of AdWords\nprogramme requires deployment of assets and functions of different entities located in\ndifferent geographical locations in order to ultimately deliver services as the combined\neffort generate revenues. It is also settled proposition of law that Profit Slip Method\n(PSM) can be adopted as most appropriate method in cases involving multiple inter-\nrelated international transactions which cannot be evaluated separately. Reliance in this\nregard can be placed on the decision of the co-ordinate bench in the case of Orange\nBusiness Services India Networks (P) Ltd. Vs. Dy.CIT (2015) 63 taxmann.com 304\n(Delhi-Trib), Global One India (P.) <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"25524996\" id=\"span_369\"><a href=\"/doc/25524996/\" id=\"a_519\">Ltd. Vs. Asst</a>. CIT (2014) 44 taxmann.com\n100(Delhi-Trib). Thus, all the grounds of appeal relating to TP adjustment are set aside\nto the file of the AO/TPO for de novo assessment after affording due opportunity of\nbeing heard. These grounds of appeal are partly allowed for statistical purposes.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_725\">                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_370\">                                       Page 294 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_726\">239. We consider it not necessary to deal with the selection of comparables, as we\nrestored to the file of the AO/TPO to decide the issue of characterisation of the functional\nprofile of the assessee-company in respective segments.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_727\">240. In ground No.24, the assessee-company challenges rejection of book results. The\nAO had noticed that the assessee-company had not credited the gross receipts on account\nof AdWords business to its P &amp; L account, but credited only the share of advertisement\nrevenue. Considering this, show-cause notice dt.01.01.2013, was issued to the assessee-\ncompany, calling upon to explain why all the receipts from AdWords business were not\naccounted on gross basis in the P &amp; L account filed and also explain as to why the P &amp;\nL account should not be recast in accordance with the Accounting Standards and further\nexplain as to why the books should not be rejected as it has failed to follow the standard\naccounting practices.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_728\">241. The assessee-company filed explanation to the show- cause notice, vide letter\ndt.15.02.2013, stating that its books of account were duly audited by the chartered\naccountants who had opined that the books of account have been prepared in conformity\nwith the standard auditing practices and the income has been disclosed on net basis and\nthe assessee-company has offered entire income from distribution service to tax.\nTherefore, it was contended that there was no need for recasting the P &amp; L account or\nre-working the profits of the company. It was further contended that there was no need\nfor rejecting the books<a href=\"/doc/488246/\" id=\"a_520\"> u/s.145</a>, as the conditions for invoking <a href=\"/doc/488246/\" id=\"a_521\">section 145</a> were not\nsatisfied in the present case. It was further contended that the books of account are\ncorrect and complete and the correct method of accounting has been followed by the\nassessee. The assessee-company had complied with the accounting standards notified\nby the Central Government and the same method of account has been regularly followed\n                                          IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                           IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                         IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_371\">                                     Page 295 of 331</span>\n\nfrom year to year. However, the AO rejecting the above contentions, held that the books\nare liable for rejection, vide para.17 of the assessment order which reads as under and\nrecast the P &amp; L account :\n</p>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_377\">     &quot;17. The submissions made by the assessee company are considered. It\n     is seen that as per this contract dated 12-12-2005 with M/s. Google\n     Ireland Ltd, the assessee company was required to conduct the Adwords\n     marketing on its own account and as an independent distributor of the\n     said programme. The contract mentions in as many words that the\n     Adwordsprogramme is sold by the assessee for its own account, in its own\n     name, and not as an agent, employee, partner or franchisee of Google&quot;.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_729\">     The various other clauses of the said agreement also make if clear that\n     the assessee company was required to run the businesses on its own\n     account. Moreover the assessee company was expected to pay &quot;fees for\n     distribution right&#x27; to M/s. Google and all this fees and payments were\n     made subject to DTAA and Indian Tax Laws, The assessee is an Indian\n     company incorporated under the <a href=\"/doc/1353758/\" id=\"a_522\">Companies Act</a> with the Registrar of\n     companies. The Indian companies Act makes the accounting standards\n     given by Institute of Chartered Accountants of India (ICA) mandatory for all\n     the Indian companies. The revenue generated from the said activity\n     must be declared in its entirety on a gross basis as per the accounting\n     principles. The assessee company has failed to do so and has claimed that\n     the revenue shown as per Profit and Loss account filed is as per a non-\n     existent Indian &#x27;GAAP&#x27; accounting norms. By claiming to show the revenue\n     on a net basis, the assessee company has tried to avoid\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_730\">      a)   The TDS provisions applicable<a href=\"/doc/1329583/\" id=\"a_523\"> u/s. 195</a> of the Income Tax Act,\n           specifically with respect to fees paid to Google Ireland ltd.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_731\">      b)   Working out and showing correct income based on the gross revenue\n           received by the assessee year on year.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_732\">      c)    Showing correct revenue for the purpose of Transfer Pricing\n           adjustment which is on a markup basis and correct Transfer Pricing\n           evaluation method.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_733\">                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_372\">                                       Page 296 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_734\">          d) Correct and proper method of arriving at ALP adjustment by the\n             TPO of the department.&quot;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_735\">  The findings of the AO have been affirmed by the Hon&#x27;ble DRP.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_736\">242. Being aggrieved the assessee-company is in appeal before us with the present\nappeal.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_737\">243. The learned AR for the assessee vehemently contended that the relationship\nbetween Google Ireland and assessee-company is of principal to principal basis. He\nfurther contended that the AO was not justified in rejecting the book results without\ndischarging the onus lying upon it to prove that the books of account maintained by the\nassessee were not correct and complete. It was further argued that the assessee company\nmade a complete disclosure in the financial statements prepared in accordance with the\nprescribed accounting standard issued by the Institute of Chartered Accountants of India\nand there was no change in the accounting policy followed by the assessee from the\nearlier years and the financial statements have been duly audited by the statutory auditor\nwho, after due verification of the books of account opined that the financial statements\nof the assessee are prepared in conformity with the accounting principles generally\naccepted. The AO was not justified in rejecting the books of account without bringing\nany evidence on record to demonstrate that the conditions specified in sub-section (3) of\n<a href=\"/doc/488246/\" id=\"a_524\">Section 145</a> are met. Further the Ld. Counsel submitted that the profit computed by the\nAO even after recasting the accounts is the same as computed by the assessee company.\nThus, it was contended that the AO was not justified in invoking the provisions of\n<a href=\"/doc/488246/\" id=\"a_525\">Section 145</a> of the Act.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_738\">                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_373\">                                       Page 297 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_739\">244. On the other hand, the Ld. Sr. Counsel for the Revenue vehemently contended\nthat the AO was justified in rejecting the books of account inasmuch as the financial\nstatements prepared by the assessee company do not reflect the complete picture of the\nbusiness operations of the assessee.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_740\">245. We heard the rival submissions and perused the material on record. From the\nmere perusal of the assessment order, it is clear that the AO though mentioned that the\nprovisions of <a href=\"/doc/488246/\" id=\"a_526\">Section 145</a> were invoked for the purpose of recasting the profit and loss\naccount to show the gross receipts of the assessee-company from the AdWords business\noperations, this, in effect has no material impact or change on the profits disclosed by\nthe assessee company. The difference in the method adopted by the AO and the assessee\ncompany is only in the matter of presentation of the financial statements. It has no\nimpact on the profit or loss disclosed by the assessee company. The issue becomes only\nacademic in nature and hence we hold that the rejection of books of account is not proper.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_741\">246. In ground Nos.25 to 32, the assessee-company challenges the disallowance made\nby the AO<a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_527\"> u/s.40(a)(ia)</a> of the Act. The AO also disallowed the payment made to Google\nIreland by the assessee company on account of distribution fees paid to it on the ground\nthat no tax was deducted at source. The same was confirmed by the Hon&#x27;ble DRP.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_742\">247. Being aggrieved, assessee-company is in appeal in the present appeal.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_743\">248. The learned Senior Counsel for assessee vehemently argued that when the AO had\ninvoked the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/488246/\" id=\"a_528\">Section 145</a>, no further disallowance invoking the provisions\nof <a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_529\">Section 40(a)(ia)</a> can be made. In this regard, reliance was placed on the following\ndecisions:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_378\">   <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"40260516\" id=\"span_374\">\uf0b7 <a href=\"/doc/40260516/\" id=\"a_530\">Teja Constructions v. ACIT</a> [(2010) 29 TTJ 57 (Hyd - Trib)]\n                                           IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                            IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos.</span> 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                          IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_375\">                                      Page 298 of 331</span>\n\n\n   \uf0b7 Indwell Constructions v. CIT [(1999) 151 CTR (AP HC)\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_744\">249. It was further argued that the AO had not brought any evidence on record to justify\nthat payments made by the assessee-company to Google Ireland in the form of\ndistribution fee is in the nature of Royalty. It is further contended that the evidence\nbrought on record by the assessee-company, the agreement between the assessee and\nGoogle Ireland and the agreement between the assessee and the Indian advertiser clearly\nestablish that assessee-company acquired right to market and distribute Google\nAdWords programme. The assessee-company had acted on its own and not on behalf of\nGoogle Ireland. It is further submitted that if the contention of the Revenue is to be\naccepted that the assessee-company is only a dependent agent PE of Google Ireland Ltd,\nthe question of disallowance<a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_531\"> u/s.40(a)(ia)</a> does not arise. Reliance in support of this\nproposition was placed on the following decisions :\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_379\">   <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"1351789\" id=\"span_376\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"356373\" id=\"span_377\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"819875\" id=\"span_378\">\uf0b7 <a href=\"/doc/819875/\" id=\"a_532\">CIT v. Heros Publicity Services</a> [(2001) 248 ITR 256 (Bom)]\n\n   \uf0b7 SET Satellite (Singapore) P. Ltd [307 ITR 205]\n\n   \uf0b7 <a href=\"/doc/356373/\" id=\"a_533\">CIT v. Century Building Industries P. Ltd</a> [(2007) 293 ITR 80 (Kar)]\n\n   \uf0b7 <a href=\"/doc/1351789/\" id=\"a_534\">CIT v. Cargo Linkers</a> [(2008) 218 CTR (Del) 695]</span></span></span>\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_745\">250. Without prejudice to the above, it was further contended that the reasoning given\nby the TDS officer in the orders passed<a href=\"/doc/1049794/\" id=\"a_535\"> u/s. 201(1)</a>, cannot be upheld in the light of the\naffidavit dt.14.03.2018 filed by the director of the assessee company, submissions made\non the factual aspect of the issue before the bench on 19.03.2018 and the legal\nsubmissions made dt.20.03.2018 in the assessee&#x27;s appeals against the 201(1) order. The\nassessee also placed reliance on the following decisions of the coordinate benches :\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_380\">   <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1919127\" id=\"span_379\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"131633874\" id=\"span_380\">\uf0b7 <a href=\"/doc/131633874/\" id=\"a_536\">Pinstorm Technologies P. Ltd v. ITO</a> [(2012) 54 SOT 78 (Mumbai)]\n   \uf0b7 <a href=\"/doc/1919127/\" id=\"a_537\">ITO v. Right Florists (P) Ltd</a> [(2013) 25 ITR (T) 639 (Kol)]\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos.</span></span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"89613940\" id=\"span_381\">69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_382\">                                       Page 299 of 331</span>\n\n\n   \uf0b7 <a href=\"/doc/89613940/\" id=\"a_538\">Yahoo India P. Ltd v. DCIT</a> [(2011 46 SOT 105 (Mum)</span>\n\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_746\">251. Further the learned Senior Counsel for assessee submitted that when the assessee\ncompany is under bona fide belief that it was not under obligation to deduct tax at source\non the impugned payments, no disallowance<a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_539\"> u/s.40(a)(ia)</a> of the Act is not called for. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"159132010\" id=\"span_383\">In\nsupport of this submission he relied on the Hon&#x27;ble Bombay High Court decision in the\ncase of <a href=\"/doc/159132010/\" id=\"a_540\">CIT vs. Kotak Securities Ltd.</a> (340 ITR 333)</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_747\">252. On the other hand, the Learned Senior Standing Counsel vehemently contended\nthat the payment made by the assessee- company to Google Ireland in terms of the\nAdWords Agreement entered into is in the nature of licence fee paid to its various AEs\nand therefore the payment constitutes royalty which is liable for deduction of tax<a href=\"/doc/1329583/\" id=\"a_541\"> u/s.195</a>\nof the Act. Since the assessee had failed to deduct tax at source the assessee-company\nhas been rightly treated as an assessee in default<a href=\"/doc/1049794/\" id=\"a_542\"> u/s.201(1)</a> of the Act. He reiterated the\nsame submissions made in the appeals against the orders<a href=\"/doc/1049794/\" id=\"a_543\"> u/s.201(1)</a> of the Act, to say\nthat the payments made by the assessee company to Google Ireland constitute &#x27;Royalty&#x27;.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_748\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"159132010\" id=\"span_384\">253. We heard the rival submissions and perused the material on record. In the appeals,\nfiled by the assessee-company in ITA Nos.1190/Bang/2014, 949 &amp; 950/Bang/2017\nagainst the orders passed<a href=\"/doc/1049794/\" id=\"a_544\"> u/s.201(1)</a> of the Act, after considering the relevant facts and\nmaterial on record, it was held by us that the payments made by the assessee-company\nto Google Ireland in terms of AdWords Distribution Programme constitutes &#x27;Royalty&#x27;\nand therefore the assessee-company is under an obligation to deduct tax at source<a href=\"/doc/1329583/\" id=\"a_545\"> u/s.195</a>\nof the Act. Consequent to the failure by the assessee-company to do so, disallowance<a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_546\">\nu/s.40(a)(ia)</a> of the Act is automatically attracted. The plea of the assessee-company\nthat when it is under bona fide belief that there was no obligation to deduct tax at source\n                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_385\">                                       Page 300 of 331</span>\n\nu/s.195, cannot be accepted as no factual foundation was laid before us or lower\nauthorities as to how the assessee-company entertained bona fide belief that it was not\nunder obligation to deduct tax at source on such payments. The ratio of the decision of\nthe Hon&#x27;ble Bombay High Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/159132010/\" id=\"a_547\">Kotak Securities Ltd.</a> (supra) cannot be\napplied to the facts of the present case as the Hon&#x27;ble Bombay High Court in the said\ncase granted relief to the assessee considering the fact that payee has already paid the\ntax and nearly for a period of decade both revenue and the assessee proceeded on a\nfooting that provisions of <a href=\"/doc/503717/\" id=\"a_548\">section 194J</a> was not applicable to the payment of transaction\ncharges. It is also pertinent to note that the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_549\">section 40(a)(ia)</a> starts with\nnon obstante clause. A non- obstante clause is usually used in a provision to indicate\nthat the provision should prevail despite anything to the contrary in the mentioned in\nsuch non-obstante clause.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"743169\" id=\"span_386\">In case there is any inconsistency or a departure between the\nnon-obstante clause and another provision, one of the objects of such a clause is to\nindicate that it is the non-obstante clause which would prevail over teh other clause [see\n<a href=\"/doc/743169/\" id=\"a_550\">CIT vs. Navabharat Enterprises (P) Ltd. (No.2</a>)(1988) 170 ITR 332)(AP)] Therefore,\nthe plea of bona fide belief has no place in the non-obstante provisions of the Act.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_749\">254. We accordingly confirm the order of the DRP in this regard\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_750\">255. The miscellaneous ground of appeal raised by the assessee-company relates to\nchallenge of levy of interest<a href=\"/doc/380949/\" id=\"a_551\"> u/s.234B</a> and initiation of penalty proceedings<a href=\"/doc/144487481/\" id=\"a_552\"> u/s.271(1)(c)</a>\nof the Act. These are all consequential in nature, which do not require any adjudication,\nhence, dismissed as such.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_751\">256. In the result, the appeals filed by the assessee-company is partly allowed for\nstatistical purposes.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_752\">                                                 IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                       IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_387\">                                            Page 301 of 331</span>\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_753\">257. IT(TP)A No.191/Bang/2014 [Revenue&#x27;s appeal for assessment year 2009-10]:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_381\">      The revenue raised the following grounds of appeal:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_382\">   1. &quot;The order of the Dispute Resolution Panel is opposed to law and the facts and circumstances\n      of the case.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_383\">   2. The DRP erred in not accepting the application of the residual profit method wherein the joint\n      profit of the assessee and M/s Google Ireland Ltd. has been computed by the TPO.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_384\">   3. The DRIP erred in concluding that the action of the TPO in merging the two segments and then\n      giving a rebate from the ITES profits from the residual profits of the two parties as not proper\n      without appreciating that the had calculated profit on ITES activities which was not being\n      deducted from the residual profit.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_385\">   4. The DRP erred in holding that the assessee has rendered ITES to a wider client base without\n      appreciating that as per records the assessee has rendered ITES to M/s Google Ireland Ltd.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_754\">      only and that as per the 3CEB report also ITES has been rendered by the assessee to M/s Google\n      Ireland Ltd. only.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_755\">   5. The DRP erred in holding that for implementing residual profit split method the normal profit\n      of M/s Google Ireland Ltd. also needs to computed and reduced without appreciating that there\n      is no routine profit of M/s Google Ireland Ltd. as no other functions have been performed by\n      M/s Google Ireland Ltd. apart from contributing only the tangibles which is a non-routine\n      function and for which the TPO had already attributed 50% of residual profit.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_756\">   6. The DRP erred in holding that for the purposes of bench marking, ITES and Adwords\n      distribution segments are to be treated separately, without appreciating that the international\n      transactions relating to ITES and Adwords are connected to each other and cannot be analyzed\n      and evaluated separately for determination of ALP.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_757\">   7. The DRP erred in directing to adopt profit split method for Adwords distribution segment\n      instead of profit split method without appreciating the fact that the TPO had demonstrated the\n      necessary evidences for the various functions the assessee has performed such as rendering\n      ITES to M/s Google Ireland Ltd., providing marketing services and contribution of marketing\n      tangibles while M/s Google Ireland Ltd. has only contributed the technology related\n      intangibles.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_758\">   8. The Hon&#x27;ble DRP erred in directing the AO to follow the ratio <a href=\"/doc/743169/\" id=\"a_553\">laid down by</a> the Hon&#x27;ble Court\n      in the case Tata Elxsi Limited 349 ITR 98 and exclude telecommunication expenses of Rs.\n      10,99,83,459/- from export turnover also while computing the deduction u/s 1OA of the I.T.Act\n      without appreciating the fact that there is no provision in <a href=\"/doc/1904729/\" id=\"a_554\">section 10A</a> that such expenses should\n      be the total turnover also, as clause (iv) of the explanation to <a href=\"/doc/1904729/\" id=\"a_555\">section 10A</a> provides that such\n                                               IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                              IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_388\">                                         Page 302 of 331</span>\n\n   expenses are to be reduced only from the export turnover.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_759\">9. The DRP erred in not appreciating the fact that the jurisdictional High Court&#x27;s decision in the\n   case of Tata Elxsi Limited 349 ITR 98 has not been accepted by the department and an appeal\n   has been filed before the Hon&#x27;ble Supreme Court.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_760\">10. The DRP erred in directing to exclude Coral Hubs on the ground of functional incomparability\n    without appreciating the fact that TNMM was applied as the most appropriate method and while\n    applying TNMM the functions of the comparable need not be identical but a broad comparison\n    is sufficient as many of the differences are evened out at the net margin level.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_761\">11. The DRP erred in directing that unallocated expenditure be taken into consideration in the case\n    of Acropetal Technologies without appreciating that the comparable company itself has not\n    taken the unallocated expenditure as operating expenditure while publishing the segmental\n    results.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_762\">12. The DRP erred in not appreciating the fact that since M/s Google Ireland Ltd. has neither filed\n    its return of income nor was willing to admit any tax liability in India. Hence, the additional\n    profit of Rs. 30,65,47,268/- has been taxed rightly in the hands of the assessee.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_763\">13. The DRP erred in not appreciating the fact the additional profit of Rs. 30,65,47,268/- represents\n    the profits evaded from being charged to income tax in India of the service, Google Adwords\n    run through the medium of Google website in India.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_764\">14. The DRP erred in not appreciating the fact as per the contract the assessee is neither a captive\n    service provider nor a cost centre to M/s Google Ireland Ltd but the assessee claims to have\n    been an independent distributor acting on its own account.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_765\">15. The DRP, on the facts and in the circumstance of the case erred in not appreciating the fact\n    that the assessee should have declared incomes with respect to its revenues and not with respect\n    to its cost as the whole agreement with Ms/ Google Ireland Ltd is a ploy to evade payment of\n    taxes in India.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_766\">16. The DRP erred in not appreciating the fact that it has been brought out in great detail in the\n    assessment order that the entire advertising business (Adwords) is treated as one enterprise in\n    India and that there is only one profit earned out of the said enterprise.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_767\">17. The DRP erred in not appreciating the fact that for the lack of a better alternative the total\n    Adwords revenue from the advertisers during the year was considered at Rs. 403,34,38,319/-\n    rather than consider the actual Adwords revenue of Rs. 241,71,64,576/- in line with the global\n    profits of Google Inc USA and the profit represents the entire profit of the enterprise to be\n    charged to tax in India in the hands of M/S Google India Pvt. Ltd. as well as M/s Google Ireland\n    Ltd.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_768\">18. The DRP erred in not appreciating the fact that Rs. 117.37 cores being the difference between\n                                                  IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                   IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                        IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                                 IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_389\">                                             Page 303 of 331</span>\n\n       the total turnover and the cost has been held to be the escaped profit of the enterprise in India\n       considering the fact that Google Ireland Ltd. has neither filed its return if income nor is willing\n       to admit any tax liability in India.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_769\">   19. For these and such other grounds that may be urged at the time of hearing, it is humbly prayed\n       that the order of the DRP be reversed and that of the Assessing Officer be restored.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_770\">   20. The appellate craves leave to add, to alter, to amend or delete any of the grounds that may be\n       urged at the time of hearing of the appeal.&quot;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_771\">258.        Ground Nos.1, 19 and 20 are general in nature and do not require any\nadjudication.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_772\">259. Ground Nos.2 to 7 challenges computation of ALP in respect of international\ntransactions entered into by the assessee-company with its AE. In the assessee&#x27;s appeal\nfor same assessment year 2009-10 in IT(TP)A No.69/Bang./2014, the issue of\ndetermination of ALP in respect of Information Technology (IT), ITeS and AdWords\ndistribution programme is remanded to the file of the AO/TPO for the purpose of\nundertaking fresh exercise of TP analysis . In these circumstances, the grounds of appeal\nare also set aside to the file of the AO/TPO as these grounds of appeal are consequential\nto the grounds of appeal filed by the assessee-company. Thus, the grounds of appeal are\npartly allowed for statistical purposes.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_773\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"26146277\" id=\"span_390\">260. Ground Nos.8 &amp; 9challenges the finding of the Hon&#x27;ble DRP to reduce the\ntelecommunication expenditure incurred in foreign currency from both export turnover\nand total turnover following the law <a href=\"/doc/743169/\" id=\"a_556\">laid down by</a> the Hon&#x27;ble jurisdictional High Court\nin <a href=\"/doc/26146277/\" id=\"a_557\">CIT vs. Tata Elxsi</a> (349 ITR 98).</span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_774\">261.   The issue in these grounds of appeal is no longer res integra as the Hon&#x27;ble Apex\nCourt in the case of CIT vs. HCLTechnologies Ltd.[(2018)(93 taxmann.com 33)(SC)]\n                                          IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                           IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"26146277\" id=\"span_391\">69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                         IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_392\">                                     Page 304 of 331</span>\n\nconfirmed the ratio of the decision of the Hon&#x27;ble Karnataka High Court in the case of\n<a href=\"/doc/26146277/\" id=\"a_558\">CIT vs. Tata Elxsi</a> (349 ITR 98). The relevant paragraphs are reproduced hereunder:</span>\n</p>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_386\">    <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"26146277\" id=\"span_393\">&quot;17. The similar nature of controversy, akin this case, arose before the\n    Karnataka High Court in <a href=\"/doc/26146277/\" id=\"a_559\">CIT v. Tata Elxsi Ltd.</a> [2012] 204 Taxman 321/17.\n    The issue before the Karnataka High Court was whether the Tribunal was\n    correct in holding that while computing relief under <a href=\"/doc/1904729/\" id=\"a_560\">Section10A</a> of the IT Act,\n    the amount of communication expenses should be excluded from the total\n    turnover if the same are reduced from the export turnover? While giving the\n    answer to the issue, the High Court, inter-alia, held that when a particular\n    word is not defined by the legislature and an ordinary meaning is to be\n    attributed to it, the said ordinary meaning is to be in conformity with the\n    context in which it is used. Hence, what is excluded from &#x27;export turnover&#x27;\n    must also be excluded from &#x27;total turnover&#x27;, since one of the components of\n    &#x27;total turnover&#x27; is export turnover. Any other interpretation would run counter\n    to the legislative intent and would be impermissible.</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_387\">    18. Accordingly, the formula for computation of the deduction under <a href=\"/doc/1904729/\" id=\"a_561\">Section\n    10A</a> of the Act would be as follows:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_388\">                             Export turnover as defined in Explanation 2 (IV) of\nExport Profit = total        <a href=\"/doc/1904729/\" id=\"a_562\">Section 10A</a> of IT Act\n                           \u00d7\nProfit of the Business        Export turnover as defined in Explanation 2(IV) of\n                              <a href=\"/doc/1904729/\" id=\"a_563\">Section 10A</a> of the IT Act + domestic sale proceeds\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_389\">    19. In the instant case, if the deductions on freight, telecommunication and\n    insurance attributable to the delivery of computer software under <a href=\"/doc/1904729/\" id=\"a_564\">Section10A</a>\n    of the IT Act are allowed only in Export Turnover but not from the Total\n    Turnover then, it would give rise to inadvertent, unlawful, meaningless and\n    illogical result which would cause grave injustice to the Respondent which\n    could have never been the intention of the legislature.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_390\">    20. Even in common parlance, when the object of the formula is to arrive at\n    the profit from export business, expenses excluded from export turnover have\n    to be excluded from total turnover also. Otherwise, any other interpretation\n    makes the formula unworkable and absurd. Hence, we are satisfied that such\n    deduction shall be allowed from the total turnover in same proportion as well.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_775\">                                               IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                     IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                              IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_394\">                                          Page 305 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_776\">      21. On the issue of expenses on technical services provided outside, we have\n      to follow the same principle of interpretation as followed in the case of\n      expenses of freight, telecommunication etc., otherwise the formula of\n      calculation would be futile. Hence, in the same way, expenses incurred in\n      foreign exchange for providing the technical services outside shall be allowed\n      to exclude from the total turnover.&quot;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_777\">      In the light of the decision of the Hon&#x27;ble Apex Court in the case of HCL\nTechnologies Ltd.(supra), the grounds of appeal filed by revenue are dismissed.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_778\">262. Grounds No.10 and 11 challenge the selection of comparables. Since in the\nassessee&#x27;s appeal, the very issue of TP analysis of entire functions undertaken by the\nassessee-company in respect of international transactions is remanded back to the file of\nthe AO, the question of adjudication on selection of comparable does not arise. Hence,\nthe grounds of appeal filed by the revenue are also partly allowed for statistical purposes.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_779\">263. Ground Nos.12 to 18 challenges the direction of the Hon&#x27;ble DRP to delete\naddition made on account of attribution of profits to Google Ireland Ltd. The AO, vide\npara.27 of his order, opined that the profits arising out of AdWords programme\nattributable to Google Ireland are not offered to tax. Therefore, he proceeded to compute\nthe amount of profits attributable to Google Ireland as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_391\">                                                Year Ended id=\"p_780\">264. Thus an amount of Rs.117,37,50,817/- was brought to tax as additional income\nattributable to Google Ireland purportedly to be in the capacity of agent of Google\nIreland Ltd. The addition was deleted by the Hon&#x27;ble DRP on the ground that the\nrequired adjustment was already made under the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/56593887/\" id=\"a_565\">section 92CA</a> and no\nfurther adjustment was required.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_781\">265. Before us, the revenue contended that the Hon&#x27;ble DRP is not justified in directing\ndeletion of this addition on the ground that this transaction is already subject to ALP\nadjustment<a href=\"/doc/56593887/\" id=\"a_566\"> u/s 92CA</a> of the Act. Learned CIT(D)R submitted that provisions of <a href=\"/doc/56593887/\" id=\"a_567\">section\n92CA</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1369261/\" id=\"a_568\">section 9</a> operate in different fields.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_782\">266. On the other hand, learned Senior Counsel for assessee vehemently contended that\nwhen the transaction between assessee and AE is subjected to arm&#x27;s length pricing, there\ncan be no further attribution of profits to PE and reliance in this regard was placed on\nthe decision of the Hon&#x27;ble Apex Court in the case of Asst.DIT vs. E-funds IT Solution\nInc .(399 ITR 34)(SC).\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_783\">                                            IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                             IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                  IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                           IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_397\">                                       Page 307 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_784\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"584977\" id=\"span_398\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"584977\" id=\"span_399\">267. We heard rival submissions and perused material on record. It is undisputed fact\nthat the transaction of AdWords programme between the assessee-company and Google\nIreland is subjected to bench marking. Recently, the Hon&#x27;ble Supreme Court in the case\nof E-funds IT Solution Inc (supra) reiterating the ratio laid down in the case of <a href=\"/doc/584977/\" id=\"a_569\">Director\nof Income-tax (International Taxation) v. Morgan Stanley &amp;Co.</a>(292 ITR 416) held that\nonce the TP analysis undertaken, there is no further need to attribute profit to PE.\nHowever<a href=\"/doc/584977/\" id=\"a_570\">, <a href=\"/doc/584977/\" id=\"a_571\">the said judgment</a></a> in the case of <a href=\"/doc/584977/\" id=\"a_572\">Morgan Stanley &amp; Co.</a> (supra) added a rider\nthat a situation would be different if TP analysis does not adequately reflect the functions\nperformed and risks assumed by the enterprise. In such a situation there would be a need\nto attribute profit to PE for those functions and risks that have not been considered.</span></span>\nTherefore, in the assessee&#x27;s appeal, since we have restored TP analysis afresh to the file\nof AO/TPO, this issue also is set aside to the file of the AO to make de novo assessment\nin light of principle <a href=\"/doc/584977/\" id=\"a_573\">laid down by</a> the Hon&#x27;ble Supreme Court in the case of Morgan\nStanley &amp; Co. and E-funds IT Solution Inc. (supra). Thus, these grounds of appeal are\nalso partly allowed.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_785\">268. In the result, the appeal filed by the revenue is partly allowed for statistical\npurposes.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_786\">269. Erred in alternatively holding the amount payable towards purchase of advertisement\n      space under the Agreement as either Royalties or Fees for Technical Services under\n      <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_579\">Article 12</a> of the India-Ireland Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_819\">23.    Erred in upholding that the Appellant was required to deduct tax at source from the\n       amount payable by the Appellant to Google Ireland Limited and in view of its failure\n       to deduct tax, the amount payable by the Appellant to Google Ireland Limited was\n       required to be disallowed<a href=\"/doc/420239/\" id=\"a_580\"> u/s 40(a)(i)</a> of the Act.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_820\">OTHERS\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_821\">24.     Erred in considering the eligible MAT credit<a href=\"/doc/1321037/\" id=\"a_581\"> u/s 11</a> <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_582\">5JAA</a> of the Act at Rs 565,306 as\n        against the eligible brought forward MAT credit of Rs 8,81,87,191.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_822\">25.     Erred in considering the refund already issued to the Company<a href=\"/doc/184741818/\" id=\"a_583\"> u/s 143(1)</a> at Rs\n        9,37,80,480 instead of the actual amount of refund of Rs 6,58,06,480 received bythe\n        Appellant thereby increasing the total tax demand.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_823\">25.   Having erred in considering the refund amount of Rs 9,37,80,480, erred in levying the\n      interest<a href=\"/doc/132697269/\" id=\"a_584\"> u/s 234D</a> of the Act on refund amount of Rs 9,37,80,480 as against the\n      actualrefund received of Rs 6,58,06,480.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_824\">                                                IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                                 IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                      IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                               IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_404\">                                           Page 312 of 331</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_825\">26.    Erred in imposing the levy of interest<a href=\"/doc/380949/\" id=\"a_585\"> u/s 234B</a> of the Act.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_826\">27.    Erred in initiating the penalty proceedings initiated<a href=\"/doc/144487481/\" id=\"a_586\"> u/s 271(1)(c)</a> of the Act.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_827\">The Appellant craves, to consider each of the above grounds of appeal\nindependentlywithout prejudice to one another and craves leave to add, alter, delete or modify\nall or any ofthe above grounds of appeal.&quot;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_828\">270.        Ground Nos.1 to 14 relates to TP analysis and selection of comparable etc. In\nthe identical facts, in assessee&#x27;s own case for AY 2009-10, the issues raised in these\ngrounds of appeal were restored to the file of AO/TPO for the purpose of undertaking\nfresh TP analysis etc. For parity of reasons given therein, these grounds of appeal are\nrestored to the file of the AO with similar directions.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_829\">271.         Ground No.15 challenges rejection of book results. As stated by us in the\nassessee&#x27;s appeal for assessment year 2009-10 in IT(TP)A No.69/Bang)/2014, though\nthe AO has stated that book results are rejected, in effect there is no rejection of book\nresults as the profit even after recasting the P&amp;L Account remained the same. Thus, this\nground of appeal becomes academic in nature and is dismissed as such.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_830\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"434169\" id=\"span_405\">272. Ground Nos.16 &amp; 17 challenges finding of lower authorities in reducing\ncommunication expenditure only from export turnover. The issue in these grounds of\nappeal is no longer res integra as the issue is decided in favour of the assessee-company\nin the case of <a href=\"/doc/434169/\" id=\"a_587\">HCLTechnologies Ltd.HCL Technology</a> (supra). Thus these grounds of\nappeal are allowed.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_831\">273. Ground Nos.18 to 23 challenges the addition<a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_588\"> u/s 40(a)(ia)</a> of the Act on the ground\nthat the assessee-company had not deducted TDS on payments of royalty made to\n                                              IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                               IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                    IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                             IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_406\">                                         Page 313 of 331</span>\n\nGoogle Ireland Ltd. For the detailed reasons given by us in the assessee&#x27;s appeal for\nassessment year 2009-10 in IT(TP)A No.69/Bang/2014 we confirm the addition made<a href=\"/doc/1918609/\" id=\"a_589\">\nu/s 40(a)(ia)</a> of the Act. Accordingly, these grounds of appeal are dismissed.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_832\">274. The other grounds of appeal are consequential in nature and do not require\nadjudication.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_833\">275. Accordingly this appeal is partly allowed for statistical purposes.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_940\">305. In the result, assessee&#x27;s appeals IT(IT)A No.1190/Bang/2014 is dismissed and\n\nIT(IT)A No. 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017, IT(TP)A Nos. 374/Bang/2013, 68/Bang/2015,\n\n69/Bang/2014, 387/Bang/2017, 559/Bang/2016 are partly allowed for statistical\n\npurposes, and Revenue&#x27;s appeals IT(IT)A No. 1295/Bang/2014 is allowed for statistical\n                                          IT(IT)ANos.1190, 1295/Bang/2014, 2845, 949, 950/Bang/2017,\n                                           IT(TP)A Nos.374, 466/Bang/2013, 68, 205, 1299/Bang/2015,\n                                                IT(TP)A Nos. 69, 191/Bang/2014, 559, 881/Bang/2016,\n                                                                         IT(TP)A No.387/Bang/2017\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_427\">                                     Page 331 of 331</span>\n\npurposes and IT(TP)A No.466/Bang/2013 is dismissed.                  In Revenue&#x27;s appeal,\n\nIT(TP)A No. 191/Bang/2014, No.205/Bang/2015, 881/Bang/2016 are partly allowed for\n\nstatistical purposes and ITA No.1299/Bang/2015 is allowed for statistical purposes.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_941\">      Pronounced in the open court on 11th May, 2018.\n</p>\n\n\n<pre id=\"pre_25\">              Sd/-                                      Sd/-\n      (INTURI RAMA RAO)                        (SUNIL KUMAR YADAV)\n       Accountant Member                           Judicial Member\n\nBangalore.\nDated: 11th May, 2018.\n/NShylu/*\n\n\nCopy to:\n 1. Appellants        2. Respondent\n 3. CIT               4. Guard file\n                                                       By order\n\n\n                                              Sr. Private Secretary,\n                                                ITAT, Bangalore.\n </pre>",

    "doc": "<h2 class=\"doc_title\">Navtej Singh Johar vs Union Of India Ministry Of Law And ... on 6 September, 2018</h2>\n\n<h3 class=\"doc_citations\">Equivalent citations: AIR 2018 SUPREME COURT 4321, AIR 2018 SC( CRI) 1169, (2018) 4 MAD LJ(CRI) 306, (2018) 4 BOMCR(CRI) 289, (2018) 10 SCALE 386, 2019 (1) SCC (CRI) 1, (2018) 4 CURCRIR 1, (2018) 105 ALLCRIC 258, (2018) 3 ALLCRIR 3234, (2018) 3 CRIMES 233, 2018 (10) SCC 1, 2018 (190) AIC (SOC) 1 (SC), 252 (2018) DLT 1 (CN)(SC), 2018 (3) KCCR SN 325 (SC), 2018 (4) KLT SN 1 (KER), AIRONLINE 2018 SC 146</h3>\n\n<h3 class=\"doc_bench\">Bench: <a href=\"/search/?formInput=benchid:d-misra\">Chief Justice</a>, <a href=\"/search/?formInput=benchid:r-f-nariman\">Rohinton Fali Nariman</a>, <a href=\"/search/?formInput=benchid:a-m-khanwilkar\">A.M. Khanwilkar</a>, <a href=\"/search/?formInput=benchid:d-y-chandrachud\">D.Y. Chandrachud</a>, <a href=\"/search/?formInput=benchid:i-malhotra\">Indu Malhotra</a></h3>\n\n<pre id=\"pre_1\">                                                         1\n\n\n                                                                       REPORTABLE\n\n                                       IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA\n\n                                       CRIMINAL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION\n\n                                   WRIT PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO. 76 OF 2016\n\n                         NAVTEJ SINGH JOHAR &amp; ORS.                     \u2026Petitioner(s)\n\n                                                     VERSUS\n                         UNION OF INDIA\n                         THR. SECRETARY\n                         MINISTRY OF LAW AND JUSTICE                    \u2026Respondent(s)\n\n                                                       WITH\n\n                                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 572 OF 2016\n                                   WRIT PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO. 88 OF 2018\n                                   WRIT PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO. 100 OF 2018\n                                   WRIT PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO. 101 OF 2018\n                                   WRIT PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO. 121 OF 2018\n\n                                                 JUDGMENT\n</pre>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_1\">                         Dipak Misra, CJI (for himself and A.M. Khanwilkar, J.)\n\n\n                                                  CONTENTS\n                      S. No(s).                      Heading                      Page No(s)\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_1\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n                         A.       Introduction\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026                       3-11\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_2\">Digitally signed by</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_3\">CHETAN KUMAR</span>\n\n                                  The Reference\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\nDate: 2018.09.06\n15:12:35 IST\nReason:                  B.                                                         11-15\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_4\">\f                            2</span>\n\n\nC.   Submissions on behalf of the petitioners\u2026\u2026       15-30\n\nD.   Submissions on behalf of the respondents\n     and other intervenors.\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026.\u2026               31-44\n\nE.   Decisions in Naz Foundation and Suresh\n     Koushal\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026..\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026..                     45-48\n\nF.   Other judicial pronouncements on <a href=\"/doc/1836974/\" id=\"a_1\">Section 377</a>\n     IPC \u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026.\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026..                       48-57\n\nG.   The Constitution \u2013 an organic charter of\n     progressive rights\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026                  57-64\n\nH.   Transformative constitutionalism and the\n     rights of LGBT community\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026.               65-74\n\nI.   Constitutional morality and <a href=\"/doc/1836974/\" id=\"a_2\">Section 377</a> IPC\u2026.    74-81\n\nJ.   Perspective of human dignity\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026             81-89\n\nK.   Sexual orientation\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026.                 89-96\n\nL.   Privacy and its concomitant aspects\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026...      96-111\n\nM.   Doctrine of progressive realization of\n     rights\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026.                      111-118\n\nN.   International perspective\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026.             118\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_2\">     (i)     United States\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026               118-122\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_3\">     (ii)    Canada\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026..                  123-125\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_5\">     (iii)   South Africa\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026.                125</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_4\">     (iv)    United Kingdom\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026.               126-127\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_5\">     (v)     Other Courts/Jurisdictions\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026..       127-129\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_6\">\f                                     3</span>\n\n\nO.       Comparative analysis of <a href=\"/doc/623254/\" id=\"a_3\">Section 375</a> and\n         <a href=\"/doc/1836974/\" id=\"a_4\">Section 377</a> IPC\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026.\u2026                              129-140\n\nP.       The litmus test for survival of <a href=\"/doc/1836974/\" id=\"a_5\">Section 377</a>\n         IPC\u2026\u2026.\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026                                    140-156\n\nQ.       Conclusions\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026                                 156-166\n\n\n\nA.    Introduction\n\n\n      Not for nothing, the great German thinker, Johann Wolfgang\n\nvon Goethe, had said, \u2015I am what I am, so take me as I am\u2016 and\n\nsimilarly, Arthur Schopenhauer had pronounced, \u2015No one can escape\n\nfrom their individuality\u2016. In this regard, it is profitable to quote a few\n\nlines from John Stuart Mill:-\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1\">      \u2015But society has now fairly got the better of individuality;\n      and the danger which threatens human nature is not\n      the excess, but the deficiency of personal impulses\n      and preferences.\u2016\n\n      The emphasis on the unique being of an individual is the salt of\n\nhis/her life. Denial of self-expression is inviting death. Irreplaceability\n\nof individuality and identity is grant of respect to self. This realization\n\nis one\u2018s signature and self-determined design. One defines oneself.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_2\">That is the glorious form of individuality. In the present case, our\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_7\">\f                                     4</span>\n\n\ndeliberation and focus on the said concept shall be from various\n\nspectrums.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_6\">2.    Shakespeare through one of his characters in a play says\n\n\u2015What\u2018s in a name? That which we call a rose by any other name\n\nwould smell as sweet\u2016. The said phrase, in its basic sense, conveys\n\nthat what really matters is the essential qualities of the substance and\n\nthe fundamental characteristics of an entity but not the name by\n\nwhich it or a person is called. Getting further deeper into the meaning,\n\nit is understood that the name may be a convenient concept for\n\nidentification but the essence behind the same is the core of identity.\n\nSans identity, the name only remains a denotative term. Therefore,\n\nthe identity is pivotal to one\u2018s being. Life bestows honour on it and\n\nfreedom of living, as a facet of life, expresses genuine desire to have\n\nit. The said desire, one is inclined to think, is satisfied by the\n\nconception of constitutional recognition, and hence, emphasis is laid\n\non the identity of an individual which is conceived under the\n\nConstitution. And the sustenance of identity is the filament of life. It is\n\nequivalent to authoring one\u2018s own life script where freedom broadens\n\neveryday. Identity is equivalent to divinity.\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_8\">\f                                    5</span>\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_7\">3.    The overarching ideals of individual autonomy and liberty,\n\nequality for all sans discrimination of any kind, recognition of identity\n\nwith dignity and privacy of human beings constitute the cardinal four\n\ncorners of our monumental Constitution forming the concrete\n\nsubstratum of our fundamental rights that has eluded certain sections\n\nof our society who are still living in the bondage of dogmatic social\n\nnorms, prejudiced notions, rigid stereotypes, parochial mindset and\n\nbigoted perceptions. Social exclusion, identity seclusion and isolation\n\nfrom the social mainstream are still the stark realities faced by\n\nindividuals today and it is only when each and every individual is\n\nliberated from the shackles of such bondage and is able to work\n\ntowards full development of his/her personality that we can call\n\nourselves a truly free society.    The first step on the long path to\n\nacceptance of the diversity and variegated hues that nature has\n\ncreated has to be taken now by vanquishing the enemies of prejudice\n\nand injustice and undoing the wrongs done so as to make way for a\n\nprogressive and inclusive realisation of social and economic rights\n\nembracing all and to begin a dialogue for ensuring equal rights and\n\nopportunities for the \u2015less than equal\u2016 sections of the society. We\n\nhave to bid adieu to the perceptions, stereotypes and prejudices\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_9\">\f                                      6</span>\n\n\ndeeply ingrained in the societal mindset so as to usher in inclusivity in\n\nall spheres and empower all citizens alike without any kind of\n\nalienation and discrimination.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_8\">4.        The natural identity of an individual should be treated to be\n\nabsolutely essential to his being. What nature gives is natural. That\n\nis called nature within. Thus, that part of the personality of a person\n\nhas to be respected and not despised or looked down upon. The said\n\ninherent nature and the associated natural impulses in that regard are\n\nto be accepted. Non-acceptance of it by any societal norm or notion\n\nand punishment by law on some obsolete idea and idealism affects\n\nthe kernel of the identity of an individual. Destruction of individual\n\nidentity would tantamount to crushing of intrinsic dignity that\n\ncumulatively encapsulates the values of privacy, choice, freedom of\n\nspeech and other expressions. It can be viewed from another angle.\n\nAn individual in exercise of his choice may feel that he/she should be\n\nleft alone but no one, and we mean, no one, should impose solitude\n\non him/her.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_9\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"195593310\" id=\"span_10\">5.        The eminence of identity has been luculently stated in <a href=\"/doc/195593310/\" id=\"a_6\">National\n\nLegal Services Authority v. Union of India</a> and others1, popularly\n\n\n1   (2014) 5 SCC 438\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_11\">\f                                   7</span>\n\n\nknown as NALSA case, wherein the Court was dwelling upon the\n\nstatus of identity of the transgenders. Radhakrishnan, J., after\n\nreferring to catena of judgments and certain International Covenants,\n\nopined that gender identity is one of the most fundamental aspects of\n\nlife which refers to a person\u2018s intrinsic sense of being male, female or\n\ntransgender or transsexual person. A person\u2018s sex is usually\n\nassigned at birth, but a relatively small group of persons may be born\n\nwith bodies which incorporate both or certain aspects of both male\n\nand female physiology. The learned Judge further observed that at\n\ntimes, genital anatomy problems may arise in certain persons in the\n\nsense that their innate perception of themselves is not in conformity\n\nwith the sex assigned to them at birth and may include pre-and post-\n\noperative transsexual persons and also persons who do not choose\n\nto undergo or do not have access to operation and also include\n\npersons who cannot undergo successful operation. Elaborating\n\nfurther, he said:-</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_3\">      \u2015Gender identity refers to each person\u2018s deeply felt\n      internal and individual experience of gender, which\n      may or may not correspond with the sex assigned at\n      birth, including the personal sense of the body which\n      may involve a freely chosen, modification of bodily\n      appearance or functions by medical, surgical or other\n      means and other expressions of gender, including\n      dress, speech and mannerisms. Gender identity,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_12\">\f                                    8</span>\n\n\n      therefore, refers to an individual\u2018s self-identification as\n      a man, woman, transgender or other identified\n      category.\u2016\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_4\">6.    Adverting to the concept of discrimination, he stated:-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_5\">      \u2015The discrimination on the ground of \u2015sex\u2016 under\n      <a href=\"/doc/609295/\" id=\"a_7\">Articles 15</a> and <a href=\"/doc/211089/\" id=\"a_8\">16</a>, therefore, includes discrimination\n      on the ground of gender identity. The expression \u2015sex\u2016\n      used in <a href=\"/doc/609295/\" id=\"a_9\">Articles 15</a> and <a href=\"/doc/211089/\" id=\"a_10\">16</a> is not just limited to\n      biological sex of male or female, but intended to\n      include people who consider themselves to be neither\n      male nor female.\u2016\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_6\">7.    Dealing with the legality of transgender identity, Radhakrishnan,\n\nJ. ruled:-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_7\">      \u2015The self-identified gender can be either male or\n      female or a third gender. Hijras are identified as\n      persons of third gender and are not identified either as\n      male or female. Gender identity, as already indicated,\n      refers to a person\u2018s internal sense of being male,\n      female or a transgender, for example hijras do not\n      identify as female because of their lack of female\n      genitalia or lack of reproductive capability. This\n      distinction makes them separate from both male and\n      female genders and they consider themselves neither\n      man nor woman, but a \u2015third gender\u2016.\u2016\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_8\">8.    Sikri, J., in his concurring opinion, dwelling upon the rights of\n\ntransgenders, laid down that gender identification is an essential\n\ncomponent which is required for enjoying civil rights by the\n\ncommunity. It is only with this recognition that many rights attached to\n\nthe sexual recognition as \u2015third gender\u2016 would be available to the said\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_13\">\f                                     9</span>\n\n\ncommunity more meaningfully viz. the right to vote, the right to own\n\nproperty, the right to marry, the right to claim a formal identity through\n\na passport and a ration card, a driver\u2018s licence, the right to education,\n\nemployment, health and so on. Emphasising on the aspect of human\n\nrights, he observed:-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_9\">      \u2015\u2026there seems to be no reason why a transgender\n      must be denied of basic human rights which includes\n      right to life and liberty with dignity, right to privacy and\n      freedom of expression, right to education and\n      empowerment, right against violence, right against\n      exploitation and right against discrimination. The\n      Constitution has fulfilled its duty of providing rights to\n      transgenders. Now it is time for us to recognise this\n      and to extend and interpret the Constitution in such a\n      manner to ensure a dignified life for transgender\n      people. All this can be achieved if the beginning is\n      made with the recognition of TG as third gender.\u2016\n\n      The aforesaid judgment, as is manifest, lays focus on\n\ninalienable \u2015gender identity\u2016 and correctly connects with human rights\n\nand the constitutionally guaranteed right to life and liberty with dignity.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_10\">It lays stress on the judicial recognition of such rights as an\n\ninextricable component of <a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_11\">Article 21</a> of the Constitution and decries\n\nany discrimination as that would offend <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_12\">Article 14</a>, the \u2015fon juris\u2016 of\n\nour Constitution.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_11\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"100472805\" id=\"span_14\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"58730926\" id=\"span_15\">9.
